[E02] HYDRAULIC ENGINEERING -- E02 is a CPC subclass under Fixed Constructions that covers hydraulic engineering. It includes the design, construction and operation of structures and systems that use fluid power such as dams, canals, pipelines, pumps, turbines and other water-based machinery. This subclass groups inventions related to managing, controlling and converting hydraulic energy.
[E02B] HYDRAULIC ENGINEERING -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Man-made devices for using or controlling natural bodies of water;  Artificial bodies of water.
[E02B 1/00] Equipment or apparatus for, or methods of, general hydraulic engineering -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Big scale hydraulic projects for controlling water or water level (groundwater, seawater, rivers, lakes) and small scale models for such projects.  Methods and devices relating to use or control of ice, e.g. protection against ice strains or use of ice formation.  Small-scale models for modelling big scale projects.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Protection of offshore constructions against ice-loads -> E02B17/0021 - Ice-structures as artificial islands -> E02B17/028 - Computer-models of water flow -> G06F
[E02B 1/003] Mechanically induced gas or liquid streams in seas, lakes or water-courses for forming weirs or breakwaters; making or keeping water surfaces free from ice, aerating or circulating water, e.g. screens of air-bubbles against sludge formation or salt water entry, pump-assisted water circulation  moving sediments  E02B3/023 ; for ice breakers  B63B35/08 ; aerating or circulating water in fish-tanks  A01K63/042 ; purification of waste water with addition of air  C02F ; distributing gases in liquids in general  B01F23/20 -- E02B 1/003 covers devices or methods that create mechanically driven streams of gas or liquid in seas, lakes or rivers to shape hydraulic structures, prevent ice, aerate water, circulate sediments, or combat sludge and saltwater intrusion. The classification includes applications such as air-bubble screens, pump-assisted circulation and related techniques for fish-tank aeration or wastewater treatment.
[E02B 1/006] Arresting, diverting or chasing away fish in water-courses or water intake ducts, seas or lakes, e.g. fish barrages, deterrent devices  E02B8/085  takes precedence ; Devices for cleaning fish barriers
[E02B 1/02] Hydraulic models -- E02B 1/02 covers hydraulic models, which are scaled physical or computational representations used to study the behavior of water flow and related forces in hydraulic systems. These models are employed in general hydraulic engineering to analyze and predict performance of structures such as canals, dams, or pipelines.
[E02B 11/00] Drainage of soil, e.g. for agricultural purposes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and devices for horizontal drainage of ground in general.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Improving or stabilising soil by managing underground water flow -> E02D3/00 - Drainage of sewage into the ground -> E03F1/002
[E02B 11/005] Drainage conduits -- The entry E02B 11/005 pertains to drainage conduits used in hydraulic engineering for removing excess water from soil, especially in agricultural settings. It includes pipes, culverts and similar channels designed to convey water away from fields, encompassing their construction, placement and related features.
[E02B 11/02] Drainage device- laying apparatus, e.g. drainage ploughs -- E02B 11/02 pertains to devices for laying drainage systems in soil, such as drainage ploughs and similar apparatus that create subsurface channels for agricultural or land-drainage purposes. It belongs to the fixed constructions group within hydraulic engineering.
[E02B 13/00] Irrigation ditches, i.e. gravity flow, open channel water distribution systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Open free flow water channels for irrigation;  Devices for sequential water distribution, e.g. between several irrigation channels or seepage drains.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Retaining waterborne material in irrigation canals -> E02B5/085 - Other irrigation methods and systems, e.g. for agriculture, horticulture -> A01G25/00 - Built-in irrigation systems for sports fields -> E01C13/083 - Open sewage channels -> E03F3/046
[E02B 13/02] Closures for irrigation conduits -- This subclass covers devices that shut or regulate flow in open-channel irrigation systems, such as gates, weirs, plugs, or other closure mechanisms fitted to gravity-flow ditches and canals to start, stop, or control water distribution.
[E02B 15/00] Cleaning or keeping clear the surface of open water -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Floating barriers and devices for controlling and/or collecting oil pollution on open water surfaces, e.g. sea, harbours, lakes, rivers).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Separation in general -> B01D - General constructional aspects of waterborne vessels -> B63B - Emptying sunken ships -> B63C7/006 - Covering oil spills on well heads -> E21B43/0122
[E02B 15/02] from ice
[E02B 15/04] Devices for cleaning or keeping clear the surface of open water from oil or like floating materials by separating or removing these materials -- The code E02B 15/04 falls under the fixed-construction branch of hydraulic engineering and covers devices that clean or keep clear the surface of open water by separating or removing oil, grease or similar floating materials; examples include floating skimmers, oil-collecting booms and absorbent mats designed to collect and extract the contaminants from the water.
[E02B 15/041] Devices for distributing materials, e.g. absorbed or magnetic particles over a surface of open water to remove the oil, with or without means for picking up the treated oil  E02B15/042  takes precedence -- E02B 15/041 covers devices that spread absorbent or magnetic particles across the surface of open water to collect oil, optionally combined with mechanisms for retrieving the oil-laden material. It is a subcategory of fixed hydraulic engineering constructions for cleaning water surfaces.
[E02B 15/042] Devices for removing the oil by combustion with or without means for picking up the oil -- Devices that eliminate oil films on water by burning the oil, optionally after collecting it with a pickup mechanism, are covered by this subclass. The technology is applied in harbors, canals, or other open-water settings to restore surface cleanliness and reduce environmental impact.
[E02B 15/043] Devices or methods for removing oil by means of freezing -- E02B 15/043 covers apparatus and processes that freeze oil on water surfaces to facilitate its removal, typically using chilling plates, freezing agents or cold water jets. The frozen oil can then be mechanically collected or floated for retrieval, aiding in oil spill remediation and waterway cleaning.
[E02B 15/045] Separating means for recovering oil floating on a surface of open water  E02B15/048  takes precedence; separation in general  B01D -- E02B 15/045 covers devices designed to separate and recover oil that has spilled and remains on the surface of open water. It falls under the fixed constructions branch of hydraulic engineering, specifically the category for cleaning or keeping open-water surfaces clear. The more general separation class E02B15/048 and the chemical-engineering class B01D are considered prior art.
[E02B 15/046] Collection of oil using vessels, i.e. boats, barges -- E02B 15/046 is a CPC subclass that deals with the collection of oil from the surface of open water using floating vessels such as boats or barges, forming part of hydraulic-engineering fixed-construction methods for cleaning or keeping water surfaces clear.
[E02B 15/047] provided with an oil collecting boom arranged on at least one side of the hull -- A vessel whose hull includes an oil-collecting boom positioned on at least one side, allowing the device to intercept and retain oil that reaches the water surface while the ship moves. The arrangement is used in fixed-construction hydraulic engineering to clean or keep open water clear of oil contaminants.
[E02B 15/048] Oil collectors moved over the water skimming the water surface -- E02B 15/048 pertains to devices that collect oil from the surface of open water by moving across it. The entry falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering and concerns cleaning or keeping clear the surface of open water.
[E02B 15/06] Barriers therefor -- E02B 15/06 relates to barriers employed in hydraulic engineering to clean or keep the surface of open water clear, such as floating screens, booms, or protective structures that block debris, algae, or other floating material.
[E02B 15/08] Devices for reducing the polluted area without removing the material -- This subgroup covers apparatus that limit the spread of contaminants on water surfaces by containment, dispersion control, or absorption without physically extracting the pollutant, such as floating booms, skirts, covers or chemical neutralizers. They are intended to reduce the polluted area and prevent further environmental damage.
[E02B 15/0807] with stabilising elements -- E02B 15/0807 covers hydraulic engineering fixed constructions designed to clean or keep clear the surface of open water by using stabilising elements such as floating booms, cages, or anchored screens. The stabilising components prevent drift and maintain the position of the cleaning device during operation.
[E02B 15/0814] with underwater curtains -- The code E02B 15/0814 classifies hydraulic-engineering devices used to clean or keep clear the surface of open water by employing underwater curtains. Such curtains are positioned below the waterline to guide debris, pollutants or algae away from the surface, facilitating water-surface maintenance without surface-level barriers.
[E02B 15/0821] adapted for protection against fire or extreme heat -- The code E02B 15/0821 covers hydraulic-engineering constructions that clean or keep open-water surfaces clear and are specially adapted to resist fire or extreme heat, such as fire-proof floating barriers, heat-resistant pumps, or heat-shielded water-treatment structures used in fixed installations. It falls under the Fixed Constructions branch of the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[E02B 15/0828] fixed to the side of a boat for containing small leaks in the hull -- The CPC entry E02B 15/0828 covers devices that are fixed to the side of a boat to seal small hull leaks, such as patches, clamps or other sealing members that are attached externally to contain water ingress. These solutions fall under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering, specifically for cleaning or keeping the surface of open water clear by preventing leakage.
[E02B 15/0835] fixed to permanent structure, e.g. harbour wall or river bank -- The E02B 15/0835 subclass covers devices and methods for cleaning or keeping clear the surface of open water that are attached permanently to a fixed structure such as a harbour wall, river bank, or other fixed hydraulic works. It includes installations integrated into the structure for debris removal, oil spill containment, or water-surface cleaning. This subclass falls under the broader categories of fixed constructions and hydraulic engineering.
[E02B 15/0842] adapted to be towed for operation -- This subclass covers fixed hydraulic engineering structures used to clean or clear the surface of open water that are designed to be towed while operating. It includes floating skimmers or similar devices mounted on a fixed frame that are pulled by a towing vessel during use.
[E02B 15/085] Details of connectors -- E02B 15/085 covers the design and specifications of connectors employed in hydraulic structures that maintain clear water surfaces, such as those used in fountains, canals, or dams. The classification addresses the geometry, materials, sealing methods and attachment mechanisms of these fittings within fixed constructions.
[E02B 15/0857] Buoyancy material -- The CPC entry E02B 15/0857 covers materials that impart buoyancy to devices used for cleaning or keeping open-water surfaces clear, such as floating skimmers, booms and barriers in hydraulic engineering. These buoyant elements are integral to fixed construction systems that operate on the water's surface to collect debris or oil.
[E02B 15/0864] Air  E02B15/0878  takes precedence
[E02B 15/0871] self-inflating barriers -- Self-inflating barriers are expandable structures that automatically fill with water or air to form a temporary wall, used to contain, divert or protect open-water surfaces during cleaning, flood control or debris removal. They are installed in hydraulic engineering projects where rapid deployment and easy removal are needed, providing a flexible solution for maintaining clear water channels.
[E02B 15/0878] Air and water -- The entry covers devices or processes that employ a mixture of air and water to clean the surface of open water bodies such as canals, lakes or harbours, removing debris, algae or other contaminants. It includes jets, aeration systems or spray mechanisms that generate bubbles or water streams to lift and transport material for collection.
[E02B 15/0885] Foam -- Foam is employed in hydraulic engineering for fixed constructions to clean or keep the surface of open water clear, such as in canals, reservoirs or other water bodies. The method involves spreading a suitable foam on the water surface to trap and remove contaminants, oil films, debris or algae, thereby improving visibility and water quality. This technology falls under CPC class E02B 15/0885.
[E02B 15/0892] using expanded polystyrene foam -- E02B 15/0892 relates to hydraulic engineering methods for maintaining clear water surfaces, specifically the use of expanded polystyrene foam to collect or remove debris, algae, or oil from open water bodies. The technique involves deploying foam mats or balloons that adhere to contaminants, facilitating their removal.
[E02B 15/10] Devices for removing the material from the surface -- The E02B 15/10 subclass relates to devices that extract or clear material floating on the surface of open water, such as debris, oil or algae, using hydraulic or mechanical means. It pertains to fixed constructions in waterways, ports or harbors that keep the surface clear for navigation or environmental purposes.
[E02B 15/101] Means floating loosely on the water absorbing the oil  absorbing materials  C02F -- E02B 15/101 covers devices that float loosely on water and contain oil-absorbing materials, intended for cleaning or clearing the surface of open water. Such means are used to collect spilled oil by passive flotation and absorption, falling under the hydraulic engineering sub-section of fixed constructions.
[E02B 15/102] Discs -- E02B 15/102 covers disc-type devices employed in hydraulic engineering to clean or keep the surface of open water clear, such as floating or rotating discs that collect debris, oil, or algae. The classification applies to designs, installations and operation of these disc mechanisms within fixed water-treatment constructions.
[E02B 15/103] Rotary drums -- The CPC code E02B 15/103 covers rotary drum devices used in hydraulic engineering for cleaning or maintaining the surface of open water. These drums rotate to collect debris, algae or other contaminants, often as part of floating skimmers, aerators or surface-cleaning installations that keep waterways clear.
[E02B 15/104] Conveyors; Paddle wheels; Endless belts  E02B15/101  takes precedence -- The CPC subclass E02B 15/104 classifies fixed hydraulic devices that use conveyors, paddle wheels or endless belts to move water and remove debris, keeping open water surfaces clean. It includes apparatus such as chain-driven or belt-driven cleaning systems and paddle-wheel mechanisms employed in harbors, canals or lakes.
[E02B 15/105] Archimedian screws -- E02B 15/105 covers Archimedean screws used in hydraulic engineering for moving water to clean or clear open water surfaces. The device consists of a helical screw inside a cylinder that lifts water upward and can be employed for dewatering, aeration, or debris removal.
[E02B 15/106] Overflow skimmers with suction heads; suction heads -- The class E02B 15/106 pertains to overflow skimmers that incorporate suction heads for removing floating material from the surface of open water. It falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering and relates to devices that clean or keep water surfaces clear by drawing debris into a suction chamber.
[E02B 15/107] Whirling means forming a vortex in the water; cyclones -- E02B 15/107 covers fixed hydraulic devices that create a vortex in water, using whirling or cyclonic action to agitate the fluid and lift debris for surface cleaning or to keep the water clear. The technology is applied in structures such as cyclonic cleaners, vortex generators or whirlpool pumps employed in open-water treatment and maintenance.
[E02B 15/108] Ejection means -- E02B 15/108 pertains to devices that eject fluid or other media to clean or maintain the surface of open water, such as jets, pumps or spray mechanisms used in hydraulic engineering for debris removal or surface clearing. This falls under the fixed-construction category for water-related cleaning systems.
[E02B 17/00] Artificial islands mounted on piles or like supports, e.g. platforms on raisable legs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Constructional features of ground-based offshore constructions;  Method for their installation or removal.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Anchoring floating platforms -> B63B21/00 - Floating or semi-submersible offshore platforms -> B63B35/44 - Construction methods for floating platforms -> B63B73/00 - Constructional features of underwater reservoirs -> B65D88/78
[E02B 17/0004] Nodal points -- The entry covers nodal points, i.e., the intersection points of structural members or support elements in artificial islands built on piles or similar supports such as platforms on raisable legs. It pertains to hydraulic engineering and fixed constructions, detailing design, reinforcement and connection methods at these nodes.
[E02B 17/0008] Methods for grouting offshore structures; apparatus therefor  cementing boreholes  E21B33/13
[E02B 17/0013] Tube closures for releasable sealing hollow tubes -- E02B 17/0013 relates to releasable sealing devices that close hollow tubes on fixed hydraulic structures such as artificial islands, platforms or piles. The tube closures enable quick connection, isolation or maintenance of pipelines, conduits or other utility tubes mounted on these supports.
[E02B 17/0017] Means for protecting offshore constructions -- Means for protecting offshore constructions cover devices and systems designed to safeguard structures such as artificial islands, platforms, or other installations mounted on piles or similar supports in marine environments. These may include protective barriers, anchoring arrangements, corrosion control, impact absorbers, or other measures that prevent damage from waves, currents, collisions, or environmental degradation.
[E02B 17/0021] against ice-loads -- The CPC entry E02B 17/0021 covers artificial islands, platforms or similar structures that are mounted on piles, legs or other supports and are specifically designed to withstand ice loads. It falls under the fixed-construction branch of hydraulic engineering, addressing the structural measures required for ice-prone water bodies.
[E02B 17/0026] against corrosion -- E02B 17/0026 relates to methods and materials for preventing or mitigating corrosion of artificial islands, platforms and other structures supported on piles or similar foundations in hydraulic engineering. It includes protective coatings, cathodic protection systems, corrosion-resistant alloys and maintenance procedures designed for marine environments.
[E02B 17/003] Fenders -- E02B 17/003 covers fender devices that protect structures such as artificial islands, offshore platforms and other fixed hydraulic constructions mounted on piles or similar supports from impact with vessels or floating objects. These fenders absorb kinetic energy to prevent damage to the installation and surrounding works.
[E02B 17/0034] Maintenance, repair or inspection of offshore constructions -- The code E02B 17/0034 covers activities related to keeping offshore structures-such as artificial islands, pile-mounted platforms or raisable-leg installations-operational. It includes procedures for routine maintenance, repair work and inspection tasks required to preserve the integrity and safety of these hydraulic engineering constructions.
[E02B 17/02] placed by lowering the supporting construction to the bottom, e.g. with subsequent fixing thereto -- E02B 17/02 covers the installation of artificial islands or offshore platforms that are supported on piles, legs or similar structures and are positioned by lowering the support to the seabed, often followed by a fixing operation. The classification includes methods and equipment for placing such constructions, for example removable platforms on raisable legs that are set down and anchored on the ocean floor.
[E02B 17/021] with relative movement between supporting construction and platform -- E02B 17/021 classifies fixed hydraulic-engineering constructions where an artificial island or platform is mounted on piles, legs or similar supports and includes features that permit relative movement between the supporting structure and the platform, such as adjustable or raisable legs allowing the platform to shift vertically or horizontally.

[E02B 17/024] shock absorbing means for the supporting construction -- The code E02B 17/024 covers devices that dampen or absorb dynamic forces acting on fixed hydraulic structures such as artificial islands, pile-supported platforms, and raisable-leg installations. It includes springs, dampers, elastomeric pads or other mechanisms designed to protect the supporting construction from wave impact, vibration or settlement stresses.
[E02B 17/025] Reinforced concrete structures  E02B17/021  takes precedence -- E02B 17/025 pertains to reinforced concrete structures used in hydraulic engineering for artificial islands or platforms that are mounted on piles or similar supports, including those with raisable legs. It specifies concrete construction methods for such fixed constructions, and is subordinate to E02B 17/021.
[E02B 17/027] steel structures  E02B17/021  takes precedence -- E02B 17/027 covers steel structures employed in artificial islands that are mounted on piles or comparable supports, including platforms built on legs that can be raised or lowered. The subclass is part of the Fixed Constructions - Hydraulic Engineering group and follows the broader subclass E02B 17/021, which takes precedence.
[E02B 17/028] Ice-structures -- E02B 17/028 covers fixed constructions that incorporate ice as a structural material, such as ice walls, platforms or shelters used in hydraulic engineering. The class is applied to artificial islands or similar installations mounted on piles or other supports where ice forms the primary load-bearing element. It is used for patents concerning the design, fabrication and deployment of ice-based structures in water environments.
[E02B 17/04] Equipment specially adapted for raising, lowering, or immobilising the working platform relative to the supporting construction -- E02B 17/04 covers devices designed to raise, lower or lock a working platform that is mounted on piles, legs or similar supports of artificial islands, such as offshore platforms. The classification includes winches, hydraulic jacks, elevators, locking mechanisms and other equipment that control the platform's vertical position relative to its supporting structure. It is used for safe access, maintenance or operational positioning of offshore work sites.
[E02B 17/06] for immobilising, e.g. using wedges or clamping rings -- E02B 17/06 relates to methods and devices for immobilising artificial islands or offshore platforms mounted on piles or similar supports, for example by employing wedges, clamps or clamping rings to secure the structure in place.
[E02B 17/08] for raising or lowering -- E02B 17/08 covers artificial islands or similar platforms that are supported on piles, legs or other movable foundations and are equipped with mechanisms for raising or lowering the structure, as used in hydraulic engineering of fixed constructions.

[E02B 17/0836] with climbing jacks  climbing cranes  B66C23/32 ; hydraulic lifting jacks  B66F3/24  and subgroups -- This subclass covers artificial islands or platforms supported on piles or similar foundations that use climbing jacks or climbing cranes together with hydraulic lifting jacks to raise, lower, or adjust the structure.
[E02B 17/0845] with inflatable clamping rings -- E02B 17/0845 pertains to hydraulic engineering constructions where artificial islands or platforms are supported on piles or similar supports and are secured using inflatable clamping rings. The rings can be inflated to clamp the structure to the piles, allowing easy installation, adjustment or removal. This element is used in fixed constructions such as offshore platforms, bridge piers or flood-control islands.

It sits within the Fixed Constructions hierarchy under Hydraulic Engineering for artificial islands mounted on piles or like supports.
[E02B 17/0863] with rack and pawl mechanism -- E02B 17/0863 covers artificial islands or offshore platforms that are supported on piles or similar structures and incorporate a rack-and-pawl mechanism for raising, lowering, or securing the platform. The classification falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering, specifically for devices that adjust the platform's height or position.

[E02B 17/089] with subtentation blocks, feet and so on -- The code E02B 17/089 designates structures such as artificial islands or platforms that are mounted on piles or similar supports and may be raised on legs, specifically using sub-tensioning blocks, feet or comparable foundation elements to secure the construction. It falls under the Fixed Constructions - Hydraulic Engineering category of the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[E02B 2015/005] Tent-like structures for dealing with pollutant emissions below the water surface -- E02B 2015/005 relates to underwater tent-like structures designed to capture, contain, or treat pollutant emissions that arise below a water surface. Such structures may form a flexible or rigid enclosure, a dome, or a canopy that directs gases or liquids to treatment systems or prevents their release into the surrounding water.
[E02B 2017/0039] Methods for placing the offshore structure -- The subclass E02B 2017/0039 covers techniques for installing and positioning offshore structures such as oil platforms, wind turbines, or subsea facilities. It includes methods of transport, placement, anchoring, alignment and securing of the structure on the seabed or in the water column.
[E02B 2017/0043] Placing the offshore structure on a pre-installed foundation structure -- This CPC subgroup E02B 2017/0043 relates to the installation of offshore structures by positioning them onto foundations that have already been placed on the seabed, such as piles, jackets or gravity bases. It covers methods, equipment and procedures for aligning, lowering and anchoring the structure onto the pre-installed support, facilitating efficient offshore construction.
[E02B 2017/0047] using a barge -- E02B 2017/0047 relates to the use of a barge in processes such as transport, construction, dredging or other operations on waterways, describing how the barge is employed as a floating platform or carrier for materials, equipment or personnel.
[E02B 2017/0052] Removal or dismantling of offshore structures from their offshore location -- The subclass covers methods, apparatus and procedures for taking offshore installations out of service and retrieving them to the sea floor or surface, including cutting, lifting, separating and transporting components of platforms, wind turbines, pipelines or other marine structures for dismantling, removal or disposal.
[E02B 2017/0056] Platforms with supporting legs -- The E02B 2017/0056 subclass covers platforms that are raised or supported by legs, including work platforms, scaffolding, raised walkways and similar structures where the primary load-bearing elements are supporting legs. It addresses the design and arrangement of those legs, such as fixed, telescopic or hinged configurations, for stationary or mobile applications in construction, maintenance or transport.
[E02B 2017/006] with lattice style supporting legs -- The subclass E02B 2017/006 covers foundations or supporting structures that incorporate lattice-style legs to bear loads. It pertains to earth-retaining or subsoil foundations where the supporting legs are formed as a grid or trellis of intersecting members, providing a lightweight, high-strength framework for distributing forces in construction or civil-engineering applications.
[E02B 2017/0065] Monopile structures -- E02B 2017/0065 designates monopile structures, the single-shaft foundations commonly used for offshore installations such as wind turbines or bridges. It covers the geometry, material selection, installation techniques, anchoring and associated equipment, as well as methods for monitoring and repairing these foundations.
[E02B 2017/0069] Gravity structures -- E02B 2017/0069 covers gravity structures, which are civil-engineering works that rely on their own weight to retain earth, water or other loads. It includes gravity retaining walls, dams, bulkheads and similar massive constructions where stability is achieved through mass and geometry rather than anchoring or reinforcement.
[E02B 2017/0073] Details of sea bottom engaging footing -- The code E02B 2017/0073 covers structural elements that secure offshore installations directly to the seabed, such as piles, suction caissons, gravity bases or other footings designed to engage the sea floor and transmit loads from the superstructure to the substrate. It includes details of shape, anchoring mechanisms, installation methods and material configurations that enable stable grounding in marine environments.
[E02B 2017/0078] Suction piles, suction cans -- E02B 2017/0078 covers ground-support techniques that use negative pressure to install vertical elements such as suction piles or suction cans. The method creates a vacuum at the base of the element to draw soil into the hollow, allowing rapid placement and load-bearing capacity in foundations, especially in soft or saturated ground.
[E02B 2017/0082] Spudcans, skirts or extended feet -- E02B 2017/0082 relates to foundation elements that spread load from piles or footings, such as spudcans, skirts or extended feet, used to improve bearing capacity and stability on weak or variable soils.
[E02B 2017/0086] Large footings connecting several legs or serving as a reservoir for the storage of oil or gas -- E02B 2017/0086 covers large foundation footings that are designed to connect several structural legs or piles and can also function as a reservoir for storing oil or gas. It falls within CPC section E (Construction), class E02 (Foundations), subgroup B, addressing combined load-distribution and fluid-storage functions in substructure engineering.
[E02B 2017/0091] Offshore structures for wind turbines -- E02B 2017/0091 covers offshore support structures for wind-turbine generators, including design, construction, anchoring, mooring and foundation concepts such as floating platforms, tension-leg systems, gravity-based bases and jacket or monopile structures used to install and operate wind turbines at sea.
[E02B 2017/0095] Connections of subsea risers, piping or wiring with the offshore structure -- E02B 2017/0095 relates to the technical means for joining subsea risers, pipelines or electrical cables to an offshore installation. It includes fittings, connectors, clamps and sealing arrangements that secure the lines to the platform or jacket while accommodating movement and pressure. The classification covers both the structural components and the procedures used to achieve a watertight, mechanically stable connection.
[E02B 2201/00] Devices, constructional details or methods of hydraulic engineering not otherwise provided for -- The CPC subclass E02B 2201/00 covers devices, constructional details or methods used in hydraulic engineering that are not specifically covered by other subclasses. It falls under the fixed constructions group and relates to general hydraulic structures, such as miscellaneous components, arrangements or techniques not otherwise provided for. This provides a catch-all category for unique or uncommon hydraulic engineering solutions.
[E02B 2201/02] Devices and methods for creating a buffer zone in the water to separate, e.g. salt and sweet water -- The CPC entry E02B 2201/02 covers devices and methods that establish a buffer zone within a water body to prevent mixing of different water types, such as separating saline from fresh water. It falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering for specialized water-management structures not otherwise classified.
[E02B 2201/04] using old tires for hydraulic engineering
[E02B 2201/50] Devices for sequentially discharging constant liquid quantities, e.g. into different irrigation channels -- This CPC entry covers devices that release a steady flow of liquid in a timed or sequenced manner, typically used to feed multiple irrigation channels with equal amounts. It includes mechanisms such as flow dividers, rotating distributors or sequential gates that ensure constant quantity discharge to each outlet.
[E02B 3/00] Engineering works in connection with control or use of streams, rivers, coasts, or other marine sites -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Protecting, maintaining or preparing waterways or the border between water and land for use.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Barrages or weirs -> E02B7/00 - Methods or apparatus for dredging -> E02F - Drilling -> E21B , E21D

It covers techniques and structures used in fixed constructions for controlling rivers, coasts and other marine sites.

[E02B 3/04] Structures or apparatus for, or methods of, protecting banks, coasts, or harbours -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structures for protection against beach erosion, in general not completely submerged, but close to shoreline;  Flood protection devices.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Sealings or joints -> E02B3/16 - Artificial reefs specially adapted to fish farming -> A01K - Flood protection for buildings -> E04H9/145 - Flood protection for building openings -> E06B9/00  **Glossary:** - Quay wall: fixed construction forming the coast line for mooring boats - Groyne: breakwater perpendicular to coast line - Breakwater: construction for coast protection, generally positioned underwater - Pier: fixed construction on pillars perpendicular to coast line - Mole: massive construction perpendicular to coast line
[E02B 3/041] using active mechanical means, e.g. fluidizing or pumping -- E02B 3/041 covers hydraulic engineering methods that actively manipulate water flow, such as fluidisation or pumping, to control streams, rivers, coasts or other marine sites. It pertains to engineered works that use mechanical means to influence or manage water movement.
[E02B 3/043] Artificial seaweed -- Artificial seaweed refers to synthetic or harvested flexible elements placed in water to imitate natural seaweed, serving hydraulic-engineering purposes such as shoreline protection, wave attenuation, sediment stabilization, or ecological enhancement. It is classified as a fixed construction used in the control or use of streams, rivers, coasts and other marine sites.
[E02B 3/046] Artificial reefs -- This CPC subclass E02B 3/046 covers artificial reefs, i.e., man-made structures placed in marine environments to promote ecological functions, stabilize seabeds, or protect coastlines. [E02B 3/06] Moles -- Moles are underground chambers or tunnels constructed beneath a watercourse, riverbank, or coastal area to control seepage, stabilize banks, and reduce erosion. They are typically built of concrete or steel and may be equipped with drainage or pressure-relief features to manage hydraulic loads.
[E02B 3/062] Constructions floating in operational condition, e.g. breakwaters or wave dissipating walls  floating oil barriers  E02B15/08 ; floating stream regulating devices  E02B3/02 -- The CPC entry E02B 3/062 covers floating structures employed in hydraulic engineering for the control or protection of waterways, such as floating breakwaters, wave-dissipating walls, oil-containment barriers and devices that regulate stream flow. [E02B 3/10] Dams -- Dams are fixed constructions placed across rivers or streams to retain water, regulate flow, generate hydro-electric power, supply irrigation or protect against flooding. They are classified in CPC under E02B 3/10, which groups engineering works for controlling or using natural watercourses and marine sites.
[E02B 3/102] Permanently installed raisable dykes -- E02B 3/102 covers permanently installed raisable dykes, which are hydraulic engineering structures that can be lifted or lowered to regulate water levels in rivers, canals or coastal areas. They provide temporary flood protection while allowing normal water flow when not raised.
[E02B 3/104] with self-activating means -- The E02B 3/104 classification covers hydraulic engineering works that incorporate self-activating means, such as automatically opening or closing gates, weirs, and flood-control structures that respond to water level or flow without external power. It pertains to fixed constructions used to manage streams, rivers, coasts or other marine sites. This subclass falls under the broader category of hydraulic engineering within the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[E02B 3/106] Temporary dykes -- E02B 3/106 concerns temporary dykes, which are short-term embankments or barriers erected to control water levels, protect works, or manage flow in rivers, streams, coasts or other marine sites. The classification applies to designs, materials, installation methods and removal of such provisional hydraulic structures.
[E02B 3/108] with a filling, e.g. filled by water or sand -- This subclass covers permanent hydraulic constructions that achieve their final shape or stability by adding a fill-material such as water, sand, gravel or earth, for example embankments, revetments or dikes that are built first and then filled to provide hydraulic control and structural support.
