[E02B 3/128] Coherent linings made on the spot, e.g. cast  in situ , extruded on the spot  for canal linings  E02B5/02 -- This subclass covers on-site formed coherent linings such as cast-in-place or extruded shells used to line canals and similar water-conveyance structures. It includes materials and methods that create a continuous, impermeable barrier during construction, typically for hydraulic engineering works on streams, rivers or coastal sites.
[E02B 3/129] Polyhedrons, tetrapods or similar bodies, whether or not threaded on strings -- E02B 3/129 pertains to the use of polyhedral or tetrapod blocks, including those with string attachments, as fixed hydraulic structures for dissipating water energy on streams, rivers, coasts or other marine sites. These bodies are employed to protect banks and shorelines by breaking up flow and reducing erosion.
[E02B 3/14] Preformed blocks -- The CPC subclass E02B 3/14 covers pre-formed concrete or masonry blocks used in hydraulic engineering works such as riverbanks, coastal protections, or other water-related structures. It pertains to fixed constructions where the blocks are manufactured in advance and then placed to control or direct water flow. This class falls under the broader categories of Fixed Constructions and Hydraulic Engineering.
[E02B 3/16] Sealings or joints -- This subclass covers devices, methods and structures used to create watertight or fluid-tight connections in hydraulic engineering works such as dams, canals, flood barriers and other fixed water-control constructions. It includes various types of seals, gaskets, joints and sealing systems that prevent leakage between structural elements or between structures and surrounding water.
[E02B 3/18] Reclamation of land from water -- E02B 3/18 concerns hydraulic engineering techniques and structures used to reclaim land from water, such as converting marshes, tidal zones or submerged areas into usable terrestrial surfaces. It includes methods for controlling streams, rivers, coasts or other marine sites in order to create new land.
[E02B 3/20] Equipment for shipping on coasts, in harbours or on other fixed marine structures, e.g. bollards -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices on shore or fixed in the water for accommodating ships.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Similar devices mounted on ships -> B63B - Anchoring of ships -> B63B21/00 - Buoys -> B63B22/00 - Equipment specially adapted for use in locks or dry docks -> E02C1/10
[E02B 3/24] Mooring posts -- Mooring posts are rigid vertical structures fixed to the seabed, riverbank or shoreline that serve as attachment points for ropes, chains or lines to secure vessels and floating equipment. They are engineered to resist tensile loads and marine forces such as currents, tides and waves, and are typically built of steel, concrete or composite materials as part of hydraulic-engineering works for streams, rivers, coasts or other marine sites.
[E02B 3/26] Fenders -- Fenders are protective devices fitted to vessel hulls or fixed marine structures that absorb impact and prevent damage when ships berth, dock or drift against piers, wharves, coasts or other waterway installations. They are employed in hydraulic engineering and fixed construction to safeguard streams, rivers, coasts and other marine sites from collision forces.
[E02B 3/28] Fender piles -- Fender piles are structural elements installed along waterfronts, harbors, or riverbanks to protect docks, piers and other fixed constructions from vessel impact and wave forces. They consist of driven or embedded piles, often of concrete or steel, arranged to absorb and dissipate kinetic energy, reducing damage to the main structure.
[E02B 5/00] Artificial water canals, -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Constructional aspects of canals, e.g. irrigation canals or water traffic canals.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Water ways for water power plants -> E02B9/02 - Irrigation of soil -> E02B13/00
[E02B 5/005] Canals entirely situated above ground level, e.g. on piers  canal bridges  E01D18/00 -- E02B 5/005 covers the design and construction of artificial water canals that are completely above ground level, such as those supported on piers or integrated into bridge structures. This subclass falls under fixed constructions and hydraulic engineering, addressing elevated canal systems and related support structures.
[E02B 5/02] Making or lining canals -- The classification concerns the construction and lining of artificial water canals, including techniques for excavating, shaping and forming the channel as well as applying protective or impermeable layers such as concrete, stone or synthetic membranes. It covers all processes involved in preparing the canal bed and walls to ensure structural stability and water tightness.
[E02B 5/04] Navigable canals -- A navigable canal is an artificial waterway designed for the passage of vessels, incorporating structures such as locks, gates, and side slopes to control water flow and maintain depth. It falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering for transportation and water management.
[E02B 5/06] Operating equipment in connection with canals -- The CPC subclass E02B 5/06 concerns equipment used to operate and control artificial water canals, including mechanisms for opening and closing gates, regulating flow, moving lock doors, adjusting water levels, and other devices that facilitate the functional management of canal systems. It falls under the broader groups for hydraulic engineering and fixed constructions.
[E02B 5/08] Details, e.g. gates, screens -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices used in free flow canals for enhancing usability of the canal.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Arresting devices with endlessly moving parts -> E02B8/026 - Mechanical filtering in general -> B01D29/00 , B01D33/00 - Arresting devices with Archimedean screw -> E03F5/14
[E02B 5/082] Closures  for irrigation conduits  E02B13/02
[E02B 5/085] Arresting devices for waterborne materials, e.g. gratings  fish barrages  E02B1/006 ; removing sediments  E02B3/023 ; arresting oil or similar polluants  E02B15/08 ; for waste water purification  E02F ; in sewers  E03F5/14 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Gratings without moving parts for separating solid materials from water or sewage.
[E02B 5/087] Divisors -- Divisors are structures installed in artificial water canals to split or regulate the flow of water, directing portions of the stream into separate channels, irrigation conduits, or distribution networks. They may be fixed or adjustable and are used to control water levels, balance supply, and prevent overflow, contributing to efficient hydraulic management in canal systems.
[E02B 7/00] Barrages or weirs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices placed perpendicularly to stream direction for controlling or temporarily interrupting water flow.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Devices for protection of coasts, banks or harbours -> E02B3/04 - High water or flood control devices alongside the river bank -> E02B3/10 - Sealings or joints specially adapted for hydraulic engineering -> E02B3/16 - Foundations in general -> E02D
[E02B 7/005] Deformable barrages or barrages consisting of permanently deformable elements, e.g. inflatable, with flexible walls  closures  E02B7/54 ; floating oil barrages  E02B15/08 -- E02B 7/005 covers barrages or weirs whose structures are permanently deformable, such as inflatable or flexible-wall barriers, including designs that can be submerged or floated to control water flow or contain oil spills. These devices are intended for hydraulic engineering applications where the barrier must adapt its shape or position under hydraulic pressure.
[E02B 7/02] Fixed barrages -- E02B 7/02 covers fixed barrages, which are permanent or non-adjustable structures built across waterways to regulate flow, maintain water levels, or provide flood protection. The classification includes the design, construction methods, materials, foundations and associated hydraulic components of such fixed weirs and dams.
[E02B 7/04] Dams across valleys -- The classification E02B 7/04 covers fixed hydraulic constructions that form dams spanning a valley, including barrages or weirs built across the full width of a valley to retain water, regulate flow, or create reservoirs. It falls under the broader categories of Hydraulic Engineering and Fixed Constructions in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[E02B 7/06] Earth-fill dams -- The CPC subclass E02B 7/06 covers earth-fill dams, which are structures built by compacting soil to retain water for irrigation, flood control, or power generation. It includes design, construction methods, and stability measures specific to embankments formed primarily from natural earth materials. This subclass falls under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Barrages or weirs.
[E02B 7/08] Wall dams -- Wall dams are fixed hydraulic constructions placed across a watercourse to raise the water level upstream and regulate flow, often serving flood-control, irrigation or navigation purposes. They consist of a solid barrier, typically concrete or earth-filled, with controlled openings such as spillways or gates to manage discharge.
[E02B 7/10] Gravity dams, i.e. those in which the weight of the structure prevents overturning -- E02B 7/10 pertains to gravity dams, which are fixed hydraulic structures that rely on their own weight to resist water pressure and prevent overturning, commonly used as barrages or weirs.
[E02B 7/12] Arch dams -- E02B 7/12 covers arch dams, which are concrete or masonry structures curved upstream so that hydrostatic pressure is transferred by arch action into the adjoining rock abutments. They are used in narrow, steep valleys where the side walls can bear the load, providing an efficient use of material compared with gravity dams.
[E02B 7/14] Buttress dams -- Buttress dams are fixed hydraulic structures that resist water pressure by transmitting loads from a thin upstream slab to a series of spaced supports or buttresses on the downstream side, allowing efficient use of material while maintaining stability. This form falls under the broader category of barrages or weirs in hydraulic engineering.
[E02B 7/16] Fixed weirs -- The CPC subclass E02B 7/16 covers fixed weirs, which are permanent barriers built across waterways to control water flow and level without movable parts. It falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering, specifically under barrages or weirs. This classification is used for patents relating to the design, construction and materials of such structures.
[E02B 7/18] Siphon weirs
[E02B 7/20] Movable barrages -- E02B 7/20 relates to movable barrages, hydraulic structures that can be raised, lowered or otherwise adjusted to regulate water flow in rivers, canals or reservoirs. The classification includes gates, sluices and similar devices designed to control discharge, flood levels or navigation conditions.
[E02B 7/205] Barrages controlled by the variations of the water level; automatically functioning barrages  level regulators  G05D9/00 ; the documents are filed in this subdivision and the relevant subdivision  E02B7/26  -  E02B7/50
[E02B 7/22] Stop log dams -- E02B 7/22 designates stop-log dams, which are removable, stacked planks or panels used to control water flow in barrages or weirs. These structures allow precise regulation of water level and can be added or removed as needed for flood control, navigation or irrigation.
[E02B 7/24] Needle weirs -- The CPC subclass E02B 7/24 covers needle weirs, which are adjustable barriers formed by a row of closely spaced vertical rods that can be raised or lowered to control water flow in barrages or weirs. It belongs to the fixed-construction group within hydraulic engineering.
[E02B 7/26] Vertical-lift gates -- Vertical-lift gates are movable barriers used in barrages or weirs to regulate water flow by raising and lowering a gate that moves vertically. They are fixed hydraulic constructions designed to control reservoir levels, discharge rates, and flood protection, and are commonly employed in dams, spillways, and navigation locks. The CPC code E02B 7/26 specifically covers these vertically operating gate mechanisms.
[E02B 7/28] with sliding gates -- E02B 7/28 classifies hydraulic constructions such as barrages or weirs that employ sliding gates to regulate water flow. The subclass covers designs where gate sections move horizontally or vertically along tracks or guides to open or close the passage, including the drive mechanisms and sealing arrangements used in dams, spillways and similar fixed water-control structures.
[E02B 7/30] with guide wheels or rollers for the gates -- E02B 7/30 classifies hydraulic structures such as barrages or weirs that incorporate guide wheels or rollers to assist the movement of gate components, improving control and reducing friction during operation. This subcategory falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering.
[E02B 7/32] Cylindrical or tubular gates -- E02B 7/32 covers cylindrical or tubular gates used in barrages, weirs and other fixed hydraulic structures to regulate water flow. The category includes the design, construction and operation of round or tube-shaped gate mechanisms that can be raised, lowered or rotated to control discharge.
[E02B 7/34] Flash- boards for vertical-lift gates -- Flash-boards are removable or hinged boards installed on the upstream face of vertical-lift gates in barrages or weirs to temporarily raise the water level and increase storage or head. They are designed to be raised or lowered quickly, allowing rapid adjustment of gate operation for flood control or power generation.
[E02B 7/36] Elevating mechanisms for vertical-lift gates -- E02B 7/36 covers devices that raise and lower vertical-lift gates in barrages or weirs, including hoists, winches, gear trains and hydraulic or pneumatic cylinders designed to control gate movement for water regulation.
[E02B 7/38] Rolling gates -- E02B 7/38 classifies rolling gates, a type of movable barrier used in barrages or weirs to regulate water flow. These devices are fixed-construction hydraulic engineering components that rotate or roll to open or close, allowing controlled discharge and retention of water in dams, spillways and similar structures.
[E02B 7/40] Swinging or turning gates -- The CPC subclass E02B 7/40 relates to swinging or turning gates employed in hydraulic engineering structures such as barrages or weirs. It covers the design, operation and control mechanisms of these movable gates used to regulate water flow, and is nested under the broader categories of fixed constructions and hydraulic engineering.
[E02B 7/42] Gates of segmental or sector-like shape with horizontal axis -- The classification E02B 7/42 covers gates used in barrages or weirs that have a segmental or sector-shaped profile and rotate about a horizontal axis. These fixed hydraulic structures are designed to control water flow by opening or closing the gate in water-engineering applications.
[E02B 7/44] Hinged-leaf gates -- E02B 7/44 covers hinged-leaf gates, which are movable gate sections that rotate about a horizontal or vertical hinge to regulate water flow in barrages, weirs or similar hydraulic structures. These gates serve as controllable barriers for flood control, navigation and water level management.
[E02B 7/46] Gates turning round a horizontal axis arranged midway of the flap -- E02B 7/46 covers gates that swing on a horizontal axis positioned at the centre of a flap, used to regulate water flow in barrages or weirs. The classification relates to fixed hydraulic constructions where the rotating element is mounted midway to allow opening and closing by pivoting.
[E02B 7/48] Roof or double shutter gates -- Roof or double shutter gates are movable barrier elements used in barrages and weirs to control water flow, consisting of a hinged roof panel that can be lowered to block water and a double shutter mechanism that slides horizontally to open or close the passage. They enable rapid regulation of water levels and can be fully sealed to protect against flooding.
[E02B 7/50] Floating gates -- Floating gates are movable barriers used in hydraulic structures such as barrages or weirs to regulate water flow and level. They are typically mounted on floats or buoyant supports, allowing them to rise and fall with changes in water height while maintaining a seal when closed. This design enables rapid adjustments for flood control, navigation or irrigation purposes.
[E02B 7/52] Equipment preventing vibration of gates -- This subclass covers devices installed on barrage or weir gates to suppress or dampen vibrations caused by water flow, including hydraulic dampers, mechanical braces, and other vibration-control mechanisms. The equipment aims to protect gate integrity, reduce noise, and improve operational stability in hydraulic engineering structures.
[E02B 7/54] Sealings for gates -- E02B 7/54 pertains to sealings for gates in barrages or weirs, covering water-tight gaskets, packings and other sealing arrangements that prevent leakage when a gate is closed or partially opened. This subclass is used for patents related to hydraulic engineering structures that control water flow.
[E02B 8/00] Details of barrages or weirs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Subparts of barrages or of the water body created by a barrage, devices for maintaining functioning of barrage or body of water; e.g. energy dissipating devices carried by lock or dry-dock gates.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Removing sediments from natural waterways -> E02B3/023 - Cleaning or keeping clear the surface of open water -> E02B15/00
[E02B 8/02] Sediment base gates -- Sediment base gates are fixed hydraulic structures installed at the base of barrages or weirs to regulate the passage of sediment and debris while maintaining water flow. They can be raised or lowered to release accumulated material, helping preserve reservoir capacity and protect downstream equipment. This element falls under the E02B 8/02 class for details of barrages or weirs in the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[E02B 8/023] Arresting devices for waterborne materials  E02B1/003  takes precedence; in artificial watercourses  E02B5/085 ; arresting oil or the like  E02B15/08 ; sieving devices for waste water purification  C02F ; in sewers  E03F5/14
[E02B 8/026] Cleaning devices  for fish barrages  E02B1/006
[E02B 8/04] Valves, slides, or the like -- E02B 8/04 classifies devices such as valves, sliding gates or comparable mechanisms that regulate water flow in fixed hydraulic structures like barrages and weirs, covering their design, operation and integration within hydraulic engineering projects.
[E02B 8/045] automatically movable -- E02B 8/045 covers hydraulic constructions such as barrages, weirs or spillways whose operative elements can move automatically without manual intervention, typically using hydraulic, pneumatic or mechanical systems that respond to water level or flow changes. The classification includes designs for self-adjusting gates, lifts, and other movable parts that regulate water flow.
[E02B 8/06] Spillways -- E02B 8/06 covers spillways, the structures that convey excess water from dams, barrages or weirs to downstream areas. It includes their design, construction, materials and hydraulic performance. The code falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering, focusing on detailed aspects of barrage and weir overflow facilities.
[E02B 8/08] Fish passes -- E02B 8/08 concerns fish passes, the structures that allow fish to bypass barriers such as dams, barrages or weirs. It includes the design, placement and hydraulic features of these fixed constructions to facilitate fish migration past water-flow obstacles.
[E02B 8/085] Devices allowing fish migration, e.g. fish traps  arresting or diverting fish exclusively  E02B1/006 -- E02B 8/085 pertains to hydraulic engineering devices that enable fish migration through fixed constructions such as barrages or weirs, including fish ladders, traps or diversion mechanisms that arrest, guide or release fish to allow passage.
[E02B 9/00] Water-power plants -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Constructional aspects of water power plants and their related water bearing parts.
[E02B 9/02] Water-ways -- E02B 9/02 covers structures and installations for the guidance, diversion, and control of water in canals, rivers, and other waterways, including locks, weirs and related hydraulic works used in water-power plant contexts.
[E02B 9/022] Closures -- E02B 9/022 refers to closures used in water-power plant installations, covering gates, valves, shutters, and similar devices that open or shut water conduits, tailraces, penstocks or other hydraulic passages to control flow in fixed construction hydraulic engineering systems.
[E02B 9/025] automatically movable -- E02B 9/025 covers water-power plant components that can change position or orientation automatically, such as self-adjusting gates, spillway shutters, or turbine blades that move in response to hydraulic conditions or control signals without manual intervention. The classification is used for fixed-construction hydraulic engineering systems that incorporate automated movement.
[E02B 9/027] Sliding closures  E02B9/025  takes precedence -- The CPC code E02B 9/027 pertains to sliding closures in water-power plant installations, covering gates, sluice doors, weirs and similar fixed hydraulic structures that move linearly to control water flow. It includes the mechanical, hydraulic or electric drive systems, sealing arrangements and guide ways that enable the sliding motion, whether operated manually or automatically.
[E02B 9/04] Free-flow canals or flumes -- E02B 9/04 classifies free-flow canals or flumes used in hydraulic engineering, particularly for water-power plants. The entry covers structures that convey water by gravity and channel shape without mechanical moving parts, directing flow for power generation or irrigation.
[E02B 9/06] Pressure galleries or pressure conduits -- This CPC subclass deals with the design and installation of pressure galleries and pressure conduits that convey water under pressure within water-power plants. It includes fixed structures such as tunnels, penstocks and pressure pipes that direct flow to turbines.
[E02B 9/08] Tide or wave power plants -- E02B 9/08 covers tidal and wave power plants that convert the kinetic energy of tides or ocean waves into electricity. These installations are a subcategory of water-power plants within the broader field of hydraulic engineering and fixed constructions.
[E02C] SHIP-LIFTING DEVICES OR MECHANISMS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices enabling ships or boats to travel between two stretches of water at different levels.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Dry docking of ships -> B63C1/00 - Floating docks -> B63C1/02 - Devices for lifting boats out of the water -> B63C3/00 - Salvaging sunken vessels -> B63C7/00 - Lock gates -> E02B7/20
[E02C 1/00] Locks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices enclosing a ship and enabling a water level change in the enclosed area thereby raising or lowering the ship.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Naval construction aspects -> B63C1/00 - Floating docks -> B63C1/02 - Lock or dry-dock gates -> E02B7/20
[E02C 1/02] with auxiliary basins -- E02C 1/02 pertains to ship-lifting devices or mechanisms, specifically locks that incorporate auxiliary basins. It covers designs where additional water-filled chambers are used to assist the raising or lowering of vessels, improving balance, efficiency or safety during lock operation.
[E02C 1/04] with floating throughs -- E02C 1/04 covers lock structures that employ floating throughs, meaning the sealing or moving elements of the lock are buoyant devices that rise and fall with water level to allow ships to enter or exit. The classification falls under fixed constructions, hydraulic engineering, ship-lifting devices or mechanisms, specifically locks.
[E02C 1/06] Devices for filling or emptying locks -- E02C 1/06 covers equipment that moves water into or out of lock chambers to raise or lower vessels, including gates, valves, pumps, and other hydraulic or mechanical devices that control the filling and emptying process of ship-lifting locks.
[E02C 1/08] Arrangements for dissipating the energy of the water -- E02C 1/08 covers designs used in lock chambers to reduce the kinetic energy of incoming or outgoing water, typically by means of baffles, weirs, splash plates or other energy-absorbing devices. These arrangements protect the lock gates and surrounding structures from hydraulic shock and improve safety and efficiency during vessel passage.
[E02C 1/10] Equipment for use in connection with the navigation of ships in locks -- E02C 1/10 pertains to equipment used to facilitate the navigation of ships through lock chambers, including mooring, towing, guidance and positioning devices that assist vessels in entering, transiting and exiting locks.
[E02C 3/00] Inclined-plane ship-lifting mechanisms -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for at least partially horizontal ship travel; Devices for transporting flat-bottomed vessels over land.
[E02C 5/00] Mechanisms for lifting ships vertically -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for vertical ship travel between two stretches of water.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Salvaging sunken vessels -> B63C7/00
[E02C 5/02] with floating chambers -- The sub-class E02C 5/02 covers ship-lifting devices that use one or more floating chambers to aid the vertical lift of a vessel. These chambers are sealed, buoyant enclosures that provide supplemental lift or stability during the raising operation.
[E02D] FOUNDATIONS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods, devices and machinery for making foundations;  Engineering with the ends of making foundations or underground structures involving the disturbance of the ground surface.  Investigation of foundation soil in situ;  Improving soil or rock;  Structural elements adapted to foundation engineering and equipment and methods of placing or removing them;  Preventing excavation walls or embankments from collapsing;  Keeping dry of foundation sites in the ground;  Caissons and the joining of caissons;  Testing, repairing, straightening, lowering or lifting foundation structures.
[E02D 1/00] Investigation of foundation soil -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Any investigation of a soil with respect to envisaged activities of building foundations on surface of that soil or partially or completely embedded in that soil covered by the investigation.  The investigations may be carried out before or after work for making the foundation is being done.  Investigations may also be done while the foundation work is in progress.  The investigations may comprise evaluation of physical properties, e.g. Young's modulus, compressibility, density, water saturation, weight, bearing capacity, pore-water pressure, permeability etc.  The investigation may comprise evaluation of chemical properties e.g. ph value.  The investigation distinguishes between soil itself and the fluids circulating in it.  The investigation in situ.  Sampling of probes like soil or water for off-site investigation.  The group covers the devices for exerting the investigation work as well as the method.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Apparatus for obtaining or removing undisturbed cores, e.g. core barrels, core extractors -> E21B25/00 - Containers for collecting substances in boreholes or wells -> E21B27/00 - Testing the nature of borehole walls; sampling of soil or fluids -> E21B49/00 - Devices for testing in situ the hardness or other properties of minerals -> E21C39/00 - Sampling involving an extraction tool, e.g. core bit -> G01N1/08 - Suction devices for sampling of fluids, e.g. pumps -> G01N1/14 - Investigating characteristics of particles or permeability, pore volume etc. -> G01N15/00 - Investigating or analyzing materials by use of ultrasonic, sonic or infrasonic waves -> G01N29/00 - Prospecting using seismology -> G01V1/00 - Prospecting using optical means -> G01V8/00  **Glossary:** - Foundation soil: Soil wherein or whereon a foundation for e.g. a building will be erected on - In situ: At the place and in the state of the occurence of the soil
[E02D 1/02] before construction work -- The subclass E02D 1/02 covers investigations and preparatory measures performed before foundation construction, including site surveys, geotechnical and soil testing, and assessment of ground conditions to determine suitability and design parameters for the upcoming works.
[E02D 1/022] by investigating mechanical properties of the soil  E02D1/027  takes precedence
[E02D 1/025] combined with sampling -- E02D 1/025 covers techniques that investigate foundation soils while simultaneously taking samples of the material for laboratory analysis, integrating geotechnical testing with on-site sampling to assess soil properties during construction of hydraulic foundations.
[E02D 1/027] by investigating properties relating to fluids in the soil, e.g. pore-water pressure, permeability  sampling of groundwater  E02D1/06
[E02D 1/04] Sampling of soil -- E02D 1/04 deals with sampling of soil as part of investigations of foundation soils in hydraulic engineering and fixed constructions. The code covers methods and devices for extracting representative soil specimens for analysis of bearing capacity, settlement, and other geotechnical properties.
[E02D 1/06] Sampling of ground water -- The CPC subclass E02D 1/06 covers techniques for obtaining samples of groundwater during investigations of foundation soil. It pertains to fixed-construction hydraulic engineering activities where the properties of the water column are measured or analyzed to assess foundation conditions.
[E02D 1/08] after finishing the foundation structure -- The subclass E02D 1/08 covers actions carried out after a foundation has been built, including backfilling, compaction, settlement monitoring, load testing and other post-construction procedures that verify the foundation's performance before further structural work proceeds.
[E02D 11/00] Methods or apparatus for both placing and removing sheet pile bulkheads, piles, or mould-pipes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus equally suitable for placing and removing foundation elements such as sheet piles, piles or mould-pipes. In contrast, E02D7/00 only covers the placing of foundation elements, E02D9/00 only covers the removing of such elements.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Methods or apparatus for only placing sheet pile bulkheads, piles, mouldpipes, or other moulds -> E02D7/00 - Methods or apparatus for only removing sheet pile bulkheads, piles, mouldpipes, or other moulds -> E02D9/00
[E02D 13/00] Accessories for placing or removing piles or bulkheads -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Accessories adapted to remove bulkheads, enabling control during the process of placing, removing of obstacles, follow-blocks and guide devices assisting in placing foundation elements.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Accessories for drilling tools -> E21B12/00 - Derricks; masts -> E21B15/00 - Automatic control systems specially adapted for drilling operations -> E21B44/00 - Survey of boreholes or wells -> E21B47/00
[E02D 13/005] Sound absorbing accessories in piling -- E02D 13/005 designates sound-absorbing accessories employed in piling work, such as devices that attenuate noise while placing or extracting piles or bulkheads in hydraulic-engineering foundations. It is a subcategory of fixed constructions under hydraulic engineering.
[E02D 13/02] specially adapted for placing or removing bulkheads -- E02D 13/02 covers tools and equipment specially designed to install or extract bulkheads in hydraulic-engineering works. These accessories belong to the broader group of fixed constructions and foundation accessories used for handling piles or bulkheads.
[E02D 13/04] Guide devices -- Guide devices are accessories used in hydraulic-engineering foundation work to direct piles or bulkheads while they are being placed or removed. They ensure proper alignment, control the path of movement, and help maintain stability during installation or extraction.
[E02D 13/06] for observation while placing -- The E02D 13/06 subclass pertains to accessories that enable observation of piles, bulkheads or similar foundations during their installation, such as sensors, cameras, level-gauges or other monitoring devices that help ensure correct placement and alignment.
[E02D 13/08] Removing obstacles -- E02D 13/08 defines devices and methods used to eliminate obstructions in hydraulic-engineered foundations, such as tools for extracting or displacing piles, bulkheads, and other barrier elements during construction or maintenance operations.
[E02D 13/10] Follow-blocks of pile-drivers or like devices -- E02D 13/10 covers follow-blocks, accessories used with pile-drivers or similar devices to support, guide, or control a pile as it is driven, often placed behind the hammer to limit movement and reduce vibration in hydraulic-engineering foundation work.
[E02D 15/00] Handling building or like materials for hydraulic engineering or foundations -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Handling materials when used for hydraulic engineering or for foundations;  Handling of bulk concrete including filling into shuttering, mould-pipes, pile tubes, bore-holes or narrow shafts and under water;  Sinking workpieces into water;  Placing gravel or light material under water.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Concrete piles cast in position by making use of mould-pipes or other mould -> E02D5/38 - Concrete piles cast in position by making use of mould-pipes or other mould in open water -> E02D5/40 - Placing of hollow pipes or mould pipes by means arranged inside the pile or pipe -> E02D7/28 - Devices for applying linings on banks or the water bottom -> E02B3/121 - Banks of the bodies of water -> E02B3/122 - Lining canals -> E02B5/02 - Conveying or working-up concrete or similar masses in general -> E04G21/02
[E02D 15/02] Handling of bulk concrete specially for foundation purposes -- This classification covers methods and equipment for moving, placing and processing bulk concrete used in foundation work, including transport, pouring and compaction techniques specific to hydraulic engineering structures.
[E02D 15/04] Placing concrete in mould-pipes, pile tubes, bore-holes or narrow shafts -- The CPC subclass E02D 15/04 covers methods for placing concrete into narrow forms such as mould-pipes, pile tubes, bore-holes or shafts, typically used in hydraulic-engineering foundations. It includes techniques for delivering concrete into confined spaces to create structural elements like piles or foundation columns.
[E02D 15/06] Placing concrete under water -- E02D 15/06 designates techniques for placing concrete underwater in hydraulic engineering foundations, covering methods and apparatus for handling building materials in submerged conditions.
[E02D 15/08] Sinking workpieces into water or soil -- E02D 15/08 relates to hydraulic-engineering foundation work where components are placed by sinking them into water or soil. The code covers techniques for handling building or similar materials that are lowered or driven into a fluid or ground environment to create stable foundations.
[E02D 15/10] Placing gravel or light material under water -- E02D 15/10 relates to techniques for placing gravel or other lightweight material beneath water as part of hydraulic-engineering foundations. It addresses the handling, deposition and spreading of such materials under water to form or improve foundation structures.
[E02D 17/00] Excavations -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pits for foundations and their borderings;  Narrow shafts or ditches and trenches for foundation;  Stiffening or bordering the sides of ditches or trenches by using travelling trench shores and shoring struts;  Covering and back-filling of those trenches;  Machines for making foundation slots, i.e. diaphragm walls;  Securing of slopes or inclines, e.g. to counter erosion, by flexible securing means e.g. geotextiles, prefabricated modular blocks or by sheet piles and piles.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Concrete diaphragms per se -> E02D5/18 - Foundations for dams -> E02D27/40 - Protection like protecting fences against snowslide or avalanches -> E01F7/04 , E01F15/00 - Stream regulation, e.g. breaking up subaqueous rock, cleaning the beds of waterways, directing the water flow and removing of sediment or solid rock -> E02B3/02 - Soil shifting apparatus unless used for making foundations, e.g. diaphragm wall -> E02F1/00 - Dredgers or soil shifting machines with equipment for back-filling trenches or ditches -> E02F5/12 - Dredgers or soil shifting machinesfor making embankments -> E02F5/22
[E02D 17/02] Foundation pits -- The CPC subclass E02D 17/02 covers foundation pits, the excavated voids prepared to receive a building's footings or sub-structure. It includes methods for digging, shoring, dewatering and stabilising the pit, as well as design criteria to ensure safe, functional excavations for foundations.
[E02D 17/04] Bordering or stiffening the sides of foundation pits -- E02D 17/04 covers techniques for bordering or stiffening the walls of foundation pits, such as sheet piles, secant piles, slurry walls, or other support systems used to prevent collapse during excavation. It addresses the design, installation and temporary reinforcement measures that maintain side stability in hydraulic-engineered fixed constructions.
[E02D 17/06] Foundation ditches or narrow shafts -- E02D 17/06 classifies foundation ditches or narrow shafts, a subset of fixed-construction hydraulic-engineering excavations. It covers the design, construction and engineering of narrow foundation trenches, shafts and similar openings that support structures, detailing methods for their excavation and stabilization.
[E02D 17/08] Bordering or stiffening the sides of ditches or narrow shafts for foundations -- The code E02D 17/08 covers methods and structures for reinforcing the walls of trenches or narrow shafts used as foundation excavations. It includes techniques such as installing side braces, sheet piles or other stiffening members to prevent collapse and maintain stability during construction. This falls under the hydraulic engineering subclass of fixed constructions.
[E02D 17/083] Shoring struts -- Shoring struts are temporary vertical or inclined support members placed in excavations to resist lateral earth pressure and stabilize trench walls or pit sides. Typically made of steel, timber or composite material, they transfer loads to the surrounding ground or to dedicated bearing points, allowing safe construction of foundations and other underground works.
[E02D 17/086] Travelling trench shores -- Travelling trench shores are portable shoring structures that move along the length of an open excavation to provide continuous support for the surrounding earth. They are typically employed in linear projects such as tunnelling, pipe laying or utility trenches where the excavation progresses incrementally, allowing safe and efficient advancement of the work face while maintaining stability of the sides.
[E02D 17/10] Covering trenches for foundations -- E02D 17/10 covers methods and apparatus for protecting or sealing excavation trenches used for foundations, such as applying concrete, prefabricated slabs, polymer membranes, or other coverings that prevent contamination, collapse, or water ingress before the foundation work proceeds. This falls within hydraulic engineering fixed constructions, specifically the construction of foundations.
[E02D 17/12] Back-filling of foundation trenches or ditches -- E02D 17/12 relates to the back-filling of foundation trenches or ditches after excavation, covering methods, materials and processes used to restore the voids that support structural foundations, typically in hydraulic engineering projects.
[E02D 17/13] Foundation slots -- E02D 17/13 defines foundation slots as recessed or cut openings made in a foundation during excavation, typically to accommodate utilities, reinforcement bars, anchoring devices or other structural inserts. The subclass falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering, specifically within the foundations and excavations group, and addresses the design and preparation of such slots for later installation or integration into the foundation system.
[E02D 17/16] Loosening of soil or rock, under water -- E02D 17/16 covers techniques for loosening soil or rock while submerged, such as hydraulic jetting, water-driven pistons, or suction methods that break up material to facilitate excavation or foundation work. The entry applies to underwater sites where destabilising the ground is required for construction or mining.
[E02D 17/18] Making embankments, -- The code E02D 17/18 covers methods and techniques for constructing embankments used to retain earth or water, including the preparation of foundations, placement of fill material, and compaction procedures. It applies to fixed hydraulic structures where the embankment forms part of a dam, levee, or similar water-control work.
[E02D 17/20] Securing of slopes or inclines -- A method or device for stabilising natural or artificial slopes and inclines, including retaining structures, soil nailing, shotcrete, geogrid reinforcement or drainage systems intended to prevent movement or failure of the earth mass. Falls within Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Foundations → Excavations, covering techniques that secure the side of a cut or embankment.
[E02D 17/202] with flexible securing means -- E02D 17/202 classifies foundations or fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering that are installed using flexible securing means such as elastic ties, adaptable bolts or anchoring systems that allow limited movement or adjustment, commonly applied in excavated retaining structures and sheet-pile foundations.
[E02D 17/205] with modular blocks, e.g. pre-fabricated -- This subclass covers methods and structures for building foundations by excavating and installing modular, pre-fabricated blocks, typically used in hydraulic engineering and fixed-construction projects. It includes the use of interchangeable concrete or steel modules to form stable footing systems.
[E02D 17/207] with means incorporating sheet piles or piles -- E02D 17/207 covers excavation methods for foundations that use sheet piles or driven piles as retaining or support structures. It falls under Fixed Constructions, Hydraulic Engineering, Foundations, and Excavations, and is applied to projects where the earth is restrained by these stiff vertical elements during construction.
[E02D 19/00] Keeping dry foundation sites or other areas in the ground -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Protecting areas, particularly foundation areas like foundation pits, from the entry of water;  Water may arise as open water or underground water;  Lining sumps when in trenches, ditches or other foundation pits.  The methods comprising the building of coffer-dams, making open ditches or trenches, lowering the level of ground water, e.g. by pumping, interrupting the waters passage by freezing, damming, making sealing aprons or forcing out the water by e.g. compressed air.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Consolidating soil by placing solidifying or pore-filling substances in the soil -> E02D3/12 - Sheet piles or sheet pile bulkheads -> E02D5/02 - Pneumatic caissons to keep of the water -> E02D23/04 - Permanent sheet piling boxes -> E02D27/30 - Collecting drinking water -> E02B3/00 - Sealing or joints in connection with hydraulic engineering when correcting rivers or streams -> E02B3/16 - Freezing the soil when sinking shafts, e.g. mining shafts -> E21D1/12  **Glossary:** - Sealing aprons: Diaphragms made from bituminous or clay material in contrast to diaphragm wall which is widely understood a concrete wall.
[E02D 19/02] Restraining of open water -- E02D 19/02 covers techniques and structures that restrain open water to keep foundation sites dry, such as cofferdams, sheet piles, barriers, and dewatering systems used in hydraulic engineering.
[E02D 19/04] by coffer-dams -- E02D 19/04 covers the use of cofferdams, which are temporary watertight enclosures that allow excavation or construction in foundation sites below the water level while keeping the area dry. This subclass falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering, specifically techniques for maintaining dry conditions in foundations.
[E02D 19/06] Restraining of underground water -- E02D 19/06 relates to techniques and structures that block or control groundwater from entering excavations or foundation areas, including wells, cutoff-walls, grouting, drainage or sealing methods used to keep foundation sites dry during construction.
[E02D 19/08] by employing open ditches arranged below the level of the water -- E02D 19/08 covers a method for keeping foundation sites or other ground areas dry by constructing open ditches that are placed below the water level. The ditches act as drainage channels, allowing water to flow away from the foundation while the site remains dry. This technique falls under the fixed-construction and hydraulic-engineering categories for foundation protection.
[E02D 19/10] by lowering level of ground water -- E02D 19/10 relates to methods for keeping foundation or other underground sites dry by lowering the groundwater level, including dewatering wells, pumping systems or drainage arrangements that remove water before or during construction.
[E02D 19/12] by damming or interrupting the passage of underground water -- E02D 19/12 covers techniques for keeping foundation sites dry by creating barriers or obstructions that stop or divert underground water. It includes methods that dam, block, or otherwise interrupt the natural flow of water beneath the ground to protect foundations and excavation areas.
[E02D 19/14] by freezing the soil -- E02D 19/14 covers a method for keeping foundation sites dry by artificially freezing the surrounding soil. It falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering and details the use of ground-freezing techniques to create a stable, moisture-free environment for foundations.
[E02D 19/16] by placing or applying sealing substances -- The subclass E02D 19/16 covers techniques for keeping foundation sites and other ground areas dry by placing or applying sealing substances such as membranes, coatings, grouts or injectable polymers. It includes the selection, preparation and installation of these waterproofing materials to prevent water ingress into fixed hydraulic structures.
[E02D 19/18] by making use of sealing aprons, -- The subclass E02D 19/18 covers methods for keeping foundation sites dry by using sealing aprons, which are membrane or sheet elements placed around the perimeter of a pit or footing to stop water infiltration and direct water away from the construction area. These aprons may be flexible fabrics, rubberized sheets or rigid panels and are often combined with drainage systems to maintain a dry work environment.
[E02D 19/185] Joints between sheets constituting the sealing aprons -- E02D 19/185 covers the design and construction of joints linking the individual sheets that form sealing aprons used to keep foundation sites dry in hydraulic engineering works. It addresses methods for connecting, sealing and waterproofing these joints to prevent water ingress.
[E02D 19/20] by displacing the water, e.g. by compressed air -- E02D 19/20 addresses techniques for keeping foundation sites dry by displacing water, for example through the injection of compressed air. The subclass is part of the Fixed Constructions hierarchy under Hydraulic Engineering foundations and focuses on methods that remove water rather than pump it out.
[E02D 19/22] Lining sumps in trenches -- This subclass covers methods and devices for lining the bottom of a trench sump to prevent water ingress and keep the area dry during foundation work. It includes waterproof membranes, concrete liners, sealants and related techniques applied within excavated channels used for drainage or dewatering, ensuring dry conditions for foundations or other underground sites.
[E02D 2200/00] Geometrical or physical properties -- This subclass covers inventions that deal with the geometric configuration and intrinsic physical characteristics of foundations in hydraulic engineering, such as shape, dimensions, material composition, load-bearing capacity, settlement behavior, and thermal or acoustic performance, and methods for measuring or improving these properties to enhance the stability and functionality of fixed constructions.
[E02D 2200/11] Height being adjustable -- The code E02D 2200/11 covers foundations or fixed hydraulic constructions whose height can be varied after installation, such as adjustable-height piles, liftable footings or telescoping support elements. It relates to the geometrical or physical property of height being adjustable to accommodate changes in load, settlement or water level. This classification is used for designs that allow height modification without replacing the entire foundation.
[E02D 2200/115] with separate pieces -- The CPC subclass E02D 2200/115 covers foundations in hydraulic engineering that are constructed from separate individual elements rather than a monolithic mass. It pertains to the geometrical or physical properties of such fixed constructions, detailing how distinct pieces are assembled to form the overall foundation structure.
[E02D 2200/12] corrugated -- E02D 2200/12 covers corrugated fixed constructions in hydraulic-engineering foundations, emphasizing their geometrical or physical characteristics. Corrugation is used to increase strength, stiffness and water-flow control in elements such as retaining walls, dikes, and other foundation structures.
[E02D 2200/13] having at least a mesh portion -- E02D 2200/13 pertains to fixed hydraulic-engineering foundations that include at least a mesh portion, the mesh being a structural element that influences the foundation's geometrical or physical properties. It falls under the broader categories of Fixed Constructions, Hydraulic Engineering and Foundations.
[E02D 2200/14] resilient or elastic -- The subclass E02D 2200/14 pertains to fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering, specifically foundations whose design relies on resilient or elastic materials. It addresses the geometric and physical properties of such structures, covering aspects like elasticity, deformation behaviour and load-distribution characteristics of elastic foundation elements.
[E02D 2200/143] helically or spirally shaped -- E02D 2200/143 covers fixed hydraulic-engineered foundations whose elements are formed in a helical or spiral shape. It classifies constructions such as screw piles, helical anchors, and other spirally shaped foundation components, focusing on their geometric and physical characteristics.
[E02D 2200/146] Springs -- E02D 2200/146 is the CPC subgroup for springs employed in hydraulic engineering foundations, covering their design, geometry, material properties and performance characteristics. It addresses fixed-construction elements that provide elastic energy storage or load distribution in structures such as dams, spillways or water-storage facilities. The code groups patents dealing with spring types, dimensions, stiffness and related calculations.
[E02D 2200/15] including at least a hinge -- The CPC group E02D 2200/15 refers to foundations that incorporate at least one hinge, covering designs where a hinged joint is used to allow movement, adjustment or rotation of structural elements such as piles, footings or supporting frames in hydraulic engineering.
[E02D 2200/16] Shapes -- E02D 2200/16 classifies the geometric or physical shape of foundation elements used in fixed hydraulic-engineering structures. It covers the form, profile and dimensional characteristics of foundations such as footings, piles or retaining walls, focusing on how shape influences stability and load distribution.
[E02D 2200/1607] round, e.g. circle -- The CPC subclass E02D 2200/1607 covers round, i.e., circular, shapes used in fixed hydraulic constructions such as foundations. It classifies designs, methods and structural elements where a circular geometry defines the physical or geometrical properties of the component.
[E02D 2200/1614] made from single element -- This CPC subgroup covers fixed hydraulic-engineering foundations that are constructed as a single, indivisible element, emphasizing their geometric or physical characteristics.
[E02D 2200/1621] made from multiple elements -- E02D 2200/1621 classifies foundations that are built from two or more distinct elements, emphasizing their geometric or physical characteristics. It applies to fixed hydraulic constructions where composite materials or components are combined to achieve required structural performance. This subclass is used for patents describing multi-element foundation designs.
[E02D 2200/1628] rectangular -- E02D 2200/1628 is a CPC subgroup under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Foundations → Geometrical or physical properties that pertains to rectangular shapes or dimensions used in foundation design and analysis for hydraulic structures.
[E02D 2200/1635] made from single element -- E02D 2200/1635 covers foundations used in hydraulic engineering that are constructed as a monolithic unit, i.e., made from a single element rather than assembled from multiple parts. The code addresses the geometric and physical properties of these fixed constructions, focusing on design and material considerations for single-element foundation structures.
[E02D 2200/1642] made from multiple elements -- E02D 2200/1642 covers foundations in hydraulic engineering that are constructed from multiple distinct elements, focusing on their geometric or physical characteristics. It classifies fixed constructions where the composite nature of the foundation influences performance, stability, or interaction with water-related structures.
[E02D 2200/165] polygonal -- E02D 2200/165 is a Cooperative Patent Classification for fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering, specifically foundations, dealing with geometrical or physical properties. It pertains to the design, analysis, or implementation of polygonal shapes and configurations in foundation elements such as footings, piles or retaining structures.
[E02D 2200/1657] made from single element -- This subgroup covers foundations in hydraulic engineering that are formed from a single, homogeneous element, emphasizing the geometric and physical properties of monolithic foundation components such as a solid concrete block. It relates to the design, analysis and construction of such fixed constructions.
[E02D 2200/1664] made from multiple elements -- This subclass addresses foundations composed of more than one constituent material or component, describing how their geometric or physical characteristics are defined by the combination of elements. [E02D 2200/1671] helical or spiral -- E02D 2200/1671 covers the geometrical and physical characteristics of helical or spiral components employed in hydraulic-engineering foundations, such as helical piles, screw anchors and spiral load-bearing members. The code addresses shape, pitch, diameter and related dimensions that influence load transfer and installation in soil or rock.

[E02D 2250/00] Production methods -- E02D 2250/00 covers methods for producing foundation structures that involve hydraulic engineering, such as concrete placement, grout or water-based construction techniques, and other processes that use fluid pressure to form or install fixed constructions. [E02D 2250/0007] using a mold -- The subclass E02D 2250/0007 covers methods of constructing fixed hydraulic or foundation structures that employ a mold to shape the material, such as casting concrete in-situ or using prefabricated molds to form structural elements. [E02D 2250/0015] using extrusion -- E02D 2250/0015 refers to production methods for foundation elements in hydraulic engineering that employ extrusion techniques. The subclass covers processes where concrete or other building materials are forced through a die to form continuous structural members used in fixed constructions such as piles, shafts or retaining walls.
[E02D 2250/0023] Cast, i.e. -- E02D 2250/0023 relates to the casting of foundations in hydraulic engineering, covering methods for forming, placing and curing concrete or other solidifying materials to create stable base structures for dams, canals, pump stations and similar works. The classification addresses the preparation of molds, reinforcement insertion and curing processes to achieve the required strength and water resistance.
[E02D 2250/003] Injection of material -- The CPC subclass E02D 2250/003 covers methods that inject fluids, grouts, mortars or other materials into the ground to form or reinforce foundation elements. It includes techniques such as pressure-grouting, jet-grouting and chemical injection used to fill voids, improve soil bearing capacity or create waterproof barriers in hydraulic-engineering fixed constructions.
[E02D 2250/0038] using an auger, i.e. continuous flight type -- The subclass E02D 2250/0038 covers foundation production methods in fixed-construction hydraulic engineering that employ a continuous-flight auger, a screw-type device that rotates to convey material while drilling or installing foundation elements. It specifies the use of this auger for creating or reinforcing foundations.
[E02D 2250/0046] using prestressing techniques -- E02D 2250/0046 covers production methods for foundations in hydraulic engineering that employ prestressing techniques, such as applying tensioned reinforcement or pre-stressed concrete elements to improve load-bearing capacity and reduce settlement. It relates to fixed constructions where the structural integrity is enhanced by inducing compressive stresses before service loads are applied.
[E02D 2250/0053] using suction or vacuum techniques -- This classification covers production methods for constructing foundations that employ suction or vacuum techniques, such as drawing soil into a cavity or using negative pressure to install piles or caissons. The approach is used to enhance stability and reduce excavation in hydraulic engineering of fixed constructions.
[E02D 2250/0061] for working underwater -- E02D 2250/0061 covers methods and apparatus for constructing foundations and other fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering that are carried out underwater. [E02D 2250/0076] Drilling -- E02D 2250/0076 refers to drilling methods used in the production of foundations within hydraulic engineering. [E02D 2250/0084] using pneumatical means -- The code designates production methods that employ pneumatic devices-for example air-driven tools, actuators or pressure-controlled equipment-in the construction or treatment of foundations for fixed hydraulic structures. [E02D 2300/0057] PE -- The code denotes the use of polyethylene as a material for foundations in hydraulic engineering, covering elements such as liners, membranes or pipe sections that form part of fixed construction foundations.
[E02D 2300/0059] PP -- PP denotes the use of polypropylene as a construction material in hydraulic-engineering foundations, such as pipe sections, linings or prefabricated elements. The classification covers applications where polypropylene components form part of the fixed structures that support or transmit water or hydraulic loads.
[E02D 2300/006] being recycled -- E02D 2300/006 pertains to foundation materials that have been reclaimed or reused, covering the use of recycled construction waste, demolition debris, reclaimed concrete, reclaimed steel, and other salvaged substances in hydraulic engineering foundation work. It addresses methods for processing, treating, and incorporating these recycled materials into new fixed-construction foundations.
[E02D 2300/0062] Shredded car tires -- It covers the use of shredded automobile tires as a construction material for foundations in hydraulic engineering works such as dams, levees, or other water-retaining structures. The code includes methods of placement, compaction, and performance considerations.
[E02D 2300/0064] made from metal -- E02D 2300/0064 designates foundations in hydraulic engineering that are constructed from metal. The subclass covers metal piles, caissons, and other metal structural elements used to support fixed constructions such as bridges, dams and offshore installations.
[E02D 2300/0065] made from textile -- E02D 2300/0065 covers fixed hydraulic engineering foundations that are constructed using textile materials, such as geotextiles or fabric-reinforced structures, to provide support, stability or filtration in water-related works. It includes methods and devices where textiles form the primary component of the foundation element.
[E02D 2300/0067] made from cellulose -- The subclass E02D 2300/0067 refers to fixed constructions-specifically foundations in hydraulic engineering-that are made from cellulose. It covers foundations built with cellulose-based or cellulose-composite materials, detailing their use, preparation and performance in water-related structures.
[E02D 2300/0068] made from carbon -- The code E02D 2300/0068 designates foundation or other fixed-construction elements in hydraulic engineering that are manufactured from carbon-based materials, such as carbon fibre or carbon-composite components.
[E02D 2300/007] Resins including glasfibers -- E02D 2300/007 covers resin compositions that incorporate glass fibers for use in hydraulic-engineering foundations and other fixed construction applications. It falls under the broader categories of Fixed Constructions, Hydraulic Engineering, Foundations, and Materials.
[E02D 2300/0071] Wood -- A wood material used in hydraulic-engineering foundations, such as timber piles, piles or other structural elements that support or retain water-related constructions. It covers the design, installation and treatment of wooden components that form the fixed foundations of dams, levees, bridge substructures and similar hydraulic works.
[E02D 2300/0073] Explosives -- E02D 2300/0073 relates to the use of explosives in hydraulic-engineering foundation work for fixed constructions such as dams, piles and underwater structures. It addresses the selection, placement and detonation of explosive materials for rock breaking, trenching or soil displacement during foundation preparation.
[E02D 2300/0075] Textiles -- The CPC code E02D 2300/0075 designates patents that concern textile materials used as components of hydraulic-engineering foundations, such as geotextiles, fabric mats, and other woven or non-woven fabrics employed to reinforce, separate, filter, or protect foundation structures. This subclass falls under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Foundations → Materials.
[E02D 2300/0076] non-woven -- E02D 2300/0076 concerns the use of non-woven materials in hydraulic engineering foundations. [E02D 2300/0078] woven -- The E02D 2300/0078 classification covers woven materials-such as fabrics, mats or geotextiles-used in the construction of foundations for hydraulic engineering projects. It pertains to woven items that provide reinforcement, filtration, separation or erosion control within fixed constructions.
[E02D 2300/0079] Granulates -- E02D 2300/0079 covers granulated materials such as crushed stone, sand or slag that are employed in foundation works for hydraulic engineering structures. It pertains to the selection, preparation and use of these granular aggregates to provide load-bearing capacity, stability and drainage in fixed constructions like dams, weirs or flood control foundations.
[E02D 2300/0081] Ceramics -- Ceramics in the context of hydraulic engineering refer to inorganic, non-metallic building materials such as bricks, tiles, pipes and blocks that are employed in fixed constructions like foundations, dams and water-retaining structures. The classification covers the design, manufacture, installation and performance of ceramic components that provide durability, chemical resistance and structural stability in water-exposed environments. It includes both traditional clay-based products and advanced engineered ceramics used for hydraulic foundation applications.
[E02D 2300/0082] Cellulose -- E02D 2300/0082 designates the use of cellulose as a material in foundation work for fixed constructions within hydraulic engineering. It covers cellulose-based products employed for purposes such as reinforcement, drainage, or sealing in foundation structures. This subclass groups inventions that incorporate cellulose into the materials used to build or improve foundations.
[E02D 2300/0084] Geogrids -- E02D 2300/0084 defines geogrids as polymeric or metallic grid-like materials employed in civil works to reinforce soil, increase load distribution and improve stability of foundations, retaining walls and other fixed hydraulic structures. The entry falls under Materials within Foundations of Hydraulic Engineering.
[E02D 2300/0085] Geotextiles -- Geotextiles are permeable fabric materials used in hydraulic-engineering foundations to separate, filter, reinforce, protect, or drain soils and structures. They improve stability of dikes, retaining walls, and embankments by preventing soil migration while allowing water flow, thereby enhancing the performance and longevity of fixed constructions.
[E02D 2300/0087] woven -- E02D 2300/0087 classifies woven materials such as fabrics, mats or geotextiles used in fixed constructions for hydraulic engineering foundations. The code covers woven reinforcement, filtration or separation products incorporated in foundation structures to improve stability or water control.
[E02D 2300/0089] non-woven -- The CPC entry E02D 2300/0089 defines non-woven materials employed in foundation works for hydraulic engineering, such as geotextile fabrics, filter mats or felt-type layers that are placed to provide drainage, separation, reinforcement or protection in fixed constructions. These products are typically made from synthetic or natural fibers bonded without weaving.
[E02D 2300/009] with multi-layer structure -- The CPC entry E02D 2300/009 classifies foundations for fixed hydraulic-engineering structures that employ a multi-layer structure, such as layered piles, mats or ground-improvement layers. It pertains to the material composition and arrangement of these layered foundations within the broader categories of Fixed Constructions, Hydraulic Engineering and Foundations.
[E02D 2300/0092] including a liquid tight layer -- Classification E02D 2300/0092 relates to foundation structures that incorporate a liquid-tight layer, such as a waterproof membrane, slurry, or other sealing material, to prevent water penetration into the footing or pile.
[E02D 2300/0093] including bentonite -- The code E02D 2300/0093 designates the use of bentonite as a material in foundation works for hydraulic engineering, covering applications such as sealing, grouting, slurry formation or ground improvement in fixed constructions. It falls under the hierarchy Fixed constructions > Hydraulic engineering > Foundations > Materials, indicating bentonite's role in enhancing water-tightness and stability of foundation structures.
[E02D 2300/0095] including a plastic membrane -- The code E02D 2300/0095 falls under Fixed Constructions - Hydraulic Engineering - Foundations - Materials and refers to foundation elements that incorporate a plastic membrane. It covers methods and devices where a synthetic barrier is integrated with concrete or other foundation materials to provide waterproofing or chemical resistance.
[E02D 2300/0096] with external friction enhancement features -- E02D 2300/0096 covers foundation structures that improve load-bearing capacity by adding external friction-enhancement elements, such as skirts, plates, surface roughening or other devices that increase the shear resistance between the foundation and surrounding soil or rock. These features are applied to fixed hydraulic constructions to augment bearing performance without altering the primary structural material.
[E02D 2300/0098] Bitumen -- E02D 2300/0098 covers the use of bitumen as a material in hydraulic engineering foundations for fixed constructions, such as dams, levees, and other water-retaining structures. It includes bituminous binders, membranes and sealants applied to improve water tightness, corrosion resistance and structural stability of foundation elements.
[E02D 23/02] Caissons able to be floated on water and to be lowered into water -- Caissons that are designed to be floated on the surface and then lowered into water to form foundations for hydraulic-engineering structures. This class covers the construction, deployment and anchoring methods for such floating caissons used in fixed constructions.
[E02D 23/04] Pneumatic caissons -- E02D 23/04 refers to pneumatic caissons, watertight retaining structures employed as foundations for bridges, piers and similar works, where compressed air is used to keep the work chamber dry and counteract water pressure. This entry falls under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Foundations → Caissons.
[E02D 23/06] Bringing persons or material into or out of compressed air caissons -- The E02D 23/06 classification pertains to the means and procedures used to introduce workers, equipment or supplies into or remove them from compressed-air caissons during construction. It includes air-lock chambers, pressurised hoists, transfer hatches and related safety systems designed to maintain the internal pressure while allowing safe entry and exit.
[E02D 23/08] Lowering or sinking caissons -- E02D 23/08 designates inventions concerned with the process of lowering or sinking caissons, the large watertight chambers used as foundations in hydraulic engineering. It covers techniques for controlling descent, such as ballast adjustment, hydraulic jacking, or guided placement, aiming to ensure stability and accurate positioning during installation.
[E02D 23/10] Caissons filled with compressed air -- Caissons filled with compressed air are watertight foundation structures whose interior is pressurised to prevent water and soil ingress during excavation and construction. The air pressure enables safe work below the water table, allowing reinforcement or concrete to be placed for bridge piers, offshore platforms and other hydraulic engineering foundations.
[E02D 23/12] Inclined lowering -- The CPC subclass E02D 23/12 covers methods for lowering a caisson or other foundation element along an inclined path, typically using external devices such as jacks, winches or guide rails to control the descent. It includes techniques that achieve the desired inclination while the element is being sunk into the ground, ensuring proper alignment and stability during installation.
[E02D 23/14] Decreasing the skin friction while lowering -- The CPC subclass E02D 23/14 covers techniques for reducing the skin-friction resistance that a caisson or other deep foundation experiences while it is being lowered into the ground. It includes methods such as applying lubricants, water jets, sleeves, vibrations or suction devices to facilitate a smoother descent.
[E02D 23/16] Jointing caissons to the foundation soil, specially to uneven foundation soil -- E02D 23/16 covers methods for joining caissons to the surrounding foundation soil, with special emphasis on uneven ground conditions to secure a stable connection. In the CPC hierarchy it is classified under Fixed Constructions > Hydraulic Engineering > Foundations > Caissons.
[E02D 2450/00] Gaskets -- E02D 2450/00 relates to gaskets employed in fixed hydraulic constructions and foundation systems, covering sealing elements that prevent fluid leakage between mating surfaces of pipes, pumps or other components. The subclass addresses the materials, shapes and installation methods used for such gaskets in pressure-containing structures.
[E02D 2450/10] Membranes -- E02D 2450/10 covers membranes incorporated in gaskets used in hydraulic engineering foundations, providing sealing or protective functions in fixed construction elements.

[E02D 2450/102] for liquids -- E02D 2450/102 covers gaskets employed in hydraulic engineering foundations for fixed constructions that are designed to seal or contain liquids. [E02D 2450/103] for gases -- E02D 2450/103 covers gaskets that are installed in fixed hydraulic-engineering foundations and are specifically designed to seal gases, preventing leakage through joints or connections in structures such as dams, reservoirs and other water-control works.
[E02D 2450/105] impermeable -- E02D 2450/105 classifies impermeable hydraulic-engineering gaskets used in foundations and fixed constructions to prevent fluid passage. It covers seal designs, materials and constructions that provide a water-tight or fluid-tight barrier in structures such as dams, tunnels, retaining walls and similar works. This auxiliary group specifies the impermeability requirement within the broader gasket category.
[E02D 2450/106] for liquids -- In CPC subclass E02D 2450/106, the focus is on gaskets employed in fixed-construction hydraulic-engineering foundations that are designed to contain or seal liquids. It covers gasket types, materials, and configurations intended for liquid-tight applications in such structural settings.
[E02D 2450/107] for gases -- This CPC subgroup (E02D 2450/107) relates to gaskets used in fixed hydraulic-engineering foundations that are specifically designed to seal or contain gases, such as gas-tight joints in tunnels, pressure vessels or support structures where a gas-tight seal is required.
[E02D 2450/108] multi-layered -- E02D 2450/108 designates multi-layered gaskets employed in hydraulic-engineering foundations of fixed constructions, covering configurations where two or more gasket layers are combined to enhance sealing performance, pressure resistance and durability.

[E02D 2600/00] Miscellaneous -- E02D 2600/00 Miscellaneous classifies fixed hydraulic engineering constructions that serve as foundations but do not fall under more specific categories, covering assorted foundation types, supporting structures, and ground-work solutions used in water-related projects such as dams, bridges and flood control works.
[E02D 2600/10] comprising sensor means -- The CPC subclass E02D 2600/10 pertains to fixed-construction hydraulic-engineering foundations that incorporate sensor means for monitoring structural behavior, load distribution, or environmental conditions, enabling real-time data collection and performance assessment.

It pertains to designs where anchoring is an integral part of the foundation.
