[E02D 27/00] Foundations as substructures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Foundations when flat on the foundation soil, deeply embedded foundations such as pile foundations;  Foundations for special purposes, e.g. in earthquake territories, in frozen ground, in moors, for masts or machines;  special type of fixing the foundation to the ground such as anchoring or building underneath existing buildings for their recovery.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Consolidating foundation soil in general -> E02D3/02 - E02D3/12 - Sinking worpieces into water or soil -> E02D15/08 - Equipment for dwelling or working under water -> B63C11/00 - Tanks per se -> B65D88/00 - Foundations for railway sleepers -> E01B - Foundations for paving of roads -> E01C3/00 - Bridges for supporting conduits -> E01D18/00 - Dams per se -> E02B3/10 , E02B7/04 - Elevated canals -> E02B5/005 - Arrangements or adaptations of tanks for water supply -> E03B11/00 - Making of a new substructure subsequent to lifting or moving of buildings -> E04G23/06 - Building constructions with protection arrangements against earthquakes -> E04H9/02 - Sockets or holders for poles or masts per se -> E04H12/22 - Special lay-out of foundations with respect to the particularities of the machines to be supported -> F16M9/00
[E02D 27/01] Flat foundations -- Flat foundations are shallow, level base structures that support loads directly on the ground without deep excavation or piling. In hydraulic engineering they are used for small dams, canals, and other water-related works where soil conditions allow a uniform bearing surface, providing a simple, cost-effective solution for distributing structural weight.
[E02D 27/013] Shuttering specially adapted therefor -- E02D 27/013 covers shuttering that is specially designed for use in foundation work, including temporary forms or molds adapted to particular shapes, loads or construction methods. The classification applies to hydraulic-engineered fixed constructions where the shuttering is tailored to the geometry or requirements of the foundation substructure.
[E02D 27/016] made mainly from prefabricated concrete elements -- An E02D 27/016 foundation constructed primarily from prefabricated-concrete components, serving as substructures in hydraulic-engineering works such as dams or bridges. It covers design, installation and performance of these fixed concrete foundation systems.
[E02D 27/02] Flat foundations without substantial excavation -- E02D 27/02 pertains to flat foundations that are installed with little or no excavation, relying on the existing ground surface for support. It includes shallow, level footings or slabs used where soil conditions allow direct bearing without extensive earth removal. This subclass falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering, specifically foundations as substructures.
[E02D 27/04] in water or on quicksand -- The CPC class E02D 27/04 pertains to fixed-construction foundations that are installed in water or on quicksand, covering the design and construction methods for substructures built under these challenging hydraulic conditions. It falls under the hierarchy of Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Foundations.
[E02D 27/06] Floating caisson foundations -- Floating caisson foundations are large watertight concrete or steel structures that are constructed on or near the water surface and then lowered onto prepared seabed or riverbed locations to support bridges, piers or offshore installations. They provide stable support in soft or deep water conditions by displacing water and transferring loads through suction or ballast. This subclass falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering.
[E02D 27/08] Reinforcements for flat foundations -- E02D 27/08 refers to the technical features and methods used to strengthen flat foundations, such as ground-improvement techniques, reinforcing mats, piles, grouting or other devices that increase bearing capacity and limit settlement. It includes design, installation and material specifications for reinforcing the base of structures built on level ground.
[E02D 27/10] Deep foundations -- E02D 27/10 covers deep foundations, which are structural elements transferred through weak surface soils to competent strata below, such as driven piles, drilled shafts, caissons and micropiles. The class addresses design, installation and material considerations for these sub-structures used to support loads of buildings, bridges and other civil works.
[E02D 27/12] Pile foundations -- E02D 27/12 designates pile foundations, which are vertical structural elements driven or drilled into the ground to support loads when surface soils lack sufficient bearing capacity. The code applies to fixed-construction hydraulic engineering works where piles transfer forces from superstructures to deeper, more stable strata, often using concrete, steel or timber.
[E02D 27/14] Pile framings -- Pile framings are structural systems that connect and distribute loads from piles to the over-lying foundation or superstructure, often using caps, beams, or gridworks. They provide lateral stability and allow for settlement control in fixed construction projects such as bridges, marine works, and offshore platforms. This subcategory falls under hydraulic engineering foundations within the CPC hierarchy.
[E02D 27/16] Foundations formed of separate piles -- This subclass covers foundations that are constructed from individual piles inserted into the ground to support structures. It includes driven, bored or otherwise placed piles used singly or in groups to transmit loads to deeper strata, and related methods of installation for sub-structures.
[E02D 27/18] Foundations formed by making use of caissons -- E02D 27/18 concerns foundation systems that utilise caissons - prefabricated hollow or solid shafts placed in excavated pits or driven into the ground or water to support structures. The caisson can be open or closed and is commonly applied to bridges, piers, offshore platforms and other heavy-load constructions.
[E02D 27/20] Caisson foundations combined with pile foundations -- E02D 27/20 concerns caisson foundations that are used together with pile foundations. It covers combined substructure systems where caissons are integrated with driven or bored piles to support loads in hydraulic engineering works.
[E02D 27/22] Caisson foundations made by starting from fixed or floating artificial islands by using protective bulkheads -- The code E02D 27/22 covers caisson foundations that are constructed by beginning on fixed or floating artificial islands and using protective bulkheads to create the substructure. This falls under the broader categories of fixed constructions, hydraulic engineering, and foundations as substructures. It details the method of forming caissons from man-made islands to support structures.
[E02D 27/24] Foundations constructed by making use of diving-bells -- The CPC entry E02D 27/24 covers foundations that are built by employing diving-bells, which are lowered to the work site to create a dry, pressurised chamber for excavating, placing formwork or pouring concrete underwater. It pertains to hydraulic engineering methods for constructing sub-surface foundations using this specialized underwater construction technique.
[E02D 27/26] Compacting soil locally before forming foundations -- The E02D 27/26 entry covers methods of locally compacting soil as a preparatory step before constructing foundation elements. It includes techniques that increase density and bearing capacity of the ground directly beneath the planned foundation, improving stability for fixed construction works.
[E02D 27/28] Stressing the soil or the foundation structure while forming foundations -- E02D 27/28 relates to techniques that apply stress to the soil or the foundation structure during the formation of foundations, such as pre-loading, surcharge loading, or vibration, to improve bearing capacity, settlement control or overall stability of the substructure. This classification falls under the fixed-construction hierarchy for hydraulic engineering foundations.
[E02D 27/30] Foundations made with permanent use of sheet pile bulkheads, walls of planks, or sheet piling boxes -- The code E02D 27/30 classifies foundations that are constructed with permanent sheet-pile bulkheads, planked walls, or sheet-pile box structures. These fixed-construction methods are used in hydraulic-engineering projects to create durable substructural foundations.
[E02D 27/32] Foundations for special purposes -- E02D 27/32 concerns the design and construction of foundations that serve special functions beyond ordinary load-bearing, such as floating or buoyant foundations, foundations for ice or permafrost, seismic isolation systems, and foundations incorporating built-in utilities or transport mechanisms. It includes methods, materials, and structural arrangements tailored to these unique environmental or operational requirements.
[E02D 27/34] Foundations for sinking or earthquake territories -- The code E02D 27/34 covers design and construction of foundations intended for use in areas subject to settlement or seismic activity. It includes measures such as deep piles, ground improvement, and seismic isolation to ensure stability of structures on unstable or earthquake-prone ground. This falls under Fixed Constructions - Hydraulic Engineering - Foundations, specifically foundations as substructures.
[E02D 27/35] Foundations formed in frozen ground, e.g. in permafrost soil -- This subclass covers foundations constructed in permanently frozen ground, including permafrost soils, and the methods used to ensure stability while preventing thawing of the surrounding material. It addresses design, installation and protective measures specific to building support structures in such cryogenic conditions.
[E02D 27/36] Foundations formed in moors or bogs -- E02D 27/36 covers foundations that are constructed in wetland conditions such as moors, bogs or other water-saturated soils. It includes design, installation and ground-improvement techniques for substructures placed in soft, marshy terrain, such as driven or screwed piles, floating or raft foundations, and methods to manage high water tables and low bearing capacity.
[E02D 27/38] Foundations for large tanks, e.g. oil tanks -- E02D 27/38 covers the design and construction of foundations that support large storage tanks, such as oil tanks, with emphasis on structural stability, load distribution and settlement control within hydraulic engineering projects.
[E02D 27/40] Foundations for dams across valleys or for dam constructions -- E02D 27/40 refers to the design and construction of foundations for dams placed across valleys or used in dam projects, covering substructural elements that support the dam body. It falls under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Foundations, addressing the specific engineering requirements of valley-spanning dam foundations.
[E02D 27/42] Foundations for poles, masts or chimneys -- E02D 27/42 addresses the design and construction of foundations that support poles, masts or chimneys, detailing foundation types, installation methods and load-bearing arrangements for these vertical structures. It is a subclass within fixed constructions, hydraulic engineering and foundations as substructures.
[E02D 27/425] specially adapted for wind motors masts  wind motors  per se   F03D1/00 -- The CPC entry E02D 27/425 describes foundations and substructures that are specially adapted for wind-motor masts and wind-driven machinery, focusing on hydraulic engineering solutions for stability and load transfer. It covers foundation types, anchoring and support methods designed to withstand the dynamic forces of wind turbines, and cross-references the related class F03D1/00.
[E02D 27/44] Foundations for machines, engines or ordnance -- E02D 27/44 pertains to foundations that support machines, engines or ordnance, focusing on substructure designs that distribute heavy loads to the ground. It includes concrete pads, spread footings, and specialized bases engineered to provide stable, load-bearing platforms for such equipment.
[E02D 27/46] Foundations for supply conduits or other canals -- E02D 27/46 classifies foundations that are constructed to support supply conduits, canals or similar water-carrying structures, addressing the substructure design, load-bearing elements and installation methods needed to ensure stable, long-term service of these hydraulic installations.
[E02D 27/48] Foundations inserted underneath existing buildings or constructions -- E02D 27/48 relates to foundations that are placed beneath existing buildings or constructions, enabling additional support or load transfer without removing the superstructure. These retrofitted foundations are used in renovation, strengthening or expansion projects when the original foundation is insufficient.
[E02D 27/50] Anchored foundations -- Anchored foundations are substructures that transmit loads from a hydraulic construction to the underlying ground by using mechanical devices such as anchors, piles, or tie-back systems, allowing the foundation to resist uplift, lateral, and overturning forces. They are employed where the soil has limited bearing capacity or where hydrostatic pressures demand additional stability. This classification covers design, installation, and performance criteria for such anchored systems.
[E02D 27/52] Submerged foundations -- E02D 27/52 covers design, construction and analysis of foundations that are permanently situated below water level, such as piles, caissons and other subaqueous substructures that support loads for bridges, offshore platforms and similar works.
[E02D 27/525] using elements penetrating the underwater ground  sinking work pieces  E02D15/08 , sinking caissons  E02D23/02 -- E02D 27/525 classifies foundation work in hydraulic engineering that employs elements driven into the seabed or riverbed to create substructures. It covers techniques for sinking work pieces such as caissons or similar prefabricated units, enabling the construction of fixed foundations beneath water by penetrating the underwater ground.

[E02D 29/02] Retaining or protecting walls -- E02D 29/02 classifies retaining or protecting walls used in fixed constructions, especially in hydraulic-engineering projects such as foundations for underground or underwater structures. [E02D 29/0208] Gabions -- E02D 29/0208 covers gabions, which are wire mesh cages filled with rock or other material used as flexible retaining elements in hydraulic engineering foundations, particularly for underground or underwater structures such as retaining walls, erosion control barriers, and riprap.

[E02D 29/0225] comprising retention means in the backfill -- This subclass refers to foundation structures built underground or underwater that incorporate retention devices within the backfill material. It applies to constructions like caissons, cofferdams or pile caps where the backfill is reinforced or sealed.
[E02D 29/0233] the retention means being anchors  details of anchors  E02D5/80 -- E02D 29/0233 concerns the retention means formed by anchors and the specific details of those anchors used in fixed hydraulic constructions such as foundations for underground or underwater structures. [E02D 29/0241] the retention means being reinforced earth elements -- This subclass covers retention structures that are formed from reinforced earth, such as mechanically stabilized earth walls, using geosynthetic or metal reinforcement to retain soil in foundations for underground or underwater construction.
[E02D 29/025] made up of similar modular elements stacked without mortar -- This CPC subclass covers fixed hydraulic-engineering foundations made from identical modular elements that are stacked without mortar, such as prefabricated blocks used in underground or underwater structures. [E02D 29/0258] characterised by constructional features -- E02D 29/0258 refers to underground or underwater foundation structures that are distinguished by particular constructional features. [E02D 29/0266] made up of preformed elements -- The code covers fixed hydraulic-engineering foundations that are built underground or underwater using prefabricated components such as concrete blocks, steel shells or other pre-shaped elements. It applies to structures like tunnels, caissons or seabed foundations that are assembled from these pre-made parts rather than cast in situ. This subcategory falls under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Foundations.
[E02D 29/0275] cast  in situ -- E02D 29/0275 covers the method of casting concrete or other material directly at the construction site to form foundations for fixed hydraulic structures that are built underground or underwater. It includes techniques for in-situ placement, formwork, and curing of the cast material.
[E02D 29/0283] of mixed type -- This subclass refers to foundations that use a combination of two or more construction methods-such as spread footings together with piles or caissons-specifically for underground or underwater structures in hydraulic-engineering projects. It covers designs where mixed-type foundations are employed to achieve the required stability and load-bearing capacity.
[E02D 29/0291] made up of filled, bag-like elements -- The code covers foundations that are formed from flexible containers, such as bags or tubes, that are placed in an excavated pit or on the seabed and then filled with material like sand, grout, or concrete. When the filled bags are arranged together they create a solid, load-bearing base for piers, retaining walls or other underground or underwater structures.
[E02D 29/04] Making large underground spaces, e.g. for underground plants, e.g. stations of underground railways; Construction or layout thereof  E02D29/10  takes precedence; water-supply or sewerage plants  E03 ; layout of water power plants  E02B9/00 ; making large underground chambers by underground methods only  E21D13/00 -- E02D 29/04 concerns the design and construction of large underground spaces such as railway stations or underground plant facilities, covering their layout and building methods. It is subordinate to the more specific E02D 29/10 and is separate from classifications dealing with water-supply, sewerage or water-power plant layouts.
[E02D 29/045] Underground structures, e.g. tunnels or galleries, built in the open air or by methods involving disturbance of the ground surface all along the location line -- E02D 29/045 covers the design and construction of underground structures such as tunnels or galleries that are built in the open air or by techniques that disturb the ground surface continuously along the alignment, including methods like cut-and-cover, open-cut excavation, and surface-based boring where the whole path is exposed during construction.
[E02D 29/05] at least part of the cross-section being constructed in an open excavation or from the ground surface, e.g. assembled in a trench -- E02D 29/05 relates to fixed hydraulic structures whose foundations are formed, at least partly, by building the cross-section in an open excavation or directly from the ground surface, such as assembling elements within a trench. It addresses construction methods where the foundation is exposed rather than built in confined or underwater spaces.
[E02D 29/055] further excavation of the cross-section proceeding underneath an already installed part of the structure, e.g. the roof of a tunnel -- In hydraulic-engineering fixed constructions, this subclass covers methods for extending an underground or underwater structure by excavating a new cross-section beneath an already installed part, such as the roof of a tunnel.
[E02D 29/06] Constructions, or methods of constructing, in water  E02D15/00  takes precedence; submerged foundations  E02D27/52 -- The code E02D 29/06 covers constructions and construction methods performed in water, specifically dealing with foundations that are built underwater or submerged. It falls under fixed constructions and hydraulic engineering and is subordinate to E02D 15/00, which governs general underwater construction techniques. This subclass addresses the design, installation and support of submerged foundation elements.
[E02D 29/063] Tunnels submerged into, or built in, open water -- E02D 29/063 covers tunnels that are placed under water or built directly in open-water, such as submerged passages or underwater bridge tunnels. The class falls under fixed constructions, hydraulic engineering and foundations for underground or underwater structures.
[E02D 29/067] Floating tunnels -- E02D 29/067 covers floating tunnels, which are buoyant or anchored tunnel structures placed under water bodies for vehicular or utility transport. The classification includes design, construction and support methods for these submerged passages within the broader groups of fixed constructions, hydraulic engineering and foundations.
[E02D 29/07] Tunnels or shuttering therefor preconstructed as a whole or continuously made, and moved into place on the water-bed, e.g. into a preformed trench -- E02D 29/07 covers the construction of tunnels or their formwork that are fabricated as complete units or continuously produced and then placed on a water-bed, for example by lowering them into a pre-formed trench.
[E02D 29/073] Tunnels or shuttering therefor assembled from sections individually sunk onto, or laid on, the water-bed, e.g. in a preformed trench -- E02D 29/073 covers the construction of underground or underwater tunnels and the temporary shuttering used for them, where the structure is built from individual sections that are each placed onto or onto a water-bed, such as in a pre-formed trench.
[E02D 29/077] Tunnels at least partially built beneath the water-bed characterised by being made by methods involving disturbance thereof all along the location line, e.g. by cut-and-cover or caisson methods
[E02D 29/08] Siphons  for sewerage  E03F5/20 ; siphon weirs  E02B7/18 ; siphons in general  F04F10/00
[E02D 29/10] Tunnels or galleries specially adapted to house conduits, e.g. oil pipe-lines, sewer pipes  for pressure water conduits  E02B9/06 ; for cables  H02G9/02 ; layout of tunnels or galleries in general  E21D9/14 ; road kerbs with housings for pipes or the like  E01C11/222 ; Making conduits  in situ , e.g. of concrete  combined with digging of trenches or ditches  E02F5/10 ; making or lining tunnels or galleries  E21D ; constructing tunnels or galleries in open excavations  E02D29/045 ; in open water  E02D29/063 ; Casings, i.e. manhole shafts, access or inspection chambers or coverings of boreholes or narrow wells  wells for drinking water  E03B3/08 ; boreholes or wells formed by deep drilling  E21B ; shafts  E21D
[E02D 29/12] Manhole shafts -- Manhole shafts are vertical access passages installed in underground or underwater foundations to allow entry for inspection, maintenance, and service of utility networks such as water, sewage, or drainage systems. They typically consist of a cylindrical or rectangular shaft with a removable cover and may integrate sealing and support features to resist hydraulic pressure.
[E02D 29/121] characterised by the connection between shaft elements, e.g. of rings forming said shaft -- E02D 29/121 covers shafts whose structural integrity relies on how individual shaft elements are joined, such as rings that are connected to form the complete shaft. The classification applies to fixed hydraulic-engineering constructions, especially foundations for underground or underwater structures, where the method of linking the elements defines the shaft's performance.
[E02D 29/122] Steps or handrails for shafts -- E02D 29/122 covers the provision of steps, ladders or handrails installed within vertical or inclined shafts that form part of underground or underwater foundation works. The code relates to fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering, specifying the design, attachment and safety features of access provisions used for personnel movement inside shafts.
[E02D 29/124] Shaft entirely made of synthetic material -- A shaft constructed wholly from synthetic material, intended for use in fixed constructions such as hydraulic-engineering foundations that are built underground or beneath water. The entry covers design, fabrication and installation of these wholly non-metallic shafts for structural support and fluid conveyance.
[E02D 29/125] characterised by the lining of the shaft -- This subclass covers underground or underwater shafts whose structural integrity is achieved by a lining, commonly used in hydraulic engineering foundations such as wells, caissons, or tunnels. The lining may be concrete, steel, or composite and serves to prevent collapse, control water ingress, and distribute loads.
[E02D 29/127] with devices for impeding fall or injuries of persons
[E02D 29/128] Repairs of manhole shafts -- The entry covers techniques and materials for restoring or reinforcing manhole shafts in underground or underwater utility tunnels, including sealing, structural strengthening, lining replacement, and accessory refurbishment to maintain safe and functional access points.
[E02D 29/14] Covers for manholes or the like -- Covers for manholes or similar access openings are devices that seal underground or underwater structures while allowing periodic entry for inspection, maintenance or drainage. They are typically placed over a circular or rectangular aperture and may be hinged, removable, or lockable, and are made from materials such as cast iron, steel, concrete, or composites to withstand loads and corrosion. This classification covers the design, mounting, sealing and locking features of such covers.
[E02D 29/1409] adjustable in height or inclination -- This subclass covers foundation elements for underground or underwater structures that can be altered after installation to change their vertical position or tilt. It includes devices such as hydraulic jacks, screw adjusters and other mechanisms that make the height or inclination of the construction adjustable.
[E02D 29/1418] with implements to assist in lifting, e.g. counterweights, springs  lifting devices for covers  B66F19/005
[E02D 29/1427] Locking devices  of bayonet type  E02D29/14 -- E02D 29/1427 covers bayonet-type locking devices employed in fixed-construction foundations for hydraulic engineering projects, particularly those involving underground or underwater structures such as tunnels, caissons and other submerged foundations. The devices provide a quick, secure connection by rotating a protruding latch into a matching slot, allowing rapid assembly and reliable holding under pressure and load. This entry falls under the broader categories of fixed constructions, hydraulic engineering and foundations.
[E02D 29/1436] with overflow or explosion control means, e.g. check or relief valves -- This CPC subclass covers fixed underground or underwater foundation constructions that incorporate devices to control overflow or explosive pressure, such as check valves or relief valves. It applies to hydraulic engineering solutions where safety or pressure-release mechanisms are integral to the structural design.
[E02D 29/1445] Tools for positioning or removing cover frames -- A tool designed to position or extract cover frames used in underground or underwater structures such as tunnels, shafts or submerged chambers. It belongs to the fixed-construction category of hydraulic engineering and aids in assembling, aligning or disassembling the protective covers that form part of the structure's foundation.
[E02D 29/1454] Non-circular covers, e.g. hexagonal, elliptic -- The subclass E02D 29/1454 covers non-circular protective covers, such as hexagonal, elliptical or other shaped lids, used in fixed-construction foundations for hydraulic-engineering works, especially for underground or underwater structures. These covers serve to seal openings while accommodating specific load or flow requirements.
[E02D 29/1463] Hinged connection of cover to frame -- E02D 29/1463 classifies the hinged attachment of a cover to its supporting frame in fixed underground or underwater structures such as foundations, shafts or tunnels, enabling the cover to swing open for inspection, maintenance or ventilation while remaining securely fastened when closed.
[E02D 29/1472] Cover entirely made of synthetic material -- A cover constructed wholly from synthetic material is classified under E02D 29/1472, which pertains to fixed hydraulic-engineering foundations for underground or underwater structures. This subclass specifies that the protective covering is made entirely of non-metallic, man-made substances such as plastics or composites, used to seal or protect foundations against water ingress and chemical corrosion.
[E02D 29/1481] Security devices, e.g. indicating unauthorised opening  E02D29/1427  takes precedence -- E02D 29/1481 classifies security devices incorporated in underground or underwater foundation structures, such as sensors or alarms that indicate unauthorized opening or tampering. It falls under the Fixed Constructions - Hydraulic Engineering - Foundations hierarchy and supersedes the broader subclass E02D 29/1427.
[E02D 29/149] Annular gaskets -- Annular gaskets are ring-shaped sealing elements used in underground or underwater foundation structures to prevent fluid leaks and accommodate pressure differentials between adjoining components such as piles, shafts or caissons. In hydraulic engineering they provide a reliable seal between fixed constructions, ensuring durability and water tightness in complex subterranean installations.
[E02D 29/16] Arrangement or construction of joints in foundation structures -- The code E02D 29/16 covers the design and detailing of joints used in foundation structures that are built underground or underwater. It includes methods for connecting concrete, masonry or steel elements to accommodate loads, movement, water pressure and corrosion protection, ensuring the stability and durability of the foundation.
[E02D 3/00] Improving or preserving soil or rock, e.g. preserving permafrost soil -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Improving of soil by adding or mixing with materials such as fibres, filaments, open mesh, stones, lost bodies, water, sand, gravel, chemical agents like grout or cement.  Improving of soil by compacting using mechanical, thermal, electrical or electro-chemical means.  Mechanical means include rolling, tamping, vibrating etc. of the soil on its surface or by penetrating the soil.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Securing of slopes or inclines -> E02D17/20 - Placing or applying sealing substances -> E02D19/16 - Wheels or wheel attachments designed for increasing traction -> B60B15/00 - Machines, tools or auxiliary devices for preparing or distributing paving materials, for working the placed materials, or for forming, consolidating, or finishing the paving -> E01C19/00 - Apparatus or processes for surface or soil stabilisation for road building or like purposes, e.g. mixing local aggregate with binder -> E01C21/00 - Auxiliary devices or arrangements for constructing, repairing, reconditioning, or taking-up road or like surfaces -> E01C23/00  **Glossary:** - Improving or preserving: Improving a foundation soil shall in this context be understood as increasing its load bearing capacity by excluding disadvantages such as high content of water or lack of compaction. - Trench roller: Compaction roller designed to be operated on the bottom of a trench - Slope roller: Compaction roller designed to be operated on a slope or on a largely non-limited area - To vibrate: To move or cause to move forth and back rapidly - To tamp: To force or pack down firmly by repeated blows
[E02D 3/005] Soil-conditioning by mixing with fibrous materials, filaments, open mesh or the like -- The subclass E02D 3/005 addresses methods of conditioning soil for foundations by mixing it with fibrous materials, filaments or open-mesh structures to enhance strength, drainage, thermal control or freeze-thaw resistance, aiding fixed-construction and hydraulic-engineering projects.
[E02D 3/02] Improving by compacting -- The E02D 3/02 subclass covers techniques for improving soil or rock by compacting it to increase strength, reduce settlement, and enhance stability of foundations and other fixed hydraulic structures. It includes methods such as mechanical rolling, vibration, tamping, and impact compaction, and may be applied to preserve permafrost or other sensitive ground conditions.
[E02D 3/026] by rolling with rollers usable only for or specially adapted for soil compaction, e.g. sheepsfoot rollers -- This subclass deals with improving or preserving soil or rock foundations by compacting the ground with rollers that are either usable only for, or specially adapted to, soil compaction, such as sheepsfoot rollers. It falls under fixed-construction hydraulic-engineering methods for foundation work.
[E02D 3/0265] Wheels specially adapted therefor; Cleats for said wheels -- E02D 3/0265 falls under Fixed Constructions, Hydraulic Engineering, Foundations and relates to methods for improving or preserving soil or rock, such as permafrost. The entry covers wheels specially adapted for such work and cleats attached to those wheels to enhance traction on soil or rock surfaces.
[E02D 3/032] Trench rollers -- Trench rollers are machines that travel within excavated trenches to compact and level the backfill, improving the load-bearing capacity of foundations and helping preserve soil structure such as permafrost. They are used in hydraulic engineering projects where stable, compacted soil is required for pipelines, drainage or other underground installations.
[E02D 3/039] Slope rollers -- E02D 3/039 covers slope rollers, machines that travel along a hillside or embankment to compact, level or otherwise improve the ground. They are used in hydraulic engineering fixed-construction foundations to stabilise soil or rock, often where permafrost preservation is required.
[E02D 3/046] by tamping or vibrating, e.g. with auxiliary watering of the soil -- A technique for enhancing the stability of soils or rock used in foundations, especially in hydraulic engineering projects, that employs mechanical tamping or vibratory compaction, often supplemented by auxiliary watering to improve moisture content and reduce settlement. This method is applied to preserve or improve ground conditions such as permafrost.
[E02D 3/054] involving penetration of the soil, e.g. vibroflotation -- The CPC subclass E02D 3/054 covers techniques that improve or preserve soil or rock by penetrating the ground, for example using vibroflotation to densify or stabilize the substrate. It falls under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Foundations and addresses soil-or-rock treatment by forceful insertion.
[E02D 3/061] Tampers with directly acting explosion chambers -- The subclass E02D 3/061 covers tampers or tamping devices used inside directly acting explosion chambers for foundation works. It relates to hydraulic-engineering methods that improve or preserve soil or rock, such as permafrost, by compacting the ground with explosive-driven impacts.
[E02D 3/068] Vibrating apparatus operating with systems involving reciprocating masses -- The E02D 3/068 subclass covers vibrating equipment that creates oscillations through reciprocating masses to treat soils or rock, improving foundation conditions and preserving ground such as permafrost. It includes devices that use cyclic motion to densify, consolidate, or thaw material in hydraulic-engineering construction.
[E02D 3/074] Vibrating apparatus operating with systems involving rotary unbalanced masses -- The subclass covers vibrating devices that generate ground motion by means of rotary unbalanced masses, employed to improve or preserve soil and rock foundations. Such apparatuses are used in hydraulic-engineered works to compact, stabilize or thaw ground, including the protection of permafrost layers.
[E02D 3/08] by inserting stones or lost bodies, e.g. compaction piles -- This subcategory covers methods of stabilising or strengthening soil or rock by inserting stones, broken rock or similar bodies such as compaction piles, used to improve bearing capacity or preserve ground conditions like permafrost.
[E02D 3/10] by watering, draining, de-aerating or blasting, e.g. by installing sand or wick drains -- The E02D 3/10 subclass classifies methods for improving or preserving soil and rock foundations by controlling moisture or gases, such as watering, draining, de-aerating, or blasting, and includes techniques like installing sand or wick drains to stabilise permafrost or other ground conditions. This falls under Fixed Constructions > Hydraulic Engineering > Foundations.
[E02D 31/00] Protective arrangements for foundations or foundation structures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Protecting foundations from harmful influence such as water, humidity, noxious gases, corrosion by soil or water, transmission of vibrations, soil pressure, upward hydraulic pressure or frost heave.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Anchored foundations -> E02D27/50 - Sealings for hydraulic engineering in general -> E02B3/16 - Sealings for building structures in general -> E04B1/66
[E02D 31/002] Ground foundation measures for protecting the soil or subsoil water, e.g. preventing or counteracting oil pollution  not used,  see  subgroups and  E02D31/00 -- E02D 31/002 covers methods and devices for protecting the soil or subsoil water around ground foundations, such as preventing or counteracting oil contamination. It belongs to the hierarchy Fixed constructions → Hydraulic engineering → Foundations → Protective arrangements for foundations or foundation structures.
[E02D 31/004] Sealing liners -- E02D 31/004 (Sealing liners) covers protective arrangements for foundations that employ liners to seal foundation structures against water, soil or chemical infiltration, improving durability and preventing leakage.
[E02D 31/006] Sealing of existing landfills, e.g. using mining techniques -- The CPC subclass E02D 31/006 covers methods for sealing existing landfills, typically using mining-type excavation or backfilling techniques to create an impermeable barrier that protects the underlying foundation. It is part of the protective arrangements for foundations category within hydraulic engineering, addressing the prevention of leachate migration and the stabilization of foundation soils.
[E02D 31/008] against entry of noxious gases, e.g. Radon -- The CPC subclass E02D 31/008 covers protective arrangements for foundations that prevent the ingress of hazardous gases such as radon, including gas-tight membranes, sealing systems, venting devices and other barrier techniques designed to keep noxious gases out of foundation structures.
[E02D 31/02] against ground humidity or ground water -- E02D 31/02 concerns protective arrangements for foundations that guard against ground humidity or groundwater. It falls under the Fixed Constructions > Hydraulic Engineering > Foundations hierarchy and addresses measures such as waterproofing, drainage or barrier systems to prevent moisture ingress in foundation structures.
[E02D 31/025] Draining membranes, sheets or fabric specially adapted therefor, e.g. with dimples -- This subclass relates to protective arrangements for foundations that employ specially adapted draining membranes, sheets or fabrics, often shaped with features such as dimples to enhance water removal from foundation structures. It covers the design and use of these drainage elements within fixed hydraulic constructions.
[E02D 3/103] by installing wick drains or sand bags -- The entry E02D 3/103 covers techniques for stabilising or enhancing foundations in hydraulic-engineering works by placing wick drains or sand-bag systems within the soil or rock. These measures are used to improve drainage, accelerate consolidation, or preserve ground conditions such as permafrost, thereby strengthening the underlying construction.
[E02D 31/04] Watertight packings for use under hydraulic pressure -- The CPC subclass E02D 31/04 covers devices that provide a watertight seal for foundation elements subjected to hydraulic pressure, such as seals, gaskets and packings used in dams, bridge piers or underwater structures to prevent water ingress. These packings are designed to retain integrity under hydraulic loads, ensuring the stability and durability of the foundation.
[E02D 3/106] by forming sand drains containing only loose aggregates  sand piles  E02D3/08
[E02D 31/06] against corrosion by soil or water -- E02D 31/06 covers protective measures applied to foundations or foundation structures to prevent corrosion caused by soil or water. It falls under the Fixed Constructions - Hydraulic Engineering - Foundations category in the CPC used by the EPO and USPTO.
[E02D 31/08] against transmission of vibrations or movements in the foundation soil -- The E02D 31/08 class covers measures that isolate a foundation from ground-borne vibrations or movements, protecting the superstructure from dynamic loads transmitted through the soil. It includes damping, isolation, and vibration-attenuation techniques applied to foundations or foundation structures. This falls under protective arrangements for foundations within the fixed-construction and hydraulic-engineering hierarchy.
[E02D 3/11] by thermal, electrical or electro-chemical means -- This subgroup covers methods that use thermal, electrical or electro-chemical treatments to improve or preserve soil or rock in foundation work, such as thawing permafrost, drying, consolidating ground or preventing frost heave. It is placed under Fixed Constructions, Hydraulic Engineering, Foundations.
[E02D 31/10] against soil pressure or hydraulic pressure -- E02D 31/10 covers protective measures applied to foundations or foundation structures to resist soil pressure or hydraulic pressure. It includes design features such as retaining walls, waterproof membranes, pressure-relief systems, and other construction techniques that prevent structural failure from lateral earth or water forces.
[E02D 31/12] against upward hydraulic pressure -- E02D 31/12 pertains to protective measures for foundations that counteract upward hydraulic pressure, such as uplift caused by groundwater or seepage. It includes drainage systems, pressurized membranes, counterweights or other devices that prevent foundation displacement.
[E02D 31/14] against frost heaves in soil -- This subclass covers methods and devices that protect foundations from frost-induced heaving, including thermal insulation, heated foundation elements, ground-freezing control, and drainage systems that keep the soil temperature above freezing. It also encompasses design features such as insulated slabs, heating coils, or vapor barriers used to prevent ice formation that could lift or damage foundation structures.
[E02D 3/115] by freezing -- E02D 3/115 covers techniques that improve or preserve soil or rock by freezing, a method used in hydraulic-engineering foundations to stabilize ground, control water, or protect permafrost during construction.
