[E02D 5/00] Bulkheads, piles, or other structural elements specially adapted to foundation engineering -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sheet piles made of steel and their locking forms;  Sheet piles of concrete and their locking forms;  Bulkheads made of concrete in situ, of prefabricated parts and concrete in situ;  Piles made of timber, steel or concrete;  Prefabricated piles or piles cast in position, e.g. concrete piles;  Concrete piles cast in position with or without the use of mould-pipes or moulds;  Mould-pipes or moulds for making piles or bulkheads;  Pile shoes;  Means for anchoring piles or bulkheads.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Foundation slots (made of concrete) -> E02D17/13 - Structural elongated elements designed for load-supporting -> E04C3/00 - Pegs, stakes or the like -> E04H15/62 - Drilling rods or pipes -> E21B17/00 - Anchoring-bolts for roof, floor -> E21D21/00  **Glossary:** - Bulkhead: A Wall or partition built to hold back earth or water - In situ: Fabricated at the place of the intended use - Mould-pipe: A Pipe used as the mould for the pile, normally a concrete pile cast in situ
[E02D 5/02] Sheet piles or sheet pile bulkheads -- Sheet piles are interlocking vertical elements, usually of steel, concrete or timber, driven or placed in the ground to create a continuous wall that retains earth, water, or other materials, commonly used as bulkheads, quay walls, or foundation barriers. In this CPC subgroup the focus is on designs where the sheet piles function as a permanent structural bulkhead within hydraulic and foundation engineering.
[E02D 5/03] Prefabricated parts -- This subclass covers the use of prefabricated components such as pre-cast concrete members, steel sections, or modular units that are manufactured off-site and installed as bulkheads, piles, or other foundation elements in hydraulic engineering works. It includes design, fabrication, transport, and assembly methods for these fixed construction elements.
[E02D 5/04] made of steel -- E02D 5/04 covers foundation elements such as bulkheads, piles or similar structures that are specially adapted for hydraulic engineering and are fabricated from steel. The code is used to classify inventions that employ steel components to support, retain or protect water-related works and other fixed constructions.
[E02D 5/06] Fitted piles or other elements specially adapted for closing gaps between two sheet piles or between two walls of sheet piles -- This subclass covers piles or other specially shaped elements installed to close the voids between adjacent sheet-piles or between the walls of sheet-pile structures, providing a continuous and watertight foundation.
[E02D 5/08] Locking forms -- E02D 5/08, Locking forms, covers devices that secure formwork in hydraulic-engineering foundation work such as bulkheads, piles and other structural elements, keeping the forms precisely aligned and stable while concrete is placed.
[E02D 5/10] made of concrete or reinforced concrete -- The subclass E02D 5/10 covers bulkheads, piles and other structural foundation elements that are built of concrete or reinforced concrete, focusing on their use in fixed constructions for hydraulic engineering. It classifies foundation components specially adapted to resist water pressure or support hydraulic works.
[E02D 5/105] of prestressed concrete  E02D5/12  takes precedence
[E02D 5/12] Locking forms -- Locking forms are devices or arrangements that interlock or otherwise secure formwork used in the construction of foundations, bulkheads, piles and similar structural elements, ensuring precise alignment and stability of concrete during placement. The classification covers the design, operation and application of such forms within hydraulic engineering and fixed-construction projects.
[E02D 5/14] Sealing joints between adjacent piles -- The CPC subclass E02D 5/14 covers methods and devices for sealing the joints between adjacent piles in hydraulic-engineering foundations such as bulkheads and piles. It addresses techniques that prevent water ingress and enhance the structural integrity of pile-to-pile connections.
[E02D 5/16] Auxiliary devices rigidly or detachably arranged on sheet piles for facilitating assembly -- Auxiliary devices that are rigidly or detachably mounted on sheet piles to simplify their assembly in foundation works. Such devices include guides, clamps, handling tools or alignment aids that facilitate positioning, driving or removal of sheet piles during construction of bulkheads, piles and other hydraulic-engineering foundations.
[E02D 5/18] Bulkheads or similar walls made solely of concrete -- E02D 5/18 covers concrete bulkheads and similar retaining walls that are constructed entirely of concrete, used in foundations and hydraulic engineering for stabilizing soil or water pressures. These structures are designed as permanent, monolithic elements without reinforcement of other materials.
[E02D 5/182] using formworks to separate sections -- This subclass covers the use of formwork to create separate zones within a foundation element such as a bulkhead, pile or other structural component, enabling staged pouring, differential reinforcement, or isolated construction of distinct parts. It includes temporary or permanent partitions that define individual sections during casting or installation.
[E02D 5/185] with flexible joint members between sections  joints in foundation structures  E02D29/16 ; flexible joints in hydraulic engineering  E02B3/16 , in building  E04B1/68 -- E02D 5/185 covers foundation elements such as bulkheads, piles or other structural components that incorporate flexible joint members between sections, allowing movement or adjustment. It applies to hydraulic-engineering and building contexts where such joints relieve stresses in foundations, for example in flexible connections between piles or wall sections.
[E02D 5/187] the bulkheads or walls being made continuously, e.g. excavating and constructing bulkheads or walls in the same process, without joints -- E02D 5/187 covers the construction of bulkheads or retaining walls in a single, uninterrupted process where excavation and the building of the wall are performed together, resulting in a continuous, joint-free structure. It applies to foundation engineering techniques that integrate digging and wall formation without separate steps.
[E02D 5/20] Bulkheads or similar walls made of prefabricated parts and concrete, including reinforced concrete, -- The subclass E02D 5/20 covers bulkheads and comparable retaining walls that are built from prefabricated parts combined with concrete, including reinforced concrete, for use in hydraulic-engineering foundations such as fixed constructions, piles or other specially adapted structural elements.
[E02D 5/22] Piles -- Piles are long, slender structural elements driven or drilled into the ground to transmit loads from a superstructure through weak surface soils to deeper load-bearing strata, providing stable foundations for bridges, buildings and other works. They can be made of timber, steel, concrete or composite materials and may be installed by driving, boring, or jetting methods.
[E02D 5/223] Details of top sections of foundation piles  E02D5/54  takes precedence -- Subclass E02D 5/223 concerns the design and construction details of the upper sections of foundation piles, including caps, heads, and connections to over-structure or protective elements. It is part of the fixed-construction group within hydraulic-engineering foundations that deal with bulkheads, piles and similar structural components.
[E02D 5/226] Protecting piles  piles with protecting cases  E02D5/60 -- E02D 5/226 covers protective casings or sleeves for foundation piles, including methods and devices that shield piles from soil, water, corrosion, impact or other damaging influences during installation and service. It is a sub-category of fixed-construction hydraulic-engineering foundations that deal with bulkheads, piles and related structural elements specially adapted to foundation engineering.
[E02D 5/24] Prefabricated piles -- E02D 5/24 - Prefabricated piles denotes off-site manufactured pile elements that are driven, drilled or otherwise placed into the ground to form foundations for civil engineering works. The classification covers their design, fabrication, installation methods and related foundation applications within fixed construction and hydraulic engineering contexts.
[E02D 5/26] made of timber with or without reinforcement -- The CPC code E02D 5/26 pertains to foundation structures-such as bulkheads, piles or similar elements-specifically built from timber, either plain or reinforced, for use in hydraulic-engineering projects. It classifies inventions that employ wood as the primary material in fixed constructions designed to support or retain water-related works.
[E02D 5/28] made of steel -- E02D 5/28 designates hydraulic-engineering foundation elements such as bulkheads, piles and similar structural components that are specifically adapted for foundation work and are constructed of steel. The code is used in the Cooperative Patent Classification system shared by the EPO and USPTO.
[E02D 5/285] tubular, e.g. prefabricated from sheet pile elements  sheet pile boxes  E02D27/30 ; concrete piles with metal casings  E02D5/30 -- E02D 5/285 addresses tubular foundation components, including prefabricated sheet-pile elements and sheet-pile boxes, as well as concrete piles that incorporate metal casings. These tubular elements are employed as bulkheads, piles, or other structural parts specially adapted for hydraulic-engineering foundations.
[E02D 5/30] made of concrete or reinforced concrete or made of steel and concrete -- This subclass covers bulkheads, piles and similar foundation elements that are constructed from concrete, reinforced concrete, or a combination of steel and concrete. It includes structures specifically adapted for foundation engineering in hydraulic works.
[E02D 5/32] with arrangements for setting in position by fluid jets -- E02D 5/32 covers foundation elements such as bulkheads, piles or other specially adapted structural members that are positioned by means of high-pressure fluid jets. The subclass includes methods in which water or other fluids are directed to fluidise, loosen or displace soil so the element can be set in its final location, often used for underwater or difficult-ground installations.
[E02D 5/34] Concrete or concrete-like piles cast in position -- E02D 5/34 covers piles made of concrete or similar materials that are formed and hardened in place to serve as foundation elements. These cast-in-situ piles are used to support structures, retain soil, or form bulkheads in hydraulic engineering projects.
[E02D 5/36] making without use of mouldpipes or other moulds
[E02D 5/38] making by use of mould-pipes or other moulds -- E02D 5/38 covers techniques for constructing foundation elements such as bulkheads, piles or other structural components by casting concrete in mould-pipes or similar moulds, enabling the creation of fixed hydraulic works through prefabricated or in-situ formwork. This subclass falls under Fixed Constructions, Hydraulic Engineering, Foundations.
[E02D 5/385] with removal of the outer mould-pipes  documents also covered by one or several of the groups  E02D5/40  -  E02D5/44  are classified in all the relevant groups -- E02D 5/385 designates foundation elements such as bulkheads, piles, or similar structures in hydraulic engineering where the outer mould-pipes are removed after installation. The classification applies to any documents also classified in the related sub-groups E02D5/40 to E02D5/44.
[E02D 5/40] in open water -- This CPC subclass covers foundation elements such as bulkheads, piles or similar structures that are installed in open water environments. It relates to hydraulic engineering works where the foundations are placed in lakes, seas or rivers rather than on land.
[E02D 5/42] by making use of pressure liquid or pressure gas for compacting the concrete -- A technique for compacting concrete in foundations that employs pressurised liquid or gas to achieve densification, used in hydraulic engineering works such as bulkheads, piles and other structural elements adapted to foundation construction. This falls under the Cooperative Patent Classification E02D 5/42 within the Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Foundations hierarchy.
[E02D 5/44] with enlarged footing or enlargements at the bottom of the pile -- E02D 5/44 covers piles or similar foundation elements that incorporate an enlarged footing or a bottom-end enlargement. The enlarged section increases bearing area, improves load transfer to the soil, and enhances stability for structures requiring specialized foundation solutions such as bulkheads or other hydraulic-engineered works.
[E02D 5/445] by application of pyrotechniques -- This subclass covers the use of explosive or fire-based methods to install, drive, cut or shape bulkheads, piles and other structural elements specially adapted to foundation engineering, employing pyrotechnic techniques such as blasting or thermite.
[E02D 5/46] making
[E02D 5/48] Piles varying in construction along their length -- E02D 5/48 covers piles whose construction changes along their length, such as varying material, diameter, or reinforcement sections. By tailoring these sections to differing soil layers or load demands, the piles provide optimized bearing capacity and stability in hydraulic engineering foundations. The classification is used for designs where the pile profile is not uniform from tip to head.
[E02D 5/50] Piles comprising both precast concrete portions and concrete portions cast -- The class covers foundation piles that combine precast concrete sections with concrete that is cast in place, detailing structures where part of the pile is fabricated off-site and the remaining portion is formed on site to complete the pile.
[E02D 5/52] Piles composed of separable parts, e.g. telescopic tubes -- E02D 5/52 covers piles that are built from separable components, such as telescopic tubes that can be extended or assembled on site. These modular piles are designed for foundation work in hydraulic engineering, allowing adjustable length and easier installation in bulkheads, piles or other structural foundation elements.
[E02D 5/523] composed of segments -- The CPC class E02D 5/523 covers fixed constructions in hydraulic-engineering foundations, such as bulkheads, sheet-pile walls or similar structural elements that are built from multiple joined segments rather than a single monolithic piece. It addresses the design, manufacture and assembly of these segmented foundation components.
[E02D 5/526] Connection means between pile segments -- E02D 5/526 pertains to the means used to join adjacent pile sections in foundation structures, including couplers, threaded sleeves, bolts, clamps, and welding techniques that provide a secure, load-transmitting connection between individual pile segments.
[E02D 5/54] Piles with prefabricated supports or anchoring parts -- These piles incorporate prefabricated supports or anchoring parts such as brackets, plates or tubes that are attached during installation to improve stability and load transfer in foundation work. The classification covers methods and structures where the supporting elements are manufactured off-site and integrated with the pile in hydraulic or civil engineering projects.
[E02D 5/56] Screw piles -- E02D 5/56 refers to screw piles, helical steel shafts that are rotated into the ground to create a deep foundation element, providing load-bearing support for structures such as bulkheads, retaining walls or other hydraulic engineering installations.
[E02D 5/58] Prestressed concrete piles -- E02D 5/58 refers to prestressed concrete piles, which are foundation elements driven or drilled into the ground where the concrete is pre-tensioned or post-tensioned to improve load capacity and reduce settlement. These piles are used in hydraulic engineering projects for supporting structures on soft or variable soils.
[E02D 5/60] Piles with protecting cases -- The entry refers to piles that incorporate protective casings or sleeves, detailing designs that shield the pile from soil, water, or mechanical damage during installation and service. It applies to foundation elements such as bulkheads where the casing improves durability and load-transfer performance.
[E02D 5/62] Compacting the soil at the footing or in a casing by forcing cement or like material through tubes -- The code E02D 5/62 covers methods for improving soil bearing capacity at footings or within casings by injecting cement or similar material through tubes to compact the ground. It applies to foundation works such as bulkheads, piles or other structural elements where hydraulic engineering techniques are used to consolidate the substrate.
[E02D 5/64] Repairing piles -- E02D 5/64 covers techniques for restoring and strengthening piles that support foundations or hydraulic structures. It includes methods for repairing damaged, corroded or undermined piles, such as grout injection, sleeve installation, reinforcement, and other remedial treatments that restore load-bearing capacity and durability.
[E02D 5/66] Mould-pipes or other moulds -- The CPC code E02D 5/66 covers mould-pipes and other moulds used in the construction of foundations, bulkheads, piles and similar hydraulic-engineering structures. These forms may be temporary or permanent and shape, support or protect concrete, soil or rock during installation or operation.
[E02D 5/665] for making piles -- E02D 5/665 covers methods and apparatus for fabricating piles used in foundation work, including techniques for driving, installing, or forming pile elements in soil or water. It falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering, relating to bulkheads, piles and other structural elements specially adapted to foundation engineering.
[E02D 5/68] for making bulkheads or elements thereof -- The subclass E02D 5/68 addresses techniques and devices for producing bulkheads or similar structural elements employed in foundation engineering, including piles and other components specially adapted for hydraulic construction of fixed foundations.
[E02D 5/70] for making sheet piles -- The E02D 5/70 subclass covers inventions related to the manufacturing of sheet piles, which are long, thin structural members driven into the ground to form continuous retaining walls or bulkheads. It includes processes, machines, and methods for shaping, joining, and treating the metal or other material sheets used as foundation supports in hydraulic engineering.
[E02D 5/72] Pile shoes -- Pile shoes are fittings mounted on the tip of driven or bored piles to enlarge the bearing area, protect the pile end from damage and improve load transfer to the surrounding soil or rock. They are used in foundation works where bulkheads, piles or similar structural elements require enhanced stability and reduced settlement.
[E02D 5/74] Means for anchoring structural elements or bulkheads -- This subclass covers devices and methods used to secure or anchor bulkheads, piles, and other foundation elements in hydraulic engineering works. It includes mechanical anchors, grouting systems, tension piles and similar means ensuring stability of fixed structures against water pressure and soil forces.
[E02D 5/76] Anchorings for bulkheads or sections thereof -- E02D 5/76 classifies devices and methods that secure bulkheads, piles or similar structural elements to the ground, focusing on anchorings for whole bulkheads or individual sections. It falls under hydraulic engineering foundations and covers solutions designed to improve the stability and load-transfer of these fixed constructions.
[E02D 5/765] removable -- E02D 5/765 covers foundation elements such as bulkheads, piles or similar structures that are designed to be removed, repositioned or disassembled after installation, allowing temporary or adaptable hydraulic engineering works. The code applies to removable components used in fixed-construction foundations for water-related projects.
[E02D 5/80] Ground anchors -- Ground anchors are structural devices installed in soil or rock to transmit tensile forces from a supported element to the surrounding ground, thereby stabilising foundations, retaining walls or other civil works. They are employed where conventional mass or friction foundations are impractical, providing a reliable method for resisting uplift, lateral or seismic loads.
[E02D 5/801] driven by screwing -- E02D 5/801 pertains to hydraulic engineering foundations where bulkheads, piles or similar structural elements are installed by a screwing-in method rather than impact-driving. It covers the design, construction and application of helical or screw-type foundation devices used to achieve load-bearing capacity in soil or water environments. This subclass falls under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Foundations.
[E02D 5/803] with pivotable anchoring members -- The E02D 5/803 classification pertains to foundation elements such as bulkheads, piles or similar structures that include pivotable anchoring members, enabling the anchor to rotate or adjust relative to the element. This feature is used in hydraulic engineering to accommodate movements or loads while securing the foundation.
[E02D 5/805] with deformable anchoring members -- Subclass E02D 5/805 relates to foundations, especially bulkheads, piles or similar structural components, that incorporate anchoring members designed to deform under load. The deformable anchors allow the element to adjust to soil movements, enhancing stability and load distribution.
[E02D 5/806] involving use of explosives -- E02D 5/806 covers methods that employ explosives to create or modify bulkheads, piles, or other structural elements used in hydraulic-engineering foundations. The classification applies to explosive techniques for installing, shaping, or removing these foundation components in projects such as dams, harbors, or water-control structures.
[E02D 5/808] anchored by using exclusively a bonding material -- The entry E02D 5/808 pertains to fixed hydraulic-engineering foundations such as bulkheads, piles or similar structural elements that are anchored solely by a bonding material, for example grout, mortar or cement paste, without the use of mechanical anchors or fasteners. It addresses the design and construction methods in which the sole retention force is provided by the chemical adhesion of the bonding agent to the surrounding soil or structure.
[E02D 7/00] Methods or apparatus for placing sheet pile bulkheads, piles, mouldpipes, or other moulds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Inserting sheet piles, piles or mould-pipes for purpose of foundation into a soil.  Specifying the process of placing the above elements which may be done by driving, by vibrating, by pressure or pulling power, by screwing down, by using fluid jets, by using several means simultaneously, by means arranged inside a hollow pipe or mould pipe.  Machines and equipment for exerting these operations.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - For both placing and removing -> E02D11/00 - Sockets or holders for posts driven into the ground -> E04H12/2215 - Sockets or holders for posts driven into the ground by screwing -> E04H12/2223 - Devices for erecting or removing fences -> E04H17/26 - Earth drilling, e.g. deep drilling -> E21B  **Glossary:** - To drive: To force or bring down a foundation element by means other than by vibrating, by pressure or pulling power, by screwing down, by fluid jets, or by means arragned inside the pile. - To vibrate: To move or cause to move forth and back rapidly
[E02D 7/02] Placing by driving -- E02D 7/02 covers methods and equipment for installing sheet-pile bulkheads, piles, mouldpipes or similar moulds by driving them into the ground. It includes impact, vibration or hydraulic hammer techniques that embed the elements to form foundations, retaining walls or other fixed hydraulic-engineering structures.
[E02D 7/04] Hand pile-drivers -- Hand pile-drivers are manually operated tools that drive piles, sheet-pile bulkheads, mouldpipes or similar elements into the ground. They are used in foundation work and hydraulic-engineering projects where precise placement of piles or moulds is required without powered equipment.
[E02D 7/06] Power-driven drivers -- Power-driven drivers are machines that use hydraulic or mechanical power to drive sheet-pile bulkheads, piles, mouldpipes or similar moulds into the ground for foundation work. They are typically mounted on excavators, specialized carrier frames or self-propelled units and provide rapid, continuous placement of hydraulic-engineering structures.
[E02D 7/08] Drop drivers with free-falling hammer -- E02D 7/08 covers hydraulic or mechanical devices that drive sheet-pile bulkheads, piles, mouldpipes or similar structures by releasing a hammer that falls freely under gravity. The drop driver uses the hammer's kinetic energy to embed the element without external power during the impact, allowing rapid, repeatable placement in foundation work.
[E02D 7/10] with pressure-actuated hammer, -- The subclass E02D 7/10 covers methods or apparatus for installing sheet-pile bulkheads, piles, mouldpipes or similar moulds that employ a pressure-actuated hammer to drive the elements into the ground, typically in hydraulic-engineering foundation work. It specifies the use of a hydraulic or pneumatic impulse device to impart the striking force required for placement.
[E02D 7/12] Drivers with explosion chambers -- E02D 7/12 covers drivers that use explosion chambers to generate the force needed for installing sheet-pile bulkheads, piles, mouldpipes or similar moulds in hydraulic-engineering foundations. The classification includes devices where a controlled blast propels the driving element into the ground.
[E02D 7/125] Diesel drivers -- E02D 7/125 covers diesel-powered devices for driving or installing sheet-pile bulkheads, piles, mouldpipes and similar foundation elements. It includes diesel hammers, impact drivers and related apparatus used in hydraulic-engineering construction of fixed foundations.
[E02D 7/14] Components for drivers -- E02D 7/14 covers the ancillary parts that make up sheet-pile or mould-pipe driving machines, such as hydraulic cylinders, pistons, control valves, power packs, clamping devices and other mechanical or hydraulic components that enable the driver to position and embed the pile or mould in place.
[E02D 7/16] Scaffolds for drivers -- The E02D 7/16 subclass concerns scaffolding or support structures specifically designed for drivers that install sheet-pile bulkheads, piles, mouldpipes or similar moulds. It includes apparatus that provides alignment, stability and access for the driving equipment during placement, ensuring accurate and safe installation of hydraulic-engineered foundations.
[E02D 7/165] of variable length, e.g. foldable or telescopic -- This CPC subclass E02D 7/165 covers methods and apparatus for installing sheet-pile bulkheads, piles, mouldpipes or other moulds whose length can be varied, for example foldable or telescopic elements. It is situated in the hierarchy under fixed constructions, hydraulic engineering, foundations.
[E02D 7/18] Placing by vibrating -- The entry covers methods or apparatus that employ vibration to drive and position sheet-pile bulkheads, piles, mouldpipes or similar moulds during installation, facilitating their placement in hydraulic engineering foundations.This entry covers methods or apparatus that use vibration to drive and position sheet-pile bulkheads, piles, mouldpipes or similar moulds during installation, facilitating their placement in hydraulic-engineering foundations.
[E02D 7/20] Placing by pressure or pulling power -- This subclass covers techniques for installing sheet-pile bulkheads, piles, mouldpipes or similar moulds by applying pressure or pulling forces, including hydraulic or mechanical devices that push or draw the element into its final position. It pertains to fixed-construction foundation work where placement relies on force-driven methods rather than manual handling.
[E02D 7/22] Placing by screwing down -- E02D 7/22 covers methods and devices for installing sheet-pile bulkheads, piles, mouldpipes or similar moulds by screwing them down into the ground, often using hydraulic driving equipment. It is classified under fixed constructions, hydraulic engineering and foundations.
[E02D 7/24] Placing by using fluid jets -- E02D 7/24 pertains to methods or apparatus that employ high-pressure fluid jets to drive sheet-pile bulkheads, piles, mouldpipes or similar moulds into the ground as part of hydraulic-engineering foundation work. The classification captures techniques that use water, air or other fluids to place these fixed constructions.
[E02D 7/26] Placing by using several means simultaneously -- E02D 7/26 covers methods and apparatus that install sheet-pile bulkheads, piles, mouldpipes or similar moulds by employing two or more placement techniques at the same time, such as simultaneous driving, vibrating, suction or pressing, to improve speed, accuracy or stability during foundation work.
[E02D 7/28] Placing of hollow pipes or mould pipes by means arranged inside the piles or pipes -- The subclass E02D 7/28 covers techniques and devices for installing hollow or mould pipes in foundations by using equipment positioned inside the pipe itself, such as internal jacks, airbags or hydraulic rams that push or pull the pipe into place while it is being driven or set.
[E02D 7/30] by driving cores -- E02D 7/30 covers techniques for installing foundations in hydraulic engineering by driving core elements such as sheet-pile bulkheads, piles, mouldpipes or similar moulds. It includes apparatus and methods that embed these cores into the ground to form fixed constructions for retaining walls, cofferdams or other water-related structures.
[E02D 9/00] Removing sheet piles bulkheads, piles, mould-pipes or other moulds -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Removing the top section of piles,  Removing by withdrawing,  Removing by cutting-off an element under water  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Methods or apparatus for both placing and removing sheet pile bulkheads, piles, or other mould-pipes -> E02D11/00 - Devices for erecting or removing fences -> E04H17/26 - Derricks or masts for drilling machines -> E21B15/00
[E02D 9/005] removing the top of placed piles of sheet piles  E02D9/04  takes precedence -- This subclass covers methods for removing the upper portion of installed sheet piles, bulkheads, piles, mould-pipes or other moulds in hydraulic-engineering foundations, such as cutting, pulling or otherwise extracting the top of placed sheet piles. It is designated E02D 9/005 and is subordinate to the broader E02D9/04.
[E02D 9/02] by withdrawing -- This subclass covers the removal of sheet piles, bulkheads, piles, mould-pipes or similar moulds by pulling them out of the ground, typically using hydraulic, mechanical or other withdrawal techniques. It pertains to foundation work in hydraulic engineering where structures are withdrawn rather than broken or cut.
[E02D 9/04] by cutting-off under water -- This subclass covers underwater removal of sheet piles, bulkheads, piles, mould-pipes or similar moulds by cutting them off while submerged, applied in foundation work of hydraulic engineering. It addresses fixed-construction techniques for extracting these elements without disturbing the surrounding structure.
[E02F] DREDGING -- **Definition:** This place covers:  **Glossary:** - Work implement: those parts that actually carry out the work of shifting soil and are attached to the vehicle or vessel body. For example: boom + dipper-arm + bucket.
[E02F 1/00] General working methods with dredgers or soil-shifting machines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This group has to be considered as residual to all the other groups and subgroups of E02F . This group is very rarely used to classify documents.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Dredgers; Soil-shifting machines -> E02F3/00 - Dredgers or soil-shifting machines for special purposes -> E02F5/00
[E02F 3/00] Dredgers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  the following vehicles or vessels, with their corresponding methods, their particular components and the drives and control systems specially adapted for each of those particular vehicles:  With digging screws ( E02F3/06 )  WO2004/043832  With digging elements on an endless chain ( E02F3/08 )  US3813125  With digging wheels turning round an axis ( E02F3/18 )  FR1267198  With a pair of digging wheels, slotting machines ( E02F3/205 )  EP1630301  With digging tools mounted on a single (bucket-) arm ( E02F3/283 )  EP0445933  EP1294993  With digging tools mounted on a dipper-arm pivoted on a boom ( E02F3/30 )  EP2141289  With digging tools mounted on a pair of bucket-arms ( E02F3/34 )  GB859309 (pair of arms - 50 and 52)  With bucket-arms being pivoted at the rear of the vehicle chassis, skid steer loader ( E02F3/3414 )  WO2009020509  With reciprocating digging or scraping elements moved by cables or hoisting ropes ( E02F3/46 ) with following subgroups:  EP1950353  WO2004067856  US4134221  US4184958  Bucket cars, i.e. having scraper bowls ( E02F3/64 )  DE2352214  Graders, bulldozers ( E02F3/76 ) with the following subgroups:  GB1080317  in exploded view  US2011067892 (arms 3, frame 4, blade 5)  DE2707006  GB2065749  With arrangements acting by a sucking or forcing effect, e.g. suction dredgers ( E02F3/88 )  CA2285196  US6857207  **Glossary:** - Slidable vs telescopic ( E02F3/304 , E02F3/305 , E02F3/306 ): - slidable: an arm or a boom glides or slides as a whole piece along the axis of a support- telescopic: an arm or a boom is composed of several elements sliding within each other so that the arm or boom becomes longer or shorter
[E02F 3/02] hand-operated -- E02F 3/02 covers dredging machines that are operated manually without any powered assistance. These hand-operated dredgers are used for small-scale removal of sediment in waterways where low-energy or portable equipment is needed. The class is part of fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering for dredging.
[E02F 3/04] mechanically-driven -- E02F 3/04 classifies dredging equipment that is driven by mechanical power sources such as engines, motors, or gear trains, as opposed to hydraulic or self-propelled means. It covers the design, structure and operation of mechanically powered dredgers used in hydraulic engineering projects. This subclass falls under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Dredging → Dredgers.
[E02F 3/045] with oscillating digging tools, e.g. oscillating spades -- This subgroup classifies fixed-construction dredgers that employ oscillating digging tools, such as oscillating spades, to break up and remove sediment. The oscillating motion allows the tool to cut into the material while the dredger remains stationary, enabling efficient excavation in hydraulic-engineering dredging operations.
[E02F 3/06] with digging screws -- A dredger equipped with digging screws uses helical or auger-type devices that rotate to penetrate and lift sediment from the seabed or riverbed, allowing material to be conveyed for removal or relocation. This method is applied in hydraulic engineering projects where precise, low-disturbance excavation is required.
[E02F 3/08] with digging elements on an endless chain -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Conveyors -> B65G
[E02F 3/081] mounted on floating substructures  floating substructures  per se   E02F9/06 -- The CPC subclass E02F 3/081 covers dredgers that are installed on floating substructures or that are themselves floating substructures, encompassing designs where the dredging equipment is mounted on a vessel or platform that remains buoyant during operation.
[E02F 3/082] including a belt-type conveyor for transporting the excavated material -- This subclass of dredgers in hydraulic engineering covers dredging equipment that integrates a belt conveyor to move the material that has been excavated. It describes vessels or structures where the belt system forms part of the dredging apparatus, enabling continuous removal of sediments.
[E02F 3/083] including a screw-type conveyor for transporting the excavated material -- E02F 3/083 designates dredging vessels that incorporate a screw-type conveyor to transport excavated material from the pump or hopper to a discharge point. It is classified under fixed constructions, hydraulic engineering and dredgers.
[E02F 3/085] with auxiliary or additional digging elements other than digging elements on an endless chain -- The code E02F 3/085 covers dredgers used in hydraulic engineering fixed-construction projects that employ auxiliary or additional digging elements apart from the primary endless-chain digging gear. It includes machines equipped with extra tools such as suction heads, clamshell buckets or other non-chain cutters to aid sediment removal.
[E02F 3/086] vertically shiftable relative to the frame -- The subclass E02F 3/086 designates dredgers whose working structure can be moved vertically relative to the main frame, allowing the depth of the dredging tool to be adjusted during operation. It applies to fixed-construction hydraulic dredging equipment where the lift mechanism is integrated with the vessel's structure.
[E02F 3/087] with digging unit working in a plane inclined to the direction of travel -- This subclass covers dredgers whose digging unit is arranged to cut in a plane that is inclined relative to the vessel's direction of travel, allowing material to be removed from the seabed or riverbed at an angle. It pertains to fixed-construction hydraulic-engineering equipment designed for dredging operations where the cutter bar or bucket operates off-axis from the forward motion.
[E02F 3/088] pivotable relative to the frame -- The code E02F 3/088 designates dredger features that are mounted so they can pivot with respect to the vessel's frame, such as adjustable suction arms, swing-mounted booms or rotating cutterheads, allowing the dredging equipment to be oriented independently of the hull.
[E02F 3/10] with tools that only loosen the material -- This CPC subgroup covers dredging equipment in which the hydraulic system uses tools whose function is limited to loosening or agitating the seabed or river-bed material without cutting or extracting it directly. Typical examples include suction or water-jet devices that fluidise the sediment so that it can be removed by a separate conveyance system. It falls under the broader categories of fixed hydraulic constructions for dredging.
[E02F 3/12] Component parts -- E02F 3/12 covers the component parts of dredgers, i.e., the individual elements that make up dredging machines used in hydraulic engineering. It falls under fixed constructions for hydraulic engineering and includes items such as pumps, cutters, hull sections and other structural or functional parts of dredging equipment.
[E02F 3/14] Buckets -- Buckets are reusable containers attached to dredging equipment that scoop, transport, and discharge sediment or material from the waterbed. In hydraulic dredging they are lowered, filled by suction or gravity, then lifted to a discharge point, enabling precise removal of loose substrates. This component is classified under fixed constructions for hydraulic engineering dredgers.
[E02F 3/141] buckets -- The CPC subclass E02F 3/141 covers the buckets that are mounted on dredging machines for gathering and moving sediment or other material from water bodies. These buckets are integral components of dredgers, which belong to the fixed-construction category of hydraulic engineering.
[E02F 3/142] tools mounted on buckets or chains which loosen the soil, e.g. cutting wheels, or the like  teeth  per se   E02F9/28 -- E02F 3/142 falls under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Dredging → Dredgers and covers the soil-loosing implements fitted to buckets or chains on dredging machines, such as cutting wheels, toothed tools or similar devices used to break up material during dredging operations.
[E02F 3/143] chains; chain links; scraper chains  chains or chain guides  E21C25/28 -- E02F 3/143 covers the use of chains, chain links and scraper chains, as well as chain guides, on dredging equipment. It relates to fixed hydraulic constructions for dredgers, specifying the design, arrangement and function of these chain components in moving or removing material from the seabed.
[E02F 3/144] emptying or cleaning the buckets, e.g. in combination with spoil removing equipment -- The code E02F 3/144 designates techniques or apparatus for emptying or cleaning the buckets of dredgers, often integrated with systems that remove the excavated material. It covers mechanisms that discharge, wash, or otherwise clear bucket contents during dredging operations, facilitating continuous spoil removal.
[E02F 3/145] drives -- E02F 3/145 pertains to the drive systems employed in dredgers, covering the mechanical, hydraulic or electric propulsion and power-transmission arrangements that move the dredging equipment or the vessel itself. It includes motors, gearboxes, shafts, propellers and related control devices used to generate and regulate the required motion for dredging operations.
[E02F 3/146] guides for chains or buckets, e.g. for buckets movable relative to chains  chains or chain guides  E21C25/28 -- E02F 3/146 relates to guide mechanisms for chains or buckets in dredging equipment, defining devices that keep buckets aligned and moving smoothly along a chain. It includes chain guides, bucket guides and similar structures that control the relative motion of buckets and chains in hydraulic dredgers.
[E02F 3/147] arrangements for the co-operation between buckets or buckets and wheels -- E02F 3/147 covers the mechanical arrangements that enable multiple buckets to work together or to cooperate with wheels on dredging equipment. It includes linkages, timing devices, and drive systems that synchronize bucket or bucket-wheel actions for efficient material extraction. The class is used for patents describing coordinated bucket or bucket-wheel operations in hydraulic dredgers.
[E02F 3/148] wheels, sprokets -- This subclass covers wheels and sprockets used on dredgers, including their design, arrangement and function within drive and power-transmission systems that move or operate dredging equipment such as pumps, winches or conveyors. It addresses gear arrangements that convey engine power to the dredging mechanisms of hydraulic-engineered vessels.
[E02F 3/16] Safety or control devices -- The code E02F 3/16 covers safety and control mechanisms used on dredgers, such as protective devices, alarm systems, and automated regulators that prevent accidents and manage operational parameters during dredging activities. These features are part of the broader fixed-construction and hydraulic-engineering categories for dredging equipment.
[E02F 3/18] with digging wheels turning round an axis -- This subclass includes fixed dredgers that excavate material using one or more digging wheels mounted to rotate about a horizontal axis, so the wheels turn around the axis as the vessel moves. The rotating wheels scoop or cut sediment, providing a mechanical means of dredging.
[E02F 3/181] including a conveyor -- E02F 3/181 classifies dredging equipment that incorporates a conveyor as part of a fixed hydraulic-engineering construction. The entry applies to dredgers whose material-transport system uses a belt, screw or similar conveyor to move excavated sediments away from the dredge site. This falls under the broader categories of Fixed Constructions, Hydraulic Engineering and Dredging.
[E02F 3/183] with digging unit shiftable relative to the frame -- This CPC subclass falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering for dredging, specifically dredgers whose digging unit can be shifted relative to the machine frame. It covers designs where the excavating tool is movable to adapt to varying seabed conditions, allowing the dredger to adjust its digging position without relocating the entire vessel.
[E02F 3/185] with digging unit mounted in a plane which is inclined to the direction of travel; with tools digging laterally with respect to the frame -- This subgroup of the CPC covers dredgers whose digging unit is installed on a plane that is tilted relative to the vessel's forward motion, allowing the cutting tools to operate laterally to the frame. It falls under fixed constructions, hydraulic engineering, dredging, dredgers.
[E02F 3/186] with the axis being substantially parallel to the direction of travel -- The subgroup E02F 3/186 covers dredgers whose functional axis-such as a cutterhead, suction pipe or other working element-is arranged substantially parallel to the vessel's direction of travel, enabling the machine to engage the seabed while the craft moves forward. This orientation is typical of fixed-construction dredging equipment that cuts or pumps material in line with its forward motion.
[E02F 3/188] with the axis being horizontal and transverse to the direction of travel -- E02F 3/188 refers to dredging machines whose functional axis is placed horizontally and runs transverse to the direction of travel, meaning the cutting or suction element operates across the vessel's forward motion. This subclass is used for fixed-construction dredgers in hydraulic engineering applications.
[E02F 3/20] with tools that only loosen the material -- E02F 3/20 covers dredgers that employ devices whose sole function is to loosen or break up the seabed or riverbed material, such as hydraulic hammers, rakes or rotating blades, allowing the loosened sediment to be carried away by water flow rather than being directly lifted or suctioned. This subclass falls under fixed-construction hydraulic engineering dredging equipment.
[E02F 3/205] with a pair of digging wheels, e.g. slotting machines  implements for making foundation slots with definition of the walls or foundations  E02D17/13 ; bulkheads or similar walls made solely of concrete  in situ   E02D5/18 ; with a pair of buckets  E02F3/475 -- E02F 3/205 covers dredging equipment that employs a pair of digging wheels, such as slotting machines, to cut foundation slots and shape walls or bulkheads, including concrete in-situ structures. The code also relates to similar devices equipped with a pair of buckets for the same purpose.
[E02F 3/22] Component parts -- E02F 3/22 refers to the individual elements that constitute dredging vessels, including hull sections, propulsion units, pump housings and control cabins. It classifies the component parts of dredgers used in hydraulic engineering to move sediment.
[E02F 3/24] Digging wheels -- Digging wheels are rotary implements mounted on dredgers that rotate while partially submerged to loosen, lift and convey sediment from the seabed or riverbed. They are used in hydraulic-engineering projects such as harbour or canal construction where fixed-construction dredging is required. The wheels combine cutting blades or teeth with a conveyance system to transport the material to a discharge point.
[E02F 3/241] digging wheels -- E02F 3/241 relates to digging wheels, a type of fixed construction employed in hydraulic-engineering dredgers to excavate and lift sediment from water bodies during dredging operations.
[E02F 3/243] wheels rotatable in both directions -- The code E02F 3/243 designates dredgers whose wheels can be driven to rotate forward or reverse, allowing bidirectional movement for precise positioning during excavation. This feature is part of fixed-construction hydraulic engineering equipment used in dredging operations.
[E02F 3/245] with digging elements mounted movable relative to the wheel -- This subclass covers dredging machines where the digging or scooping elements are mounted on the wheel but can be repositioned independently, allowing the operator to adjust depth or angle while the wheel maintains traction. The movable mounting enables the tools to adapt to varying seabed conditions.
[E02F 3/246] drives -- The CPC subclass E02F 3/246 relates to the drive systems that power dredgers, covering the selection, arrangement and operation of motors, engines, gearboxes, propellers and other mechanical or hydraulic transmission devices used to propel or operate the dredging equipment of fixed-construction hydraulic-engineering works.
[E02F 3/248] Cleaning the wheels or emptying the digging elements mounted on the wheels, e.g. in combination with spoil removing equipment -- The subclass E02F 3/248 covers methods and devices for cleaning the wheels of dredging machines or for emptying the digging elements that are mounted on those wheels, often integrated with equipment that removes the excavated material. It addresses the removal of debris and spoil from wheel-borne tools to maintain efficient operation during hydraulic dredging.
[E02F 3/26] Safety or control devices -- E02F 3/26 covers safety or control devices for dredgers, such as systems that monitor operating conditions, issue alarms, enable emergency shutdown, and regulate the dredging process to protect equipment and personnel.
[E02F 3/28] with digging tools mounted on a dipper- or bucket-arm , e.g. dippers, buckets -- The subclass E02F 3/28 relates to dredgers equipped with digging tools mounted on a dipper- or bucket-arm, such as dippers or buckets, used in hydraulic engineering for fixed construction projects.
[E02F 3/283] with a single arm pivoted directly on the chassis  linkage mechanism for it  E02F3/3405 -- The code E02F 3/283 classifies dredging machines that use a single arm attached directly to the chassis, where the arm's movement is controlled by a linkage mechanism. It applies to fixed-construction dredgers in hydraulic engineering that employ this specific, chassis-pivoted arm arrangement.
[E02F 3/286] telescopic or slidable  fork-lift trucks with a telescopic boom  B66F9/0655
[E02F 3/30] with a dipper-arm pivoted on a cantilever beam -- E02F 3/30 covers dredgers that use a dipper-arm attached to a cantilever beam which pivots to raise and lower the working tool. The arrangement allows precise placement of the dredge material while the main hull remains stationary.
[E02F 3/301] with more than two arms (boom included), e.g. two-part boom with additional dipper-arm -- E02F 3/301 covers dredging machines that have more than two operational arms, such as a two-part boom combined with an extra dipper arm, used for moving material in hydraulic engineering fixed-construction dredgers. This subclass refines the broader categories of dredgers and hydraulic construction equipment.
[E02F 3/302] with an additional link -- E02F 3/302 refers to dredgers equipped with an additional link, i.e., a secondary structural connection that extends the main dredging apparatus, allowing the dredging element to reach farther or adjust its position. The classification falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering and covers devices where the extra link improves reach, flexibility or operational range of the dredger.
[E02F 3/303] with the dipper-arm or boom rotatable about its longitudinal axis -- E02F 3/303 pertains to dredging machines whose dipper arm or boom can be rotated about its longitudinal axis. This feature allows the arm to swivel along its length, providing flexibility in positioning the dredge tool during operation. It falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering for dredgers.
[E02F 3/304] with the dipper-arm slidably mounted on the boom  E02F3/305  takes precedence
[E02F 3/305] with the dipper-arm slidably mounted on the boom and the boom slidably mounted on the frame -- This subclass describes fixed dredger constructions where the dipper arm is slideably mounted on the boom and the boom itself is slideably mounted on the frame, enabling adjustable reach and positioning for hydraulic removal of sediment.
[E02F 3/306] with telescopic dipper-arm or boom -- E02F 3/306 classifies dredgers that incorporate a hydraulic or mechanical telescopic dipper-arm or boom, enabling the device to extend its reach and adjust the depth of material extraction while operating from a fixed structure. This feature is applied to improve efficiency in digging, scooping or handling underwater sediments.
[E02F 3/307] the boom and the dipper-arm being connected so as to permit relative movement in more than one plane -- E02F 3/307 covers dredger structures where the boom and dipper-arm are joined by a connection that enables movement relative to each other in more than one plane, allowing multidirectional positioning during excavation.
[E02F 3/308] working outwardly -- The code E02F 3/308 designates dredging machines whose working action extends outward from the vessel, such as suction or hydraulic devices that project pipes, jets or buckets away from the hull to excavate, transport and discharge sediments. It is a subcategory of fixed-construction hydraulic engineering dredgers.
[E02F 3/32] working downwardly and towards the machine, e.g. with backhoes -- The subclass E02F 3/32 covers dredger configurations in which the working tool is directed downward and toward the machine, as with back-hoe type excavators mounted on dredging vessels. It relates to hydraulic dredging equipment that removes material by pulling it upward into the dredger's loading system. This classification falls under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Dredging → Dredgers.
[E02F 3/325] Backhoes of the miniature type -- E02F 3/325 designates miniature backhoe equipment employed on small dredgers for precise hydraulic excavation of sediment. The subclass covers compact, often portable, hydraulic backhoes that can be attached to or integrated into miniature dredging vessels, emphasizing their reduced size and specialized use in fixed-construction dredging contexts.
[E02F 3/34] with bucket-arms directly pivoted on the frames of tractors or self-propelled machines -- The CPC entry E02F 3/34 concerns dredgers in which the bucket-arms are mounted directly on the frames of tractors or self-propelled machines and pivot about that attachment point. It classifies hydraulic dredging equipment where the excavation tools are integrated with the vehicle chassis rather than using separate booms or support structures.
[E02F 3/3402] the arms being telescopic  fork-lift trucks with a telescopic boom  B66F9/0655 -- This subclass covers dredgers equipped with telescopic arms that can extend and retract to adjust reach during sediment removal, similar to the telescopic boom of a fork-lift truck. It cross-references the forklift-type telescopic boom classification B66F9/0655.
[E02F 3/3405] and comprising an additional linkage mechanism -- The subclass E02F 3/3405 refers to fixed-construction dredgers that incorporate an additional linkage mechanism, such as an extra arm, joint or connecting system, to transmit motion or force between dredging components. This feature is used to improve positioning, control or power transfer within hydraulic dredging equipment.
[E02F 3/3408] of the parallelogram-type -- The CPC subclass E02F 3/3408 covers fixed dredgers whose working element-such as a jaw, bucket or cutter-operates on a parallelogram-type linkage, keeping its orientation constant while moving relative to the seabed. This design is used in hydraulic engineering dredging machines.
[E02F 3/3411] of the Z-type -- A Z-type dredger is a hydraulic dredging vessel whose suction or cutterhead is mounted on a Z-shaped arm that pivots to extend the workhead forward and upward, allowing operation at greater depths and over uneven seabed. This configuration provides improved maneuverability and the ability to reach under obstacles while maintaining a compact hull design.
[E02F 3/3414] the arms being pivoted at the rear of the vehicle chassis, e.g. skid steer loader -- E02F 3/3414 is a CPC subclass under Fixed Constructions - Hydraulic Engineering - Dredging - Dredgers and covers dredging machines whose working arms are mounted on a chassis and pivot at the rear, a configuration typical of skid-steer loaders.
[E02F 3/3417] Buckets emptying by tilting  E02F3/342 ,  E02F3/345  take precedence -- A sub-group of fixed-construction dredging equipment covering dredgers whose bucket or container empties its load by tilting the bucket, rather than by discharge through a chute or valve. It refines the broader group for hydraulic engineering dredgers and supersedes the related sub-classes E02F 3/342 and E02F 3/345.
[E02F 3/342] Buckets emptying overhead -- The code E02F 3/342 covers dredgers that use overhead mechanisms to discharge material from the bucket, where the bucket empties by tipping or swinging upward above the vessel. This includes designs of the lifting gear, guides and controls that enable the bucket to release its load from an elevated position during dredging operations.
[E02F 3/345] Buckets emptying side-ways -- E02F 3/345 covers dredging equipment where the removal buckets discharge the dredged material sideways, typically via hinged or swiveling buckets, chute or conveyor that directs the load away from the vessel hull. This classification is used for fixed-construction dredgers employing hydraulic systems to empty buckets laterally.
[E02F 3/348] Buckets emptying into a collecting or conveying device -- The code E02F 3/348 covers dredging equipment where the buckets discharge their contents directly into a receiving or transport system such as a hopper, belt, or pipe. It pertains to fixed-construction hydraulic dredgers that use bucket chains or similar mechanisms to move material from the seabed to a collection device.
[E02F 3/3483] Buckets discharging on a conveyor or elevator mounted on the machine -- This CPC subclass pertains to dredgers that use a bucket line whose material is discharged onto a conveyor or elevator that is mounted directly on the machine, forming an integral handling system for the excavated material. It falls under fixed constructions for hydraulic engineering dredging applications.
[E02F 3/3486] Buckets discharging overhead into a container mounted on the machine -- E02F 3/3486 covers dredgers that use buckets to lift material and discharge it overhead - into a container that is fixed on the machine itself. The arrangement directs the load upward into an onboard receptacle, simplifying handling and reducing spillage during hydraulic dredging operations.
[E02F 3/352] Buckets movable along a fixed guide -- The code E02F 3/352 covers dredging machines that use a series of buckets mounted on a rigid guide or ladder and capable of moving along that fixed path. The movable buckets are driven to scoop, lift and discharge sediment or debris from the bottom of waterways, providing efficient excavation in hydraulic engineering projects.
[E02F 3/355] Buckets connected to the rear end of a tractor -- E02F 3/355 covers dredging equipment where a bucket is mounted on the rear of a tractor, allowing the tractor to scoop and transport material from waterways. The configuration is used in fixed construction and hydraulic engineering contexts as a simple dredger attachment.
[E02F 3/358] Bucket-arms pivoted on a turntable being part of a tractor frame -- The classification covers dredger designs in which the bucket-arms are mounted on a turntable that is incorporated into the tractor's frame, allowing the arms to pivot for digging operations. This arrangement is used in fixed-construction hydraulic dredging equipment.
[E02F 3/36] Component parts -- The CPC subclass E02F 3/36 pertains to the individual component parts of dredging machines used in hydraulic engineering. It includes items such as booms, hoppers, pumps, cutters, and other structural or functional elements that make up a dredger. This subclass captures patents that focus on the design, improvement, or specific features of those constituent parts.
[E02F 3/3604] Devices to connect tools to arms, booms or the like -- This subclass concerns fittings that join working tools such as cutters, grabs or suction heads to the movable arms, booms or similar structures of a dredger. The devices enable secure, often hydraulic, attachment and quick release for positioning and operation during dredging work.
[E02F 3/3609] of the quick acting type, e.g. controlled from the operator seat  quick-acting couplers to connect booms or arms to tractors  E02F3/627 ; quick-acting couplers for machines mounted on tractor  A01B59/06 ; couplings of the quick-acting type  per se   F16L37/00
[E02F 3/3613] with means for absorbing any play therebetween  E02F3/364  takes precedence -- The code E02F 3/3613 covers dredgers that incorporate devices to absorb any play between moving parts, such as flexible couplings, elastomeric pads or hydraulic dampers that reduce vibration, wear and mis-alignment. These features are applied to the dredger's propulsion, lifting or guiding mechanisms to maintain smooth operation and prolong component life. The broader subclass E02F 3/364, which deals with adjusting means, takes precedence over this specific absorption feature.
[E02F 3/3618] with two separating hooks -- The CPC subclass E02F 3/3618 classifies fixed dredging machines that are equipped with two separating hooks, a device used to split or disengage dredged material during operation. It falls under the hierarchy of Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Dredging → Dredgers.
[E02F 3/3622] with a hook and a locking element acting on a pin -- A dredger that uses a hook combined with a locking element that engages a pin to secure or release components is classified under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering. This arrangement provides a simple, reliable means of fastening parts of the dredging equipment, facilitating maintenance or operation adjustments. It falls within the broader category of dredgers in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[E02F 3/3627] with a hook and a longitudinal locking element -- A dredger classified under E02F 3/3627 is a fixed hydraulic construction used for dredging that incorporates a hook together with a longitudinal locking element to secure or guide the dredging tool or component during operation. This arrangement helps maintain alignment and connection of the dredge head or scoop while working in water.
[E02F 3/3631] with a hook and a transversal locking element -- A dredger equipped with a hook and a transversal locking element uses the hook to engage material or components while the crosswise locking piece secures the connection, preventing unintended release during operation. This arrangement is typical for fixed-construction dredging equipment that requires reliable holding of the dredge line or tooling.
[E02F 3/3636] using two or four movable transversal pins -- The classification E02F 3/3636 covers dredgers that employ two or four movable transversal pins as part of their fixed construction. It falls under hydraulic engineering, specifically the dredging subclass, and defines the structural arrangement of the pins used to control or stabilize the dredging equipment.
[E02F 3/364] using wedges -- E02F 3/364 relates to the use of wedges as a means of securing, positioning or adjusting parts of dredger structures, particularly within fixed constructions employed in hydraulic-engineering projects such as dredging operations.
[E02F 3/3645] with auto-engagement means for automatic snap-on of the tool coupler part -- A dredger with a stationary construction that incorporates an automatic snap-on coupling system for attaching the tool coupler part, allowing the tool to engage quickly without manual alignment. This mechanism is designed for hydraulic engineering applications in dredging operations.
[E02F 3/365] with redundant latching means, e.g. for safety purposes -- This CPC subclass concerns dredgers that incorporate redundant latching means, such as safety latches, to provide an additional securing function during operation or when the equipment is idle. It is grouped under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering.
[E02F 3/3654] with energy coupler, e.g. coupler for hydraulic or electric lines, to provide energy to drive(s) mounted on the tool -- E02F 3/3654 designates dredgers that incorporate an energy coupler, such as a hydraulic or electric line connector, to deliver power to the drives mounted on the tool. The subclass falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering for dredging operations.
[E02F 3/3659] electrically-operated -- E02F 3/3659 covers dredging machines or devices whose propulsion, excavation or handling functions are driven by electric power rather than fuel-based or hydraulic means. It includes electric motors, control systems and power-supply arrangements specifically designed for dredgers. The classification applies to any dredger employing an electrically-operated drive or lifting mechanism.
[E02F 3/3663] hydraulically-operated -- E02F 3/3663 covers dredgers that are driven by hydraulic power, including machines that use hydraulic cylinders, pumps or fluid-actuated systems to operate their digging tools or convey sediment. The classification applies to fixed-construction dredging equipment within the broader field of hydraulic engineering.
[E02F 3/3668] where engagement is effected by a mechanical lever or handle -- This subclass covers dredging installations that use a mechanical lever or handle to engage the working tool, such as buckets or cutters, within fixed dredger structures. It applies to hydraulic-engineered dredging equipment where manual actuation controls the engagement mechanism.
[E02F 3/3672] where disengagement is effected by a mechanical lever or handle -- The code covers dredger constructions in which the release or disengagement of the working element-such as a bucket, cutter head or suction pipe-is performed by a manually operated mechanical lever or handle, providing a simple positive control method for starting or stopping the dredging operation.
[E02F 3/3677] allowing movement, e.g. rotation or translation, of the tool around or along another axis as the movement implied by the boom or arms, e.g. for tilting buckets -- This subclass covers dredger mechanisms that allow a tool to move-by rotation or translation-about or along an additional axis driven by the boom or arms, such as tilting or swiveling buckets on hydraulic dredging equipment.
[E02F 3/3681] Rotators -- Rotators are devices fitted to dredgers that produce a circular water flow to loosen and mobilise sediment, aiding its removal. By rotating an arm or blade against the seabed, they enhance the efficiency of hydraulic dredging operations.

[E02F 3/40] Dippers -- A dipper is a scoop-type implement mounted on or integrated with a dredge that penetrates the seabed or riverbed to lift and convey sediment, sand, or other material onto a transport system. It is used in hydraulic dredging machines to extract loose deposits efficiently.
[E02F 3/401] Buckets or forks comprising, for example, shock absorbers, supports or load striking scrapers to prevent overload -- This CPC subclass covers dredger buckets or forks that incorporate shock absorbers, supporting structures or load-striking scrapers intended to limit overload during digging. It is situated in the Fixed Constructions > Hydraulic Engineering > Dredging > Dredgers hierarchy.
[E02F 3/402] with means for facilitating the loading thereof, e.g. conveyors -- E02F 3/402 classifies dredgers that incorporate load-facilitating devices such as belt or screw conveyors, chutes or other transfer mechanisms. [E02F 3/404] comprising two parts movable relative to each other, e.g. for gripping -- E02F 3/404 classifies dredgers whose working structure is built from two elements that can move relative to one another, enabling gripping, scooping or similar actions. [E02F 3/405] using vibrating means  blades or levelling tools with vibrating teeth  E02F3/8155 ; vibrating rippers  E02F5/326 -- This CPC subclass E02F 3/405, within fixed constructions, hydraulic engineering and dredging of dredgers, relates to the use of vibrating equipment such as blades, leveling tools with vibrating teeth and vibrating rippers to assist material loosening and removal.
[E02F 3/407] with ejecting device -- The subclass E02F 3/407 includes dredgers that are fitted with an ejecting device to discharge the excavated material directly from the dredge. Such devices use hydraulic or mechanical means to propel the sediments away from the dredger, improving efficiency in deep-water or high-flow conditions.
