[E02F 3/4075] Dump doors; Control thereof -- E02F 3/4075 covers the design and operation of dump doors on dredging vessels, including mechanisms for opening, closing and regulating the flow of dredged material. It includes hydraulic, pneumatic or mechanical control systems used to manage the doors during loading and discharge.
[E02F 3/413] with grabbing device -- E02F 3/413 designates dredgers that incorporate a grabbing device for extracting material from the seabed or riverbed. The classification falls under Fixed Constructions > Hydraulic Engineering > Dredging > Dredgers and is used by the EPO and USPTO to categorize patents describing grab-type dredging tools.
[E02F 3/4131] mounted on a floating substructure  floating substructures  per se   E02F9/06
[E02F 3/4133] grabs carried out as loaders or mounted on a tractor -- The code E02F 3/4133 classifies grabbing devices that are employed as loaders or are mounted on tractors for dredging operations. It falls under fixed-construction hydraulic engineering, specifically within the dredgers subgroup, and covers the mechanical arrangements used to pick up and move material in water-based excavation.
[E02F 3/4135] with grabs mounted directly on a boom -- This subclass covers dredging machines in which the grab or clamshell bucket is fixed directly to the crane boom, allowing the bucket to be lowered and raised without a separate hoist or cable system. The arrangement is used for confined-space or precision dredging where rapid pick-up of sediment, rocks or debris is required.
[E02F 3/4136] with grabs mounted on a slidable or telescopic boom or arm -- E02F 3/4136 covers dredgers equipped with a grab that is fitted to a boom or arm capable of sliding or telescopic extension, allowing the grab to be positioned precisely for scooping and removing material during hydraulic dredging operations. This arrangement enables flexible reach and depth control in fixed-construction dredging systems.
[E02F 3/4138] the grab being emptied by flushing -- The code E02F 3/4138 refers to dredgers equipped with a grab that is cleared by flushing water rather than by tipping or rotating. This method uses a hydraulic flow to empty the grab's contents, enabling faster and more controlled removal of sediment in fixed-construction dredging systems.
[E02F 3/42] Drives for dippers, buckets, dipper-arms or bucket-arms -- A drive system that supplies power to the dipper, bucket, dipper-arm or bucket-arm of a dredger, including hydraulic, mechanical, electric or combined mechanisms used to move and operate the digging tools. This classification covers the arrangement, transmission and control of such drives within dredging equipment.
[E02F 3/422] Drive systems for bucket-arms, front-end loaders, dumpers or the like -- E02F 3/422 is a CPC subgroup under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Dredging → Dredgers, covering the specific drive mechanisms that power bucket-arms, front-end loaders, dumpers and similar equipment used in dredging operations.
[E02F 3/425] Drive systems for dipper-arms, backhoes or the like -- This subclass relates to mechanical or hydraulic drive arrangements that power the dipper-arm or backhoe of dredging vessels, detailing transmission, motor, and control mechanisms. It includes configurations such as hydraulic cylinders, electric motors, and gearboxes used to actuate the digging equipment on dredgers.
[E02F 3/427] with mechanical drives  by cables or hoisting ropes  E02F3/46  take precedence -- E02F 3/427 classifies dredgers that are driven mechanically through cables or hoisting ropes rather than by onboard engines or hydraulic power. It falls under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Dredging → Dredgers and specifies the type of mechanical drive system used for the dredging operation.
[E02F 3/43] Control of dipper or bucket position -- The CPC subclass E02F 3/43 covers systems and methods for controlling the position of a dredger's dipper or bucket during operation. It includes mechanical, hydraulic or electronic devices that adjust depth, angle or swing to achieve precise material handling in fixed-construction dredging installations. This subclass is nested under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Dredging → Dredgers.
[E02F 3/431] for bucket-arms, front-end loaders, dumpers or the like -- This subclass addresses devices mounted on dredgers that handle material, including bucket-arms, front-end loaders and dumpers used to load, transport or discharge dredged material, and falls within the fixed-construction hydraulic-engineering dredging category.
[E02F 3/432] for keeping the bucket in a predetermined position or attitude -- The E02F 3/432 subclass covers devices that maintain a dredger's bucket in a set position or orientation during operation. It includes hydraulic or mechanical locking, positioning, or attitude-control systems that keep the bucket steady while lifting, lowering, or moving, ensuring consistent digging performance.
[E02F 3/433] horizontal, e.g. self-levelling -- The CPC subclass E02F 3/433 pertains to dredgers that employ horizontal mechanisms, such as self-levelling devices, to keep the working surface level during operation. It covers equipment that adjusts its position horizontally to maintain a constant elevation while dredging. This subclass falls under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Dredging → Dredgers.
[E02F 3/434] providing automatic sequences of movements, e.g. automatic dumping or loading, automatic return-to-dig -- The classification covers control arrangements for dredgers that automatically carry out a programmed sequence of operations, such as loading material, dumping it, and returning the bucket or cutter to the digging position without manual intervention. It includes hydraulic or electronic sequencing devices that coordinate these movements as part of fixed construction dredging equipment.
[E02F 3/435] for dipper-arms, backhoes or the like -- E02F 3/435 refers to the parts of dredgers that move material, specifically dipper-arms, backhoes or similar devices used on fixed hydraulic-engineering construction dredges. It classifies the mechanisms that extend, swing or lower the arm for scooping, lifting or dumping sediment during dredging operations.
[E02F 3/436] for keeping the dipper in the horizontal position, e.g. self-levelling -- E02F 3/436 covers mechanisms that keep a dredger's dipper bar in a horizontal position, typically using self-levelling or stabilising devices. It falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering for dredging equipment, addressing dipper orientation control during operation.
[E02F 3/437] providing automatic sequences of movements, e.g. linear excavation, keeping dipper angle constant -- This subclass covers control methods that automatically sequence the movements of dredger equipment to achieve linear excavation while maintaining a constant dipper angle, enabling precise, repeatable digging actions in hydraulic engineering dredging machines.
[E02F 3/438] Memorising movements for repetition, e.g. play-back capability -- E02F 3/438 concerns dredgers equipped with memory functions that can store hydraulic movement sequences and automatically replay them during subsequent dredging cycles, enabling precise repetition of dredging patterns and reducing operator intervention.
[E02F 3/439] Automatic repositioning of the implement, e.g. automatic dumping, auto-return  E02F3/438  takes precedence -- E02F 3/439 covers dredgers equipped with mechanisms that automatically reposition the working implement, such as automatically dumping dredged material and then returning the tool to its operating position, providing self-acting control of the device. It is a narrower refinement of the broader automatic control functions defined in E02F 3/438.
[E02F 3/46] with reciprocating digging or scraping elements moved by cables or hoisting ropes -- E02F 3/46 covers dredgers that use reciprocating digging or scraping tools which are driven by cables or hoisting ropes. The moving elements perform a back-and-forth motion to dislodge material from the seabed, allowing the dredged material to be lifted and removed by the hydraulic system. This subclass falls under fixed-construction hydraulic engineering for dredging operations.
[E02F 3/47] with grab buckets -- This subclass deals with dredgers that use a grab-bucket mechanism to lift and transport sediment from the seabed or riverbed. It falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering for dredging operations.
[E02F 3/475] for making foundation slots  slotting machines with a pair of digging wheels  E02F3/205
[E02F 3/48] Drag-lines -- Drag-lines are large mechanical dredgers that use a suspended bucket, hoisted by cables, to scoop and remove sediment from water bodies. They belong to fixed-construction hydraulic-engineering equipment and are employed in dredging operations such as harbor deepening, reclamation and sand extraction.
[E02F 3/50] with buckets or other digging elements moved along a rigid guideway -- This subclass addresses dredging machines in which a series of buckets or similar digging tools are mounted on a fixed frame and travel along a rigid guideway to scoop material from the seabed. The guideway may be a ladder, chain or conveyor and the system is usually powered hydraulically.
[E02F 3/52] Cableway excavators -- Cableway excavators are fixed-construction dredging machines in which buckets or grabs are suspended from a moving cable system to scoop sediment from the waterway. They are powered hydraulically and mounted on dredger vessels or platforms for continuous, controlled excavation along a predetermined line.
[E02F 3/54] Cable scrapers -- Cable scrapers are hydraulic dredging tools that use a cable or chain to pull a toothed or blade element across the seabed, scraping loose sediment into a hopper or conveyance system. They are employed on dredgers for precision excavation in ports, rivers, or coastal works.
[E02F 3/56] with hand-controlled scraper or other digging elements -- A dredger that uses a manually operated scraper or similar digging tool to remove sediment, allowing the operator to control the digging action directly. This configuration falls under fixed hydraulic-engineered dredging equipment.
[E02F 3/58] Component parts -- E02F 3/58 covers the individual components and sub-assemblies of dredgers, such as hull sections, suction pipelines, pumps, cutterheads and control systems, used in fixed hydraulic-engineering structures for dredging operations. The classification applies to the design, construction and integration of these parts within the overall dredger equipment.
[E02F 3/60] Buckets, scrapers, or other digging elements -- E02F 3/60 designates buckets, scrapers and other digging elements employed on dredgers within hydraulic engineering for fixed-construction projects. It covers the form, function and use of these implements that excavate or gather material from water-borne substrates.
[E02F 3/627] Devices to connect beams or arms to tractors or similar self-propelled machines, -- The entry covers mechanisms that attach structural beams or extending arms to a tractor, excavator or other self-propelled hydraulic dredging equipment, allowing the machine to support, maneuver or operate fixed-construction components during dredging work. It includes couplings, brackets and pivot devices designed for secure, adjustable connections.
[E02F 3/6273] using legs to support the beams or arms on the ground during the connecting process -- The CPC entry E02F 3/6273 covers methods or devices for stabilising a dredger's structural elements by placing legs on the ground to support beams or arms while they are being connected, typically in fixed-construction hydraulic engineering works such as dredging operations.
[E02F 3/6276] on one side of the frame -- A dredger with a fixed hull or structure in which the working equipment is mounted on only one side of the frame. The classification covers hydraulic engineering designs where the dredging apparatus is arranged asymmetrically, typically to simplify load distribution or accommodate site constraints. It is used for vessels or installations whose dredging components are attached to a single side of the main frame.
[E02F 3/633] Drives therefor -- E02F 3/633 pertains to the drive systems that power dredging equipment within fixed hydraulic constructions. It encompasses mechanical, hydraulic or electric transmission arrangements that convey power from engines or motors to the dredger's working components.
[E02F 3/64] Buckets cars, i.e. having scraper bowls -- E02F 3/64 covers bucket-cars fitted with scraper bowls that are used in fixed hydraulic engineering dredging installations. The classification refers to machines that combine a bucket mechanism with a moving carriage to collect, transport and discharge sediment or other material during dredging operations.
[E02F 3/6409] Self-propelled scrapers -- The code E02F 3/6409 designates dredging machines that move under their own power and employ a scraping tool to collect material from the bottom of waterways. These self-propelled scrapers are a subclass of dredgers within hydraulic engineering and are used for excavation, maintenance or land-reclamation projects.
[E02F 3/6418] with rotatable scraper bowls for dumping the soil  with only elements of the scraper bowls being pivotable  E02F3/6427 -- This subclass refers to dredgers that use rotatable scraper bowls to discharge excavated material, where only certain portions of the bowls are capable of pivoting rather than the entire bowl. It is situated under fixed-construction hydraulic-engineering dredging equipment.
[E02F 3/6427] with elements of the scraper bowls being pivotable for dumping the soil  E02F3/6445  take precedence; with an ejector having translational movement  E02F3/6436
[E02F 3/6436] with scraper bowls with an ejector having translational movement for dumping the soil  E02F3/6445  takes precedence -- E02F 3/6436 classifies dredgers that use scraper bowls equipped with a translational ejector to dump soil, a fixed hydraulic construction for moving excavated material. It falls under the Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Dredging → Dredgers hierarchy and is superseded by the related entry E02F 3/6445.
[E02F 3/6445] with conveying means for emptying the scraper bowl -- This subclass covers dredgers that have built-in conveying equipment for removing the contents of the scraper bowl. It includes any mechanism-such as conveyors, pumps or lifts-designed to transport the excavated material from the bowl to a discharge point, facilitating continuous dredging operations.
[E02F 3/6454] Towed (i.e. pulled or pushed) scrapers -- The CPC code E02F 3/6454 covers dredging equipment that is towed-either pulled or pushed-along the seabed to scrape material, classifying such mobile scrapers within the broader categories of fixed constructions, hydraulic engineering and dredgers.
[E02F 3/6463] with rotatable scraper bowls for dumping the soil  with only elements of the scraper bowls being pivotable  E02F3/6472
[E02F 3/6472] with elements of the scraper bowls being pivotable for dumping the soil  E02F3/649  takes precedence; with an ejector having translational movement  E02F3/6481 -- This CPC subclass (E02F 3/6472) pertains to dredgers whose scraper bowl sections can pivot to dump collected soil, optionally incorporating an ejector that moves translationally. It sits within fixed-construction hydraulic-engineering dredging and is narrower than the broader subclasses E02F 3/649 and E02F 3/6481.
[E02F 3/6481] with scraper bowls with an ejector having translational movement for dumping the soil  E02F3/649  takes precedence -- E02F 3/6481 covers dredgers that have scraper bowls fitted with an ejector that moves translationally to dump the excavated soil. The feature belongs to fixed-construction hydraulic-engineering dredging, and the broader subclass E02F 3/649 takes precedence.
[E02F 3/649] with conveying means for emptying the scraper bowl -- E02F 3/649 refers to dredgers equipped with a mechanism that removes material from the scraper bowl by means of a conveyor, pump or similar conveying device, enabling continuous emptying of the bowl during operation. This subclass falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering dredging.
[E02F 3/65] Component parts, e.g. drives, control devices -- E02F 3/65 covers the individual components used in dredgers, such as drive mechanisms, control devices and related parts that enable the operation of the dredging equipment. It belongs to the fixed-construction group for hydraulic-engineering dredging machines.
[E02F 3/651] Hydraulic or pneumatic drives; Electric or electro-mechanical control devices  E02F3/652 ,  E02F3/653  take precedence -- Code E02F 3/651 covers hydraulic or pneumatic drive systems and electric or electromechanical control devices used in dredgers. It sits within the hierarchy of fixed constructions, hydraulic engineering, dredging and dredgers, and is overridden by the more specific subclasses E02F3/652 and E02F3/653.
[E02F 3/652] Means to adjust the height of the scraper bowls, e.g. suspension means, tilt control, earth damping control -- This class covers mechanisms that vary the vertical position of scraper bowls on dredgers, including suspension systems, tilt control devices and earth-damping arrangements that allow precise height adjustment during dredging operations. It is used for fixed-construction hydraulic engineering dredgers.
[E02F 3/653] Connection mechanisms to the main body of the machine  connection of tools to dipper-arms, booms, bucket-arms  E02F3/3604 ; connection of beams or booms or arms to tractors in general  E02F3/627
[E02F 3/654] Scraper bowls and components mounted on them -- E02F 3/654 covers the scraper bowls used on dredgers and the components that are directly mounted on them, such as mounting frames, hinges, drive shafts and protective linings. The class includes designs that allow the bowl to be lowered, raised or rotated while preserving structural integrity during sediment removal.
[E02F 3/655] Loading or elevator mechanisms  loading devices for excavators in general  E02F7/04
[E02F 3/656] Ejector or dumping mechanisms  for buckets mounted on a dipper-arm or bucket arms  E02F3/407 -- E02F 3/656 pertains to the mechanisms that eject or dump the contents of buckets mounted on dipper-arms or bucket arms of dredgers. It covers designs such as hydraulic ejectors, gravity-assist dumpers, or mechanical release devices that facilitate rapid unloading of excavated material during dredging operations.
[E02F 3/657] Means to prevent the spilling of dredged material, e.g. apron, baffle -- E02F 3/657 covers devices integrated into dredgers that stop dredged material from escaping the work area, such as aprons or baffles that contain or redirect sediment. These solutions are used in fixed construction projects involving hydraulic engineering and dredging operations.
[E02F 3/658] Cutting edge  for graders or bulldozer blades  E02F3/8152 ,  E02F3/8155 ; teeth  per se   E02F9/28 -- The code refers to the design, mounting or replacement of cutting edges used on grader or bulldozer blades, including the individual teeth themselves, as applied to hydraulic dredging equipment.
[E02F 3/659] Conveying means for emptying scraper bowls  conveying equipment for excavators in general  E02F7/02 -- E02F 3/659 covers conveying mechanisms used to empty scraper bowls in dredging equipment, encompassing the various conveyor systems that transfer material from the bowl to a discharge point and the general conveying equipment employed on excavators for material handling.
[E02F 3/76] Graders, bulldozers, or the like with scraper plates or ploughshare-like elements -- E02F 3/76 relates to earth-moving machines such as graders, bulldozers and similar equipment that are equipped with scraper plates or ploughshare-type elements for cutting, leveling or removing soil and other material during hydraulic engineering dredging operations.
[E02F 3/7604] Combinations of scraper blades with soil loosening tools working independently of scraper blades  soil loosening attachments fixed on blades  E02F3/8152 ,  E02F3/8155 -- This subclass relates to dredger scraper blades that have soil-loosening attachments fixed to the blades, where the loosening tools function independently of the scraping action to enhance penetration and removal of compacted material.
[E02F 3/7609] Scraper blade mounted forwardly of the tractor on a pair of pivoting arms which are linked to the sides of the tractor, e.g. bulldozers -- The code E02F 3/7609 designates a dredger arrangement where a scraper blade is mounted forward of the tractor and attached to a pair of pivoting arms that are linked to the sides of the tractor, a configuration commonly found on bulldozers.
[E02F 3/7613] with the scraper blade adjustable relative to the pivoting arms about a vertical axis, e.g. angle dozers -- The CPC class E02F 3/7613 covers dredgers whose scraper blade can be rotated about a vertical axis relative to the pivoting arms, allowing adjustment of the blade angle such as in angle dozers. This feature falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering.
[E02F 3/7618] with the scraper blade adjustable relative to the pivoting arms about a horizontal axis -- This subclass covers dredger designs in which the scraper blade can be tilted relative to the pivoting arms about a horizontal axis. The adjustment lets the operator vary the blade's angle and cutting depth during operation, enhancing adaptability to different seabed conditions.
[E02F 3/7622] Scraper equipment with the scraper blade mounted on a frame to be hitched to the tractor by bars, arms, chains or the like, the frame having no ground supporting means of its own, e.g. drag scrapers -- E02F 3/7622 is a CPC sub-group under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Dredging → Dredgers. It covers scraper equipment whose blade is fixed to a frame that is attached to a tractor by bars, arms, chains or similar means, the frame having no independent ground-supporting structure, as in drag-scrapers.
[E02F 3/7627] with the scraper blade adjustable relative to the frame about a vertical axis -- The entry covers dredging machines whose scraper blade can be rotated about a vertical axis to change its position relative to the hull or frame. This adjustment lets the blade be angled for different cut depths or material types while the vessel remains stationary.
[E02F 3/7631] with the scraper blade adjustable relative to the frame about a horizontal axis -- The code designates dredging machines of fixed construction in which the scraper blade can be tilted or rotated about a horizontal axis relative to the frame, allowing adjustment of the cutting angle or depth during operation. This feature is used to adapt the blade position to varying soil or sediment conditions while the dredger remains stationary or moves along a set path.
[E02F 3/7636] Graders with the scraper blade mounted under the tractor chassis -- E02F 3/7636 pertains to dredger graders that have the scraper blade mounted beneath the tractor chassis, creating a low-profile cutting tool for fine grading of dredged material. This arrangement integrates the blade under the chassis to enhance stability and minimise obstruction during operation.
[E02F 3/764] with the scraper blade being pivotable about a vertical axis -- This subclass relates to fixed dredging constructions where the scraper blade can be pivoted about a vertical axis, allowing the cutting edge to be oriented horizontally for versatile sediment removal. The vertical-axis pivot is typically driven by hydraulic or mechanical means and is used in land-based or floating dredgers.
[E02F 3/7645] with the scraper blade being pivotable about a horizontal axis disposed parallel to the blade -- The code E02F 3/7645 pertains to dredgers whose scraper blade can be pivoted about a horizontal axis that runs parallel to the blade itself, allowing the blade's angle of attack to be varied during operation. This feature enables adjustment of cutting depth and orientation to suit different ground conditions while maintaining the hydraulic digging mechanism.
[E02F 3/765] with the scraper blade being pivotable about a horizontal axis disposed perpendicular to the blade -- The CPC subclass E02F 3/765 covers dredgers whose scraper blade can be swung about a horizontal axis that is perpendicular to the blade itself. It applies to hydraulic or mechanical dredging equipment in which the blade's pivoting action enables adjustment of cutting depth or orientation during excavation.
[E02F 3/7654] with the scraper blade being horizontally movable into a position near the chassis -- E02F 3/7654 covers dredgers whose scraper blade can be shifted horizontally into a position close to the chassis, allowing the cutting tool to be retracted or positioned for varying ground conditions during dredging operations. This feature is classified under fixed-construction hydraulic engineering dredging equipment.
[E02F 3/7659] with the vertical centre-line of the scraper blade disposed laterally relative to the central axis of the chassis -- The entry E02F 3/7659 covers hydraulic dredgers in which the vertical centre-line of the scraper blade is positioned laterally, i.e., offset from the central axis of the chassis, rather than aligned with it. This arrangement influences the blade's cutting path and the machine's overall stability during dredging operations.
[E02F 3/7663] Graders with the scraper blade mounted under a frame supported by wheels, or the like

[E02F 3/7672] with the scraper blade being pivotable about a horizontal axis disposed parallel to the blade -- E02F 3/7672 is a CPC subclass for dredgers in the fixed-construction group of hydraulic engineering, specifically covering designs where the scraper blade can pivot about a horizontal axis that runs parallel to the blade, allowing its angle to be adjusted during operation.
[E02F 3/7677] with the scraper blade being pivotable about a horizontal axis disposed perpendicular to the blade -- This subclass pertains to dredgers whose scraper blade can be rotated about a horizontal axis that runs perpendicular to the blade, enabling the blade angle to be adjusted during operation for optimal sediment removal. It is used in fixed hydraulic construction equipment to adapt cutting depth and orientation to varying seabed conditions.
[E02F 3/7681] with the scraper blade being horizontally movable into a position near the frame -- E02F 3/7681 covers dredgers equipped with a scraper blade that can be shifted horizontally to a location close to the dredger's frame, allowing adjustable scraping of the seabed.
[E02F 3/7686] with the vertical centre-line of the scraper blade disposed laterally relative to the central axis of the frame -- This subclass defines dredgers whose scraper blade is mounted so that its vertical centre line lies laterally off the frame's central axis, meaning the blade is positioned asymmetrically relative to the machine.
[E02F 3/769] Graders, bulldozers, or the like comprising loaders

This subclass is applied to designs where the earth-moving vehicle includes built-in conveyance systems to transport dredged material.
[E02F 3/78] with rotating digging elements -- This subclass of E02F 3/78 covers dredging machines that employ rotating digging tools such as buckets, augers or cutters to break up and remove sediment. It applies to dredgers whose primary excavation mechanism consists of rotary elements rather than static or linear devices.
[E02F 3/783] having a horizontal axis of rotation -- E02F 3/783 covers dredging machines whose main rotating component operates about a horizontal axis. This includes dredgers where the impeller, cutter head or similar device turns horizontally to mobilize sediment. The class is part of the Fixed Constructions - Hydraulic Engineering - Dredging - Dredgers hierarchy.
[E02F 3/786] having a vertical axis of rotation -- E02F 3/786 covers dredgers whose rotating mechanism is mounted on a vertical axis, meaning the cutter or propeller turns around a shaft that stands upright in the water. This design is used in fixed hydraulic constructions for excavating sediment or material from the bottom of waterways.
[E02F 3/80] Component parts -- This CPC class covers the individual component parts of dredgers, including hull structures, pumps, conveyors and other sub-assemblies used in hydraulic dredging operations. It is nested under Fixed Constructions, Hydraulic Engineering, Dredging and Dredgers. The code is E02F 3/80.
[E02F 3/815] Blades -- The classification E02F 3/815 refers to the blades of dredging machines, encompassing their design, shape, mounting and functional features used to cut, loosen or transport sediment on dredgers. It addresses adaptations for hydraulic engineering applications and any fixed-construction arrangements that improve the efficiency or control of the dredging process.
[E02F 3/8152] Attachments therefor, e.g. wear resisting parts, cutting edges  E02F3/8155 ,  E02F3/8157  take precedence; teeth  per se   E02F9/28 -- E02F 3/8152 covers accessories attached to dredger cutting tools, such as wear-resistant parts and cutting edges that serve as protective or functional attachments. The actual teeth are classified in separate groups, and this entry belongs to the fixed-construction branch of hydraulic-engineering dredging.
[E02F 3/8155] provided with movable parts, e.g. cutting discs, vibrating teeth or the like -- This CPC subclass classifies dredging machines that incorporate movable elements such as cutting discs, vibrating teeth or similar tools to break up material during operation. It falls under fixed constructions, hydraulic engineering, dredging and specifically addresses dredgers equipped with these active cutting or vibrating parts.
[E02F 3/8157] Shock absorbers; Supports, e.g. skids, rollers; Devices for compensating wear-and-tear, or the like -- E02F 3/8157 covers shock-absorbing devices and support elements such as skids and rollers used on dredgers, as well as mechanisms that compensate for wear and tear of the dredging apparatus.
[E02F 3/84] Drives or control devices therefor -- The CPC code E02F 3/84 pertains to the power-transmission and control mechanisms used on dredgers, encompassing drives such as electric, diesel or hydraulic motors, gearboxes, speed-regulators and automated or remote control devices that manage the operation, positioning and depth of the dredging equipment.
[E02F 3/841] Devices for controlling and guiding the whole machine, e.g. by feeler elements and reference lines placed exteriorly of the machine  construction of roads  E01C19/008 -- E02F 3/841 pertains to control and guidance systems for dredging machines, including devices that use external feeler elements or reference lines to monitor and steer the whole machine. Examples are water-level sensors, guide rails or visual markers that keep the dredger aligned during excavation and road-building operations.
[E02F 3/842] using electromagnetic, optical or photoelectric beams, e.g. laser beams -- The subclass E02F 3/842 covers dredging installations that incorporate electromagnetic, optical or photo-electric beams - for example laser beams - to sense, measure or control the removal of material. It includes systems that use light-based techniques for detecting sediment depth, guiding dredge tools, or monitoring operational parameters of fixed dredgers.
[E02F 3/844] for positioning the blade, e.g. hydraulically -- E02F 3/844 is a sub-class of the dredging section within fixed-construction hydraulic engineering that covers devices for positioning a dredger's blade, typically by hydraulic means. It is applied to inventions that adjust blade angle, depth or lateral placement using cylinders, pistons or fluid-power control systems to optimise excavation and material handling.
[E02F 3/845] using mechanical sensors to determine the blade position, e.g. inclinometers, gyroscopes, pendulums -- The subgroup E02F 3/845 relates to dredger blade control in hydraulic engineering, specifically the use of mechanical sensors such as inclinometers, gyroscopes or pendulums to measure and determine the blade's position or angle during operation.
[E02F 3/847] using electromagnetic, optical or acoustic beams to determine the blade position, e.g. laser beams -- E02F 3/847 covers techniques that employ electromagnetic, optical or acoustic beams, such as laser, to sense and locate the position of dredger blades during operation. The method provides real-time blade position data for control and safety in hydraulic dredging equipment.
[E02F 3/848] using cable drums -- E02F 3/848 pertains to the use of cable drums on dredgers, detailing how these drums manage and operate cables for positioning, anchoring or lifting during dredging work. It falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering related to dredging equipment.
[E02F 3/88] with arrangements acting by a sucking or forcing effect, e.g. suction dredgers -- E02F 3/88 designates dredgers that use suction or pressure to lift sediment, employing pumps, dragheads or similar devices to create a sucking or forcing effect for material removal and transport. These arrangements are used in fixed construction hydraulic engineering systems for underwater excavation.
[E02F 3/8808] Stationary installations, e.g. installations using spuds or other stationary supports  spuds on floating substructures  per se   E02F9/062 ; cleaning the beds of waterways  E02B3/02 -- E02F 3/8808 covers stationary installations used in dredging, such as those mounted on spuds or other fixed supports, including spuds mounted on floating substructures. The code also relates to methods for cleaning the beds of waterways.
[E02F 3/8816] Mobile land installations -- The subclass E02F 3/8816 refers to mobile land installations used for dredging operations, including transportable dredger plants, self-propelled or towable units that can be relocated on land to excavate sediment. These installations are part of fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering for dredging.

[E02F 3/8833] Floating installations  floating substructures  per se   E02F9/06

[E02F 3/885] self propelled, e.g. ship -- E02F 3/885 covers self-propelled dredging vessels, essentially ships equipped with their own propulsion system that perform dredging tasks. [E02F 3/8858] Submerged units  self propelled units for burying conduits or cables in trenches under water  E02F5/105 -- The entry E02F 3/8858 refers to submerged, self-propelled dredging units designed to bury pipelines or cables in underwater trenches. [E02F 3/8866] self propelled -- E02F 3/8866 classifies dredging vessels that are self-propelled, meaning they contain their own means of propulsion rather than being towed. These machines are used in hydraulic engineering for moving sediment and typically have integrated power units and steering systems.
[E02F 3/8875] pulled or pushed -- The CPC subclass E02F 3/8875 covers dredging machines that operate by pulling or pushing the dredging tool through the sediment rather than using suction or rotary action. It includes equipment where the digging element is moved forward or backward by a mechanical force applied from the vessel or a land-based system. This method is used for excavating material in waterways, harbors or riverbeds.
[E02F 3/8883] Using the force of explosions, e.g. by the use of internal combustion engines
[E02F 3/8891] wherein at least a part of the soil-shifting equipment is handheld -- E02F 3/8891 classifies dredgers that incorporate at least one handheld soil-shifting element, meaning part of the equipment used to move or remove sediment can be manually operated by the operator. It applies to hydraulic dredging machines that combine mechanised dredging with handheld tools such as hand-held suction heads or grabbers.
[E02F 3/90] Component parts -- E02F 3/90 falls under the CPC section for fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering, specifically dredging equipment. It covers the individual components and parts that make up dredgers, such as blades, pumps and structural elements, without addressing the complete machine. The classification is used to identify inventions related to the design, manufacture or improvement of these dredger components.
[E02F 3/902] for modifying the concentration of the dredged material, e.g. relief valves preventing the clogging of the suction pipe -- This subclass deals with ways to modify the concentration of dredged material in hydraulic dredging systems, such as using relief valves that release excess slurry to prevent suction-pipe clogging, and is part of the fixed-construction, hydraulic-engineering category for dredgers.
[E02F 3/905] Manipulating or supporting suction pipes or ladders; Mechanical supports or floaters therefor; pipe joints for suction pipes  for heave compensation  E02F9/067  takes precedence; pipelines  per se   E02F7/10 ; joints for pipes in general  F16L -- E02F 3/905 covers devices that manipulate, support or float suction pipes and ladders on dredgers, including mechanical supports, floaters and pipe joints designed for heave-compensation. It is a sub-class of Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Dredging → Dredgers and relates to specialized suction-pipe handling equipment.
[E02F 3/907] Measuring or control devices, e.g. control units, detection means or sensors  E02F3/902  takes precedence -- The code E02F 3/907 relates to measuring and control devices used on dredgers, such as control units, detection means and sensors. It is situated within the fixed constructions field of hydraulic engineering for dredging work, and it refines the broader subclass E02F 3/902.
[E02F 3/92] Digging elements, e.g. suction heads -- E02F 3/92 covers the digging elements of dredging machines, such as suction heads, nozzles or other devices that create a flow of water to draw up sediments. These components are part of hydraulic dredgers that break, loosen or convey material from the seabed or riverbed.
[E02F 3/9206] Digging devices using blowing effect only, like jets or propellers  E02F5/107  takes precedence; passive suction heads with jets  E02F3/925 ; active suction heads with jets  E02F3/9262 ; drilling by jets  E21B7/18 ; slitting by jets  E21C25/60 -- E02F 3/9206 covers dredging devices that rely solely on a blowing effect, such as jets or propellers, to displace sediment without mechanical digging tools. It is subordinate to E02F 5/107 for passive suction heads with jets, and related to active suction heads (E02F 3/925, E02F 3/9262) and jet-based drilling or slitting (E21B 7/18, E21C 25/60).
[E02F 3/9212] Mechanical digging means, e.g. suction wheels, i.e. wheel with a suction inlet attached behind the wheel  E02F3/9287  takes precedence; active suction heads  E02F3/9256 -- This subclass covers dredging equipment that uses a rotating wheel fitted with a suction inlet positioned behind the wheel to draw material into the wheel for removal. It pertains to mechanical digging means such as suction wheels, distinct from active suction heads and other suction devices covered by neighboring subclasses.
[E02F 3/9218] with jets -- E02F 3/9218 designates dredgers that employ high-pressure water jets to dislodge, transport, or break up sediment during dredging operations, a method used to improve removal efficiency in hydraulic engineering projects.
[E02F 3/9225] with rotating cutting elements -- E02F 3/9225 refers to dredgers that employ rotating cutting elements such as cutterheads, rotary blades or similar tools to break up and remove sediment or rock during hydraulic-engineering dredging operations. The subclass specifies machines where the cutting action is provided by a rotary mechanism.
[E02F 3/9231] Suction wheels with axis of rotation parallel to longitudinal axis of the suction pipe -- Suction wheels whose shaft runs parallel to the length of the suction pipe are used on dredgers to convey material into the pipe while maintaining a compact, fixed-construction layout. The parallel axis enables efficient flow of slurry or sediment, reducing turbulence and wear within the hydraulic dredging system.
[E02F 3/9237] Suction wheels with axis of rotation in transverse direction of the longitudinal axis of the suction pipe -- This subgroup covers suction wheels whose rotational axis runs across, rather than along, the suction pipe's length, used on dredgers for moving sediment. The design is a fixed hydraulic-engineering construction that directs flow by turning the wheel perpendicular to the pipe's longitudinal axis. It is applied in dredging equipment to improve material intake.
[E02F 3/9243] Passive suction heads with no mechanical cutting means  E02F5/108  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass covers dredger equipment that employs passive suction heads, which draw material by water flow without any mechanical cutting devices. It applies to dredging systems that rely solely on suction for material intake, and it is superseded by the more specific subclass E02F5/108.
[E02F 3/925] with jets -- This subclass covers dredgers that employ water jets to mobilize sediment, using high-velocity fluid streams to break up and transport material. It includes jet-assisted suction or pumping systems for excavating seabeds or riverbeds.
[E02F 3/9256] Active suction heads; Suction heads with cutting elements, i.e. the cutting elements are mounted within the housing of the suction head  E02F5/108  takes precedence -- The CPC subclass E02F 3/9256 covers dredging equipment that uses active suction heads in which cutting elements are mounted inside the suction housing, allowing material to be cut and drawn simultaneously. This feature is specifically defined for hydraulic dredgers and is overridden by the broader subclass E02F5/108.
[E02F 3/9262] with jets -- A dredger employing high-pressure water jets to loosen and transport sediment, where the jets are directed at the seabed or riverbed to create a slurry that can be suctioned or pumped away. This technique falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering, specifically dredging equipment that uses jetting action.
[E02F 3/9268] with rotating cutting elements -- E02F 3/9268 designates dredger machines that employ rotating cutting tools to loosen or fragment underwater soil, rock or sediment. The classification covers cutterhead or rotary bucket dredges where the cutting elements are driven by a rotating shaft, enabling continuous removal of material during dredging operations.
[E02F 3/9275] with axis of rotation parallel to longitudinal axis of the suction pipe -- E02F 3/9275 designates dredgers in which the rotating element - such as a propeller or cutter - is positioned so that its axis of rotation runs parallel to the longitudinal axis of the suction pipe, a configuration used in fixed-construction hydraulic-engineering dredging equipment.
[E02F 3/9281] with axis of rotation in horizontal and transverse direction of the suction pipe -- A subclass for dredgers whose suction pipe rotates about an axis that lies horizontally and across the pipe's length, allowing lateral movement of the pipe during operation. This arrangement is used to improve suction efficiency and maneuverability when removing sediment in hydraulic dredging work.
[E02F 3/9287] Vibrating suction heads -- Vibrating suction heads are devices mounted on dredger intake systems that generate rapid oscillations to loosen compacted sediment and enhance the flow of material into the suction pipe. By applying mechanical vibration, they improve dredging efficiency, reduce clogging, and allow more consistent extraction of sand, silt, or gravel during hydraulic dredging operations. This technology is classified under fixed-construction hydraulic engineering for dredgers.
[E02F 3/9293] Component parts of suction heads, e.g. edges, strainers for preventing the entry of stones or the like -- This CPC subclass concerns the parts of a dredger's suction head that are designed to block solid debris, such as edges or strainers that keep stones and similar material from entering the pump. It falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering, specifically within the dredging and dredger categories.
[E02F 3/94] Apparatus for separating stones from the dredged material -- This subclass covers equipment mounted on dredgers that removes stones or coarse aggregates from the excavated sediment. The devices typically employ sieves, screens, hydrocyclones or gravity separators to isolate solid mineral fragments before the material is conveyed or disposed of.
[E02F 3/945] for environmental purposes -- E02F 3/945 relates to dredgers that are designed or employed primarily for environmental purposes, such as restoring habitats, removing contaminated sediments, improving water quality, or mitigating ecological impacts of dredging activities. The classification covers equipment, methods, and systems that facilitate these environmentally focused dredging operations.
[E02F 3/96] with arrangements for alternate use of different digging elements -- The subclass concerns dredgers equipped with systems that allow the operator to switch between or alternate different digging elements such as buckets, suction heads or cutting tools. It covers arrangements that enable rapid replacement or simultaneous use of multiple tools to adapt to varying seabed or river-bed conditions.
[E02F 3/961] with several digging elements or tools mounted on one machine  for backhoes  E02F3/964  takes precedence
[E02F 3/962] Mounting of implements directly on tools already attached to the machine  E02F3/404  and  E02F3/8152  take precedence
[E02F 3/963] Arrangements on backhoes for alternate use of different tools  backhoes  per se   E02F3/30 ; quick-acting devices to connect tools to arms  E02F3/3609 , for arms to tractors or the like  E02F3/627 -- The classification covers backhoe machines equipped with interchangeable tools, describing how the arm can be quickly reconfigured for different functions. It includes fast-acting coupling mechanisms that attach various implements to the arm, as well as adaptations that allow the arm to be mounted on tractors or similar vehicles.
[E02F 3/964] of several tools mounted on one machine  E02F3/962  takes precedence
[E02F 3/965] of metal-cutting or concrete-crushing implements  shearing devices  B23D17/00 ; wrecking of buildings, e.g. tools therefor,  E04G23/08
[E02F 3/966] of hammer-type tools  arrangements for breaking-up hard ground  E02F5/305 ; percussion -type rippers  E02F5/323
[E02F 3/967] of compacting-type tools  compacting tools in combination with special-purpose dredges or soil-shifting machines  E02F5/30
[E02F 3/968] Storing, handling or otherwise manipulating tools when detached from the machine  E02F3/6273  takes precedence -- E02F 3/968 covers methods and devices for storing, handling or otherwise manipulating tools that have been detached from a dredger or related hydraulic-engineering equipment. It applies to fixed-construction dredging installations and specifies arrangements for tool transport, placement and retrieval, superseding the broader E02F 3/6273.
[E02F 5/00] Dredgers or soil-shifting machines for special purposes -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Machines used for uncovering conduits ( E02F5/003 )  Trenchers ( E02F5/02 and subgroups)  US2007286681  Machines for making embankments or backfilling ( E02F5/22 )  US4782606  US5097610  Machines for cleaning watercourses or other ways ( E02F5/28 )  EP0259472  FR2789414  Rippers ( E02F5/32 )  WO2007022579
[E02F 5/003] for uncovering conduits -- A dredging or soil-shifting machine specially configured to expose buried pipelines, cables or other conduits by removing surrounding material, often using suction, cutting or scrapping tools to uncover the conduit for inspection or repair.
[E02F 5/006] adapted for working ground under water not otherwise provided for  E02F3/081 ,  E02F3/4131 ,  E02F3/8833 ,  E02F5/104 ,  E02F5/125 ,  E02F7/005 ,  E02F7/023 ,  E02F7/065 ,  E02F9/026 ,  E02F9/045 ,  E02F9/06  take precedence
[E02F 5/02] for digging trenches or ditches -- The subclass E02F 5/02 includes machines and equipment specifically designed to dig linear excavations such as trenches or ditches, typically used in hydraulic-engineering dredging projects where fixed constructions shift soil for special purposes.
[E02F 5/022] with tools digging laterally with respect to the frame -- The CPC class E02F 5/022 designates dredging or soil-shifting machines that use digging tools operating laterally to the main frame, intended for special-purpose hydraulic engineering tasks such as targeted excavation or material relocation.
[E02F 5/025] with scraper-buckets, dippers or shovels -- E02F 5/025 covers dredging machines or soil-shifting devices equipped with scraper-buckets, dippers or shovels to excavate, transport and discharge material, typically for specialized applications such as channel deepening, land reclamation or underwater construction. The classification falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering.
[E02F 5/027] with coulters, ploughs, scraper plates, or the like  E02F5/102 ,  E02F5/103 ,  E02F5/106  take precedence -- E02F 5/027 classifies dredging or soil-shifting machines that are fixed in position and use hydraulic power, specifically those equipped with coulters, ploughs, scraper plates or similar implements for special-purpose dredging operations. It falls under the hierarchy of Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Dredging → Dredgers or soil-shifting machines for special purposes. Related sub-entries E02F 5/102, 5/103 and 5/106 take precedence over this group.
[E02F 5/04] with digging screws -- This subclass covers dredging or soil-shifting equipment specially designed for the removal of material using one or more rotating or helical digging screws. The screws engage the seabed or riverbed to loosen and convey sediment, often in confined or hard-to-reach locations. It falls under the CPC hierarchy Fixed Constructions - Hydraulic Engineering - Dredging - Dredgers or soil-shifting machines for special purposes.
[E02F 5/06] with digging elements mounted on an endless chain -- This subclass covers dredgers or soil-shifting machines that use an endless chain carrying digging elements to perform excavation, trenching or material removal. The chain-driven configuration is intended for special-purpose applications such as seabed mining, channel clearing or targeted soil displacement.
[E02F 5/08] with digging wheels turning round an axis -- Specialized dredging or soil-shifting machines equipped with digging wheels that rotate around a common axle, allowing continuous excavation of sediment or earth. The wheels act like large rotary shovels, cutting material as the machine advances, and are used in hydraulic engineering projects such as port maintenance, riverbed clearing, or offshore foundation work.
[E02F 5/10] with arrangements for reinforcing trenches or ditches -- E02F 5/10 covers dredgers or soil-shifting machines equipped with systems that reinforce the sides of trenches or ditches during operation. These arrangements may include temporary shoring, geotextile liners, or inflatable bags to prevent collapse and maintain stability.
[E02F 5/101] forming during digging, e.g. underground canalisations or conduits, by bending or twisting a strip of pliable material; by extrusion -- E02F 5/101 relates to forming underground ducts or conduits while digging, using techniques that bend or twist a flexible strip of material, often by extrusion, as applied in specialized dredgers or soil-shifting machines.
[E02F 5/102] operatively associated with mole-ploughs, coulters  rippers  E02F5/32 -- A subclass of fixed-construction hydraulic engineering that concerns dredgers or soil-shifting machines designed to work together with mole-ploughs, coulters or rippers, providing auxiliary or integrated functions for subsoil loosening or treatment. It falls under the special-purpose dredging category of the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[E02F 5/103] with oscillating or vibrating digging tools -- This CPC subgroup covers dredgers or soil-shifting machines designed for special purposes that employ oscillating or vibrating digging tools to dislodge material during hydraulic engineering operations. It falls under fixed constructions in the dredging field.
[E02F 5/104] for burying conduits or cables in trenches under water  floating substructures  per se   E02F9/06 -- A dredging or soil-shifting machine designed specifically to create underwater trenches for the burial of electrical or communication conduits and cables, typically employing a floating substructure that operates directly on the water surface to excavate and place the conduit in the trench.
[E02F 5/105] self-propulsed units moving on the underwater bottom -- Self-propelled units that travel along the under-water bottom to perform dredging or soil-shifting operations, typically equipped with suction, cutting or digging tools for specialized excavation tasks. They can operate autonomously or be remotely controlled, allowing precise removal of sediments in confined or deep-water environments.
[E02F 5/106] using ploughs, coulters, rippers -- This subgroup includes fixed-construction hydraulic dredgers or soil-shifting machines that employ agricultural tools such as ploughs, coulters or rippers to break up, loosen or move seabed or riverbed material for special-purpose dredging operations.
[E02F 5/107] using blowing-effect devices, e.g. jets  digging devices using a blowing effect  per se   E02F3/9206 -- The CPC class E02F 5/107 relates to special-purpose dredging or soil-shifting machines that employ a blowing effect, such as water or air jets, to excavate material. It covers equipment where the jet itself creates the digging action, rather than conventional mechanical tools, and is situated under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Dredging → Dredgers or soil-shifting machines for special purposes.
[E02F 5/108] using suction-effect devices  suction heads  per se   E02F3/9243 ,  E02F3/9256 -- E02F 5/108 covers dredging equipment designed for special-purpose operations that use suction-effect devices such as suction heads, referencing related sub-entries E02F 3/9243 and E02F 3/9256. The class is nested under Fixed Constructions, Hydraulic Engineering, Dredging, Dredgers or soil-shifting machines for special purposes.
[E02F 5/109] using rotating digging elements  rotating digging elements  per se   E02F3/18 -- The subclass E02F 5/109 covers dredgers or soil-shifting machines that excavate material primarily with rotating digging elements such as cutterheads, augers or rotary drums. It is applied to specialised hydraulic-engineering dredging equipment where the rotating element itself performs the digging action.
[E02F 5/12] with equipment for back-filling trenches or ditches -- A subclass of hydraulic-engineering dredgers or soil-shifting machines that incorporate built-in equipment for back-filling excavated trenches or ditches, allowing the displaced material to be returned immediately after removal. It is applied to special-purpose fixed-construction tasks such as pipe laying, foundation preparation or similar ground-working operations.
[E02F 5/125] underwater -- The CPC subclass E02F 5/125 covers underwater dredgers or soil-shifting machines designed for special purposes, placed under fixed constructions, hydraulic engineering and dredging. It includes equipment that operates beneath the water surface to move sediment for tasks such as harbour deepening, land reclamation or environmental remediation.
[E02F 5/14] Component parts for trench excavators, e.g. indicating devices -- This class covers component parts for trench excavators, such as indicating devices that signal position, depth or operating status and other auxiliary elements that support these specialized soil-shifting machines. It belongs to the fixed hydraulic engineering subgroup for dredging equipment.
[E02F 5/145] control and indicating devices -- The CPC class E02F 5/145 covers control and indicating devices used on dredgers or other soil-shifting machines for specialised purposes, including instrumentation, sensors, control panels and monitoring systems that regulate operation, display performance data and ensure safe, efficient hydraulic dredging.
[E02F 5/16] Machines for digging other holes in the soil -- The classification E02F 5/16 covers machines that create additional holes in the ground beyond standard trenching, such as augers, corers or other soil-shifting devices used in dredging and hydraulic engineering projects. These specialized excavators are designed for particular purposes like sampling, piling or subsurface installation.
[E02F 5/18] for horizontal holes -- E02F 5/18 relates to dredgers or soil-shifting machines specially adapted to form horizontal holes in the bottom of water bodies, typically for installing tunnels, pipelines or other underground structures.
[E02F 5/20] for vertical holes -- E02F 5/20 covers dredgers or soil-shifting machines designed to create vertical holes, such as pipe-driving or foundation piles, used in hydraulic engineering for specialized construction tasks.
[E02F 5/22] for making embankments -- E02F 5/22 covers dredging or soil-shifting equipment specifically designed for constructing embankments, such as machines that place or compact material to build levees, revetments or other permanent hydraulic structures. The classification applies to fixed-construction devices used in hydraulic engineering for the purpose of forming or reinforcing embankments.
[E02F 5/223] for back-filling  in association with trench excavators  E02F5/12 -- E02F 5/223 covers dredging equipment designed to perform back-filling operations while working together with trench excavators. It falls under the fixed-construction, hydraulic-engineering group for specialized soil-shifting machines. The code addresses machines that transport and place material into excavated trenches as part of the dredging process.
[E02F 5/226] with means for processing the soil, e.g. screening belts, separators; Padding machines -- E02F 5/226 covers dredgers or soil-shifting machines designed for special purposes that include onboard soil-processing equipment such as screening belts, separators or padding machines, enabling the material to be refined, sorted or compacted directly after excavation. This subclass falls under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Dredging.
[E02F 5/24] Depositing dredged material in mounds -- The entry E02F 5/24 relates to techniques and equipment for placing dredged spoil in the form of mounds, either on the seabed or on land, to create disposal piles used in harbor deepening, land reclamation or environmental remediation. It covers the design of dumping arrangements, positioning methods and stability considerations for the constructed mounds.
[E02F 5/26] Combined conveying-bridges and dredgers -- The subclass E02F 5/26 covers machines that unite a convey-bridge system with dredging equipment, allowing excavated material to be lifted and transferred directly across a bridge-type structure. These combined devices are used for specialized soil-shifting tasks such as land reclamation, channel deepening or port maintenance, where efficient material handling and transport are required.
[E02F 5/28] for cleaning watercourses or other ways -- E02F 5/28 covers dredgers or soil-shifting machines specifically designed to clean watercourses, canals, rivers or other waterways. The machines remove sediment, debris or vegetation to maintain flow, improve navigation or restore ecological conditions.
[E02F 5/282] with rotating cutting or digging tools -- E02F 5/282 covers dredging or soil-shifting machines for special purposes that use rotating cutting or digging tools such as rotary cutterheads, buckets or discs to break up and remove sediment, typically in hydraulic engineering tasks like harbour deepening, river maintenance or underwater excavation.
[E02F 5/285] with drag buckets or scraper plates -- The CPC subclass E02F 5/285 denotes dredgers or soil-shifting machines that are equipped with drag buckets or scraper plates for specialized dredging tasks, falling under the broader categories of fixed constructions, hydraulic engineering, and dredging.
[E02F 5/287] with jet nozzles  digging devices with blowing effect  per se   E02F3/9206 -- E02F 5/287 covers dredging or soil-shifting machines that incorporate jet nozzles to create a blowing or suction effect for digging. The devices use high-pressure fluid streams to fluidize or remove sediment, enabling excavation without mechanical cutting tools. It applies to specialized dredgers designed specifically for this jet-driven operation.
[E02F 5/30] Auxiliary apparatus, e.g. for thawing, cracking, blowing-up, or other preparatory treatment of the soil -- E02F 5/30 relates to auxiliary devices fitted to dredgers or soil-shifting machines that perform preparatory treatment of the substrate, such as equipment for thawing frozen ground, cracking compacted layers, blowing up sediments or other pre-treatment functions. It is classified under Fixed Constructions, Hydraulic Engineering, Dredging, specifically for special-purpose dredgers or soil-shifting machines.
[E02F 5/305] Arrangements for breaking-up hard ground  E02F5/32  takes precedence; hammer-type tools  E02F3/966 ; breaking-up paving of roads or the like  E01C23/12 ; breaking-up subaqueous rock  E02B3/02 -- This CPC subclass covers specific configurations of dredging or soil-shifting machines designed to fracture hard ground such as rock or compacted soil during excavation or dredging operations. It includes arrangements for applying impact, hydraulic or mechanical forces to break the substrate, complementing related subclasses for hammer-type tools, road-paving break-up, and subaqueous rock breaking.
[E02F 5/32] Rippers -- E02F 5/32 designates rippers, specialized tools mounted on dredgers that break up compacted soil, sediment, or rock to facilitate excavation and removal in hydraulic engineering projects. They are a subset of fixed-construction dredging equipment designed for particular soil-shifting tasks.
[E02F 5/323] Percussion-type rippers -- Percussion-type rippers are hydraulic dredging tools that repeatedly strike the seabed or riverbed to break up compacted soil, sand, or gravel, allowing the material to be suctioned or conveyed away. They are mounted on dredgers for specialized soil-shifting tasks where high-energy impact is needed, often in hard ground or cohesive sediments.
[E02F 5/326] oscillating or vibrating -- E02F 5/326 covers dredging machines that employ an oscillating or vibrating motion to break up and transport sediment, typically used for specialized soil-shifting tasks such as underwater trenching or fine-material removal.

[E02F 9/0808] Improving mounting or assembling, e.g. frame elements, disposition of all the components on the superstructures  for disposition of specific components,  E02F9/0858 -- E02F 9/0808 relates to the mounting, assembly and arrangement of component parts on dredgers or soil-shifting machines, such as frame elements or other superstructure components, where the parts are not limited to a specific sub-group. [E02F 9/0833] Improving access, e.g. for maintenance, steps for improving driver's access, handrails -- E02F 9/0833 classifies access-improving features for dredgers and soil-shifting machines, such as steps, handrails, ladders or other devices that facilitate an operator's reach for maintenance or control. It falls under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Dredging → Component parts of dredgers or soil-shifting machines, without being limited to a particular machine type.
[E02F 9/0841] Articulated frame, i.e. having at least one pivot point between two travelling gear units  tractor-trailer combinations  B62D53/00
[E02F 9/085] Ground-engaging fitting for supporting the machines while working, e.g. outriggers, legs  for vehicles in general  B60S9/00 , for cranes  B66C23/78 -- The entry E02F 9/085 covers ground-engaging fittings such as outriggers, support legs or similar devices that stabilize dredgers, soil-shifting machines or related vehicles while they are working. These components are not limited to any particular type of dredger and are classified under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Dredging. It links to analogous support-leg categories for vehicles (B60S9/00) and cranes (B66C23/78).
[E02F 9/0858] Arrangement of component parts installed on superstructures not otherwise provided for, e.g. electric components, fenders, air-conditioning units  E02F9/16 ,  E02F9/18  take precedence -- E02F 9/0858 classifies the layout of auxiliary parts mounted on dredger superstructures that are not otherwise specified, such as electric equipment, fenders or air-conditioning units. It falls under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Dredging, and is subordinate to the more detailed groups E02F 9/16 and E02F 9/18.
[E02F 9/0866] Engine compartment, e.g. heat exchangers, exhaust filters, cooling devices, silencers, mufflers, position of hydraulic pumps in the engine compartment -- The code designates the layout and equipment of a dredger's engine compartment, including heat exchangers, exhaust filters, cooling devices, silencers or mufflers, and the positioning of hydraulic pumps. It concerns how these components are arranged to support the vessel's power and hydraulic functions.
[E02F 9/0875] Arrangement of valve arrangements on superstructures  arrangement of hydraulic hoses  E02F9/2275  takes precedence; valves  per se   E02F9/2267 -- The CPC entry E02F 9/0875 covers component parts of dredgers or soil-shifting machines that relate to the layout of valve assemblies and hydraulic hoses on the superstructure. It addresses how valves are arranged, including connections and routing, without limiting the description to any specific valve type.
[E02F 9/0883] Tanks, e.g. oil tank, urea tank, fuel tank  for vehicles in general  B60K15/00
[E02F 9/0891] Lids or bonnets or doors or details thereof  doors for cabins  E02F9/163  takes precedence; for motor vehicles  B62D25/10 -- This CPC subclass E02F 9/0891 covers lids, bonnets, doors and related details used on cabins of dredgers or other soil-shifting machines, without restriction to a specific kind. It falls under fixed constructions, hydraulic engineering and dredging, and is overridden for motor-vehicle doors by E02F9/163 and B62D25/10.
[E02F 9/10] Supports for movable superstructures mounted on travelling or walking gears or on other superstructures -- This subgroup defines devices that support movable superstructures-such as cabins or work platforms-on dredgers, attaching them to travelling or walking gear or to other structural elements to provide a stable mounting for equipment or operators; the components are generic to dredging machines and not limited to a specific type.
[E02F 9/12] Slewing or traversing gears -- The CPC entry E02F 9/12 refers to slewing or traversing gears that are component parts of dredgers or soil-shifting machines, used to rotate or swing the machine's working elements. It falls under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering dredging.
[E02F 9/121] Turntables, i.e. structure rotatable about 360° -- This subclass covers turntables-structures that can rotate a full 360°-used as component parts of dredgers or soil-shifting machines. It pertains to fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering where rotating platforms support or position equipment during dredging operations.
[E02F 9/123] Drives or control devices specially adapted therefor  E02F9/125  and  E02F9/128  take precedence -- This CPC subclass (E02F 9/123) covers component parts of dredgers or soil-shifting machines that are not limited to the specific kinds defined in other groups, specifically drives or control devices specially adapted for those machines. It falls under the hierarchy of fixed constructions, hydraulic engineering and dredging, and takes precedence over the related subclasses E02F9/125 and E02F9/128.
[E02F 9/125] Locking devices -- E02F 9/125 pertains to locking devices that serve as component parts of dredgers or soil-shifting machines. These devices secure removable or movable elements without being limited to the specific kinds defined in other groups, and are used in hydraulic-engineering fixed constructions for dredging operations.
[E02F 9/126] Lubrication systems -- E02F 9/126 designates the lubrication-system components used in dredgers or soil-shifting machines, covering any part that supplies, distributes or stores lubricants for moving elements but not limited to the specific sub-groups listed elsewhere. It falls under Fixed Constructions → Hydraulic Engineering → Dredging, focusing on ancillary equipment that keeps mechanical parts running smoothly.
[E02F 9/128] Braking systems -- E02F 9/128 covers braking systems used on dredgers and other soil-shifting machines. It includes devices that control or stop the motion of rotating or moving components such as propellers, winches, or conveyors. The entry applies to any braking arrangement irrespective of the specific type of dredger.
[E02F 9/14] Booms -- The class E02F 9/14 "Booms" covers structural components, such as flexible or rigid barriers, mounted on dredgers or soil-shifting machines to direct, contain or control water, sediment or debris during operation. These elements are part of the dredger's fixed construction and are not confined to a particular boom type defined in other groups.
[E02F 9/16] Cabins, platforms, or the like, for drivers -- This CPC subclass E02F 9/16 covers cabins, platforms, or similar structures that provide accommodation for operators of dredging or soil-shifting machines. It includes any component that forms part of the machine but is not limited to specific types of cabins defined elsewhere. The class is used to categorize inventions related to driver stations on dredger equipment.

It falls under the fixed-construction, hydraulic-engineering category for dredging equipment, identified by code E02F 9/18.
[E02F 9/20] Drives -- E02F 9/20 covers the drive mechanisms used in dredgers or other soil-shifting machines, including motors, gearboxes, propellers and power transmission systems that provide propulsion or operating force. It applies to any drive configuration not limited to the specific sub-groups defined elsewhere in the dredging section.
[E02F 9/2004] Control mechanisms, e.g. control levers  control levers  per se   G05G -- E02F 9/2004 is a CPC subclass for component parts of dredgers or soil-shifting machines that are not covered by more specific groups, specifically focusing on control mechanisms such as control levers used to operate the equipment, classified under fixed constructions in hydraulic engineering.
[E02F 9/2008] Control mechanisms in the form of the machine in the reduced scale model -- E02F 9/2008 covers control mechanisms presented as reduced-scale models of dredging or soil-shifting machines. [E02F 9/2012] Setting the functions of the control levers, e.g. changing assigned functions among operations levers, setting functions dependent on the operator or seat orientation -- The entry covers mechanisms that assign or reassign the functions of control levers on dredging or soil-shifting machines, allowing the lever functions to be changed according to the operator's preferences or seat orientation. It includes systems for switching operational assignments among levers.
[E02F 9/2016] Winches  winches  per se   B66D -- E02F 9/2016 covers winches used as stand-alone components in dredging or soil-shifting equipment, encompassing the mechanical devices that provide pulling or hoisting force for handling lines, cables or chains in fixed dredger constructions. The classification includes all typical winch types without limiting them to a specific group within the broader hydraulic-engineering context.
[E02F 9/202] Mechanical transmission, e.g. clutches, gears  clutches  per se   F16D , gears  per se   F16H -- E02F 9/202 covers component parts of dredgers or soil-shifting machines that involve mechanical transmission elements such as clutches and gears, linked to the broader categories of fixed constructions, hydraulic engineering and dredging.
[E02F 9/2025] Particular purposes of control systems not otherwise provided for  E02F3/16 ,  E02F3/26 , sub-groups of  E02F3/43 ,  E02F3/651 , sub-groups of  E02F3/84 ,  E02F3/907 ,  E02F5/145  take precedence -- The CPC subclass E02F 9/2025 covers control-system functions for dredger or soil-shifting machine components that serve particular purposes not defined in other groups, and it overrides the listed sub-groups such as E02F 3/16, 3/26, 3/43, 3/651, 3/84, 3/907 and E02F 5/145. It is used for fixed hydraulic constructions in dredging where specialized control features are required.
[E02F 9/2029] Controlling the position of implements in function of its load, e.g. modifying the attitude of implements in accordance to vehicle speed  control for hydraulic or pneumatic drives  E02F9/2203 ,  E02F9/2221  and  E02F9/2253  take precedence -- This CPC group covers mechanisms that adjust the position or attitude of dredger implements according to load or vehicle speed, using hydraulic or pneumatic control. It includes systems that change implement angle or depth in response to load variations and takes precedence over sub-groups 9/2203, 9/2221 and 9/2253.
[E02F 9/2033] Limiting the movement of frames or implements, e.g. to avoid collision between implements and the cabin  sub-groups of  E02F3/431  of  E02F3/435  take precedence; for turntables  E02F9/123 -- This subclass covers devices or control systems that restrict the travel of frames or implements on dredgers or soil-shifting machines to prevent collisions with the cabin or other parts. It applies when more specific sub-groups (E02F3/431, E02F3/435) are not relevant; turntable-related limits fall under E02F9/123.
[E02F 9/2037] Coordinating the movements of the implement and of the frame -- The code covers mechanisms that synchronize the motion of a dredging implement with the supporting frame or vessel, ensuring coordinated positioning during soil-shifting operations. It includes hydraulic, pneumatic or mechanical control systems that adjust speed, angle or depth to improve efficiency and prevent interference between components.
[E02F 9/2041] Automatic repositioning of implements, i.e. memorising determined positions of the implement  for dipper-arms or bucket-arms  E02F3/434 ,  E02F3/437 ,  E02F3/438 ,  E02F3/439  take precedence -- This class covers mechanisms that automatically return dredging implements to pre-recorded positions, using memory or control systems for dipper-arms or bucket-arms on dredgers or soil-shifting machines. [E02F 9/2221] Control of flow rate; Load sensing arrangements  E02F9/2203  take precedence over  E02F9/2221 -- The subclass E02F 9/2221 covers components of dredgers or soil-shifting machines that regulate hydraulic flow, such as flow-control valves, pumps and load-or pressure-sensing devices used to adjust the rate of water or slurry movement. [E02F 9/2225] using pressure-compensating valves -- The CPC subgroup E02F 9/2225 pertains to component parts of dredgers or soil-shifting machines that incorporate pressure-compensating valves to regulate hydraulic flow and maintain consistent operation under varying load conditions. This entry falls within the fixed-construction, hydraulic-engineering, dredging hierarchy.
