[E04B 1/26] the supporting parts consisting of wood -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structures with posts and beams made of wood.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Space frames -> E04B1/19 - Log cabins -> E04B2/701 - Caps for the protection of the ends of wooden beams against humidity -> E04B5/12
[E04B 1/2604] Connections specially adapted therefor  shear dowels  E04B1/486 ,  E04B1/49 -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Shear dowels for wood -> E04B1/486 - Shear dowels for wood with self-penetrating parts -> E04B1/49
[E04B 1/2608] Connectors made from folded sheet metal  E04B1/2612  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Joist hangers -> E04B1/2612
[E04B 1/2612] Joist hangers -- Joist hangers are metal brackets or fixtures used to attach and support floor or ceiling joists to walls, beams or other structural members, providing a secure connection without requiring welding. They are employed in both new construction and renovation to simplify assembly, distribute loads, and maintain alignment of the joist system.
[E04B 1/28] the supporting parts consisting of other material -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structures with posts and beams made of specified material other than concrete, stone-like material, metal, wood.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Space frames -> E04B1/19 - Structures with posts and beams made of the combination of at least two different materials having a structural function -> E04B1/30
[E04B 1/30] the supporting parts being composed of two or more materials -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structures with posts and beams made of at least two specified different materials having a structural function, e.g. a steel pipe filled with concrete  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Space frames -> E04B1/19 - Structures made of elongated load supporting parts made of reinforced concrete -> E04B1/20
[E04B 1/32] Arched structures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arched structures; Vaulted structures; Arched or vaulted structures with a folded surface.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Structures deriving their rigidity from concertina folds -> E04B1/345 - Vaulted roof structures -> E04B7/08
[E04B 1/3205] Structures with a longitudinal horizontal axis, e.g. cylindrical or prismatic structures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structures in the shape of a portion of a cylinder, with a smooth or faceted surface.
[E04B 1/3211] Structures with a vertical rotation axis or the like, e.g. semi-spherical structures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structures in the shape of a portion of a sphere, with a smooth or faceted surface.
[E04B 1/34] Extraordinary structures, e.g. with suspended or cantilever parts supported by masts or tower-like structures enclosing elevators or stairs -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Structures covering a large free area -> E04B1/342 - Structures characterised by movable, separable or collapsible parts -> E04B1/343 - Structures composed or box-like or cell-like units -> E04B1/348 - Floor structures of extraordinary design; Features relating to the elastic stability of floors -> E04B5/43 - Buildings for special purposes, e.g. withstanding earthquakes -> E04H
[E04B 1/3404] supported by masts or tower-like structures -- This subclass includes buildings or other fixed constructions whose primary support consists of one or more masts, towers or similar vertical elements, such as elevated platforms, tower-supported shelters or structures anchored by pole-like members. It covers designs where the load-bearing system is provided by these upright members rather than conventional walls or foundations.
[E04B 1/3408] Extraordinarily-supported small buildings -- This subclass covers small structures such as kiosks, cabins, or shelters that are supported by atypical means like cables, tension rods, or other non-traditional supports rather than conventional foundations. It includes designs where the building relies on extraordinary support systems to remain stable.
[E04B 1/3412] mainly supported by a central column or footing  E04B1/3416  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Structures comprising mainly a central support and a cantilevered roof -> E04B1/3416
[E04B 1/3416] Structures comprising mainly a central support column and a cantilevered roof -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mushroom-type structures or elongated structures having a "T" or "Y" vertical cross-section.
[E04B 1/342] Structures covering a large free area, whether open-sided or not, e.g. hangars, halls -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Roof trusses -> E04C3/00 - Non-structural features for specified purposes -> E04H
[E04B 1/343] Structures characterised by movable, separable, or collapsible parts, e.g. for transport -- E04B 1/343 classifies building structures that incorporate movable, separable or collapsible parts for ease of transport, such as modular, fold-out or demountable constructions. The code falls under Fixed Constructions → Building → General Building Constructions and addresses temporary or transport-ready edifices.
[E04B 1/34305] telescopic -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structure with room-sized telescopic section extendable horizontally from a side of the structure or vertically from the top or bottom of the structure.
[E04B 1/3431] with only one level of nesting -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structure with a single telescopic section extendable horizontally from a side of the structure or vertically from the top or bottom of the structure.
[E04B 1/34312] Vertical telescopic structures -- Vertical telescopic structures are extendable frameworks that can be raised or lowered along a vertical axis, commonly used in construction, maintenance, or temporary installations to provide adjustable height and access. They include telescoping columns, lift towers, and modular scaffolding systems that can be assembled and reconfigured as needed.
[E04B 1/34315] characterised by separable parts -- The CPC entry E04B 1/34315 covers building constructions whose structure is made of parts that can be separated, removed or reassembled, such as modular, prefabricated or detachable components. It falls under fixed constructions, building, general building constructions and addresses designs that facilitate disassembly, transport or reconfiguration.
[E04B 1/34317] Set of building elements forming a self-contained package for transport before assembly -- A self-contained package of pre-manufactured building elements that are shipped together and assembled on site, covering components such as wall panels, modules or prefabricated sections designed for transport as a single unit before erection. This falls under Fixed Constructions → Building → General Building Constructions.
[E04B 1/34321] mainly constituted by panels -- E04B 1/34321 covers building structures whose primary components are panels, such as prefabricated wall, floor or roof panels, including modular panel systems and panelised construction methods. The classification is used for fixed constructions in the general building category where the building is mainly constituted by panels rather than other structural elements.
[E04B 1/34326] mainly constituted by longitudinal elements -- The CPC entry E04B 1/34326 covers fixed building constructions whose primary structure consists of longitudinal members such as beams, girders, or joists. It applies to general building works where the main load-bearing elements run lengthwise, forming the core framework of the structure. This falls under the hierarchy of Fixed Constructions - Building - General Building Constructions.
[E04B 1/34331] mainly constituted by three-dimensional elements -- E04B 1/34331 covers building constructions that are primarily composed of three-dimensional structural elements, such as frames, trusses, space-frame or lattice structures, where the overall form relies on the spatial arrangement of these elements rather than on planar or two-dimensional components. It applies to designs where the load-bearing and functional aspects are achieved through the geometry of the three-dimensional framework.
[E04B 1/34336] Structures movable as a whole, e.g. mobile home structures  vehicles comprising living accommodation for people, e.g. caravans, camping or like vehicles  B60P3/32 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mobile home structures.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Vehicle aspects of mobile homes -> B60P3/32
[E04B 1/34342] Skirtings therefor -- E04B 1/34342 covers skirtings designed for fixed building constructions, including baseboards and decorative trim installed at the junction between walls and floors. It falls under the general building constructions category and relates to the design, material and installation methods of these elements.
[E04B 1/34347] Anchoring means therefor -- E04B 1/34347 relates to anchoring means used in fixed building constructions. It covers devices, systems and methods that secure structural elements such as walls, columns or panels to foundations or other supporting parts, ensuring stability and load transfer in general building construction.
[E04B 1/34352] Base structures or supporting means therefor -- The CPC entry E04B 1/34352 concerns base structures and the supporting means for them in building construction. It covers foundations, sub-structures and other elements that transfer loads from the superstructure to the ground. The classification is applied to patents dealing with the design, preparation and installation of such foundational and support systems.
[E04B 1/34363] with provisions to raise or lower the whole structure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structures with means for raising and lowering them above ground level;  Structures with means for retracting them into the ground and returning them to ground level.
[E04B 1/34384] Assembling details for foldable, separable, collapsible or retractable structures -- The entry covers specific assembling methods and components used to create structures that can be folded, separated, collapsed or retracted, such as portable shelters, fold-out roofs, or retractable awnings. It addresses the design of joints, hinges, locking mechanisms and other details that enable the structure to change shape while maintaining stability and functionality. This falls within the broader category of general building constructions for fixed constructions.
[E04B 1/344] with hinged parts -- This subclass of E04B deals with building elements that incorporate hinged components, including doors, gates, shutters, panels or other parts that rotate about a hinge. It applies to general building constructions where the hinge mechanism is an integral part of the structure.
[E04B 1/3441] with articulated bar-shaped elements -- The CPC subclass E04B 1/3441 covers general building constructions that incorporate articulated bar-shaped elements, such as hinged, retractable or movable bars used in walls, fences, shutters or similar fixed structures. It pertains to fixed constructions where the bar components provide adjustable or jointed functionality within the building element.
[E04B 1/3442] folding out from a core cell -- The code covers structures where components are stored in a compact core and can be unfolded to form walls, floors or partitions. It applies to prefabricated or modular building parts that expand outward from a central cell to create usable space. Such systems are used for quickly assembled or temporary buildings.
[E04B 1/3444] with only lateral unfolding -- This CPC subclass covers fixed building constructions that unfold solely in a lateral direction. It includes designs such as fold-out walls, side-opening panels or roof sections that open horizontally while remaining otherwise stationary. The classification is used for patents describing mechanisms where only sideward unfolding is provided.
[E04B 1/3445] foldable in a flat stack of parallel panels -- A foldable structure formed by a flat stack of parallel panels, designed to be unfolded for use as a building element and refolded for storage or transport. It belongs to the general building constructions group and covers systems where panels slide or hinge together to create a compact, modular assembly. This type of construction is used for temporary shelters, collapsible walls or roofing components.
[E04B 1/3447] Portal- or saddle-shaped structures -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Illustrative example of the subject matter classified in this subgroup:
[E04B 1/3448] with rotating shell-forming segments -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structures with rotating shell-forming segments, e.g. armadillo plates type structure.  Illustrative example of the subject matter classified in this subgroup:
[E04B 1/3449] with living hinge  E04B1/345  takes precedence -- The subclass E04B 1/3449 covers building elements that incorporate a living hinge, a thin flexible section that enables movement without separate hardware, within fixed construction components. It falls under the hierarchy Fixed Constructions, Building, General Building Constructions, Constructions in general. The broader subclass E04B 1/345 is the controlling entry for this feature.
[E04B 1/345] Structures deriving their rigidity from concertina folds -- This CPC entry covers building structures whose stiffness is obtained by means of concertina or accordion-type folds, allowing walls, roofs or panels to become rigid without separate framing. It includes designs that use series of pleats or folded sections to provide structural stability, often in lightweight or deployable construction.
[E04B 1/3455] Wheeled arch-type structures -- Wheeled arch-type structures are building elements that combine an arch form with a wheel or rolling support to enable relocation, such as portable arches, mobile shelters, and relocatable bridge sections. The classification covers design, construction and use of such movable arch systems in fixed construction contexts.
[E04B 1/346] Rotary buildings -- Rotary buildings are structures that can turn as a whole around a vertical axis to change orientation, often for sun-tracking, wind-direction or ventilation purposes. The category includes rotating houses, observatories, turntables and other constructions where the entire building pivots as a single unit.
[E04B 1/3465] with adapted utilities connections, e.g. for water or electricity -- This CPC subclass E04B 1/3465 falls under Fixed Constructions → Building → General Building Constructions and covers structures that are provided with built-in utility connections, such as water supply or electricity, designed to be integrated during construction.
[E04B 1/348] Structures composed of units comprising at least considerable parts of two sides of a room, e.g. box-like or cell-like units closed or in skeleton form -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structures composed of units comprising at least considerable parts of two sides of a room, e.g. box-like or cell-like units closed or in skeleton form, i.e. only the edges of the boxes or cells are materialized (only frame, no infill panels)
[E04B 1/34807] Elements integrated in a skeleton -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Box-like units resting on shelves or beams of a supporting structure;  Stacked cells with concrete poured between the cells.
[E04B 1/34815] Elements not integrated in a skeleton -- The subclass includes building parts that are attached to a structure but are not part of its load-bearing skeleton, such as cladding, non-structural panels, interior partitions, finishes and other accessories that serve protective, aesthetic or functional purposes without contributing to the framework's structural integrity.
[E04B 1/34823] the supporting structure consisting of concrete -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structures with the main vertical structural parts consisting of concrete.
[E04B 1/3483] the supporting structure consisting of metal -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structures with the main vertical structural parts consisting of metal.
[E04B 1/34838] the supporting structure consisting of wood -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structures with the main vertical structural parts consisting of wood.
[E04B 1/34846] the supporting structure consisting of other specified material, e.g. of plastics -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structures with the main vertical structural parts consisting of specified material other than concrete, metal, wood.
[E04B 1/34853] the supporting structure being composed of two or more materials -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structures with the main vertical structural parts consisting of the combination of two or more specified structural materials.
[E04B 1/34861] particular arrangement of habitable rooms or their component parts; modular co-ordination -- The E04B 1/34861 entry covers the specific layout and spatial organization of habitable rooms and their components, focusing on how these spaces are arranged in relation to each other. It also includes modular coordination schemes that standardize the dimensions and connections of room elements for flexible building construction.
[E04B 1/34869] Elements for special technical purposes, e.g. with a sanitary equipment -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structures composed of units comprising at least considerable parts of two sides of a room where the units are pre-fitted for special technical purposes, e.g. with pre-fitted piping, plumbing, sanitary equipment or electrical wiring.
[E04B 1/35] Extraordinary methods of construction, e.g. lift-slab, jack-block -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Extraordinary structures -> E04B1/34 - Falsework, shuttering for shaping walls, floors, ceilings or roofs in situ -> E04G11/04 - Conveying or assembling of building materials -> E04G21/00 - Working measures on existing buildings -> E04G23/00
[E04B 1/3505] characterised by the  in situ  moulding of large parts of a structure  E04B1/3511 ,  E04B1/3516 ,  E04B1/3522 ,  E04B1/3533 ,  E04B1/3544 ,  E04B1/355  take precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Lift-slab;Extraordinary methods of construction characterised by a purely vertical lifting of floors or roofs or parts thereof -> E04B1/3511 - Extraordinary methods of construction characterised by erecting a vertical structure and then adding the floors from top to bottom -> E04B1/3516 - Extraordinary methods of construction characterised by raising a structure and then adding structural elements under it -> E04B1/3522 - Extraordinary methods of construction characterised by the raising of hingedly-connected building elements, e.g. arches, portal frames -> E04B1/3533 - Extraordinary methods of construction characterised by the use of a central column to lift and temporarily or permanently support structural elements -> E04B1/3544 - Extraordinary methods of construction characterised by the tilting-up of whole buildings or sections thereof, e.g. walls, portal frames -> E04B1/355
[E04B 1/3511] Lift-slab; characterised by a purely vertical lifting of floors or roofs or parts thereof -- The entry covers a construction technique in which entire concrete slabs forming floors or roofs are raised straight upward from the ground or casting platform to their final position, using vertical lifts without any horizontal displacement. This method is a subset of general building constructions and fixed constructions.
[E04B 1/3516] characterised by erecting a vertical structure and then adding the floors from top to bottom -- E04B 1/3516 covers a top-down construction method in which a vertical structural core is erected first and the floor slabs are added sequentially from the top downward, allowing work to proceed beneath completed levels while the building framework is still being assembled. This approach is used for fast-track or constrained-site building projects.
[E04B 1/3522] characterised by raising a structure and then adding structural elements under it  E04B1/3511 ,  E04B1/3516 ,  E04B1/3533  take precedence -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Structure raisable using temporary supports, e.g. scaffolding or lifting machines, or permanent supports.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Lift-slab; Extraordinary methods of construction characterised by a purely vertical lifting of floors or roofs or parts thereof -> E04B1/3511 - Extraordinary methods of construction characterised by erecting a vertical structure and then adding the floors from top to bottom -> E04B1/3516 - Extraordinary methods of construction characterised by the raising of hingedly-connected building elements, e.g. arches, portal frames -> E04B1/3533
[E04B 1/3527] the structure being a roof -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Method of construction whereby the walls are added under the raised roof, e.g. in order to enable work in dry conditions
[E04B 1/3533] characterised by the raising of hingedly-connected building elements, e.g. arches, portal frames  E04B1/355  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Extraordinary methods of construction characterised by the tilting-up of whole buildings or sections thereof, e.g. walls, portal frames -> E04B1/355
[E04B 1/3538] the elements being a floor slab with hingedly-connected wall panels -- E04B 1/3538 covers constructions where a floor slab incorporates wall panels that are attached by hinges, allowing the panels to pivot relative to the slab. This type of modular, hingedly-connected wall system is used in prefabricated or adjustable building structures.
[E04B 1/3544] characterised by the use of a central column to lift and temporarily or permanently support structural elements  E04B1/3516  and  E04B1/355  take precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Extraordinary methods of construction characterised by erecting a vertical structure and then adding the floors from top to bottom -> E04B1/3516 - Extraordinary methods of construction characterised by the tilting-up of whole buildings or sections thereof, e.g. walls, portal frames -> E04B1/355
[E04B 1/355] characterised by the tilting up of whole buildings or sections thereof, e.g. walls, portal frames -- The subclass E04B 1/355 covers construction techniques where whole buildings or large sections, such as walls or portal frames, are raised by tilting them from a horizontal to a vertical position. It falls under the hierarchy of Fixed Constructions → Building → General Building Constructions within the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[E04B 1/3555] Constructions using straw bales -- The entry covers building methods that use straw bales as primary structural or insulating components, including walls, roofs and panels, within general construction practices. It addresses the design, assembly and performance of straw-bale constructions for residential, commercial or agricultural buildings, emphasizing their lightweight, renewable and insulating properties.
[E04B 1/36] Bearings or like supports allowing movement -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Bearings allowing movement of the supported structure due to exterior influences, but also allowing movement for the purpose of adjusting horizontal or angular position of the supported structure.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Bearings for bridges -> E01D19/04 - Buildings withstanding earthquakes -> E04H9/02
[E04B 1/38] Connections for building structures in general -- E04B 1/38 covers general methods and devices for joining building components, such as bolts, welds, adhesives, mechanical fasteners or interlocking parts, used to assemble structural members in construction. It applies to a wide range of building types and does not limit itself to a specific material or structural system. The focus is on the connection technique rather than the individual elements being linked.
[E04B 1/383] Connection of concrete parts using adhesive materials, e.g. mortar or glue -- This class covers techniques for joining separate concrete components by means of adhesive substances such as mortar, cement-based grout or industrial glue. It includes the preparation, application and curing of the adhesive to create a permanent bond between prefabricated or cast-in-place concrete parts.
[E04B 1/388] Separate connecting elements  Connecting devices specially adapted for embedding in concrete  E04B1/41 ; Dowels, i.e. members adapted to penetrate the surfaces of two parts and to take the shear stresses  E04B1/48 -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Connecting devices specially adapted for embedding in concrete -> E04B1/41 - Dowels, i.e. members adapted to penetrate the surfaces of two parts and to take the shear stresses -> E04B1/48
[E04B 1/41] Connecting devices specially adapted for embedding in concrete or masonry -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Joist hangers -> E04B1/2612 - Shear dowels for concrete parts -> E04B1/483 - Spacers other than masonry wall ties for cavity walls -> E04B2/30 , E04B2/44 - Connectors for reinforcing elements -> E04C5/16 - Fastening frames to the border of openings -> E06B1/56
[E04B 1/4107] Longitudinal elements having an open profile, with the opening parallel to the concrete or masonry surface, i.e. anchoring rails -- The code designates building components that run longitudinally and possess an open-profile shape whose opening is oriented parallel to the concrete or masonry surface, primarily used as anchoring rails for attaching other structural or finishing elements.
[E04B 1/4114] Elements with sockets  E04B1/4107  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Longitudinal elements having an open profile, with the opening parallel to the concrete or masonry surface, i.e. anchoring rails -> E04B1/4107
[E04B 1/4121] with internal threads or non-adjustable captive nuts -- This subclass covers fixed building components that are supplied with internal threads or with captive nuts that cannot be adjusted, such as brackets, plates or anchors that are permanently threaded for fastening. The classification is used for constructions where the threaded element is integral to the part and not intended to be altered after installation.
[E04B 1/4128] receiving adjustable or removal nuts -- This subclass defines building elements that include a receiving device for adjustable or removable nuts, such as brackets, clamps or fastening plates used to secure structural or decorative components. It is part of the fixed-construction hierarchy under general building constructions.
[E04B 1/4135] receiving removal bolt heads  E04B1/415  takes precedence -- E04B 1/4135 pertains to fixed building constructions that incorporate means for receiving the heads of removal bolts, enabling the bolts to be extracted without harming the surrounding structure. It falls under general building constructions and is superseded by E04B 1/415.
[E04B 1/4142] with transverse hook- or loop-receiving parts  E04B1/415  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Elements with captive and extendable anchoring parts, e.g. spring-loaded bolts, hanging rings -> E04B1/415
[E04B 1/415] with captive and extendable anchoring parts, e.g. spring-loaded bolts, hanging rings -- This CPC group covers building fixed-construction elements that incorporate captive and extendable anchoring components such as spring-loaded bolts or hanging rings, used to secure parts while allowing controlled release or adjustment. It applies to general building constructions, particularly fixed constructions.
[E04B 1/4157] Longitudinally-externally threaded elements extending from the concrete or masonry, e.g. anchoring bolt with embedded head -- The subclass E04B 1/4157 covers longitudinally-externally threaded elements that protrude from concrete or masonry structures, such as anchoring bolts whose heads are embedded in the material. These components are used to secure fittings, equipment or other members to the built-in construction. It falls under Fixed Constructions → Building → General Building Constructions.
[E04B 1/4164] with an adjustment sleeve -- The CPC subclass E04B 1/4164 concerns general building constructions that incorporate an adjustment sleeve, a device enabling fine positional or dimensional tuning of fixed structural elements. It applies to fixed-construction methods where such sleeves allow alignment or expansion adjustments during assembly or service.
[E04B 1/4171] Nailable or non-threaded screwable elements  dowels  F16B13/00 -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Dowels for holding a fastener -> F16B13/00
[E04B 1/4178] Masonry wall ties  with insulation-layer locating devices  E04B1/7616 ; replacing or adding wall ties  E04G23/0222 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Separate tie rods embedded in the horizontal or vertical masonry joints, for tying a masonry wall to a non-masonry support, e.g. a concrete wall or posts, or for making a T-junction between two masonry walls.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Masonry wall ties with insulation-layer locating devices -> E04B1/7616 - Replacing or adding wall ties -> E04G23/0222
[E04B 1/4185] for cavity walls with both wall leaves made of masonry  spacers for permanent forms  E04B2/86 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Separate tie rods embedded in horizontal or vertical masonry joints, for tying two parallel masonry walls together.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Spacers for permanent forms -> E04B2/86
[E04B 1/48] Dowels, i.e. members adapted to penetrate the surfaces of two parts and to take the shear stresses -- E04B 1/48 covers dowels, which are members shaped to be driven into the adjoining surfaces of two building parts so they can transmit shear stresses between them, commonly used in general construction to secure and align structural elements.
[E04B 1/483] Shear dowels to be embedded in concrete -- E04B 1/483 pertains to shear dowels intended to be embedded in concrete within fixed building constructions. These components are used to transfer shear forces between structural elements such as walls, slabs or beams, improving load distribution and overall stability.
[E04B 1/486] Shear dowels for wood  E04B1/49  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Shear dowels with self-penetrating parts -> E04B1/49
[E04B 1/49] with self-penetrating parts, e.g. claw dowels -- This subgroup covers fixed building constructions that join components by self-penetrating devices such as claw dowels, allowing parts to interlock without separate fasteners. It is a subcategory of general building constructions within the broader fixed-construction group.
[E04B 1/541] Joints substantially without separate connecting elements, e.g. jointing by inter-engagement -- The class E04B 1/541 pertains to building joints that are formed without separate connecting elements, relying instead on inter-engagement of parts such as tongues, grooves, slots or dovetail shapes to hold components together in fixed construction.
[E04B 1/58] of bar-shaped building elements -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Connections of large longitudinal structural elements -> E04B1/21 , E04B1/2403 , E04B1/2604
[E04B 1/5806] with a cross-section having an open profile -- E04B 1/5806 covers fixed building constructions whose structural members have a cross-section with an open profile, such as channels, I-beams, or other hollow or unfilled shapes that provide strength while minimizing material use. It applies to general building elements where the open profile is a defining characteristic of the construction.
[E04B 1/5812] of substantially I - or H - form -- E04B 1/5812 refers to fixed building constructions that employ a substantially I- or H-shaped structural form, detailing the design and use of these cross-sectional shapes in general building projects.
[E04B 1/5818] of substantially U - form -- The subclass E04B 1/5818 covers fixed building elements that have a substantially U-shaped cross-section, such as channels, brackets or troughs used in general construction for supporting, guiding or enclosing other components. It is intended for structural parts whose primary function derives from the U-form geometry.
[E04B 1/5825] with a closed cross-section -- This subgroup pertains to general building constructions where structural members have a closed cross-section, such as tubular, box-type or fully enclosed profiles used for beams, columns and similar load-bearing elements.
[E04B 1/5831] of substantially rectangular form -- E04B 1/5831 covers constructions that are essentially rectangular in plan, such as houses, sheds, or other fixed building structures whose overall shape approximates a rectangle. It falls under the general building constructions category of the CPC, focusing on the geometric form rather than specific use or materials.
[E04B 1/5837] of substantially circular form -- This subclass covers building structures that have a substantially circular plan or shape, such as round houses, circular halls, domes and other forms where the footprint is primarily circular. It falls under general building constructions within the fixed constructions group.
[E04B 1/5843] with ends provided with protuberances -- E04B 1/5843 covers fixed building constructions in which the terminal parts are equipped with protuberances, such as studs, brackets or other projecting elements that aid attachment, alignment or reinforcement of the structure. This sub-group falls under general building constructions for fixed components.
[E04B 1/585] with separate connection devices -- E04B 1/585 covers fixed building constructions where the structural parts are joined by distinct connecting elements such as bolts, brackets, clips or similar devices that are separate from the main members. It includes designs that use separate hardware to link components, typical of modular or prefabricated building elements.
[E04B 1/61] of slab-shaped building elements with each other -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Connections for structural concrete panels -> E04B1/043 - Connections of sheet metal panels -> F16B5/00 - Connections for furniture -> F16B12/00
[E04B 1/6104] the overlapping ends of the slabs connected together -- The code E04B 1/6104 covers construction techniques in which the ends of building slabs are overlapped and joined to form a continuous surface, providing structural continuity and load distribution. It relates to general building constructions where fixed slab elements are connected by overlapping joints.
[E04B 1/6108] the frontal surfaces of the slabs connected together -- E04B 1/6108 deals with the design and treatment of the visible front faces of building slabs that are joined to form a continuous surface. It covers methods of connecting slab fronts, detailing joint geometry, sealing and surface finishing to achieve a uniform appearance and structural integrity.
[E04B 1/6112] by clamping, e.g. friction, means on lateral surfaces -- E04B 1/6112 classifies building elements that are joined or held in place by clamping devices rather than by adhesives, screws or welding. The clamping action, often based on friction, is applied to the lateral surfaces of the components to secure them within fixed constructions.
[E04B 1/6116] by locking means on lateral surfaces -- The subclass E04B 1/6116 covers fixed building constructions in which locking devices are integrated into the side or lateral surfaces of components to secure them together. It pertains to methods and structures that use such lateral locking means to achieve stability and alignment in general building assemblies.
[E04B 1/612] by means between frontal surfaces -- This subclass deals with methods or structures where the connection between building elements is achieved by inserting a component between their front faces, such as panels, spacers, membranes, or fillers that bond or hold the surfaces together. It is used in general building construction for fixed structures.
[E04B 1/6125] with protrusions on the one frontal surface co-operating with recesses in the other frontal surface -- This subclass covers building components whose front faces feature matching protrusions on one side and recesses on the opposite side, allowing the parts to interlock or register with each other in fixed construction assemblies. It is used for describing panels, blocks or other units that achieve alignment and stability through complementary surface features.
[E04B 1/6129] the connection made by expansion -- E04B 1/6129 covers methods and structures where two or more fixed building elements are joined by means of an expanding material or device. The connection is formed as the filler material swells, hardens or inflates, creating a permanent bond that accommodates tolerances and provides sealing or load-transfer functions in general building constructions.
[E04B 1/6133] the connection made by friction-grip -- The entry E04B 1/6133 refers to building connections that rely on friction-grip rather than bolts, screws, welds or adhesives. It covers methods where components are pressed together so that friction alone holds them in place, typically used in fixed constructions such as prefabricated panels, claddings or structural members within general building structures.
[E04B 1/6137] the connection made by formlocking -- A form-locking connection refers to a method of joining building elements where the components interlock by shape rather than by mechanical fasteners, creating a permanent bond within fixed constructions. This entry classifies such connections used in general building structures, covering the specific geometry and techniques that achieve the interlocking effect.
[E04B 1/6141] the connection made by an additional locking key -- The entry covers building connections that are secured by an extra locking key, such as a supplementary key that engages a latch or bolt to reinforce the joint. It pertains to mechanisms where the additional key provides a secondary lock for doors, panels or other structural elements, enhancing safety or access control.
[E04B 1/6145] with recesses in both frontal surfaces co-operating with an additional connecting element -- The CPC entry E04B 1/6145 falls under Fixed Constructions - Building - General Building Constructions and covers building elements that have recesses on both of their front faces designed to interlock with an additional connecting component. The design enables a secure, flush joint by allowing the two recessed surfaces to receive and lock onto a separate element, improving stability and alignment in structures such as walls, panels or façade modules.
[E04B 1/615] the connection made by expansion -- The entry covers methods for joining building components by means of expansion, such as inserting a wedge, screw or hydraulic device that enlarges to lock the parts together. It applies to fixed constructions like walls, floors or roofs where the connection is achieved by expanding a member to create a secure joint.
[E04B 1/6154] the connection made by friction-grip -- E04B 1/6154 covers building connections that rely on friction-grip, where components are held together by clamping or interlocking forces rather than fasteners, adhesives or welding. It includes methods such as pressure-fitted joints, friction-locked panels, and similar techniques used in fixed constructions for structural or architectural purposes.
[E04B 1/6158] the connection made by formlocking -- The entry covers building constructions in which components are joined by form-locking connections, such as interlocking, tongue-and-groove, snap-fit or similar mechanical joints that lock parts together without separate fasteners, typically used in timber, metal or composite building elements.
[E04B 1/6162] the connection made by an additional locking key -- E04B 1/6162 covers building connections that employ an additional locking key to secure components together, providing extra resistance against opening or displacement. This is applied to assemblies such as doors, windows or prefabricated panels where a secondary key enhances the strength and reliability of the joint.
[E04B 1/6166] with protrusions on both frontal surfaces -- This subclass covers building elements that have outward projections on the front side of both opposite faces, such as double-sided panels, cladding or structural members with symmetrical protrusions intended for aesthetic or functional reasons.
[E04B 1/617] with one protrusion on each frontal surface -- A subclass of fixed building constructions in which the element has a single protruding feature on each frontal surface, such as a rib, fin or ledge extending outward from the front face of the structure. This applies to any general building component where exactly one projection appears on each front side.
[E04B 1/6175] with two or more protrusions on each frontal surface -- E04B 1/6175 covers building elements whose front face is shaped with two or more protruding parts, such as a series of columns, pilasters or decorative ribs that project from the surface. The classification applies to any construction where each frontal surface features multiple such protrusions, typically for structural, functional or aesthetic purposes.
[E04B 1/6179] with protrusions and recesses on each frontal surface -- This CPC subclass deals with fixed building constructions whose frontal surfaces are formed with alternating protrusions and recesses across the entire face, such as corrugated, ribbed or patterned panels used for structural, decorative or functional reasons. It falls under E04B 1/6179 within the hierarchy of general building constructions.
[E04B 1/6183] with rotatable locking means co-operating with a recess -- This subclass covers fixed building elements such as doors, windows, shutters or panels that use a rotatable locking device which engages a recess in the surrounding frame or structure to secure the component in place. It pertains to constructions where the lock turns into a cavity to achieve a locked condition.
[E04B 1/6187] by means on top and/or bottom surfaces of the slabs -- A subclass of building constructions that concerns methods applied to the upper and/or lower faces of building slabs, such as treatments, coverings, or protective layers placed directly on those surfaces to enhance performance, durability, or functionality. This includes processes like surface coating, reinforcement, insulation, or finishing applied after the slab is formed. It falls under the broader category of fixed constructions for general building works.
[E04B 1/62] Insulation or other protection -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Insulation or other protection for buildings in general; Elements or use of specified material for insulation of buildings or other protection of buildings.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Insulation of partition walls -> E04B2/00 - Insulation of floor structures -> E04B5/00 - Insulation of roofs -> E04B7/00 , E04D13/16 - Insulation of suspended ceilings -> E04B9/00 - Chemical compositions -> C01 - C11 - Insulation of floorings, e.g. floating floors -> E04F15/18 - Implements for applying insulation or sealings -> E04F21/00 - Buildings to withstand or to provide protection against external undesired influences -> E04H9/00 - Sealing pipes in walls or partitions -> F16L5/02 - Shielding against dangerous radiation -> G21F
[E04B 1/625] Sheets or foils allowing passage of water vapor but impervious to liquid water; house wraps -- It covers building materials such as house-wrap membranes that are breathable to water vapor while preventing liquid water intrusion, used in exterior walls, foundations and roofing to control moisture and improve energy efficiency. These sheets or foils are typically polymer-based and may be laminated to fabrics for enhanced strength.

[E04B 1/681] for free moving parts -- The CPC subclass E04B 1/681 covers building elements that incorporate free-moving components, such as doors, windows, shutters, movable roofs, panels or other parts that can be shifted, rotated or opened without the need for external power. This subclass falls under general building constructions dealing with fixed constructions that contain movable sections.

[E04B 1/7038] Evacuating water from cavity walls, e.g. by using weep holes -- E04B 1/7038 covers techniques for draining moisture from cavity walls in building construction, primarily by incorporating weep holes or similar openings that allow water to escape from the cavity to the exterior. It is used to prevent water accumulation and related damage in fixed building structures.
[E04B 1/7046] using trays -- E04B 1/7046 covers the use of trays in fixed building constructions, describing how trays are employed for handling, storing, or arranging materials, components or products within structures such as walls, floors or ceilings. [E04B 1/7053] Grills for weep holes -- A classification for fixed building elements that covers grills installed over weep holes in walls, foundations or other structures to let moisture escape while keeping out debris, insects and rodents. The entry applies to any material or design used to protect these ventilation openings in general building construction.
[E04B 1/7061] Devices for preventing blockage of weep holes, e.g. a blockage caused by falling mortar -- The code E04B 1/7061 covers devices that keep weep holes in building walls from becoming obstructed, usually by mortar or other construction debris. These solutions ensure the intended drainage openings stay clear, allowing moisture to escape from masonry or concrete structures.

[E04B 1/7612] in combination with an air space -- This subgroup covers building elements that are formed together with an air space, such as cavity walls, double-skin façades or insulated panels where an enclosed air gap is used for thermal, acoustic or moisture control. [E04B 1/7616] with insulation-layer locating devices combined with wall ties -- The code refers to general building constructions that incorporate devices which locate an insulation layer while simultaneously serving as wall ties. [E04B 1/762] Exterior insulation of exterior walls -- The CPC entry E04B 1/762 deals with exterior insulation applied to the outside surface of building walls, covering methods, materials and constructions that add a thermal barrier to the exterior side of walls to enhance energy efficiency and moisture protection. It is classified under Fixed Constructions → Building → General Building Constructions.
[E04B 1/7625] Details of the adhesive connection of the insulation to the wall -- The code E04B 1/7625 covers methods and features for bonding thermal-insulation panels or layers to walls with adhesives, including surface preparation, adhesive selection, application techniques, and joint design to secure adhesion and meet fire-performance requirements.

[E04B 1/7633] Dowels with enlarged insulation retaining head -- E04B 1/7633 refers to dowels that feature an enlarged head designed to retain insulation within fixed building constructions. [E04B 2001/0053] Buildings characterised by their shape or layout grid -- The CPC subclass E04B 2001/0053 covers buildings whose overall form or internal arrangement is defined by a grid pattern, including modular, prefabricated or structurally repetitive layouts. It includes designs where the spatial organization follows orthogonal or regular grid lines, influencing the building's shape, circulation and functional zones.
[E04B 2001/0061] Buildings with substantially curved horizontal cross-section, e.g. circular -- E04B 2001/0061 pertains to buildings whose horizontal cross-section is substantially curved, such as circular, oval or other rounded floor plans. The code is used to classify patents for structures like rotundas, arenas, domed halls and similar constructions with a curved layout.
[E04B 2001/0069] Prismatic shaped buildings with substantially triangular vertical cross-section -- E04B 2001/0069 relates to architectural constructions whose overall form is prismatic and whose vertical cross-section is substantially triangular, covering designs such as triangular towers or buildings with a three-sided profile.
[E04B 2001/0076] Buildings with specific right-angled horizontal layout grid -- The code applies to buildings whose floor plans are organized on a regular orthogonal grid, with rooms, structural elements and circulation aligned to a right-angled horizontal layout. It is used for design and construction documentation that specifies such grid systems, common in many office, residential and industrial structures.
[E04B 2001/0084] Buildings with non right-angled horizontal layout grid, e.g. triangular or hexagonal -- E04B 2001/0084 covers building designs whose floor-plan grid is not based on right angles, including triangular, hexagonal or other polygonal arrangements. The classification applies to patents dealing with structural, architectural or construction solutions that exploit these non-orthogonal horizontal layouts. It helps identify innovations in alternative building geometries.
[E04B 2001/0092] Small buildings with hexagonal or similar horizontal cross-section -- The code E04B 2001/0092 covers small structures such as houses, cabins or auxiliary buildings whose floor plan has a hexagonal shape or a comparable polygonal layout. It applies to designs where the horizontal cross-section is defined by six or a similar number of sides, often for aesthetic, functional or space-efficiency reasons.
[E04B 2001/1915] with strut engaging means at the edges of the cube -- This CPC subgroup covers building components in which the edges of a cubic module are equipped with devices that engage or receive struts, allowing the cube to be connected to a structural framework or adjacent modules.
[E04B 2001/1918] with connecting nodes having flat radial connecting surfaces -- The CPC subclass E04B 2001/1918 pertains to building components that are joined by connecting nodes whose surfaces are flat and oriented radially, allowing the adjoining parts to be fastened by bolts, screws or adhesives. It is used for prefabricated structural elements such as modular walls, roof sections or timber frames where the radial flat surfaces facilitate reliable, repeatable connections.
[E04B 2001/1921] with connecting nodes having radial connecting stubs -- This CPC subclass covers roof structures whose junctions or connecting nodes are formed with radial stubs extending outward, providing attachment points for beams, purlins or other members. The radial stubs simplify modular assembly and allow load transfer in metal or composite roof frames.
[E04B 2001/1924] Struts specially adapted therefor -- The CPC subclass E04B 2001/1924 covers struts that are specially adapted for use in roofs, walls or other building components, including pre-stressed or adjustable support members designed to bear loads or maintain spacing. It groups inventions that provide particular shapes, attachment means or material treatments to enhance structural performance.
[E04B 2001/1927] of essentially circular cross section -- E04B 2001/1927 classifies patents dealing with foundations, footings, piles or other supporting structures that have an essentially circular cross-section, covering their design, manufacturing, installation and related engineering features. This subgroup refines the broader E04B 2001 category for circular foundations to capture specific technical solutions involving such shapes.
[E04B 2001/193] with flattened connecting parts, e.g. ends -- This CPC subgroup covers building components where the joining elements are flattened, for example the ends of beams or panels that are shaped to provide a flat surface for easier assembly.
[E04B 2001/1933] of polygonal, e.g. square, cross section -- The CPC entry E04B 2001/1933 classifies foundations, footings, shafts or retaining structures whose cross-section is polygonal, such as square or other multi-sided shapes. It falls under the broader group for masonry and building wall elements.
[E04B 2001/1936] Winged profiles, e.g. with a L-, T-, U- or X-shaped cross section -- Winged profiles are structural or decorative elements whose cross-section resembles a letter such as L, T, U or X, often used to increase stiffness, improve attachment or add aesthetic features in construction, machinery or transport components. The classification covers their design, manufacture and application.
[E04B 2001/1939] Inflatable struts -- Inflatable struts are temporary, air-filled support elements used in foundation and underground construction to brace walls, excavations or retaining structures. By expanding under pressure they provide vertical or lateral support and can be easily installed, adjusted or removed, offering a lightweight alternative to rigid steel or concrete props.
[E04B 2001/1942] Struts adjustable in length -- The CPC entry E04B 2001/1942 covers struts whose length can be varied, typically by telescoping or sliding mechanisms, for use in scaffolding, bracing or other temporary support structures. It falls within the construction-related class E04B, which deals with solid or skeletal structures and their adjustable components.
[E04B 2001/1945] Wooden struts -- Covers the use of wooden members as bracing or support elements in foundation or subsoil works, including piles, retaining walls and other earth-supporting structures. The classification applies to inventions that employ timber struts to reinforce or stabilize soil or structural loads.
[E04B 2001/1948] Concrete struts -- E04B 2001/1948 covers concrete struts, which are vertical or inclined reinforced concrete members used in retaining-wall systems, bridge abutments and other civil-engineering structures to resist lateral earth or water pressures. The classification includes design, fabrication, reinforcement detailing and installation of these load-bearing elements.
[E04B 2001/1951] uninterrupted struts situated in the outer planes of the framework -- This subclass covers building frames where the longitudinal members (struts) run continuously along the exterior planes of the structural grid without interruption, forming the outer envelope of the construction and acting as the primary load-bearing elements. It applies to both masonry and metal frame buildings where the outer struts are not broken by openings or secondary members.
[E04B 2001/1954] uninterrupted struts connecting alternately with the outer planes of the framework, e.g. zig-zagging struts -- This subclass covers continuous struts that link successive outer faces of a supporting framework in an alternating fashion, forming a zig-zag pattern without breaks. The design provides a stable, lightweight lattice for load distribution in construction or mechanical assemblies.
[E04B 2001/1957] Details of connections between nodes and struts -- E04B 2001/1957 covers inventions that describe how the nodes (junction points) and struts (structural members) of a modular or prefabricated building frame are joined together. It includes specific connection devices, assembly methods, joint geometries, fastening or welding techniques, and any features that improve rigidity, adjustability or ease of erection of the frame.
[E04B 2001/196] Screw connections with axis parallel to the main axis of the strut -- This subclass covers fastening methods where a screw is inserted so that its longitudinal axis runs parallel to the longitudinal axis of a supporting strut, typical in structural or mechanical assemblies. It includes designs where the screw engages the strut along its length rather than across it, providing axial load transfer and alignment.
[E04B 2001/1963] Screw connections with axis at an angle, e.g. perpendicular, to the main axis of the strut -- The CPC subclass E04B 2001/1963 covers screw connections whose screw axis is set at an angle-often perpendicular-to the main axis of a supporting strut, describing fasteners that join structural members by inserting the screw sideways or at a defined inclination. It is used for designs where the connection is not collinear with the strut's longitudinal direction.
[E04B 2001/1966] Formlocking connections other than screw connections -- E04B 2001/1966 covers form-locking connections used in doors, windows, skylights and similar building components where the parts interlock by their shape rather than by screws, such as tongue-and-groove, snap-fit or other mechanically shaped joints that secure the elements without threaded fasteners.
[E04B 2001/1969] Ball and socket type connection -- The CPC subclass E04B 2001/1969 covers ball-and-socket type connections used in doors, windows, shutters, gates and related building components. It includes designs where a spherical protuberance mates with a socket to provide a pivoting, detachable joint that accommodates movement while maintaining alignment of the assembled parts. This classification is applied to mechanical fastening arrangements that enable easy assembly, adjustment, or sealing of structural openings.
[E04B 2001/1972] Welded or glued connection -- The E04B 2001/1972 entry classifies methods for forming welded or glued connections in construction, especially for foundations, footings and underground structures. It includes the techniques, materials and processes used to join structural elements by welding or adhesive bonding to achieve permanent, load-bearing joints.
[E04B 2001/1975] Frameworks where the struts are directly connected to each other, i.e. without interposed connecting nodes or plates -- The code E04B 2001/1975 defines frameworks in which the load-bearing members are joined directly to each other without any intermediate nodes, plates or connectors. It covers structural systems such as trusses, space frames or lattice girders where struts are welded, bolted or otherwise fastened edge-to-edge, enabling a continuous load path throughout the framework. This classification is used for inventions relating to the design, analysis or construction of such directly connected structural assemblies.
[E04B 2001/1978] Frameworks assembled from preformed subframes, e.g. pyramids -- The code E04B 2001/1978 covers structural frameworks that are built by joining pre-manufactured subframes, such as modular pyramid or lattice assemblies, to create larger supports or enclosures. It applies to construction methods where the individual elements are fabricated off-site and then connected on-site to form the final framework, common in temporary works, exhibition stands, and certain types of lightweight building structures.
[E04B 2001/1981] characterised by the grid type of the outer planes of the framework -- The subgroup E04B 2001/1981 covers building frames whose external faces are formed as a grid, meaning the outer planes consist of intersecting longitudinal and transverse members that create a lattice-like surface. It applies to structures where this grid arrangement defines the exterior architecture or load-bearing skin of the framework.
[E04B 2001/1984] rectangular, e.g. square, grid -- The entry classifies roof-covering systems whose elements are laid out in a rectangular or square lattice, with tiles, slates or panels arranged in straight rows and columns forming a regular grid rather than a staggered pattern. It is used to identify designs that employ this straightforward, orthogonal layout for aesthetic or functional purposes.
[E04B 2001/1987] triangular grid -- E04B 2001/1987 concerns the use of a triangular grid as a structural system in buildings. The grid consists of intersecting members that form a pattern of triangles, providing stiffness and efficient load distribution for roofs, floors or façades. This arrangement is applied in prefabricated or modular construction to reduce material and improve assembly speed.
[E04B 2001/199] Details of roofs, floors or walls supported by the framework -- This subclass covers inventions related to the design, construction or reinforcement of roofs, floors or walls that are attached to a supporting framework, including joints, connections, anchoring and other structural details that ensure stability and load distribution. It includes features such as brackets, fasteners, sealing and integration of these elements within the building's supporting skeleton.
[E04B 2001/1993] Details of framework supporting structure, e.g. posts or walls -- The CPC subclass E04B 2001/1993 falls under "Construction of buildings" and covers details of framework-supporting structures such as posts, walls, or similar elements. It includes design, connection and reinforcement features that provide structural support for a building's load-bearing framework.
[E04B 2001/1996] Tensile-integrity structures, i.e. structures comprising compression struts connected through flexible tension members, e.g. cables -- E04B 2001/1996 relates to tensile-integrity structures, also known as tensegrity systems, in which discrete compression struts are held together by flexible tension members such as cables. The entry covers the design, analysis and construction of such lightweight frameworks used in roofs, bridges, towers and architectural or artistic installations.
[E04B 2001/2406] Connection nodes -- E04B 2001/2406 covers connection nodes used in building structures. It relates to the design of elements that join beams, columns or panels, providing mechanical linkage and load-transfer in prefabricated or assembled constructions. The code is applied to patents describing standardized junctions that facilitate rapid assembly and structural integrity.
[E04B 2001/2409] Hooks, dovetails or other interlocking connections -- This subclass groups interlocking joint designs such as hooks, dovetail joints, tongues-and-slots and similar shape-matched connections that enable building components like panels, frames or structural members to be assembled without separate fasteners.
[E04B 2001/2412] Keyhole connections -- E04B 2001/2412 covers the use of keyhole-shaped openings that allow fastener or pin insertion for joining doors, windows, shutters or similar building components. The classification includes designs where the hole has a larger circular head leading to a narrower stem, enabling easy assembly and disassembly while maintaining structural integrity.
[E04B 2001/2415] Brackets, gussets, joining plates -- E04B 2001/2415 relates to brackets, gussets and joining plates employed to connect structural members in foundations and other substructures. It includes metal or composite components that transfer loads between beams, columns, piles or retaining walls, enhancing stability and load distribution.
[E04B 2001/2418] Details of bolting -- E04B 2001/2418 covers the specific technical aspects of bolting used in building construction, including the design, dimensions, materials, installation methods and anchoring of bolts, nuts, screws and related fasteners that secure structural elements such as walls, floors, roofs or foundations.
[E04B 2001/2421] Socket type connectors -- This subgroup covers socket-type connectors designed to receive plug contacts and provide a mechanical and electrical link, typically used in electrical installation and wiring systems. It includes various forms such as wall outlets, power strips, and modular sockets.
[E04B 2001/2424] Clamping connections other than bolting or riveting -- E04B 2001/2424 covers methods and devices for joining building components by clamping rather than by bolts or rivets, including the use of straps, sleeves, clamps, friction, or adhesive pressure to hold parts together. It applies to construction of walls, roofs, floors, and other structural elements where a non-permanent or removable fastening is required.
[E04B 2001/2427] using adhesives or hardening masses -- E04B 2001/2427 covers the use of adhesives, binders or hardening masses to fix roof covering elements such as sheets, panels or tiles to a supporting structure. It applies to methods and compositions for bonding roofing materials during installation and is part of the CPC roof category E04B dealing with fixing means.
[E04B 2001/243] Assembling by non resilient deformation other than riveting -- This group covers techniques for joining building elements where the parts are permanently deformed in a non-elastic way rather than being fastened with rivets. It includes press-fit, crimping, clipping, bending or other deformation methods that create a fixed connection without elastic return.
[E04B 2001/2433] using a removable key -- E04B 2001/2433 covers building elements that are operated with a removable key, such as doors, windows, gates or other access points where a detachable key is used to engage or disengage a locking mechanism. It pertains to the method of using the key for securing or releasing the component.
[E04B 2001/2436] Snap-fit connections -- The CPC subclass E04B 2001/2436 covers snap-fit connections used in building and construction components. It includes devices where parts are joined by elastic deformation that creates a temporary hold, allowing assembly without separate fasteners and permitting disassembly by releasing the stored strain.
[E04B 2001/2439] Adjustable connections, e.g. using elongated slots or threaded adjustment elements -- The code covers building components such as doors, windows, panels or other structural parts whose connections can be varied after installation, typically by using elongated slots, bolts, screws or other threaded adjustment elements to fine-tune alignment, fit or positioning.
[E04B 2001/2442] Connections with built-in weakness points -- The CPC subgroup E04B 2001/2442 covers building connections that incorporate built-in weakness points intended to facilitate easy disengagement, such as breakaway joints, snap-fit fasteners, or clips that release under predetermined loads. It applies to structural or decorative links where a controlled failure mode is part of the design, enabling rapid disassembly, replacement, or safety release.
[E04B 2001/2445] Load-supporting elements with reinforcement at the connection point other than the connector -- Load-supporting elements such as beams, lintels, arches or similar structural members that incorporate reinforcement at the point where they join other parts, with the strengthening being integral to the element rather than a separate connector, are covered by this subclass. It addresses designs that improve load transfer by reinforcing the connection zone of the load-bearing component.
[E04B 2001/2448] Connections between open section profiles -- E04B 2001/2448 covers the joining of open-section structural members such as channels, I-beams or Z-profiles. It includes fastening, welding, bolting, riveting or adhesive methods that connect these profiles in building frameworks, bridges or other civil-engineering structures. The classification addresses the design and implementation of the connection details.
[E04B 2001/2451] Connections between closed section profiles -- This CPC subgroup deals with methods, devices and means for joining closed-section profiles such as tubular, square or rectangular metal or plastic sections used in building construction, furniture or transport. It covers welded, riveted, bolted, adhesive, mechanical-interlock or snap-fit connections that link these hollow sections.
[E04B 2001/2454] Connections between open and closed section profiles -- The subclass E04B 2001/2454 concerns methods and devices that join an open section profile-such as a channel or angle lacking a continuous wall-to a closed section profile, which encloses a space. It includes mechanical, welded, riveted or adhesive connections used in building structures, frames and fixtures where the two profile types interface.
[E04B 2001/2457] Beam to beam connections -- E04B 2001/2457 covers the design, manufacture and implementation of connections between two structural beams, including bolted, riveted, welded or mechanically fastened joints used in metal building frames and similar constructions. It addresses the geometry, reinforcement and load-transfer characteristics of beam-to-beam junctions.
[E04B 2001/246] Post to post connections -- The class groups devices, fittings and methods for joining two adjacent vertical members such as scaffolding, poles or fence posts. It includes brackets, clamps, bolts, pins, welds or other coupling elements that provide a fixed or adjustable post-to-post connection, enabling the construction of stable frame structures.
[E04B 2001/2463] Connections to foundations -- The CPC entry E04B 2001/2463, titled Connections to foundations, relates to the design and implementation of structural links that transfer loads from superstructures to the underlying foundation. It covers anchoring devices, bolts, plates, brackets and methods of fixing or reinforcing the interface between foundations and built elements.
[E04B 2001/2466] Details of the elongated load-supporting parts -- The CPC subclass E04B 2001/2466 covers the specific features of elongated load-supporting components such as beams, joists, girders and similar structural members used in building construction. It includes details of their shape, reinforcement, connections, material composition, surface treatment and other design characteristics that affect load-bearing performance. This subclass is used to classify patents that focus on the technical aspects of these long structural elements.
[E04B 2001/2469] Profile with an array of connection holes -- E04B 2001/2469 pertains to building construction elements, specifically profiles that incorporate a systematic arrangement of holes for joining or fastening to other components. The holes are designed to accommodate connectors, fasteners or fixtures in prefabricated walls, ceilings or structural panels. This subclass addresses the geometry and positioning of the hole array within the profile.
[E04B 2001/2472] Elongated load-supporting part formed from a number of parallel profiles -- The code pertains to building components made by joining several parallel structural profiles to create an elongated part that carries loads, such as beams, lintels or similar load-supporting elements formed from wood, metal or composite sections.
[E04B 2001/2475] Profile with an undercut grooves for connection purposes -- A profile shaped for building openings that includes undercut grooves to enable a secure, interlocking connection with adjoining parts. The grooves are cut deeper than the surrounding surface so that a complementary element can be fitted and held in place without additional fasteners, commonly used in doors, windows or shutters for precise alignment and resistance to loosening.
[E04B 2001/2478] Profile filled with concrete -- E04B 2001/2478 covers foundation elements in which a structural profile-such as a steel or composite tube, pile or form-is filled with concrete to create a solid, load-bearing member, typically used for piles, caissons or other deep-foundation applications. The classification applies to the method of concrete filling, the resulting composite profile, and related construction or reinforcement techniques.
[E04B 2001/2481] Details of wall panels -- E04B 2001/2481 covers the detailed features of wall panels employed in building construction, including material composition, fastening and joining methods, insulation, surface treatments and modular connection arrangements. It is a sub-group of CPC class E04B, which pertains to prefabricated building elements.
[E04B 2001/2484] Details of floor panels or slabs -- E04B 2001/2484 classifies inventions that relate to the specific design and construction features of floor panels or slabs, including their shape, material composition, connection methods, fastening elements and installation techniques used in building floors. This subclass covers detailed technical solutions for assembling, supporting and finishing floor units in residential, commercial or industrial structures.
[E04B 2001/2487] Portico type structures -- Portico type structures are roofed, column-supported entranceways or walkways that project from or are attached to a building, providing sheltered access. They may be freestanding or integrated into the façade and are used in residential, commercial and public architecture.
[E04B 2001/249] Structures with a sloping roof -- The CPC subclass E04B 2001/249 pertains to buildings or other structures whose roof is sloped rather than flat, encompassing single-, double-, or multi-pitched roof forms and their supporting framework, covering materials, drainage and related roof-edge elements. It includes the design, construction and integration of such roofs with the overall structure.
[E04B 2001/2493] Structures with a vaulted roof -- E04B 2001/2493 covers inventions relating to the design, construction or reinforcement of vaulted roofs, including the geometry, support systems and materials used to create arched or dome-like coverings for buildings. The classification is applied to patents describing methods for building, repairing or improving such roof structures, as well as the integration of lighting, ventilation or insulation within the vault.
[E04B 2001/2496] Shear bracing therefor -- Shear bracing therefor refers to the provision of diagonal or vertical elements that resist lateral shear forces in building structures, such as walls, frames or panels, enhancing stability against wind or seismic loads. The classification covers designs, configurations and methods for installing these bracing components within the overall construction.
[E04B 2001/2616] Hinged connections of wooden members -- E04B 2001/2616 classifies hinged connections between wooden members, covering joints that allow limited angular movement for timber frames, floors, walls or roof structures. The entry includes devices such as pins, bolts, and specialised hinges that enable rotation while maintaining structural integrity. It is used to identify patents dealing with flexible wooden joints in building construction.
[E04B 2001/262] Connection node with interlocking of specially shaped wooden members, e.g. puzzle type connection -- A connection node that joins specially shaped wooden members by interlocking them like puzzle pieces, creating a secure joint without additional fasteners. This type of node is used in furniture, building components or modular structures where the members fit together precisely to transmit loads and maintain alignment.
[E04B 2001/2624] with dovetail-type connections -- E04B 2001/2624 belongs to the CPC section for building elements and refers to doors, windows, shutters, panels or similar components that are joined by dovetail-type connections, where interlocking flanges or tongues fit together to provide a secure, often removable, mechanical fastening without the need for additional fasteners. This subclass captures designs that rely on the dovetail principle for alignment and strength.
[E04B 2001/2628] Interlocking connectors, e.g. with hooks or dovetails, added to the elongated wooden members -- The CPC subgroup E04B 2001/2628 concerns interlocking connectors such as hooks, dovetail joints or similar devices that are integrated into or attached to elongated wooden members like beams, joists or posts to provide a mechanical link between them without separate fasteners. It includes designs where the connectors are formed as part of the wood or mounted on it to achieve a secure, reusable joint.
[E04B 2001/2632] with dovetail-type connections -- E04B 2001/2632 relates to building elements such as doors, windows or panels that are joined by dovetail-type connections, where interlocking, tapered teeth provide mechanical strength without additional fasteners. The subclass covers structures in which the dovetail joint is the primary means of fixing.
[E04B 2001/2636] with connectors located in slots of the wooden members -- The CPC subclass E04B 2001/2636 covers building structures in which wooden components are joined by connectors that are positioned in slots cut into the wood members. It relates to methods and devices that embed fasteners, brackets or plates within pre-formed longitudinal or transverse grooves, allowing secure assembly while keeping the exterior surface smooth. This subclass is used for patents describing slot-mounted wooden joinery in timber construction.
[E04B 2001/264] Glued connections -- E04B 2001/264 covers building components in which parts are joined by adhesives rather than mechanical fasteners. It includes walls, panels, or structural members that are bonded using glue, resin, or similar binding agents to achieve a permanent connection.
[E04B 2001/2644] Brackets, gussets or joining plates -- E04B 2001/2644 relates to brackets, gussets or joining plates used in building construction to connect, reinforce or stiffen structural members such as beams, columns, walls or roof components. These items are typically metal or composite elements that provide additional strength, distribute loads or facilitate assembly of timber-or-metal frames.
[E04B 2001/2648] located in slots of the elongated wooden members -- The entry refers to components that are positioned inside the longitudinal slots cut in elongated wooden members such as beams, joists or studs. It covers inserts, connectors or other fittings that occupy those slots to provide support, alignment, or to house utilities within the wooden framework of a building.
[E04B 2001/2652] Details of nailing, screwing, or bolting -- The CPC subclass E04B 2001/2652 classifies inventions that describe the details of fastening building elements by nailing, screwing or bolting. It covers the design of fasteners, attachment methods, and related mechanisms used in walls, partitions and other structural components. This subclass is applied to patents concerning how such connections are made, adjusted or improved.
[E04B 2001/2656] Bent or curved bolts -- E04B 2001/2656 covers bolts that are intentionally bent or curved for use in building foundations, footings and other sub-structures. The classification applies to fasteners designed to provide anchorage in concrete, masonry or similar materials where a straight bolt cannot be accommodated, allowing connection of structural elements around irregular geometry.
[E04B 2001/266] Socket type connectors -- E04B 2001/266 is a CPC sub-group that classifies socket-type connectors used in window, door, or skylight assemblies. It covers devices that provide detachable electrical or mechanical connections between frame components, glazing, or ancillary parts, enabling easy assembly, replacement, or integration of control or sealing elements.
[E04B 2001/2664] using a removable key -- E04B 2001/2664 relates to roofing or covering structures where a removable key is employed to secure, adjust or lock components such as tiles, panels or fastening devices, allowing easy assembly, maintenance or replacement without permanent fastening.
[E04B 2001/2668] for members with a round cross-section -- This CPC entry covers patents that relate to structural members whose cross-section is circular, such as round columns, poles, or tubes used in foundations or supporting structures. It applies to designs, manufacturing methods, or uses of such rounded members in construction.
