[E04B 2001/2672] for members formed from a number of parallel sections -- This CPC subclass covers foundation members that are built by assembling a number of parallel structural sections, such as slabs or beams placed side-by-side to form a larger component. It is applied to patents describing foundations or ground beams formed from multiple parallel sections.
[E04B 2001/2676] Connector nodes -- Connector nodes are structural fittings that link individual building components such as beams, columns, or panels, allowing the transfer of loads and the alignment of elements within a framework. They are typically prefabricated elements like brackets, plates or joint modules that enable rapid, modular assembly and provide mechanical stability for the overall structure.
[E04B 2001/268] Connection to foundations -- The CPC subclass E04B 2001/268 covers devices, means and methods for linking a building's superstructure to its foundations, including anchoring bolts, brackets, ties, and other connection elements that transmit loads from the structure to the ground. It includes any arrangement that secures footings, piles or slabs to ensure stability and load transfer.
[E04B 2001/2684] with metal connectors -- The CPC subclass E04B 2001/2684 covers doors, windows, shutters or similar building closures that incorporate metal connectors such as hinges, brackets, latches or fasteners for attachment, reinforcement or operation. It is used for patents describing metal-linked assemblies that enable the opening, closing or securing of these building openings.
[E04B 2001/2688] self adjusting, e.g. for compensation of shrinkage -- The entry E04B 2001/2688 covers devices or methods for self-adjusting foundations or ground beams that automatically compensate for dimensional changes such as shrinkage in the supporting material. It includes mechanisms that modify load distribution or spacing to maintain structural integrity as the material contracts over time.
[E04B 2001/2692] End to end connections of elongated members along their common longitudinal axis -- The subclass defines patents that disclose ways to join two or more elongated structural members by connecting their ends so that the pieces lie on the same straight line. It covers end-to-end coupling methods, fasteners, welding, or other joining techniques that create a continuous member along a common longitudinal axis, typically in construction or mechanical assemblies.
[E04B 2001/2696] Shear bracing -- E04B 2001/2696 covers shear-bracing elements incorporated in foundations, retaining walls or earth-supporting structures to resist lateral shear forces. It includes diagonal braces, tie rods, anchors or similar devices that transfer shear loads to the surrounding soil or structural members, improving stability against sliding or overturning. The classification is used for patents describing the design, arrangement or material of such shear-bracing systems.
[E04B 2001/3217] Auxiliary supporting devices used during erection of the arched structures -- The subclass includes devices that give temporary support to arches while they are being built, such as scaffolding, falsework, tensioning or lifting mechanisms that hold the structure until it can support itself. It covers auxiliary equipment intended to adjust, stabilize or secure the arch during erection.
[E04B 2001/3223] Theorical polygonal geometry therefor -- This CPC entry is an index reference that points to the theoretical study of polygonal geometry as it applies to foundations, footings and underground structures, grouping literature that deals with the mathematical description of polygonal shapes used in the design and analysis of such earthworks.
[E04B 2001/3229] constructed using a boom -- E04B 2001/3229 covers roof, ceiling or wall structures that are erected by means of a boom, such as a crane arm or telescopic extension, allowing components to be lifted and positioned during construction. The classification applies to prefabricated or modular assemblies where the boom facilitates installation.
[E04B 2001/3235] having a grid frame -- In CPC E04B, the subclass 2001/3235 covers foundation designs that incorporate a grid frame, a lattice of intersecting beams or ribs that provides structural support and spreads loads across a footing or slab. This arrangement improves rigidity, reduces settlement and enables modular construction of foundations.
[E04B 2001/3241] Frame connection details -- E04B 2001/3241 covers the specific ways building frames are joined, including design of joints, connectors, fasteners and welding methods used to link structural members such as beams, columns and trusses. It addresses detailed solutions for achieving load transfer, rigidity and assembly efficiency in timber, metal or composite frames.
[E04B 2001/3247] Nodes -- E04B 2001/3247 designates patent documents that focus on nodes, meaning the structural connection points where members of a public-works element such as a bridge, frame, or building meet and transfer loads. The classification covers the geometry, reinforcement, anchoring and assembly methods of these junctions.
[E04B 2001/3252] Covering details -- E04B 2001/3252 falls within the CPC section for building construction, specifically for covering details of roofs, walls, or other structures. It addresses design and installation of protective layers such as membranes, cladding or waterproofing and related methods for preventing water ingress or environmental exposure.
[E04B 2001/3258] comprised entirely of a single self-supporting panel -- E04B 2001/3258 covers constructions in which the entire building or structural element is formed from a single self-supporting panel, meaning the panel bears its own load without additional framing or support structures. This classification applies to prefabricated wall, roof or floor panels that function as complete, load-bearing units in the finished building.
[E04B 2001/3264] hardened  in situ -- E04B 2001/3264 refers to building elements whose structural material hardens in place, covering cast-in-situ concrete, mortar or similar binders used for walls, floors, foundations and other components. The code addresses the preparation, placement and curing processes that give the material its load-bearing properties after it sets on the construction site.
[E04B 2001/327] comprised of a number of panels or blocs connected together forming a self-supporting structure -- E04B 2001/327 relates to modular or prefabricated building constructions in which a number of panels or blocks are assembled and interconnected to create a self-supporting structure, eliminating the need for a separate load-bearing frame. This classification covers the design and joining methods that allow the assembled units to bear loads independently.
[E04B 2001/3276] Panel connection details -- E04B 2001/3276 relates to the design and specification of connections between building panels, including methods for joining, fastening, sealing and aligning panels in walls, floors or roofs. It covers mechanical joints, interlocking systems, adhesives and detailed provisions for ensuring structural integrity and weather-tightness.
[E04B 2001/3282] Panels connected together exclusively at their corners -- This CPC class covers roof panels that are joined only at their corners, forming a continuous surface through corner-to-corner connections. It includes modular or prefabricated panels that interlock without side or edge fastening, used in roofing and cladding systems.
[E04B 2001/3288] Panel frame details, e.g. flanges of steel sheet panels -- E04B 2001/3288 covers the design and construction of panel-frame features that secure or support sheet panels, especially metal panels, in building envelopes. It includes specifications for flanges, stiffeners, mounting brackets and related detailing that enable attachment, alignment, sealing or reinforcement of steel-sheet façades.
[E04B 2001/3294] with a faceted surface -- The CPC subclass E04B 2001/3294 covers building walls whose exterior is formed by a faceted surface, meaning the wall is divided into flat, angled planes that create a geometric or ornamental pattern. This includes any construction technique, material or design where the wall's outer face is intentionally broken into distinct facets for aesthetic, functional or structural purposes.
[E04B 2001/34876] with a sloping or barrel roof -- The subclass includes roof constructions whose primary shape is inclined or cylindrical, covering designs, supporting structures and covering materials for buildings, shelters or similar enclosures that feature a sloping or barrel-type roof profile. It addresses the geometry, load-bearing elements and methods of forming such roofs.
[E04B 2001/34884] creating a living space between several units -- This CPC entry covers concepts, designs, and structures that create a shared living area situated between separate residential units, such as common corridors, atriums, courtyards or other interstitial spaces that connect the units and provide communal functions within a building.
[E04B 2001/34892] Means allowing access to the units, e.g. stairs or cantilevered gangways -- E04B 2001 covers devices that provide entry to underground or sub-surface work areas, such as stairs, ladders, or cantilevered gangways that enable personnel or equipment to reach the units. The classification is used for inventions that facilitate safe and convenient access within foundations, piles or other underground structures.
[E04B 2001/3561] adding living space above existing structures -- E04B 2001/3561 covers methods and structures for creating additional residential floor area on top of an existing building, such as converting attics, adding mezzanines, or constructing new upper levels. It includes design, reinforcement, and integration of the new space with the original roof and support systems to provide habitable living quarters.
[E04B 2001/3566] mounting masonry against a forwork -- E04B 2001/3566 concerns methods and devices for attaching masonry units directly to a formwork during construction, including mechanical fasteners, adhesives or integrated mould systems that enable the masonry to be built up against a temporary or permanent formwork.
[E04B 2001/3572] using templates or jigs to set out buildings or parts thereof -- E04B 2001/3572 covers the use of templates, jigs or similar positioning aids to lay out and mark building components on site, enabling accurate placement of structural parts during construction.
[E04B 2001/3577] prefabricating a building and moving it as a whole to the erection site -- The code covers methods and devices for constructing a building off-site as a complete unit and then transporting that whole structure to the construction site for installation, including handling, moving and positioning of the prefabricated building as a single element.
[E04B 2001/3583] using permanent tensioning means, e.g. cables or rods, to assemble or rigidify structures (not pre- or poststressing concrete), e.g. by tying them around the structure -- This classification covers methods of using permanently tensioned elements such as cables or rods to join or stiffen structural components, excluding techniques for pre- or post-stressed concrete, typically by wrapping or anchoring the tension members around the structure.
[E04B 2001/3588] using special lifting or handling devices, e.g. gantries, overhead conveying rails -- E04B 2001/3588 covers construction techniques that employ special lifting or handling devices, such as gantry cranes, overhead conveying rails or similar equipment, to move and position building components, especially heavy or bulky elements. The subclass is relevant to methods and apparatuses that facilitate the safe and efficient handling of structural parts during erection.
[E04B 2001/3594] inflatable lifting or handling devices -- E04B 2001/3594 covers inflatable lifting or handling devices such as air bags, cushions or pneumatic bladders that are filled with fluid or gas to raise, support or move heavy objects during construction, transport or maintenance. These devices provide a temporary, adaptable lifting surface.
[E04B 2001/386] Nailable or screwable inserts for foam panels -- The entry designates nailable or screwable inserts intended for attachment to foam panels used in building construction. These inserts provide secure points for fixing fixtures, fittings or decorative elements to insulated walls, ceilings or partitions, and are classified under the CPC subgroup for wall-finishing components.
[E04B 2001/389] Brackets -- E04B 2001/389 covers brackets used as supporting or fastening elements for doors, windows, shutters, panels or similar opening components in building construction. The classification pertains to devices that attach to frames, walls or structural members to hold, adjust or reinforce such openings, including adjustable, fixed or decorative brackets. It falls under the CPC section for ancillary parts of openings.
[E04B 2001/4192] attached to concrete reinforcing elements, e.g. rods or wires -- E04B 2001/4192 covers inventions for fixing or securing elements directly to concrete reinforcement such as rods or wires. The subclass includes devices, methods or accessories that attach components-like brackets, anchors or fittings-to the reinforcing material embedded in concrete structures. It is a subcategory of the building and foundations section of the CPC.
[E04B 2001/5856] using the innerside thereof -- E04B 2001/5856 covers inventions that exploit the inner side of a building component, such as a wall, roof or panel, for a functional purpose. It includes designs where the interior surface is used to provide insulation, mounting, fluid channels or other technical effects, rather than merely serving as a structural element.
[E04B 2001/5862] Angularly adjustable connections without hinge pin -- The subclass E04B 2001/5862 pertains to building-related structures in which the connection between components can be set at various angles without using a separate hinge pin, typically employing a sliding, rotating, or locking mechanism that provides angular adjustability while maintaining structural integrity. This includes adjustable joints for frames, panels or support members where the angle is changed by tightening or releasing a built-in locking feature rather than inserting a pin.
[E04B 2001/5868] Hinged connections -- E04B 2001/5868 relates to hinged connections for building components such as doors, windows, shutters and partitions. It covers designs of hinge mechanisms, mounting arrangements, swing angles, sealing and reinforcement that allow a panel to pivot relative to a frame or supporting structure.
[E04B 2001/5875] using exterior clamping plates or shells -- The CPC subgroup E04B 2001/5875 covers building-enclosure techniques that employ exterior clamping plates or shells to secure cladding, panels, windows or similar elements to a structure. It includes devices and methods where the plates or shells are applied on the outside of the building envelope to provide pressure-fit attachment without penetrating the substrate.
[E04B 2001/5881] using an undercut groove, e.g. dovetail groove -- The subclass E04B 2001/5881 covers building or construction elements that incorporate an undercut groove, such as a dovetail groove, to secure or interlock components. It pertains to methods and devices that create or utilize these recessed, taper-shaped profiles for joining, guiding or reinforcing parts within a structure. This includes designs where the groove's geometry provides a mechanical lock without additional fasteners.
[E04B 2001/5887] using connectors with sockets -- E04B relates to building construction, specifically roof coverings, and subclass 2001 addresses roofing membranes. The further subdivision 5887 covers the use of connectors with sockets to join membrane sections or attach accessories. This classification is applied to patents that describe connector or socket systems for assembling or securing roofing membranes.
[E04B 2001/5893] Puzzle type connections -- E04B 2001/5893 covers building connections that employ puzzle-type interlocking elements, where components fit together like pieces of a puzzle rather than using conventional fasteners. The classification applies to prefabricated or modular structures in which walls, floors or roof sections are joined by complementary profiles, slots or tongues that lock into place, facilitating rapid assembly and disassembly.
[E04B 2001/6191] by means on the corners of the slabs -- The subgroup refers to roof or floor constructions that employ devices placed on the corners of slab members to secure, connect or support the slabs, such as brackets, cleats, fastening or anchoring means fitted at the slab corners.
[E04B 2001/6195] the slabs being connected at an angle, e.g. forming a corner -- E04B 2001/6195 covers constructions in which building slabs are joined together at an angle to create a corner or other angular junction, such as the meeting point of two walls or floors. The classification pertains to methods and devices that enable the angled connection of prefabricated or cast concrete, masonry, or similar slab elements.
[E04B 2001/6818] Joints with swellable parts -- E04B 2001/6818 pertains to construction joints that integrate swellable components, such as water-absorbing polymers or expandable foams, which enlarge on exposure to moisture or heat to seal, buffer or insulate the joint. The classification covers methods and devices for installing these joints in roofs, walls, doors or windows to improve weatherproofing, acoustic control or thermal performance.
[E04B 2001/741] Insulation elements with markings, e.g. identification or cutting template -- The class concerns insulating building elements that incorporate printed or embossed markings to aid identification, positioning or as templates for cutting or shaping. Such markings may include reference lines, symbols or cut guides that facilitate installation or customisation of thermal or acoustic insulation.
[E04B 2001/742] Use of special materials;  Materials having special structures or shape -- The entry E04B 2001/742 designates inventions that involve the use of special-material types or materials possessing distinctive structures or shapes in roof-covering and related construction elements. It covers applications where the material's unique geometry, cellular form, or engineered configuration provides functional advantages such as improved insulation, load distribution, or weather resistance.
[E04B 2001/743] Animal products, e.g. wool, feathers
[E04B 2001/745] Vegetal products, e.g. plant stems, barks -- The CPC subclass E04B 2001/745 relates to vegetal products used in construction, landscaping or related fields, specifically plant stems, barks and similar natural materials. It covers inventions employing these organic components for structural, protective or decorative purposes, such as building panels, fences, or erosion control devices made from or incorporating raw or processed plant stems and bark.
[E04B 2001/746] Recycled materials, e.g. made of used tires, bumpers or newspapers -- E04B 2001/746 covers construction techniques that incorporate recycled materials such as ground tire rubber, reclaimed automotive bumpers or processed newspaper fibers into foundations, walls and related structural elements.
[E04B 2001/747] Corrugated materials -- E04B covers building elements for walls and partitions; the subgroup 2001/747 denotes corrugated materials such as metal, plastic or composite sheets and panels used in construction, roofing, cladding or interior partitions. It includes designs, manufacturing methods and fastening arrangements specific to the corrugated form, as well as related accessories and protective coatings.
[E04B 2001/748] Honeycomb materials -- E04B 2001/748 covers honeycomb materials, including the design, manufacture and use of honeycomb-type structures and panels made from metal, polymer, composite or other substances. It addresses honeycomb cores, sandwich panels and related components employed in construction, transport, aerospace and other engineering applications, as well as methods for producing and assembling these lightweight, high-strength elements.
[E04B 2001/7679] Means preventing cold bridging at the junction of an exterior wall with an interior wall or a floor -- This subclass covers devices or construction methods that eliminate thermal bridges where an exterior wall meets an interior wall or a floor. It includes thermal breaks, insulating inserts, specialized connection profiles, and sealing techniques that maintain continuous insulation across the junction, thereby reducing heat loss and condensation risk.
[E04B 2001/7683] Fibrous blankets or panels characterised by the orientation of the fibres -- This CPC subclass covers fibrous blankets or panels whose performance is defined by the specific orientation of the fibres. It includes inventions that arrange fibres in particular directions-such as unidirectional, cross-laminated or layered configurations-to achieve desired mechanical, thermal or acoustic properties.
[E04B 2001/7687] Crumble resistant fibrous blankets or panels using adhesives or meltable fibres -- E04B 2001/7687 classifies fibrous blankets or panels designed to resist crumbling by incorporating adhesives or melt-able fibres that bind the textile structure. The definition covers products such as insulation boards, acoustic panels or reinforcement layers where the binder is either a thermoplastic fibre that melts on heating or a chemical adhesive applied during manufacture. The entry falls under the CPC subgroup for building elements involving fibre composites.
[E04B 2001/7691] Heat reflecting layers or coatings -- E04B 2001/7691 pertains to heat-reflecting layers or coatings applied to building components such as roofs, facades, or windows to reduce solar heat gain. The classification includes materials, structures and processes that achieve thermal reflection for energy efficiency in construction.
[E04B 2001/7695] Panels with adjustable width -- E04B 2001/7695 covers building panels whose width can be varied after manufacture, typically by sliding, telescoping or folding mechanisms. The classification includes adjustable-width wall, ceiling, floor or façade panels used to modify openings, control light, ventilation or acoustics, and related devices that lock or secure the panels at the selected width.
[E04B 2001/8245] Machines for manufacturing, shaping, piercing or filling sound insulating elements -- This CPC subgroup covers machines that produce or treat acoustic insulation components for building applications. It includes equipment that shapes, cuts, pierces or fills sound-absorbing boards, panels, foams or fibrous mats, such as cutting presses, punching tools and filler injectors used to create or modify sound-insulating elements.
[E04B 2001/8254] Soundproof supporting of building elements, e.g. stairs, floor slabs or beams, on a structure -- This subclass concerns ways of mounting building parts such as stairs, floor slabs or beams so that sound transmission is minimized. It includes acoustic isolation methods like resilient mounts, vibration-damping brackets, floating slabs or other devices that decouple the element from the supporting structure, thereby reducing noise and vibration passing through the building.
[E04B 2001/8263] Mounting of acoustical elements on supporting structure, e.g. framework or wall surface -- The CPC subclass E04B 2001/8263 covers techniques for attaching acoustic components such as sound-absorbing panels, baffles or insulation to a building's structural elements, including frames, walls or ceilings. It includes mechanical fixings, adhesives, clips or integrated mounting systems designed to improve interior sound control.
[E04B 2001/8272] Resiliently mounted wall cladding acting as a diaphragmatic sound damper -- The code designates a building-wall system in which the external cladding is attached to the structure by resilient mounts, allowing the panels to act as a diaphragm that absorbs and reduces airborne sound transmission. It covers constructions where the mounting creates a flexible interface that dampens vibrations, improving acoustic insulation of façades and interior spaces.
[E04B 2001/8281] Flat elements mounted parallel to a supporting surface with an acoustically active air gap between the elements and the mounting surface -- This CPC entry covers constructions where flat panels are mounted parallel to a wall, floor or other supporting surface, leaving a deliberately designed air space that functions as an acoustic cavity. The air gap is engineered to interact with sound waves, providing absorption, isolation or damping of noise transmitted through the structure.
[E04B 2001/829] Flat elements mounted at an angle, e.g. right angle, to the supporting surface -- Flat elements that are attached to a supporting surface at an angle, such as a right angle, are covered by this class; examples include brackets, gussets, angle brackets and similar plates that provide reinforcement, support or a mounting point for other components.
[E04B 2001/8414] with non-planar face, e.g. curved, egg-crate shaped -- E04B 2001/8414 pertains to building wall or façade elements that have a non-planar surface, such as curved, undulating, or egg-crate shaped panels. It covers designs and constructions where the exterior face departs from a flat plane, providing architectural or functional benefits like improved aesthetics, ventilation, or lighting. This subclass groups patents that address the shape, manufacturing, or attachment of such non-flat façade components.
[E04B 2001/8419] Acoustical cones or the like, e.g. for anechoic chambers -- The CPC subclass E04B 2001/8419 covers acoustical cones and similar devices designed to control or absorb sound, such as the conical elements used in anechoic chambers for electromagnetic or acoustic testing. It falls within the building-construction group for structures and equipment that provide sound isolation or attenuation.
[E04B 2001/8423] Tray or frame type panels or blocks, with or without acoustical filling -- The code refers to construction components shaped as trays or frames that form panels or blocks, which may be left empty or filled with acoustic material. These elements are used in walls, ceilings or partitions to provide structural support and improve sound insulation.
[E04B 2001/8428] containing specially shaped acoustical bodies, e.g. funnels, egg-crates, fanfolds -- This subgroup covers building components or interior elements that incorporate specially shaped acoustic bodies such as funnels, egg-crate or fan-fold structures to control sound. It is applied to walls, ceilings and panels designed for noise reduction.
[E04B 2001/8433] with holes in their face -- E04B 2001/8433 covers foundations, footings or similar subterranean structures whose surface includes holes or perforations. The holes may be provided for drainage, passage of utilities, weight reduction or to improve soil interaction. This subclassifies the broader category of foundations with face perforations.
[E04B 2001/8438] Slot shaped holes -- E04B 2001/8438 relates to the design of elongated or slot-shaped openings in building elements such as walls, floors or roofs. These slots are provided for routing services, ventilation, drainage or decorative purposes and may include provisions for sealing or reinforcement. The entry addresses the geometry, placement and functional integration of such openings in construction.
[E04B 2001/8442] Tray type elements -- E04B 2001/8442 covers tray-type elements used in horticultural or agricultural plant cultivation, such as containers, trays, racks or similar structures that support seedlings, cuttings or growing media during propagation, transport or growth. The code applies to the design, arrangement and accessories of these trays, including stacking, modular or interchangeable components.
[E04B 2001/8447] with two facing trays -- A roofing system that incorporates two opposite-facing trays, typically mounted on the roof deck to hold insulation, roofing material or to create a ventilated cavity for drainage and airflow. This configuration is used to improve thermal performance and manage water runoff on flat or low-slope roofs.
[E04B 2001/8452] with peripheral frame members -- The CPC entry E04B 2001/8452 refers to roof covering components that incorporate a frame around their outer edge. It includes panels, tiles or sheets designed with peripheral framing to provide structural support, sealing or easy attachment to the roof structure. This classification is used for inventions relating to such framed roofing elements.
[E04B 2001/8457] Solid slabs or blocks -- E04B 2001/8457 covers the use of solid slabs or block elements in construction, such as monolithic concrete, stone, ceramic or similar materials employed as load-bearing or non-load-bearing walls, floors or roofs, including their shaping, joining and installation methods.
[E04B 2001/8461] layered -- E04B 2001/8461 classifies patents dealing with construction of foundations or earthworks that are built in distinct layers, such as layered piling, multi-layer soil improvement, or stepped ground structures, focusing on the arrangement, formation, or use of layered material systems in supporting structures.
[E04B 2001/8466] with an intermediate layer formed of lines or dots of elastic material -- The entry refers to structural or protective panels such as roof or wall cladding where an intermediate layer between the outer and inner sheets consists of a pattern of elastic lines or dots. These elastic elements provide flexibility, shock absorption, or sealing while maintaining overall rigidity. Used in building constructions for improved durability and weather resistance.
[E04B 2001/8471] with non-planar interior transition surfaces between layers, e.g. faceted, corrugated -- E04B 2001/8471 covers building components whose interior interfaces between material layers are intentionally non-flat, featuring shapes such as facets or corrugations to improve strength, insulation or aesthetic effect. The classification applies to walls, panels or similar elements with these textured transition surfaces.
[E04B 2001/8476] with acoustical cavities, with or without acoustical filling -- The E04B 2001/8476 subclass groups building elements that incorporate acoustic cavities, which may be left empty or filled with sound-absorbing material, to improve sound insulation or control reverberation within walls, floors, ceilings or other structural components. It covers designs that use these cavities with or without dedicated acoustic filling.
[E04B 2001/848] the cavities opening onto the face of the element -- The CPC subclass E04B 2001/848 pertains to wall or fence elements that contain cavities whose openings are situated on the outer face of the element. It covers components designed to provide access to internal spaces for functions such as routing services, ventilation, or mounting, while the element remains part of a structural wall or fence system.
[E04B 2001/8485] the opening being restricted, e.g. forming Helmoltz resonators -- This CPC entry covers building structures whose openings are intentionally limited-such as partially closed vents, ducts or wall apertures-to create Helmholtz-type resonators that control acoustic transmission. It includes designs where the restriction forms a cavity that absorbs or attenuates sound, commonly applied in ventilation, façade panels or other architectural elements for noise reduction.
[E04B 2001/849] Groove or slot type openings -- E04B 2001/849 falls under the CPC group for building foundations and subsoil works and specifically covers the provision of groove or slot type openings in walls, floors or other structural elements. These openings are intended for routing services, anchoring, or accommodating inserts and are defined by their linear cross-section rather than circular or irregular shapes.
[E04B 2001/8495] the openings going through from one face to the other face of the element -- E04B 2001/8495 covers through openings that extend from one surface of a building element to the opposite surface, such as windows, doors, passages, ducts or other apertures that completely penetrate walls, floors or roofs. The classification is used for patents describing the design, construction or sealing of such openings.
[E04B 2001/925] Protection against harmful electro-magnetic or radio-active radiations, e.g. X-rays -- E04B 2001/925 designates protective measures in building and civil structures that shield occupants from harmful electromagnetic or radioactive radiation such as X-rays, encompassing shielding materials, filters and construction techniques that reduce exposure.
[E04B 2001/949] Construction elements filled with liquid, e.g. water, either permanently or only in case of fire -- The class covers building components that incorporate a liquid core, such as water-filled panels, tubes, or cavities, used either as a permanent feature or to provide fire-extinguishing or fire-resistance functions when activated by heat. It includes designs where the liquid remains contained under normal conditions and is released or expands only during a fire event.
[E04B 2002/0202] Details of connections -- E04B 2002/0202 covers the specific ways structural elements are joined in foundations and supporting constructions, including anchors, bolts, welds, reinforcement ties and other connection details that link foundation components to each other or to the superstructure. It also addresses the design and arrangement of these joints to ensure load transfer, stability and durability.
[E04B 2002/0204] Non-undercut connections, e.g. tongue and groove connections -- E04B 2002/0204 relates to building construction joints where the parts interlock without being cut away, such as tongue-and-groove connections. It covers the design and detailing of these non-undercut fittings used in walls, floors, panels or similar elements to provide alignment, stability and a continuous surface.
[E04B 2002/0206] of rectangular shape -- E04B 2002/0206 covers roof-covering or roofing components that are essentially rectangular in shape, such as flat tiles, plates or panels. The entry applies to designs and constructions where the rectangular geometry of the element is a defining technical feature of the roof structure.
[E04B 2002/0208] of trapezoidal shape -- CPC E04B 2002/0208 classifies roofing or roof-related components that have a trapezoidal shape. It covers panels, coverings, structural members or roof sections whose plan or cross-section forms a trapezoid, such as sloping roof elements, skylights or prefabricated roof modules designed in that geometry.
[E04B 2002/021] of triangular shape -- E04B 2002/021 refers to foundations, footings or groundworks that are designed with a triangular shape, covering the structural and construction aspects of three-sided base elements used in building foundations.
[E04B 2002/0213] of round shape -- This subgroup covers building elements that are circular in plan, such as round columns, cylindrical walls, domes or other structural parts having a round shape. It applies to designs where the primary geometric form is a circle.
[E04B 2002/0215] with separate protrusions -- The CPC subclass E04B 2002/0215 relates to building foundations that incorporate distinct protruding members such as piles, pilings or separate footings extending from the main foundation body to transmit loads to the ground. It classifies structural solutions where these individual protrusions are defined as separate elements within the overall foundation design.
[E04B 2002/0217] of prismatic shape -- The code designates building components such as doors, windows, shutters or gates that are formed with a prismatic cross-section, covering prisms used as structural, decorative or light-guiding elements within those assemblies.
[E04B 2002/0219] of pyramidal shape -- The CPC entry E04B 2002/0219 covers building and structural components that are formed with a pyramidal geometry, such as pyramidal roofs, towers, supports or other elements whose shape tapers to a point. It addresses the design, construction methods and applications of these pyramid-shaped features in civil and architectural works.
[E04B 2002/0221] of conical shape -- E04B 2002/0221 pertains to building components that are conical in form, such as conical roofs, spires, funnels or chimneys used in construction. It classifies inventions where the primary geometric feature is a cone shape within the broader building elements category.
[E04B 2002/0223] of cylindrical shape -- E04B 2002/0223 covers foundation or ground-anchoring members that have an essentially cylindrical shape, such as piles, caissons or bored shafts. It is part of the CPC group dealing with foundations, footings, piles and retaining structures.
[E04B 2002/0226] with tongues and grooves next to each other on the end surface -- The CPC subclass E04B 2002/0226 covers building elements whose end surfaces are equipped with adjoining tongue-and-groove fittings, such as panels, boards or cladding that interlock side by side to form a continuous joint. This feature is used to enhance alignment, sealing and structural continuity in construction assemblies.
[E04B 2002/0228] with tongues next to each other on one end surface and grooves next to each other on opposite end surface -- The CPC subgroup E04B 2002/0228 covers building elements-typically doors, windows or panels-where adjacent tongue-like protrusions are placed side by side on one end surface and matching grooves are placed side by side on the opposite end surface, allowing the two surfaces to interlock securely. This arrangement provides a straightforward mechanical joint for assembling and sealing structural components.
[E04B 2002/023] with rabbets, e.g. stepped -- The CPC grouping E04B 2002/023 covers foundation or pile elements that incorporate rabbets or stepped profiles, typically used to reduce soil pressure, improve drainage or facilitate connections with adjacent structural parts. It includes designs where the lower section recedes or is shaped in steps rather than a plain solid footing.
[E04B 2002/0232] Undercut connections, e.g. using undercut tongues and grooves -- E04B 2002/0232 covers undercut connections, such as undercut tongues and grooves, that provide a mechanical interlock between building components, often in timber or metal structures, without the need for additional fasteners. The entry includes methods and devices that create recessed profiles to lock parts together.
[E04B 2002/0234] Angular dovetails -- The CPC class E04B 2002/0234 relates to angular dovetail joints used in the construction of doors, windows, shutters and similar assemblies. It covers the design, manufacture and installation of interlocking angular dovetail connections that provide a secure, self-locking fastening of components without additional hardware.
[E04B 2002/0236] tapered, i.e. dovetail narrows in the direction of tongue or groove -- This CPC subclass covers building components whose dovetail or similar interlocking profile is tapered, meaning the width of the profile decreases toward the tongue or groove side. The tapering improves fit and eases assembly for prefabricated panels, blocks, or other modular elements.
[E04B 2002/0239] Round dovetails -- Round dovetails are cylindrical wooden pins fitted into matching round mortises to create a concealed, load-bearing joint in timber structures such as floors, decks or roof members. The CPC entry E04B 2002/0239 covers this joinery method, where the round pins may be tapered or grooved to lock with the surrounding wood and resist shear and uplift forces.
[E04B 2002/0241] tapered, i.e. dovetail narrows in the direction of tongue or groove -- The CPC subclass E04B 2002/0241 covers building elements that use a tapered dovetail joint, where the dovetail profile narrows toward the tongue or groove to create a tight, self-locking fit. This design is employed in construction and joinery to improve alignment and load distribution.
[E04B 2002/0243] Separate connectors or inserts, e.g. pegs, pins or keys -- A subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification concerning building elements that use detachable or replaceable fastening parts. It covers devices such as pegs, pins, keys or similar inserts that join, align or secure components of a construction, allowing assembly, disassembly or adjustment without permanent bonding.
[E04B 2002/0245] Pegs or pins -- E04B 2002/0245 is a CPC subgroup for building construction that deals with pegs or pins used to join structural elements. It includes devices such as wooden dowels, metal pins and similar fasteners that are inserted to connect or support components in timber framing and related structures.
[E04B 2002/0247] Strips or bars -- E04B 2002/0247 covers building elements that are fashioned as strips or bars, such as metal, plastic or composite profiles used for cladding, reinforcement, spacing or joining of structural components. The classification includes any strip-shaped or bar-shaped parts that form part of walls, roofs, floors or other building assemblies.
[E04B 2002/025] U-shaped, e.g. brackets -- The E04B 2002/025 entry covers U-shaped brackets, typically metal or composite pieces with a U-profile that are used to connect, support or brace structural elements. They may be fixed to walls, floors or other components to hold pipes, panels, beams or similar parts.
[E04B 2002/0252] Dovetail keys -- E04B 2002/0252 falls under the CPC group for doors, windows, shutters and similar constructions and specifically covers dovetail keys, the tapered metal or wooden strips used to lock panels, doors or shutters together. The subclass includes patents describing the design, manufacturing and application of these interlocking keys in timber and other door components.
[E04B 2002/0254] Tie rods -- Tie rods are tension members, usually steel rods or bars, used to connect opposite sides of a structure to provide lateral stability. In civil engineering they are employed in retaining walls, bridge decks, and building façades to counteract tensile forces and prevent deformation. This CPC subclass covers designs, installation methods, and related components of such tie rods.
[E04B 2002/0256] Special features of building elements -- E04B 2002/0256 classifies patents concerning special features of building elements, such as added functional or protective components, accessories, or modifications that enhance performance, safety, aesthetics or adaptability of walls, roofs, floors, doors, windows and similar structural parts. It captures inventive aspects that go beyond the basic construction of these elements.
[E04B 2002/0258] Gripping or handling aids -- E04B 2002/0258 covers devices and accessories that assist in gripping, holding or manipulating building components, such as handles, fixtures, clamps, or tools used to align, move, secure or service doors, windows, shutters, skylights and related elements during construction, installation or maintenance. It includes any aid that improves handling or control of these parts.
[E04B 2002/026] Splittable building elements -- E04B 2002/026 covers building elements designed to be divided or assembled into smaller parts, such as modular wall panels, prefabricated sections, or detachable components used in construction. The category includes designs that facilitate easier transport, installation, or adaptation of structures by splitting the element into separate pieces.
[E04B 2002/0263] Building elements for making angled walls -- E04B 2002/0263 covers building components designed specifically to create angled walls, including prefabricated wall sections, brackets, connectors and related fittings that enable construction of walls at non-right-angle or sloped positions. The entry addresses both the structural and joining aspects required for forming such angled wall geometries.
[E04B 2002/0265] Building elements for making arcuate walls -- E04B 2002/0265 covers building components specifically designed for constructing curved or arcuate walls such as arches, vaults and circular partitions. It includes prefabricated panels, blocks, mouldings and other structural elements that facilitate the erection of non-straight wall surfaces.
[E04B 2002/0267] Building elements with the appearance of several bricks -- The E04B 2002/0267 subclass covers building components that are designed to look like a group of individual bricks, such as facade panels, modular panels or prefabricated units whose surface texture, colour and joint lines imitate the appearance of multiple separate bricks while being a single element. This classification is used for patents relating to aesthetic brick-like solutions that simplify construction and improve thermal or structural performance.
[E04B 2002/0269] Building elements with a natural stone facing -- E04B 2002/0269 concerns building components that are clad or faced with natural stone, including walls, facades and other structural elements where stone serves as an exterior veneer for aesthetic or protective purposes.
[E04B 2002/0271] Building elements with the appearance of rock layers -- This group covers building components that are fabricated to resemble natural rock strata, including façade panels, cladding sheets, prefabricated blocks or tiles whose surface texture, colour and layering imitate sedimentary rock. The elements are intended primarily for aesthetic integration of structures with a rock-like appearance.
[E04B 2002/0273] Adhesive layers other than mortar between building elements -- This subclass covers the use of non-mortar bonding materials such as adhesives, glues, or chemical bonding agents applied as continuous or spot layers to join building components like bricks, blocks, panels, or prefabricated elements. It includes description of adhesive types, application methods, and purposes such as structural, thermal, acoustic, or water-tight joints.
[E04B 2002/0276] Separate layers or strips -- The code E04B 2002/0276 refers to building components made of separate layers or strips, such as laminated timber panels, composite walls, or strip boards, used in construction of roofs, walls or floors. It covers methods of assembling, joining and fixing these layered elements to form structural or non-structural elements.
[E04B 2002/0278] forming a unity with the building elements -- E04B 2002/0278 relates to techniques for forming a unity with building elements, i.e., methods of connecting or integrating structural components such as walls, floors, roofs or prefabricated modules so that they function as a single, continuous structure. It includes fastening, bonding, interlocking or other means that create a cohesive building unit.
[E04B 2002/028] Spacers between building elements -- Spacers between building elements refer to components inserted between parts of a structure to create a controlled gap, provide insulation, absorb vibration, or allow differential movement, including shims, thermal or acoustic separators used in walls, floors, roofs and panels.
[E04B 2002/0282] Separate spacers -- E04B 2002/0282 covers the use of separate spacers in roofing assemblies, where individual insulating or ventilating elements are positioned between roof layers to create and maintain a defined air gap. These spacers are employed to improve thermal performance, moisture control and structural stability.
[E04B 2002/0284] forming a unity with the building elements -- E04B 2002/0284 covers methods and devices for joining separate building components so they function as a single structural unit. It includes techniques such as mechanical fastening, adhesive bonding, welding or interlocking that create a unified assembly of walls, floors, roofs or other elements during construction or prefabrication.
[E04B 2002/0286] Building elements with coatings -- E04B 2002/0286 covers building components such as walls, ceilings, floors, roofs or panels that are provided with a coating, including paints, varnishes, protective films, insulating or waterproof layers applied to improve durability, appearance or performance.
[E04B 2002/0289] Building elements with holes filled with insulating material -- E04B 2002/0289 covers building elements such as walls, floors, or roofs that contain drilled, cut or otherwise formed openings which are subsequently filled with insulating material to improve thermal or acoustic performance, including hollow masonry units, panels with voids filled with foam, mineral wool or similar insulators.
[E04B 2002/0291] loose material -- The CPC code E04B 2002/0291 refers to the use of loose material such as earth, sand, gravel, or other unbound fill in foundation, basement or ground-floor construction. It covers processes for placing, compacting, and stabilising these materials during earthworks or ground preparation.
[E04B 2002/0293] solid material -- The entry E04B 2002/0293 in the CPC concerns solid material used in doors, windows, skylights and similar building opening constructions. It covers patents that define or claim the material composition of these elements, such as metal, wood, polymer or composite solids, rather than their shape or operation.
[E04B 2002/0295] of which the width is equal to the wall thickness -- This CPC group relates to foundation structures where the planar width of the element is the same as the thickness of its wall, meaning the wall is not wider than its own thickness. It is used for describing thin-wall footings or base elements whose dimensions are defined by that equivalence.
[E04B 2002/0297] of which the width is less than the wall thickness -- The CPC code E04B 2002/0297 classifies foundation or wall-related structural elements whose horizontal width is smaller than the thickness of the adjoining wall. It is applied to patents describing thin footings, pads or similar components where the width dimension is less than the wall thickness.
[E04B 2002/565] with a brick veneer facing -- E04B 2002/565 designates building walls or facades that incorporate a brick veneer as the external facing. It covers structures where a non-load-bearing layer of bricks is applied to a structural substrate for aesthetic, protective or insulating purposes, typically fixed by mechanical or adhesive means.
[E04B 2002/567] with posts or pillars made from a plurality of smaller prefabricated elements -- The code E04B 2002/567 relates to building structures that incorporate posts or pillars assembled from several smaller prefabricated components rather than being monolithic. It covers modular, stacked or connected pillar systems used in construction to form walls, supports or frames. This classification is applied to inventions that describe such composite post or pillar arrangements.
[E04B 2002/725] Corner or angle connection details -- E04B 2002/725 covers design and construction of corner or angle connections in building structures, detailing how intersecting walls, beams, or panels are joined. The classification includes various mechanical, welded, bolted, or adhesive methods that provide stability, load transfer, and weather sealing at junctions. It is used to categorize patents related to joint geometry, reinforcement, and sealing techniques for architectural and civil engineering applications.
[E04B 2002/726] Clips for butt-joining plasterbords -- The CPC subclass E04B 2002/726 covers devices that secure the edges of adjacent plasterboard panels when they are joined butt-to-butt. It includes clips, staples or other fasteners designed to hold the boards in alignment and maintain the joint during installation and finishing. These elements are used in interior wall and ceiling construction to improve stability and ease of mounting.
[E04B 2002/728] Butt-joining plasterboards in the space between two studs -- A method for creating a butt joint between adjacent plasterboards located in the cavity between two vertical studs, typically employing mechanical fasteners, adhesives, or tape to secure the boards and achieve a continuous, load-bearing surface without the need for additional framing.
[E04B 2002/7418] Accessories supported on the free upper edge, e.g. auxiliary panels, noise abatement devices -- The subclass concerns accessories that are mounted on the free upper edge of a building element, such as the roof or façade. Typical examples include auxiliary panels extending from the edge and devices that reduce noise transmission, both of which are attached without altering the main structure.
[E04B 2002/742] Details of panel top cap -- E04B 2002/742 covers the design and features of the top cap that closes the upper edge of a roof or building panel. It includes specifications for the cap's shape, material, attachment method and sealing provisions to protect the panel from weather ingress.
[E04B 2002/7446] Post-like profiles for connecting panels at an angle -- E04B 2002/7446 concerns post-shaped connector profiles that join building panels at an angle, allowing modular assembly of walls, facades or other panel systems. These profiles provide a rigid, often extruded, support element that secures adjacent panels while accommodating angular joints and simplifying installation.
[E04B 2002/7461] Details of connection of sheet panels to frame or posts -- This CPC subgroup covers the design and implementation of joints that fasten sheet panels - such as metal, wood, or composite boards - to supporting frames or vertical posts in building construction. It includes bolts, screws, clips, welds, adhesives and other specific connection details that secure the panels to the structural members.
[E04B 2002/7462] using resilient connectors, e.g. clips -- The subclass E04B 2002/7462 covers the use of resilient connectors such as clips to join building components, especially in wall and partition systems. It includes methods that rely on the elastic or spring-like properties of the connector to maintain a secure, yet flexible, connection between structural elements.
[E04B 2002/7464] clasping a flange of a profile -- The code E04B 2002/7464 covers a fastening method in which a flange of a profile is clamped or locked in place, typically using a securing element such as a screw, clip or latch. It is applied to construction or assembly of building components where profiles with protruding flanges need to be joined securely without welding.
[E04B 2002/7466] using hooks -- E04B 2002/7466 covers the use of hooks as functional elements in the construction or repair of foundations, footings and retaining structures. It includes devices or methods where hooks are employed to secure, lift or anchor reinforcement, formwork or other components during installation or maintenance.
[E04B 2002/7468] using magnets -- The entry covers inventions that employ magnetic fields or magnetic devices to improve or control building foundations, floors or other substructures, such as magnetically anchored piles, magnetic dampers for vibration reduction, or magnetic fastening systems for structural components. It is part of the CPC group for building elements and foundations.
[E04B 2002/747] using hook and loop-type fasteners -- The CPC subgroup E04B 2002/747 covers inventions that employ hook-and-loop fasteners to attach building components, panels, protective coverings or temporary structures, enabling quick assembly and disassembly without conventional screws, nails or adhesives. It includes methods, devices and systems that replace or supplement traditional mechanical connections in construction applications.
[E04B 2002/7472] using adhesives -- E04B 2002/7472 covers the use of adhesives in the construction of buildings, including bonding of structural elements such as walls, floors, ceilings and roofing components. It relates to methods and systems that employ adhesive materials to join, seal or reinforce building parts.
[E04B 2002/7474] using releasable connectors actuable with a key or a tool -- The subclass covers doors, gates or similar closures that are secured with releasable connectors that can be disengaged only by operating a key-actuated or tool-actuated mechanism. It includes hinges, latches, bolts or other fasteners released by turning a key, inserting a tool, or a comparable action to allow opening. Applications relate to building, vehicle or industrial assemblies where controlled release is required.
[E04B 2002/7475] using connectors with claws penetrating the sheet panels -- The entry covers roof-sheet fastening techniques that employ claw-type connectors driven through the sheet panels to lock them to the supporting structure. It includes methods where the claws penetrate the panel material to provide a secure, often reversible, attachment for metal, bitumen or similar roofing sheets.
[E04B 2002/7477] Details of connections using screws or nails -- E04B 2002/7477 is a CPC subgroup concerning the construction of building elements where the joining of components is achieved with screws, nails or similar mechanical fasteners. It includes details of fastening techniques, the geometry of fastener holes, and arrangements that improve connection strength or ease of assembly.
[E04B 2002/7479] Details of connection of flexible sheets to frame or posts -- E04B 2002/7479 relates to construction of building envelopes where flexible covering materials such as membranes, tarpaulins or curtains are attached to supporting frames, rails or posts. The category includes fastening methods, sealing joints, clamps, adhesives, mechanical latches and other devices that secure the sheet to the structure while allowing tensioning or movement.
[E04B 2002/7481] Locating rails with adjustable curvature -- The CPC subclass E04B 2002/7481 covers systems for positioning railway rails whose curvature can be varied, typically using adjustable or movable support structures that allow the track geometry to be changed for testing, maintenance or temporary line installations.
[E04B 2002/7483] Details of furniture, e.g. tables or shelves, associated with the partitions -- E04B 2002/7483 covers interior fittings that are integral to or associated with partitions, such as tables, shelves, and other furniture components designed to be mounted on, fitted into, or used with wall or room dividers. This subclass details the form, attachment means and functional aspects of such partition-related furniture.
[E04B 2002/7485] Load supports therefor placed between wall studs -- E04B 2002/7485 covers load-supporting elements that are positioned between wall studs to bear and transfer forces, such as brackets, joist hangers or similar devices used in timber or metal framing. The classification includes designs that improve load distribution and structural stability within stud walls.
[E04B 2002/7487] Partitions with slotted profiles -- E04B 2002/7487 is a CPC subclass for agricultural or horticultural buildings that covers partition walls equipped with slotted profiles, allowing adjustable or modular sections. The code is applied to patents describing such flexible partition systems in barns, stables, greenhouses and similar structures.
[E04B 2002/7488] Details of wiring -- E04B 2002/7488 covers the specific design and arrangement of electrical wiring within a building, including the routing of cables, placement of conduits, junction boxes, connectors and protective measures to ensure safety and functionality. It relates to detailed wiring plans and installations that are part of overall building construction.
[E04B 2002/749] Partitions with screw-type jacks -- E04B 2002/749 covers interior partitions that are moved or adjusted using screw-type jacks, allowing height or position changes for flexible space layouts. The classification applies to designs where the partition's support or leveling mechanism relies on a threaded screw system.
[E04B 2002/7492] used in partitions extending from floor to ceiling -- The code E04B 2002/7492 falls under the Cooperative Patent Classification for building elements and refers specifically to partitions that run continuously from the floor to the ceiling. This entry covers the design, construction and use of full-height walls that divide interior spaces, providing structural support, fire resistance or acoustic isolation as required.
[E04B 2002/7494] the jacks being located at the top or the side of the partition -- E04B 2002/7494 pertains to doors, windows or movable partitions in which the supporting or lifting jacks are positioned on the upper edge or on the side of the partition, defining a specific arrangement for adjusting the panel's height or movement. This classification is used for inventions that locate the jacks at the top or side rather than elsewhere in the structure.
[E04B 2002/7496] using wedges to level or hold the partition in place -- The CPC entry E04B 2002/7496 covers methods and devices that use wedges to adjust, level or secure partitions such as walls, panels or movable screens. It includes techniques for inserting wedges to maintain alignment, stability and proper positioning of the partition during installation or use.
[E04B 2002/7498] Partitions for clean rooms -- E04B 2002/7498 classifies structural elements that divide clean-room spaces, including walls, curtains, panels and modular partitions designed to maintain controlled contamination levels. The entry covers materials, sealing methods and mounting arrangements that enable flexible, hygienic separation within aseptic or contamination-sensitive environments.
[E04B 2002/867] Corner details -- The CPC subclass E04B 2002/867 relates to architectural corner details, covering structural or decorative elements applied at the junctions of walls, such as caps, brackets, mouldings, protective plates or ornamental fittings that finish or reinforce building corners.
[E04B 2002/8676] Wall end details -- The CPC entry E04B 2002/8676 falls under the building-element group E04B, which covers walls and partitions. It pertains specifically to the design, construction and finishing of wall-end components such as coping, trims, flashing, and junctions where a wall meets a roof, floor or adjoining structure, detailing how these terminations are protected, sealed or aesthetically treated.
[E04B 2002/8682] Mixed technique using permanent and reusable forms -- The subclass covers construction of roof or ceiling components that use a hybrid method where part of the formwork stays permanently in place while other parts are removable and reused. This mixed technique allows the finished structure to include both fixed and detachable elements, improving speed, cost and flexibility in building roofs or ceilings.
[E04B 2002/8688] Scaffoldings or removable supports therefor -- This CPC subclass falls under section E for civil engineering, group 04B covering scaffolding and temporary structures. It covers inventions related to scaffoldings or removable supports, including their design, connection, adjustment, and quick-release mechanisms, as well as accessories that facilitate erection, stability, or dismantling.
[E04B 2002/8694] with hinged spacers allowing the formwork to be collapsed for transport -- The subclass E04B 2002/8694 covers concrete formwork that incorporates hinged spacers, enabling the panels or components to be folded or collapsed for compact transport and easier handling on site. This feature improves mobility and storage of formwork systems used in building construction.
[E04B 2005/173] with permanent forms for the floor edges -- E04B 2005/173 covers building elements in which the edges of a floor are shaped by a permanent form that remains integral to the structure after construction, such as fixed concrete moulds that create steps, curbs or decorative perimeters. This subclass details the design and use of such enduring edge profiles.
[E04B 2005/176] with peripheral anchors or supports -- E04B 2005/176 falls within the CPC group for building construction and concerns structures that are secured or stabilized by peripheral anchors or supports. It covers walls, panels, foundations or similar elements that employ devices placed around the outer edges to transfer loads to the ground or to adjacent components, improving resistance to wind, seismic activity or settlement.
[E04B 2005/232] with special provisions for connecting wooden stiffening ribs or other wooden beam-like formations to the concrete slab -- E04B 2005/232 covers concrete floor slabs that incorporate special means for attaching wooden stiffening ribs or other timber beam-like elements. It includes designs, fastening methods and connection details that integrate wood reinforcement into the concrete slab structure. This subclass addresses the combined use of concrete and timber to enhance slab rigidity and load-bearing capacity.
[E04B 2005/235] Wooden stiffening ribs or other wooden beam-like formations having a special form -- This CPC subgroup covers wooden stiffening ribs and other specially shaped wooden beam-like elements designed to reinforce structural components such as walls, floors or roofs. It includes ribs with particular cross-sections or profiles that provide added rigidity or load-bearing capacity.
[E04B 2005/237] Separate connecting elements -- E04B 2005/237 covers separate connecting elements used in masonry and other building structures, such as ties, brackets, anchors and prefabricated connectors that join or secure components like bricks, blocks, panels or timber. The class includes devices that are not integral to the structural parts themselves but are installed to provide mechanical linkage or reinforcement.
[E04B 2005/322] with permanent forms for the floor edges -- E04B 2005/322 covers building techniques in which the formwork used to shape the edges of a floor during construction is retained as a permanent part of the structure. The classification includes methods and devices that create lasting edge profiles, such as integral mouldings or form panels that remain embedded in the finished floor.
[E04B 2005/324] with peripheral anchors or supports -- This CPC subgroup classifies patents that address building foundations or platforms reinforced with peripheral anchors or supports such as ground anchors, piles or peripheral structural members that enhance lateral and vertical stability around the foundation's edges.
[E04B 2007/066] for conservatories -- E04B 2007/066 relates to inventions directed specifically at conservatories, including the design, construction, glazing, roof structures, heating, ventilation and other technical features of these light-filled extensions attached to a building. The subclass groups patents that address the particular functional and architectural requirements of conservatory installations.
[E04B 2009/026] the supporting ceiling grid acting as air diffusers -- The CPC subclass E04B 2009/026 pertains to building ceiling constructions in which the supporting grid is designed to serve also as an air-diffusing system. It covers integrated ventilation solutions where the structural ceiling framework doubles as a conduit for distributing conditioned air throughout a space.
[E04B 2009/0492] with fabrics tensioned on frames -- The CPC group E04B 2009/0492 covers roof or shelter constructions where fabric panels are stretched over supporting frames to create a tensioned covering, such as tents, canopies, awnings or similar structures. It includes methods and devices that tension the fabric on the frame to achieve a stable, weather-tight surface.
[E04B 2009/062] Caps covering visible surfaces of the supporting construction -- The CPC entry E04B 2009/062 relates to caps or covers that are applied to visible parts of a building's supporting structure, such as column tops, beam ends or exposed wall sections, to protect them from weather, corrosion or damage and to improve appearance. It covers protective or decorative elements that conceal structural members while remaining exposed.
[E04B 2009/186] with arrangements for damping vibration -- The CPC entry E04B 2009/186 covers foundations, foundation elements or ground-improvement structures that incorporate devices or configurations to reduce or dampen vibrations. It includes piles, mats, footings or other support systems equipped with vibration-absorbing layers, dampers, isolators or similar arrangements designed to minimise oscillatory motion transmitted from loads or environmental sources.
[E04B 2/02] built-up from layers of building elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Walls for buildings built-up from layers of building elements, e.g. brick or block walls, as well as building elements of block or brick shape for the construction of parts of buildings. The walls can be bearing walls or non-bearing walls, e.g. partition walls, and the building elements accordingly.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Toys building blocks -> A63H33/04 - A63H33/14 - Positioning wedges -> E04G21/1841  **Glossary:** - Block shape: block shape is not restricted to parallelepiped shape
[E04B 2/04] Walls having neither cavities between, nor in, the solid elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Walls having neither cavities between, nor in, the solid building elements, as well as solid brick or block shape building elements having no cavities in them.
[E04B 2/06] using elements having specially-designed means for stabilising the position -- The subclass concerns wall or partition systems in buildings that incorporate components with built-in stabilising features such as brackets, clips or locking mechanisms, enabling the elements to maintain their position during and after installation. It addresses constructions where the stabilising means are integral to the wall elements.
[E04B 2/08] by interlocking of projections or inserts with indentations, e.g. of tongues, grooves, dovetails -- This class includes wall or partition constructions for buildings that are assembled by interlocking projections with matching indentations, such as tongues, grooves or dovetail joints, rather than by separate fasteners. The interlocking elements fit together to form a fixed, self-supporting structure.
[E04B 2/10] by filling material with or without reinforcements in small channels in, or in grooves between, the elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Walls built-up from layers of solid building elements of brick or block shape, and having no cavities in the elements, wherein the joints, e.g. small channels or grooves between the building elements and/or between the successive layers are filled with material, with or without additional reinforcements in the joints, small channels or grooves.
[E04B 2103/00] INDEXING SCHEME ASSOCIATED WITH -- E04B 2103/00 deals with indexing schemes employed to organise or retrieve information about fixed building constructions, such as plans, documents or component data within general building works. It falls under the Cooperative Patent Classification system for inventions related to building and construction.
[E04B 2103/02] of ceramics, concrete or other stone-like material -- The CPC subclass E04B 2103/02 covers fixed building constructions that are made of ceramics, concrete or other stone-like materials, including structural elements such as walls, floors and similar components. It falls under the hierarchy of Fixed Constructions → Building → General Building Constructions. This group is used to classify patents relating to the design, manufacture or use of such stone-based building elements.
[E04B 2103/04] of plastics, fibrous material or wood -- E04B 2103/04 covers fixed building constructions made predominantly of plastics, fibrous materials such as composites, or wood. The subclass includes walls, roofs, floors, and structural components where these materials are used as primary building elements. It falls under general building constructions in the CPC hierarchy.
[E04B 2103/06] of metal -- E04B 2103/06 designates fixed building constructions where metal is the primary material, covering metal frames, supports, fittings and other structural elements used in general building construction. It groups inventions related to the use of metal in permanent, non-movable building components.
[E04B 2/12] using elements having a general shape differing from that of a parallelepiped -- This subclass concerns walls or partitions for buildings that are formed from individual elements whose overall shape is not a rectangular parallelepiped, such as curved, polygonal or otherwise irregular shapes. It covers construction methods and designs employing these non-box building units.
[E04B 2/14] Walls having cavities in, but not between, the elements, i.e. each cavity being enclosed by at least four sides forming part of one single element -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Walls built-up from layers of building elements of brick or block shape having cavities in, but not between, the building elements, as well as brick or block shape building elements having cavities in them.
[E04B 2/16] using elements having specially-designed means for stabilising the position -- E04B 2/16 relates to wall or partition systems that incorporate specially-designed components such as brackets, clips, interlocking sections or adjustable supports whose purpose is to stabilize the position of the construction element. The subclass covers methods and devices that secure the wall against movement, deformation or displacement during or after assembly.
[E04B 2/18] by interlocking of projections or inserts with indentations, e.g. of tongues, grooves, dovetails -- E04B 2/18 covers wall or partition elements for buildings that are assembled by interlocking projections and corresponding indentations, such as tongues and grooves, dovetails, or similar joint forms. The system relies on the shaped surfaces to lock components together without additional fasteners.
[E04B 2/20] by filling material with or without reinforcements in small channels in, or in grooves between, the elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Walls built-up from layers of building elements of brick or block shape, and having cavities in the elements, wherein the joints, e.g. small channels or grooves between the building elements and/or between the successive layers are filled with material, with or without additional reinforcements in the joints, small channels or grooves.
[E04B 2/22] using elements having a general shape differing from that of a parallelepiped -- The code E04B 2/22 covers wall or partition constructions that employ building elements whose overall shape is not a standard parallelepiped, such as curved, tapered, polygonal or otherwise irregular panels, frames or modules. It addresses methods and devices for assembling such non-rectangular components to form structural or interior walls in buildings.
[E04B 2/24] the walls being characterised by fillings in some of the cavities forming load-bearing pillars or beams -- The CPC entry E04B 2/24 covers walls whose cavities are partially filled to create load-bearing pillars or beams within the structure. It falls under Fixed Constructions → Building → General Building Constructions and addresses the integration of reinforced or filled wall sections that serve as structural supports.
[E04B 2/26] the walls being characterised by fillings in all cavities in order to form a wall construction -- E04B 2/26 covers fixed building walls in which every cavity is filled to create a solid wall construction. The classification applies to partitions and other walls where the filling material - such as concrete, masonry or insulation - occupies all voids, forming a continuous structural element.
[E04B 2/28] Walls having cavities between, but not in, the elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Walls made from layers of building elements of brick or block shape, the walls having cavities between, but not in, the elements, e.g. walls made of solid building elements having special profiles on their small side faces in order to create a cavity when connected with the adjacent elements;  Walls made from layers of building elements of brick or block shape, each consisting of two or more parts kept in distance, in a direction perpendicular to the wall, by means of spacers, all parts of the elements being solid.
[E04B 2/30] using elements having specially designed means for stabilising the position -- **Glossary:** - Spacers for cavity walls: spacers between solid parts of brick or block shaped elements for building cavity walls, e.g. spacers integrated in a block consisting of two parallel solid slab shaped parts kept at a distance by the spacer
[E04B 2/32] by interlocking of projections or inserts with indentations, e.g. of tongues, grooves, dovetails -- E04B 2/32 covers wall and partition constructions in buildings that are assembled by interlocking projections such as tongues, grooves, or dovetail joints, where the parts lock together without additional fasteners. The method provides a fixed, self-locking structure for interior or exterior walls.
[E04B 2/34] by filling material with or without reinforcements in small channels in, or in grooves between, the elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Walls made from layers of building elements of brick or block shape, the walls having cavities between, but not in, the elements, wherein the joints, e.g. small channels or grooves between the building elements and/or between the successive layers are filled with material, with or without additional reinforcements in the joints, small channels or grooves.
[E04B 2/36] using elements having a general shape differing from that of a parallelepiped -- The subclass E04B 2/36 covers wall or partition constructions that employ building elements whose overall shape is not that of a rectangular parallelepiped, such as curved, triangular, or otherwise irregular sections. It includes methods, systems and components that use these non-rectangular elements to form walls in buildings.
[E04B 2/38] the walls being characterised by fillings in some of the cavities forming load-bearing pillars or beams -- This entry (E04B 2/38) in the Cooperative Patent Classification covers building walls whose cavities are partially filled to function as load-bearing pillars or beams. It belongs to the hierarchy of fixed constructions, building, general building constructions, walls for buildings.
[E04B 2/40] the walls being characterised by fillings in all cavities in order to form a wall construction -- The class covers wall constructions in which all cavities or voids within the wall are filled with a material-such as concrete, insulation, mortar or other substances-to create a solid or composite wall, including partitions and other building walls where no cavity is left empty.
[E04B 2/42] Walls having cavities between, as well as in, the elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Walls made from layers of building elements of brick or block shape, the walls having cavities between, as well as in the elements, e.g. walls made of building elements having cavities and having special profiles on their small side faces in order to create a cavity when connected with the adjacent elements;  Walls made from layers of building elements of brick or block shape, each consisting of two or more parts kept in distance, in a direction perpendicular to the wall, by means of spacers, at least one of the parts of the elements having cavities.
