[E05B 2047/0063] Energy transfer from key to lock, e.g. for emergency opening -- The subclass E05B 2047/0063 covers lock systems in which the key supplies the energy needed to move the lock bolt, allowing the door to be opened, particularly for emergency release. It includes mechanisms that transfer mechanical or other energy from the key to the lock to achieve rapid unlocking.
[E05B 2047/0064] by solar cells -- E05B 2047/0064 covers devices or methods that generate power directly from solar cells, including the integration of photovoltaic modules into systems for electricity production or charging. It is used for inventions where solar cells are the primary means of energy conversion.
[E05B 2047/0065] Saving energy  E05B2047/0053 ,  E05B2047/0074 ,  E05B2047/0077 ,  E05B2047/0079 ,  E05B2047/0081  take precedence
[E05B 2047/0066] Reduced holding current -- E05B 2047/0066 designates electrically operated locking or latching devices that use a reduced holding current to keep the mechanism in its locked position after actuation, meaning the current required to maintain the closed state is lower than that needed for the initial movement, thereby improving energy efficiency in doors, gates or safes.
[E05B 2047/0067] Monitoring -- E05B 2047/0067 pertains to devices, methods or systems for monitoring the condition or performance of building structures, including sensors and techniques that detect strain, displacement, vibration or other parameters to assess structural health.
[E05B 2047/0068] Door closed -- E05B 2047/0068 classifies inventions that provide means for keeping a door closed, including latches, locks, sealing devices and automatic closing mechanisms used in buildings, vehicles, containers or other structures.
[E05B 2047/0069] bolt position -- This CPC subclass concerns the placement of bolts within building or civil-engineering structures, including the spacing, pattern and orientation of bolts used to join components such as beams, plates or foundations. It covers methods and criteria for selecting bolt positions to achieve required strength, load distribution and stability in construction assemblies.
[E05B 2047/0071] Connecting lockparts by electronic communication means only, e.g. bus systems, time multiplexing -- The code classifies locking systems whose components are linked solely by electronic signals rather than mechanical connections. It includes arrangements using bus networks, time-multiplexed channels or other electronic communication methods to coordinate lock parts such as actuators, sensors and controllers.
[E05B 2047/0072] Operation -- This subgroup defines the means of operating doors, windows, shutters, skylights or similar openings, covering manual levers, hydraulic or electric drives, gear arrangements and control systems that effect opening or closing. It includes the mechanical and electromechanical features that enable the movement of the opening device.
[E05B 2047/0073] Current to unlock only -- E05B 2047/0073 covers vehicle closure systems in which electrical power is supplied only for the unlocking function, such as key-less entry or electric lock-release mechanisms that do not energise the entire door or window assembly. This subclass is used for designs that limit current to the unlocking actuator alone.
[E05B 2047/0074] holding means other than current (mechanical, magnetic) -- E05B 2047/0074 covers devices that secure or hold rotating parts of an electrical machine using methods other than electric or magnetic forces, such as mechanical brakes, clutches, locks, or hydraulic-pneumatic retainers. These solutions are applied to generators, motors, or similar equipment where non-current based holding is required.
[E05B 2047/0075] locked by gravity -- E05B 2047/0075 covers locking mechanisms that rely on gravitational force to engage or retain a door, gate, or similar opening. The lock automatically closes or stays closed when the weight of a component pulls a latch or bolt into the locked position, without the need for external actuation.
[E05B 2047/0076] Current to lock only, i.e. "fail-safe" -- E05B 2047/0076 in the Cooperative Patent Classification refers to door-locking mechanisms that are energized only while electrical power is applied, releasing automatically when the supply is cut off. Such "fail-safe" locks are used where safety requires the door to open in a power loss, for example in emergency-exit or fire-protection systems. This entry falls under the broader category of safety devices for building doors.
[E05B 2047/0077] holding means other than current -- The CPC subclass E05B 2047/0077 covers devices that secure or retain doors, gates, hatches, covers or similar openings using non-electrical means. It includes mechanical latches, springs, magnets, hydraulic or pneumatic locks, gravity-based catches and any other holding mechanisms that do not rely on an electric current. This grouping excludes purely electric or electro-magnetic actuated fasteners.
[E05B 2047/0078] unlocked by gravity -- Covers devices that release a lock or restraint solely by the action of gravity, such as gates, doors or safety covers that open when a weighted element drops or a pendulum moves, without the need for external power or manual actuation.
[E05B 2047/0079] Bi-stable electromagnet(s), different pulse to lock or unlock -- E05B 2047/0079 covers bi-stable electromagnets that change state with a distinct pulse, using one pulse to lock the device and a different pulse to release it. The classification applies to magnetic locking mechanisms where the electromagnet remains latched without continuous power until an unlock pulse is applied.
[E05B 2047/0081] Same pulse to lock or unlock -- E05B 2047/0081 pertains to building or structural components that use a single control pulse to both engage and release a locking mechanism, such as bolts, latches or connectors, enabling the same signal to perform lock and unlock functions.
[E05B 2047/0082] Induction for charging or current transformation -- E05B 2047/0082 covers inductive devices used within building electrical installations to transfer energy without direct contacts, such as wireless chargers for batteries or systems that convert one current type to another by magnetic coupling. The entry applies to the design and implementation of induction-based charging stations, floor-embedded power pads, or other infrastructure that supplies or transforms electrical power through electromagnetic fields.
[E05B 2047/0083] Devices of electrically driving keys, e.g. to facilitate opening -- This CPC subclass covers devices that electrically actuate keys to facilitate opening doors, cabinets or other closures, including motor-driven or solenoid-driven key actuators used in security systems, automotive locks and similar applications.
[E05B 2047/0084] Key or electric means;  Emergency release -- E05B 2047/0084 covers door, gate or barrier fittings that are operated by a key, an electric actuator or an emergency-release device. It includes mechanisms that allow manual or powered unlocking, especially those designed for quick egress in emergencies, such as panic bars and electronic exit controls.
[E05B 2047/0085] Key and electromagnet -- The subclass covers locking devices in which a key actuates an electromagnet to retain or release a bolt, including electronic door strikes, magnetic locks triggered by a key and similar combined key-electromagnet mechanisms.
[E05B 2047/0086] Emergency release, e.g. key or electromagnet  E05B63/0073  takes precedence
[E05B 2047/0087] Electric spare devices, e.g. auxiliary batteries or capacitors for back up -- E05B 2047/0087 covers electric spare devices that supply backup power to elevators, escalators, moving walks and similar equipment, such as auxiliary batteries or capacitors used to keep the system running during a power interruption.
[E05B 2047/0088] Key-operated switch -- A key-operated switch is a device in which turning a key opens or closes an electrical circuit, providing manual control that is restricted to authorized users. It is commonly employed in security panels, lighting systems and equipment where activation must be limited to personnel possessing the correct key. This type of switch combines mechanical key action with an electrical contact mechanism.
[E05B 2047/0089] with electronic learning functions -- E05B 2047/0089 categorises building elements or facilities that are equipped with electronic learning functions, covering integrated digital teaching aids, interactive displays, networked instructional systems and other e-learning technologies built into the structure of educational or training spaces.
[E05B 2047/0091] Retrofittable electric locks, e.g. an electric module can be attached to an existing manual lock -- E05B 2047/0091 pertains to retrofittable electric locks, where an electric actuation module can be attached to an existing manual lock to provide electronic control without replacing the original hardware.
[E05B 2047/0092] including means for preventing manipulation by an external magnetic field, e.g. preventing opening by using a strong magnet
[E05B 2047/0093] including means for preventing manipulation by external shocks, blows or the like -- E05B 2047/0093 covers door, gate, window or similar closure devices that incorporate structures or mechanisms designed to resist manipulation caused by external shocks, blows or impacts, thereby enhancing security against forced entry or vandalism. It includes shock-absorbing elements, reinforced frames or impact-mitigating fittings.
[E05B 2047/0094] Mechanical aspects of remotely controlled locks -- E05B 2047/0094 covers the mechanical design of locks that can be operated remotely, including the structures and components that enable actuation by electronic, magnetic, pneumatic or hydraulic signals. It addresses lock cylinders, bolts, deadbolts and associated control mechanisms that allow unlocking or locking without direct manual interaction.
[E05B 2047/0095] Mechanical aspects of locks controlled by telephone signals, e.g. by mobile phones -- E05B 2047/0095 covers lock devices whose mechanical operation is triggered by telephone or mobile-phone signals, including the structures and components that translate received communication signals into locking or unlocking actions. It pertains to electromechanical mechanisms that enable remote control of entry security.
[E05B 2047/0096] Mechanical aspects of locks whose actuation is dependent on geographical location, e.g. by means of a GPS system -- This classification covers devices where the lock's mechanical operation is controlled by its position, using location detection like GPS to enable or disable opening. It includes mechanisms that sense geographic coordinates and translate them into lock actuation.
[E05B 2047/0097] including means for monitoring voltage, e.g. for indicating low battery state -- E05B 2047/0097 relates to energy-generating installations that include voltage-monitoring components, such as circuitry or sensors that detect low battery conditions and provide an indication. The code is applied when the monitoring function is integrated into systems like wind turbines, solar panels, or other renewable-energy devices to manage power supply and alert users to insufficient charge levels.
[E05B 2047/0098] Arrangements for grounding electrical locks or elements thereof, e.g. for avoiding shocks or failure due to static electricity -- E05B 2047/0098 covers arrangements that provide an earthing connection for electrical locks or their components to prevent shock hazards or malfunction caused by static electricity. The classification includes grounding paths, conductive contacts, and protective circuits designed to safely dissipate stray charges from lock actuators, sensors, or electromagnets. It applies to access-control hardware where electrical safety is essential.
[E05B 2051/026] with manually generated fluid pressure -- E05B 2051/026 covers crane, hoist or winch mechanisms that employ fluid power generated manually, for example by a hand-operated pump, to create hydraulic or pneumatic pressure that assists the lifting or moving of a load. It includes designs where the operator's manual effort is used to pressurise a fluid system that then drives the lifting cylinder or actuator.
[E05B 2063/0026] Elongated, e.g. stud-like, striker entering into an opening in which movable detent means engage the elongated striker -- The CPC entry E05B 2063/0026 covers door or gate locking arrangements where an elongated, stud-like striker is inserted into a recess and a movable detent such as a latch or bolt engages the striker to hold the opening shut. It addresses designs in which the striker extends along the plane of the door and the detent can be released to allow opening.
[E05B 2063/0039] Ground mounted locks or lock elements -- E05B 2063/0039 pertains to ground-mounted locking devices, covering lock mechanisms and lock elements that are installed directly in or on the floor, such as floor-mounted deadbolts, ground-anchored padlocks, and lock boxes secured to the ground surface. This subclass addresses their structural features, mounting arrangements, and means of securing them to ensure resistance against tampering or removal.
[E05B 2063/0082] Locking modes -- E05B 2063/0082 relates to the different ways a lock can be set, released or operated, including key-turn, thumb-turn, push-button, electronic or combination modes, and the transition between engaged and disengaged states.
[E05B 2063/0086] Handles used only for locking, while unlocking is achieved by other means -- The code covers door or gate handles that function solely to secure a closure, with the release of the lock performed by a separate mechanism such as a key, button, sensor or remote device. It applies to hardware where the handle itself does not provide the unlocking action.
[E05B 2063/0091] Lock actuation dependent on temperature -- A temperature-responsive locking mechanism that activates or releases the lock when a predefined temperature threshold is reached, using thermal expansion, phase-change materials, or heat-sensitive components. The system may be employed in safes, doors, or containers where temperature changes serve as a trigger for securing or unlocking. It is classified under E05B 2063/0091 for lock actuation dependent on temperature.
[E05B 2063/0095] Locking devices actuated or controlled by seismic activity, e.g. earthquakes -- This subclass covers lock mechanisms that are triggered or controlled by seismic activity such as earthquakes, using ground motion to engage or release the device. It includes safety locks for doors, windows and equipment that automatically secure during an earthquake to prevent damage or injury.
[E05B 2063/207] Automatic deadlocking -- A deadlocking mechanism that engages automatically when a door, gate or comparable closure is shut, preventing it from being opened without a separate unlocking action. It typically uses a spring-biased bolt or latch that snaps into a locked position on closure, providing security for vehicles, locks, cabinets and similar structures.
[E05B 2065/0039] with emergency release -- The CPC subclass E05B 2065/0039 refers to door-locking or securing devices that incorporate an emergency-release feature, allowing a locked door to be opened quickly in a crisis such as fire or panic. It covers mechanisms that automatically or manually disengage the lock when a specific emergency condition is detected or when a release device is activated. This includes safety-exit hardware used in buildings, vehicles and other structures.
[E05B 2065/0096] Locks for sport stadiums -- E05B 2065/0096 covers locking devices specially adapted for sport stadiums, including crowd-control gate locks, turnstile mechanisms and large-capacity door systems that secure entrances, exits and seating areas.
[E05B 2065/0805] with a handle operating in both directions -- A bidirectional hand-operated winch in which a single handle can be turned in either direction to wind or unwind the rope or cable, enabling manual lifting or lowering without changing the tool. This arrangement is used in crane, hoist or similar lifting devices where quick reversal of motion is required.
[E05B 2065/469] for drawers which can be opened from both sides -- E05B 2065/469 covers drawer designs that allow opening from either side of the unit, such as dual-access cabinet or bedside drawers, enabling simultaneous access from both left and right openings. The classification is used for furniture and storage solutions where bidirectional entry improves ergonomics or space efficiency.
[E05B 2067/025] Bumpers -- E05B 2067/025 pertains to bumpers, the structural elements mounted on the front, rear or sides of vehicles to absorb impact energy and protect the vehicle body and occupants during collisions; the classification includes the design, materials, attachment methods and functional features of such impact-absorbing devices for road, railway and other transport vehicles.
[E05B 2067/066] Shackle extensions -- The CPC entry E05B 2067/066 relates to extensions that are attached to a shackle to provide additional length or a movable attachment point for ropes, cables, chains or similar load-bearing elements. These extensions are typically tubular or hinged members fastened to the shackle body to adapt to varying geometries in construction, lifting or rigging applications.
[E05B 2067/386] Padlock holders -- The CPC subclass E05B 2067/386 covers devices designed to hold or secure padlocks on structures such as fences, gates, or bars. It includes brackets, loops, or mounts that keep a padlock in place while allowing easy attachment and removal, facilitating access control and theft deterrence.
[E05B 2073/0088] being lockable or unlockable by means of the office machine itself, e.g. by its keyboard -- This subclass covers office machines that incorporate internal locking or unlocking mechanisms, such as controls on the keyboard, enabling the device to be secured or released without external accessories. It relates to built-in security features that can be activated or deactivated by the machine itself.
[E05B 21/00] Locks with rotary keys moving lamelliform tublers perpendicular to the key -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks with plate tumblers wherein, when the correct key is inserted into the lock, the tumblers are not immediately moved to their unlocked positions. Only when the key is thereafter turned, the tumblers are moved to their unlocked positions. The movement of the tumblers does not follow the movement of the bolt, which means that the tumblers usually are mounted to the lock housing. This group has the following structure:
[E05B 21/003] comprising tumblers moving in opposite directions, e.g. tumbler sets on opposite sides of the keyhole for operation by a double-bit key -- E05B 21/003 covers lock mechanisms in which a rotary key actuates lamellar tumblers that slide perpendicular to the key's axis, with sets of tumblers moving in opposite directions - such as a double-bit key engaging tumblers on both sides of the keyhole.
[E05B 21/006] the bolt being operated by other means than the key bit, e.g. via a key-operated thrower -- E05B 21/006 relates to rotary-key locks with lamelliform tumblers where the bolt is released by a separate mechanism rather than direct pressure from the key bit, for example a key-operated thrower that transfers motion to the bolt. This subclass covers designs that use an intermediate actuator to move the bolt.
[E05B 21/06] Cylinder locks, e.g. protector locks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cylinder locks wherein the tumblers are not set by pushing the key in.
[E05B 21/063] of the sliding-plate tumbler type -- E05B 21/063 covers locks in which a rotary key operates lamelliform tumblers that move perpendicularly to the key, specifically of the sliding-plate tumbler type. In these mechanisms the tumblers are thin plates that slide within a housing to align shear lines when the correct key is turned, providing a fixed construction lock.
[E05B 21/066] of the rotary-disc tumbler type  tumblers which are set by inserting the key  E05B29/0013 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cylinder locks with rotary-disc tumblers wherein the tumblers are not set by pushing the key in.
[E05B 23/00] Locks with lamelliform tumblers in which the tumblers follow the movement of the bolt -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks, other than cylinder locks, wherein when the key is turned to move the bolt the tumblers follow the movement of the bolt, e.g. the tumblers are pivotally mounted on the bolt.
[E05B 27/00] Cylinder locks with tumbler pins or balls that are set by pushing the key in -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cylinder locks with tumbler pins or balls which are set by pushing the key in and parts thereof, i.e. rotors, stators and tumblers.
[E05B 27/0003] Details
[E05B 27/0007] Rotors -- The subclass E05B 27/0007 covers rotors used in cylinder locks whose tumblers-pins or balls-are engaged by pushing the key in. It pertains to the rotating element that translates the key's motion into the locking and unlocking of the mechanism.
[E05B 27/001] having relatively movable parts, e.g. coaxial- or split-plugs  for anti-picking purposes  E05B27/0075
[E05B 27/0014] Stators  cylinder lock casings in general  E05B9/04
[E05B 27/0017] Tumblers or pins -- E05B 27/0017 relates to cylinder locks that employ tumbler pins or balls as the locking elements, which are set into their locking positions by the insertion of a key. The code is nested under fixed constructions and locks, describing the internal pin or ball mechanism that engages the key to secure or release the lock.
[E05B 27/0021] having movable parts -- The subclass includes cylinder locks whose tumbler pins or spherical balls are displaced by pushing the key into the lock, causing internal movable parts to shift and allow the plug to rotate. It covers designs where the key's axial pressure, rather than twisting, sets the lock's retaining elements, enabling the unlocking action.
[E05B 27/0028] Other locks than cylinder locks with tumbler pins or balls -- This subclass covers lock mechanisms that are not based on cylinder designs using tumbler pins or balls. It includes various types such as lever, disc, or electronic locks where the key or actuation does not rely on a rotating cylinder. The classification is used for devices where the locking principle differs from conventional pin-tumbler cylinder locks.
[E05B 27/0032] with both axially and radially arranged tumbler pins or balls -- This subclass refers to cylinder locks whose key engages tumbler pins or balls arranged both along the axis of the cylinder and perpendicular to it, allowing simultaneous axial and radial movement to lock or unlock. It is nested under fixed constructions and the broader category of locks.
[E05B 27/0035] with indirectly actuated tumblers -- Cylinder locks in which the key does not press the tumblers directly but moves a cam, lever or other intermediate mechanism that then sets the pins or balls to the required positions for unlocking. This indirect actuation allows the key to engage the locking mechanism without direct contact with each tumbler.
[E05B 27/0039] with pins which slide and rotate about their axis -- This subclass covers cylinder locks whose locking elements are pins that both slide and rotate about their own axis when the key is inserted. The pins move linearly and pivot to align with the key's cuts, enabling the plug to turn. It is commonly used in residential and commercial deadbolt locks.
[E05B 27/0042] with additional key identifying function, e.g. with use of additional key operated rotor-blocking elements, not of split pin tumbler type  E05B27/0078  takes precedence -- Cylinder locks that use tumbler pins or balls set by inserting the key and include an extra key-driven identifying function, such as a rotor-blocking element, which is not of the split-pin tumbler type. This provides an additional security feature.
[E05B 27/0046] Axially movable rotor -- E05B 27/0046 classifies cylinder locks whose rotor moves axially along the lock axis as the key is inserted, allowing the tumblers or balls to be set and the locking mechanism to engage or release. This subcategory falls under fixed-construction locks that are set by pushing the key in.
[E05B 27/005] with changeable combinations -- Cylinder locks that employ pin or ball tumblers set by inserting a key, where the lock's combination can be changed after it is installed. The mechanism allows the user to reset the pin or ball positions to accept a new key, providing a re-keyable security solution within the fixed-construction lock category.
[E05B 27/0053] for use with more than one key, e.g. master-secondary key -- This subclass pertains to cylinder locks using tumbler pins or balls that can be opened by more than one key, such as master-secondary keying arrangements, allowing multiple keys to operate the same lock.
[E05B 27/0057] with increased picking resistance -- Cylinder locks that use tumbler pins or balls set by pushing the key in, designed with features that make them harder to pick, such as anti-pick mechanisms, complex pin configurations, or enhanced spring tension. These designs increase security by resisting common lock-picking techniques while maintaining normal key operation.
[E05B 27/006] whereby a small rotation without the correct key blocks further rotation of the rotor  E05B27/0067  takes precedence -- E05B 27/006 covers cylinder locks that use tumbler pins or balls which are positioned by the forward motion of the key; the mechanism allows a slight rotation of the rotor, but without the correct key the rotor is blocked from further turning. This feature is superseded by the more specific subclass E05B 27/0067.
[E05B 27/0064] whereby the rotor is irreversibly blocked or can only be moved back with an authorized tool or key -- This subclass defines cylinder locks whose rotor becomes irreversibly blocked after actuation and can be released only with a specific tool or an authorized key, falling under fixed-construction locks that use tumbler pins or balls.
[E05B 27/0067] whereby a small rotation without the correct key blocks further movement of a tumbler  E05B27/0082  takes precedence -- This subclass covers cylinder locks whose tumblers are designed to stop further movement if an incorrect key causes a slight rotation, acting as a built-in blocking mechanism that enhances security. The preceding subclass E05B 27/0082, which deals with related lock features, takes precedence over this provision.
[E05B 27/0071] by means preventing opening by using the bump-technique -- E05B 27/0071 covers cylinder locks that use tumbler pins or balls set by pushing the key in, where the lock is designed to resist opening by the bump-technique. It falls under the fixed-construction locks category and addresses security features that prevent bumping attacks.
[E05B 27/0075] by movable rotor elements -- This subclass covers cylinder locks where the key actuates a movable rotor that rotates to align the tumbler pins or balls into the unlocking position. The rotor element replaces fixed pins, allowing the lock to be set by pushing the key in and turning it.
[E05B 27/0078] Asymmetrical tumbler pins, e.g. with a key operating on a radial protrusion of a tumbler pin -- The code E05B 27/0078 classifies cylinder locks in which the tumbler pins are asymmetrical, often featuring a radial protrusion that the key engages to set the pin. This arrangement differs from standard symmetrical pins and is used to enhance security or key control in lock mechanisms.
[E05B 27/0082] Side bar locking -- The entry relates to cylinder locks that use a side-bar as the primary locking element; the bar slides into a corresponding recess when the key is inserted, securing the lock. It applies to pin-tumbler or ball-tumbler mechanisms where the side bar's movement is set by the key's action.
[E05B 27/0085] wherein the key can be inserted or withdrawn in different positions or directions  in general  E05B63/0047 -- The CPC subclass E05B 27/0085 addresses cylinder locks that use tumbler pins or balls and allow the key to be inserted or withdrawn in multiple positions or directions. It falls under fixed-construction locks where key entry is not limited to a single orientation, covering general arrangements for versatile key manipulation.
[E05B 27/0089] wherein the key can be withdrawn in several angular positions -- This subclass covers cylinder locks whose key can be removed in multiple angular positions after turning, indicating designs where the key can be withdrawn at various angles rather than a single fixed orientation. It falls under fixed constructions, locks, cylinder locks with tumbler pins or balls.
[E05B 27/0092] wherein the key can be inserted from both sides of the lock -- This subclass refers to cylinder locks that use tumbler pins or balls and are designed so the key can be inserted from either side of the lock, allowing operation regardless of the direction of entry.
[E05B 27/0096] reversible keys -- A reversible key is a cylinder lock key that can be inserted and turned in either direction to set the tumbler pins or balls, allowing the lock to be opened from both orientations. This design is used in lock systems where convenient access from multiple positions is required.
[E05B 27/02] operated by the edge of the key -- E05B 27/02 designates cylinder locks whose tumblers, pins or balls, are moved by the edge of the inserted key. The key's profile pushes the pins to align the shear line, allowing the cylinder to rotate and release the lock.
[E05B 27/08] arranged axially
[E05B 27/083] of the split-pin tumbler type -- E05B 27/083 covers cylinder locks that use split-pin tumblers, where the key pushes pins to align shear lines for unlocking. These are fixed-construction locks employing pins or balls that are displaced by the key.
[E05B 27/086] of the bar-tumbler type, the bars having slots or protrusions in alignment upon opening the lock -- A cylinder lock of the bar-tumbler type where each bar carries slots or protrusions that line up when the key is inserted, allowing the lock to open. The alignment of these features releases the bolt or latch, characterising this specific mechanism within fixed-construction locks.
[E05B 27/10] operated by other surfaces of the key, e.g. openings receiving projections on the tumblers -- Cylinder locks in which the key actuates the tumblers via surfaces other than the traditional ridges, such as openings that receive projections on the pins or balls, allowing the lock to be set by inserting the key and engaging these specially shaped recesses. This design falls under the fixed constructions category of lock mechanisms.
[E05B 29/00] Cylinder locks with plate tumblers which are set by pushing the key in -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cylinder locks with plate tumblers which are set by pushing the key in. The plate tumblers can slide, rotate or float, e.g. slide and rotate.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Lock cylinder arrangements vehicles -> E05B85/06
[E05B 29/0006] Other locks than cylinder locks with plate tumblers -- E05B 29/0006 covers lock mechanisms that are not cylinder locks using plate tumblers. It includes any other lock types such as wafer, pin, magnetic, electronic or other mechanical constructions that employ different actuation principles. The class is used for patents describing alternative locking designs.
[E05B 29/0013] with rotating plate tumblers  E05B29/0033  takes precedence; plate tumblers which are not set by inserting the key  E05B21/06 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cylinder locks with rotary tumblers, wherein, when the key is inserted into the lock the tumblers are set to the right combination.
[E05B 29/002] rotating about an axis perpendicular to the key axis -- This subclass covers cylinder locks that employ plate tumblers set by pushing the key, where the tumblers rotate about an axis perpendicular to the key's axis. It falls under fixed-construction lock designs.
[E05B 29/0026] with longitudinally movable cylinder -- This subclass covers cylinder locks that use plate tumblers and are actuated by pushing the key in, where the entire cylinder moves longitudinally during operation. It is part of the fixed-construction locks hierarchy, focusing on the mechanical action of a moving cylinder rather than rotating tumblers.
[E05B 29/0033] with floating plate tumblers, i.e. sliding or sliding and rotating in a plane -- This subclass covers cylinder locks in which the key pushes plate tumblers that are not fixed but can float within the cylinder, moving linearly or both linearly and rotationally in a plane to align with the shear line. Such floating plate tumblers are used to improve lock reliability and resistance to picking.
[E05B 29/004] with changeable combinations -- This subclass pertains to cylinder locks that employ plate tumblers operated by pushing the key into the cylinder, wherein the lock's combination can be changed after manufacture. It is grouped under fixed-construction locks in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[E05B 29/0046] for use with more than one key, e.g. master-secondary key -- This subgroup covers cylinder locks that use plate tumblers and are actuated by pushing the key, specifically those configured to accept more than one key, such as master-secondary key arrangements.
[E05B 29/0053] with increased picking resistance
[E05B 29/006] by movable rotor elements -- E05B 29/006 pertains to cylinder locks that use plate tumblers positioned by a key that is simply pushed in, where the tumblers are actuated by movable rotor elements that rotate as the key turns. The classification captures mechanisms in which the key drives a rotor that lifts or releases the tumblers to allow the lock to open.
[E05B 29/0066] Side bar locking -- Side bar locking is a mechanism in cylinder locks with plate tumblers where a sliding side bar moves into a recess in the bolt or housing to secure the lock when the key is inserted and turned. The bar is typically driven by the key's action and prevents the bolt from retracting until the key is removed.
[E05B 29/0073] wherein the key can be inserted or withdrawn in different positions or directions  in general  E05B63/0047 -- The code E05B 29/0073 designates cylinder locks that employ plate tumblers and are operated by pushing the key in, with the distinctive feature that the key may be inserted or withdrawn in different positions or directions, allowing multiple orientations for actuation.
[E05B 29/008] wherein the key can be withdrawn in several angular positions -- This CPC subclass (E05B 29/008) pertains to cylinder locks with plate tumblers whose key can be withdrawn in several angular positions after insertion, a feature of fixed-construction locks.
[E05B 29/0086] wherein the key can be inserted from both sides of the lock -- This CPC subgroup concerns cylinder locks that employ plate tumblers set by a pushing key action, designed so the key can be inserted from either side of the lock housing. Such double-sided key entry is used to facilitate access in situations where the lock orientation may vary.
[E05B 29/0093] reversible keys -- A reversible key is a key that can be inserted and turned in either orientation to operate a cylinder lock whose plate tumblers are set by simply pushing the key in. This design lets the lock be opened from both ends of the key, improving convenience and key-control flexibility.
[E05B 3/00] Fastening handles to lock or latch parts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fastening the knob or handle to lock or latch parts, in particular to the lock spindle, as well as fastening the spindle to the lock follower.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Mounting handles to a vehicle wing -> E05B79/06
[E05B 3/003] Fastening knobs or handles to hollow cylindrical spindles, e.g. of tubular locks -- The subclass E05B 3/003 covers methods and devices for attaching knobs or handles to hollow cylindrical spindles, such as the spindle of a tubular lock, within the broader group of fastening handles to lock or latch parts. It is part of the fixed-construction locks hierarchy.
[E05B 3/02] Fastening handles to the spindle by pinning or riveting -- The CPC subclass E05B 3/02 covers devices that attach a handle to a spindle using pins or rivets. It falls within the broader groups for fixed constructions and locks, specifically addressing fastening methods for handles that enable manual operation of the lock or latch.
[E05B 3/04] Fastening the handle shank to the spindle by screws, springs or snap bolts -- This subclass details ways to attach the handle shank to the spindle of a lock by means of screws, springs or snap bolts, providing a secure connection between the handle and the locking mechanism. It is coded E05B 3/04 within the Cooperative Patent Classification for fixed constructions and locks.
[E05B 3/06] by means arranged in or on the rose -- E05B 3/06 covers fastening handles to a lock or latch by using mechanisms that are arranged in or on the rose-the rotating dial or wheel of the lock. It includes designs where the handle is integrated with the dial or attached to it so that turning the rose also operates the handle, providing a combined control and fastening function.
[E05B 3/065] with spring biasing means for moving the handle over a substantial distance, e.g. to its horizontal position -- The subclass E05B 3/065 covers lock mechanisms in which the handle is spring-biased so that, when released, it travels a substantial distance-typically moving to a horizontal position-to secure or release the latch. This design is used for fastening handles that lock or latch parts, providing a rapid, self-acting motion aided by a spring.
[E05B 3/08] Fastening the spindle to the follower -- The E05B 3/08 sub-group covers devices and methods for securing the spindle-the rotating shaft that drives a lock-to the follower, the moving component that engages the bolt or latch. It includes various fastening techniques such as screws, pins, clips or adhesive means that bind the spindle directly to the follower in fixed-construction locking systems.
[E05B 3/10] by a bipartite or cleft spindle in the follower or in the handle shank -- This subclass covers fastening handles to lock or latch mechanisms where a two-part or split spindle is provided either in the follower component or within the shank of the handle to transmit motion. The design uses a bipartite or cleft spindle to engage the lock.
[E05B 31/00] Cylinder locks with both tumbler pins or balls and plate tumblers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cylinder locks with both tumbler pins or balls and plate tumblers which are set by pushing the key in.
[E05B 33/00] Cylinder locks in which the bolt is moved by means other than the key -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cylinder locks with tumblers which are set by pushing the key in and wherein the rotor is moved by means other than the key.
[E05B 35/00] Locks for use with special keys or a plurality of keys -- **Definition:** This place covers:  This group has the following structure:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Keys adjustable before use -> E05B19/18 - Mechanical locks operated by cards having magnets -> E05B47/0043 - Keys with magnets -> E05B47/0045 - Mechanical keys for electric permutation locks -> E05B49/00 - Non-mechanical keys for electronic locks -> G07C9/00944
[E05B 35/001] with key identifying function -- A lock in which the key itself determines the lock's operation, using an identifying feature such as a coded profile, magnetic strip, RFID chip, or other encoded element. This subclass covers locks designed for special keys or for situations where multiple distinct keys must be recognised to grant access.
[E05B 35/002] for flexible keys -- This subcategory covers lock mechanisms designed to be operated by flexible keys, such as leaf or wire keys that can be bent or shaped to fit the lock. It applies to locks that accept a plurality of such keys or special key designs, typically used in high-security or multi-user applications.
[E05B 35/003] for keys with movable bits  keys adjustable before use  E05B19/18 -- Code E05B 35/003 classifies locks designed to be operated with special keys whose biting elements can be moved or adjusted prior to use. Such keys allow the user to change the pattern of the bits, enabling a single key blank to be configured for different lock settings or to accommodate multiple users. The classification falls under locks for use with special or multiple keys.
[E05B 35/004] pivoting about an axis perpendicular to the main key axis -- The entry covers fixed-construction locks that operate with special or multiple keys and feature a mechanism that pivots around an axis perpendicular to the main key axis, enabling the locking element to rotate in a different plane than the key turn.
[E05B 35/005] pivoting about an axis parallel to the main key axis -- The sub-category E05B 35/005 concerns lock mechanisms in which the moving bolt or latch pivots about an axis that runs parallel to the main key axis, enabling operation by special or multiple keys. It is part of the fixed-construction locks designed for use with distinct or plural key configurations.
[E05B 35/006] for rack- or pinion-like keys -- E05B 35/006 relates to fixed lock constructions that are operated by rack-or pinion-shaped keys, intended for use with special keys or a set of multiple keys.
[E05B 35/007] the key being a card, e.g. perforated, or the like  E05B47/0045  takes precedence
[E05B 35/008] for simple tool-like keys -- E05B 35/008 covers lock mechanisms that are opened with simple tool-like keys, such as screwdrivers, hex wrenches or other basic implements rather than conventional key blanks. These locks are intended for applications where a single specialized tool or a set of interchangeable tools serves as the key, providing a cost-effective and robust security solution.
[E05B 35/02] which can be shifted laterally -- This CPC subgroup covers locks in which the bolt or latch moves sideways rather than straight forward or backward, allowing operation by special keys or multiple keys. It applies to fixed-construction locking devices whose locking element is shifted laterally to engage or release.
[E05B 35/06] for screw keys -- This subclass covers locks that are operated by screw keys - a type of special key that engages a threaded component to actuate the lock, commonly used in safes, cabinets, and certain industrial locking mechanisms.
[E05B 35/08] operable by a plurality of keys -- E05B 35/08 designates locks that can be opened by more than one key, for example master-key systems or locks built to accept several distinct keys. The classification falls under fixed constructions and the broader group of locks for use with special or multiple keys.
[E05B 35/083] with changeable combination  E05B35/125  takes precedence; for cylinder locks  E05B27/005 ,  E05B29/004 -- This CPC entry covers fixed lock constructions that use special or multiple keys and feature a changeable combination, particularly for cylinder-type locks; it supersedes the E05B35/125 subclass and is related to the E05B27/005 and E05B29/004 categories.
[E05B 35/086] using a pair of interlocked keys, one of the keys being non-removable; key-storing devices from which only one key can be removed at a time -- The entry covers lock arrangements that employ a pair of interlocked keys, one fixed to the lock and the other removable, as well as key-storing devices designed so that only a single key can be taken out at a time, providing controlled access and preventing simultaneous removal of multiple keys.
[E05B 35/10] with master and pass keys -- Locks designed to operate with a master key that can open multiple locks and with individual pass keys that grant selective access. This arrangement allows a hierarchy of key authority while maintaining the ability to restrict certain users to specific lock groups.
[E05B 35/105] Locks allowing opening by official authorities, e.g. master key opening of luggage locks by customs officials -- E05B 35/105 covers locks that can be opened by official authorities using a master key, such as customs officers unlocking luggage. These devices are designed to permit access by designated personnel while remaining secure against ordinary users.
[E05B 35/12] requiring the use of two keys, e.g. safe-deposit locks -- E05B 35/12 covers locks that must be opened with two distinct keys, such as those used in safe-deposit boxes, where each key performs a separate function to release the lock mechanism.
[E05B 35/125] with changeable combination -- This classification covers locks that allow the combination to be altered, such as changeable-combination locks used with multiple or special keys.
[E05B 35/14] with keys of which different parts operate separate mechanisms -- E05B 35/14 relates to locks that employ a single key composed of separate parts, each part actuating a different internal mechanism, so distinct sections of the key control separate locking or unlocking actions within the same device.
[E05B 37/00] Permutation locks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Mechanical combination locks, padlocks with combination locks and puzzle locks.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Handles with combination locks -> E05B13/103 - Keyhole guards with combination locks -> E05B17/145 - Electric permutation locks -> E05B49/00
[E05B 37/0003] preventing detection of the combination -- This subgroup relates to mechanisms that conceal the selected arrangement of a permutation lock so an observer cannot tell which sequence has been set, for example by using cover plates, false markings or dummy wheels. It covers security features that hide the combination from visual inspection.
[E05B 37/0006] by avoiding unauthorized viewing of the combination -- The subclass E05B 37/0006 relates to permutation locks that include means to prevent an unauthorized person from seeing the combination, such as covers, shields, or obscuring devices that hide dial settings or code entries while the lock is being operated.
[E05B 37/001] through listening or feeling -- E05B 37/001 covers techniques for opening permutation locks by using auditory or tactile cues, such as listening for clicks or feeling resistance to identify the correct sequence of wheels or tumblers. These methods rely on sensory feedback rather than visual inspection to determine when the lock aligns properly.
[E05B 37/0013] by producing noise -- A permutation lock that signals correct alignment or opening by generating an audible sound, such as a click, tone or alarm, rather than by visual or tactile feedback. The noise may be produced mechanically or electronically to confirm the correct combination or to deter tampering. This approach is used in various security devices where an audible indication of successful unlocking is desired.
[E05B 37/0017] by producing false clicks; by use of false gates -- E05B 37/0017 covers permutation locks that gain security by incorporating false clicks or false gate positions into the combination mechanism. The false tactile feedback creates additional, non-functional positions that mislead an operator or attacker, making it harder to determine the correct sequence. This subclass is a refinement of fixed-construction lock designs that use deceptive mechanical stops to protect against picking.
[E05B 37/002] Speed sensors
[E05B 37/0024] limited number of tries -- E05B 37/0024 covers permutation locks that incorporate a mechanism limiting the number of possible combination attempts before the lock becomes inoperable or resets, thereby enhancing security by preventing repeated trial-and-error entry. This feature is applied to fixed-construction locks where the user must stop after a predetermined count of tries.
[E05B 37/0027] X-ray protection
[E05B 37/0031] Locks with both permutation and key actuation  E05B17/145  takes precedence -- E05B 37/0031 covers lock devices that open only when a correct permutation sequence is set and a key is turned, combining combination-type and key-operated mechanisms. It falls under fixed constructions → locks → permutation locks. For classification purposes the broader subclass E05B 17/145 is considered the primary reference.
[E05B 37/0034] actuated by either -- Permutation locks that may be opened by more than one type of actuation, such as a key or a manual combination mechanism, using rotating elements to align internal pins. The device typically incorporates a set of dials or tumblers whose positions can be altered to permit unlocking by either method.
[E05B 37/0037] with a key requiring successive operations, e.g. different mechanisms in a predetermined order  E05B35/003  takes precedence -- E05B 37/0037 covers permutation locks that operate with a single key but demand a series of successive actions, each engaging a different locking mechanism in a predetermined sequence. The lock's security derives from the ordered combination of these mechanisms, and its classification is subordinate to the broader E05B35/003 category.
[E05B 37/0041] Key actuation allowing recovery of a lost/forgotten combination
[E05B 37/0044] with telephone-type dial -- Permutation locks that employ a telephone-style rotary dial to select the correct sequence of numbers or symbols, where the dial is turned to specific positions and then released to lock or unlock the mechanism. The dial typically resembles a telephone handset dial and may include a resetting spring or detent mechanism to return to a neutral position after each entry. This design is used in safes, lockers and some security devices.
[E05B 37/0048] with changeable combination
[E05B 37/0051] by moving pins to another hole -- E05B 37/0051 classifies permutation locks that achieve a new combination by relocating pins into alternate holes in the locking mechanism. The move of pins to another hole changes the alignment required to release the lock, distinguishing these designs from fixed-pin or cam-type locks.
[E05B 37/0055] by radial disengagement between hub and rim of tumbler discs or rings -- The subclass E05B 37/0055 covers permutation locks that disengage by a radial separation of the hub from the rim of the tumbler discs or rings, a mechanism used in fixed-construction lock designs. It specifies the way the lock's tumblers move outward relative to the central hub to release the locking position.
[E05B 37/0058] by axial disengagement between hub and rim of tumbler discs or rings -- This subclass covers permutation locks in which the tumbler discs or rings are released by an axial separation of the hub from the rim, allowing the lock to open.
[E05B 37/0062] Operation by feeling, e.g. in darkness using Braille signs -- E05B 37/0062 pertains to fixed lock constructions that are operated by tactile perception, allowing users to manipulate the lock without visual cues, for example by employing Braille symbols or other feeling-based indicators to work the mechanism in darkness.
[E05B 37/0065] Master combination -- E05B 37/0065 covers the master combination of a permutation lock - the single key or code that opens the lock regardless of the individual settings of its rotating wheels or tumblers. It relates to the central setting that aligns all internal elements to permit unlocking, distinct from the multiple independent combinations normally required for operation. This classification is used for devices where a universal unlocking position is provided for maintenance or emergency access.
[E05B 37/0068] in padlocks  E05B37/025 ,  E05B37/10  and  E05B37/14  take precedence -- E05B 37/0068 refers to features found in padlocks, covering the arrangement and operation of the locking mechanism within a padlock body. It falls under the broader classes of fixed constructions and permutation locks, and is subordinated to the more specific groups E05B 37/025, E05B 37/10 and E05B 37/14, which therefore take precedence.
[E05B 37/0072] Sign-, picture- or letter-based permutations -- This class covers permutation lock mechanisms whose combination is set by aligning symbols, pictures or letters rather than numbers. It includes devices where the user selects a sequence of characters to open the lock, such as combination locks using alphabetic or graphic wheels.
[E05B 37/0075] Automatic scrambling
[E05B 37/0079] when unlocking -- E05B 37/0079 concerns the actions or mechanisms that occur at the moment a permutation lock is opened, such as automatic resetting, release of retaining elements or transition to an unlocked state. It covers features that are triggered specifically when the lock is unlocked.
[E05B 37/0082] when locking -- This subgroup covers the condition or act of a permutation lock being engaged, including mechanisms that indicate, maintain or respond to the locked state of the device. It pertains to features such as locking bolts, detectors or visual signals that show the lock is set.
[E05B 37/0086] when the wing is opened -- The entry E05B 37/0086 relates to permutation locks in which a hinged wing is moved to an open position to enable setting, resetting or releasing the lock, commonly found in safes and secure containers. It covers designs where opening the wing triggers the internal tumblers or disengages the locking mechanism.
[E05B 37/0089] when the wing is closed -- The E05B 37/0089 class defines permutation locks whose wing (the rotating or sliding element that engages the bolt) is in the closed position, meaning the lock is set and the mechanism is secured. It falls under the broader category of fixed-construction locks and specifies the geometry and operation when the wing is fully closed.
[E05B 37/0093] Obligatory manual scrambling -- Obligatory manual scrambling is a feature of permutation locks that requires the user to manually rearrange the lock's internal elements or code sequence before the lock can be opened. This mandatory step ensures that only a correctly scrambled configuration permits unlocking, adding an extra layer of security.
[E05B 37/0096] with tumbler discs arranged on concentric axes, each axis being operated by a different rotating knob -- E05B 37/0096 classifies permutation locks that employ tumbler discs mounted on concentric axes, with each axis driven by a separate rotating knob, allowing the user to set a combination by aligning the discs for unlocking.
[E05B 37/02] with tumbler discs or rings arranged on a single axis, each disc being adjustable independently of the others -- This classification covers permutation locks whose security mechanism consists of multiple tumbler discs or rings mounted on a common axis, where each disc can be set to a different position independently of the others. The independent adjustability of the discs allows a large number of possible combinations for unlocking.
[E05B 37/025] in padlocks -- E05B 37/025 covers padlocks that employ a permutation or combination mechanism, where a sequence of numbers or symbols set on rotating disks or dials releases the shackle. These devices lock by aligning internal tumblers to the correct code, allowing the lock body to open without a separate key.
[E05B 37/04] with tumbler discs on a single axis, all the discs being adjustable by rotating a shiftable knob -- This CPC subclass concerns permutation locks that employ several tumbler discs mounted on a single axis, with all discs being set to different positions by turning a shiftable knob. The knob adjusts the alignment of the discs, allowing the lock combination to be changed.
[E05B 37/08] with tumbler discs on a single axis, all the discs being adjustable by a rotary knob which is not shifted -- A permutation lock comprising a series of tumbler discs mounted on one common axis, where each disc can be set individually by turning a single rotary knob that remains in a fixed position and does not shift laterally. This arrangement allows the lock combination to be adjusted without moving the knob along the axis.
[E05B 37/10] in padlocks -- The CPC subclass E05B 37/10 covers padlocks that employ permutation mechanisms, requiring a specific sequence of numbers, symbols or positions to be aligned for the lock to open. It is situated in the hierarchy of fixed constructions, locks, and permutation locks.
[E05B 37/12] with tumbler discs on several axes -- A permutation lock of the fixed-construction type that secures the opening mechanism by using several tumbler discs mounted on different axes. Each disc can rotate independently to a preset position, and the correct alignment of all discs along their respective axes releases the lock. This arrangement allows a combination to be set by the relative angular positions of the discs rather than a single rotating shaft.
[E05B 37/14] in padlocks -- E05B 37/14 pertains to the design of permutation locks incorporated in padlocks. It includes mechanisms where a series of rotating dials or wheels align to produce a specific combination that releases the lock, covering keyless, dial-set unlocking systems within a fixed-construction padlock housing.
[E05B 37/16] with two or more push or pull knobs, slides, or the like -- A permutation lock in the fixed-construction group that secures a lock by requiring a specific sequence of movements of two or more push-or-pull knobs, sliders or similar actuating elements.
[E05B 37/163] the knobs being pushed in a prescribed sequence -- E05B 37/163 covers permutation locks in which multiple knobs must be pushed or turned in a specific, pre-determined sequence to operate the lock. It falls under the Fixed Constructions → Locks hierarchy for mechanical locking devices.
[E05B 37/166] each knob being pushed a predetermined number of times  E05B37/163  takes precedence -- E05B 37/166 covers permutation-lock mechanisms in which each knob must be pushed a predetermined number of times to reach the correct combination, distinguishing this operation from other permutation-lock variations. It follows the broader E05B 37/163 class that defines general features of permutation locks, and is applied to patents describing fixed-construction locks that require a set number of pushes on individual knobs.
[E05B 37/20] Puzzle locks -- E05B 37/20 refers to puzzle locks, a type of permutation lock where the unlocking sequence is set by a specific arrangement of movable elements rather than a conventional key. These devices are placed in the fixed constructions category and provide security through a series of discrete steps that must be solved to open the lock.
[E05B 39/00] Locks with indicating or timing devices -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks with means giving indication of authorised or unauthorised unlocking and also locks with non-electronic counting or registering devices.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Closures for postal containers with special means for indicating unauthorised opening -> B65D27/30 - Locking devices for container closures with means for indicating unauthorised opening -> B65D55/02 - Time recording locks (electronically) -> G07C1/32 - Security seals -> G09F3/03
[E05B 39/002] by releasing a liquid, e.g. ill-smelling or dye -- A lock that incorporates a deterrent or signalling system which, when triggered, discharges a liquid such as an unpleasant-smelling substance or a coloured dye to mark or discourage tampering. This liquid-release feature is used as an indicating or timing device within the locking mechanism. It falls under the fixed-construction class for locks with indicating or timing devices.
[E05B 39/005] Locks with means for tracking the location of locked items, e.g. freight containers -- This subclass covers locks that incorporate location-tracking features, allowing the position of the secured object - such as a freight container - to be monitored remotely. The tracking may use RFID, GPS, or other signalling means integrated with the lock mechanism.
[E05B 39/007] Indication by a tactile impulse to the user, e.g. vibration of a lock-, handle- or key-part -- The CPC entry E05B 39/007 covers locks that provide a tactile impulse, such as a vibration of the lock, handle or key, to indicate a status or event to the user. It falls under fixed constructions, specifically locks equipped with indicating or timing devices.
[E05B 39/02] with destructible seal closures or paper closures -- E05B 39/02 pertains to locks that include indicating or timing devices and are secured with a destructible seal, such as a paper tag or tape, which is broken to reveal any opening.
[E05B 39/025] with fracturable glass or the like, e.g. for emergency exits -- The code classifies locks that incorporate a breakable pane of glass or similar material designed to shatter for rapid release, such as emergency-exit mechanisms, falling under fixed constructions and locks with indicating or timing devices.
[E05B 39/04] with counting or registering devices -- The CPC entry E05B 39/04 covers fixed-construction locks that incorporate counting or registering devices, such as mechanisms that tally each operation, record usage numbers, or indicate the number of successful or attempted openings, typically combined with indicating or timing features.
[E05B 41/00] Locks with visible indication as to whether the lock is locked or unlocked -- E05B 41/00 pertains to lock mechanisms that include a visual indicator-such as a window, flag, light or colour change-showing whether the bolt is engaged or released, thereby providing an immediate, external signal of the lock's locked or unlocked condition.
[E05B 43/00] Time locks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Time locks, e.g. with delaying means and timers devices controlling electrically operated locks.
[E05B 43/005] Timer devices controlling electrically operated locks -- Timer devices controlling electrically operated locks are mechanisms that integrate a timing function with an electronic locking system, automatically releasing or engaging the lock after a preset interval. They are used in security applications where access must be limited to specific time periods, such as safes, vaults, and timed entry points. This class covers the design and operation of such combined timer-lock assemblies.
[E05B 45/00] Alarm locks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks provided with mechanical or electrically operated alarms, including chain, cables or padlock with alarms.
[E05B 45/005] Chain-locks, cable-locks or padlocks with alarms -- This subclass covers chain-locks, cable-locks and padlocks that incorporate an alarm device to warn of tampering or forced entry, providing a combined mechanical restraint and audible or visual alert. It includes any fixed lock where the alarm is integrated with the locking element.
[E05B 45/02] with mechanically-operated bells -- The CPC subclass E05B 45/02 covers fixed lock constructions that incorporate an alarm system employing mechanically-operated bells. It applies to locks in which the bell is driven directly by mechanical action, such as a lever or trigger activated by forced entry or tampering, rather than by electronic or pneumatic means.
[E05B 45/04] with detonating alarm devices -- E05B 45/04 refers to fixed-construction locks that incorporate detonating alarm devices; these are locking systems where an explosive or pyrotechnic charge is activated when the lock is tampered with, providing an audible and visual warning and potentially damaging tools used for forced entry.
[E05B 45/06] Electric alarm locks -- E05B 45/06 covers electric alarm locks, which integrate a locking mechanism with an electrical alarm system that signals unauthorized access. The class includes devices where the lock is triggered or powered electrically and combined with warning signals such as sound, light or radio alerts, typically used in doors, gates or other entry points.
[E05B 45/0605] for preventing of leaving the key  key ejection  E05B63/003
[E05B 45/061] for combination locks -- E05B 45/061 refers to combination locks used as alarm locks within fixed-construction lock systems. It covers devices whose locking mechanism is released by aligning a preset sequence of numbers, symbols or positions instead of employing a traditional key, providing a mechanical means of security activation.
[E05B 45/08] with contact making inside the lock or in the striking plate -- Alarm locks in which an electrical contact is made either inside the lock mechanism or on the striking plate, causing a signal to activate an alarm when the lock is operated or tampered with. This design integrates the triggering element directly into the fixed lock assembly.
[E05B 45/083] with contact making either in the striking plate or by movement of the bolt relative to the striking plate -- This subclass refers to alarm locks in fixed door constructions where the electrical contact that activates the alarm is produced either within the striking plate or by the bolt moving relative to that plate, signalling a forced or released condition.
[E05B 45/086] by transverse movement -- Alarm locks that engage a warning or signalling device when a lock component shifts sideways relative to its normal position. The classification covers mechanisms that detect transverse movement of the bolt, latch or other operative parts and activate an audible, visual or electronic alarm to indicate tampering or forced entry. It is used for security devices where lateral displacement, rather than rotation or linear pull, triggers the alarm response.
[E05B 45/10] by introducing the key -- E05B 45/10 covers alarm locks that are operated by the insertion of a key, where the key's introduction activates the locking mechanism and triggers an alarm or warning signal. The code is used for devices that combine mechanical key entry with electronic or acoustic alarm functions to deter unauthorized access.
[E05B 45/12] by movement of the bolt -- E05B 45/12 covers alarm locks that are activated by the movement of the bolt. It falls under the fixed constructions group, specifically within the locks category and the alarm-locks subclass, describing devices where the bolt's displacement triggers the alarm function.
[E05B 45/125] by transverse movement -- The CPC subclass E05B 45/125 relates to alarm locks that are actuated by a transverse movement of the locking element. It falls under fixed constructions and concerns locks in which a sideways shift of a bolt or latch triggers an alarm mechanism.
[E05B 47/00] Operation or control of locks by non-mechanical means, e.g. from a distance -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks or other fastening devices comprising electric or magnetic means for operation or control the lock functions. It has the following main structure:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Electric permutation locks -> E05B49/00
[E05B 47/0001] with electric actuators; Constructional features thereof  for vehicles  E05B81/04 -- The CPC subclass E05B 47/0001 covers locks whose operation or control is achieved by non-mechanical means, specifically electric actuators used in vehicle locking systems. It includes the design and constructional features of such electrically powered lock mechanisms for automotive applications.
[E05B 47/0002] with electromagnets -- This subclass covers fixed lock constructions that are operated by non-mechanical means using electromagnets, such as solenoid-driven bolts, magnetic latches or other electromagnetic actuators that open or close the lock when powered. It applies to devices where the lock's movement is generated by magnetic force rather than manual or purely mechanical action.
[E05B 47/0003] having a movable core -- It refers to locks whose internal core can be repositioned or removed remotely, enabling rekeying or access changes without physical disassembly. This allows the lock to be operated or reconfigured by non-mechanical means such as electronic, magnetic or hydraulic signals.
[E05B 47/0004] said core being linearly movable -- This subclass covers lock designs in which the locking core can be displaced in a straight line rather than rotated, and the lock is operated or controlled by non-mechanical means such as electronic, magnetic or remote signals.
[E05B 47/0005] said core being rotary movable -- E05B 47/0005 covers lock systems in which the lock core is moved rotarily by non-mechanical means, such as remote or electronic activation, rather than by a manual key or lever. It pertains to the operation or control of locks from a distance using a rotary movable core.
[E05B 47/0006] having a non-movable core; with permanent magnet -- E05B 47/0006 covers fixed-construction locks whose core does not move and is actuated by a permanent magnet, allowing the lock to be operated remotely or by non-mechanical means such as magnetic fields. This entry falls under "Locks - operation or control of locks by non-mechanical means" in the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[E05B 47/0009] with thermo-electric actuators, e.g. heated bimetals -- This subclass covers lock mechanisms that are actuated using thermo-electric devices such as heated bimetal elements, where temperature changes cause movement to open or close the lock, allowing remote or non-mechanical control. The approach enables operation of the lock without direct mechanical input, relying on thermal expansion or contraction to drive the locking action.
[E05B 47/0011] with piezoelectric actuators  piezoelectric devices  per se   H10N30/00
[E05B 47/0012] with rotary electromotors  actuators with rotary electromotors  per se   H02K7/06 ,  H02K23/68 -- It covers lock systems whose operation is powered by rotary electric motors, allowing the lock to be controlled remotely or electronically rather than by direct mechanical action. The subclass falls under fixed constructions and concerns non-mechanical control of locks.
[E05B 47/0038] using permanent magnets -- E05B 47/0038 covers the use of permanent magnets to actuate or control lock mechanisms without direct mechanical action. This includes remote or non-mechanical operation of fixed lock constructions, such as magnetic fields generated by permanent magnetic materials to unlock or lock doors.
[E05B 47/004] the magnets acting directly on the bolt -- E05B 47/004 pertains to fixed lock constructions where the bolt is moved by magnetic forces rather than mechanical actuation, allowing the lock to be operated remotely or automatically. It falls under the category of non-mechanical control of locks.
[E05B 47/0041] with tumbler pins suspended for a pendulum movement -- E05B 47/0041 designates lock mechanisms in which the tumbler pins are not fixed but hung so they can swing like pendulums, enabling the lock to be operated or controlled by non-mechanical means such as remote or electronic actuation. This falls under the fixed constructions category for locks.
[E05B 47/0042] with rotary magnet tumblers -- E05B 47/0042 covers fixed lock constructions that are operated or controlled by non-mechanical means-such as remote or electronic activation-and that employ rotary magnetic tumblers as the locking mechanism. This subclass falls under the broader categories of fixed constructions and locks within the CPC system.
[E05B 47/0043] Mechanical locks operated by cards having permanent magnets -- This subclass covers locks that are opened or controlled by a card carrying a permanent magnet, which interacts with the lock's magnetic components to release the mechanism without a traditional key. The classification falls under fixed constructions, locks, and non-mechanical operation of locks.
[E05B 47/0044] Cylinder locks with magnetic tumblers  E05B47/0041 ,  E05B47/0042  take precedence -- The CPC subclass E05B 47/0044 relates to cylinder locks that employ magnetic tumblers, enabling the lock to be operated or controlled without direct mechanical action, for example by magnetic fields or remote activation. It is nested under fixed constructions and locks and refines the broader groups E05B 47/0041 and E05B 47/0042, which take precedence.
[E05B 47/0045] keys with permanent magnets  E05B47/0043 ,  E05B47/0044 ,  E05B73/0052  take precedence
[E05B 47/0046] Electric or magnetic means in the striker or on the frame; Operating or controlling the striker plate  E05B47/0696 ,  E05B63/248  take precedence -- E05B47/0046 covers electric or magnetic devices incorporated in the striker or frame of a lock to operate or control the striker plate, enabling remote or non-mechanical actuation of the locking mechanism. This falls under the broader categories of fixed constructions and locks, specifically the control of locks by non-mechanical means. The classification supersedes related entries such as E05B47/0696 and E05B63/248.
[E05B 47/0047] Striker rotating about an axis parallel to the wing edge -- A striker that rotates about an axis parallel to the lock's wing edge, enabling the locking action to be engaged or released by non-mechanical means such as remote or electronic control rather than manual turning. This design is used in lock systems where the striking surface pivots horizontally to allow actuation from a distance.
[E05B 47/02] Movement of the bolt by electromagnetic means -- E05B 47/02 covers lock systems where the bolt is driven by electromagnetic actuation rather than by manual or purely mechanical force. It includes designs that use electric coils, solenoids or similar devices to move the bolt when a signal or power is applied, enabling remote or automated lock operation. This subclass falls under the broader categories of fixed constructions and locks in the Cooperative Patent Classification.
[E05B 47/023] the bolt moving pivotally or rotatively -- E05B 47/023 covers lock bolts that are moved by pivoting or rotating action rather than straight linear motion, typically when the motion is generated by electronic, magnetic, hydraulic or other non-mechanical means that can be operated from a distance.
[E05B 47/026] the bolt moving rectilinearly -- The subclass deals with lock bolts that are actuated to slide straight in or out rather than rotate, using non-mechanical control such as electronic, magnetic, hydraulic or pneumatic means. It includes mechanisms where the bolt moves rectilinearly to lock or release a door, gate or similar closure.
[E05B 47/06] Controlling mechanically-operated bolts by electro-magnetically-operated detents -- This subclass covers lock mechanisms in which a mechanically-driven bolt is held or released by an electromagnetically-operated detent, enabling electronic or remote control of the locking action. It relates to fixed-construction locks whose operation is managed by non-mechanical means.
[E05B 47/0603] the detent moving rectilinearly -- E05B 47/0603 concerns devices where a lock's detent moves in a straight line to engage or release the locking mechanism, typically used in systems that operate locks by electronic, magnetic, hydraulic or other non-mechanical means.
[E05B 47/0607] the detent moving pivotally or rotatively -- This class covers locks in which the detent-a device that holds a component in place-is moved pivotally or rotatively by non-mechanical actuation such as electronic, magnetic or remote control means. It belongs to the fixed-construction locks hierarchy and relates to operation of locks without direct mechanical force.
[E05B 47/0611] Cylinder locks with electromagnetic control -- The E05B 47/0611 entry covers cylinder locks that are actuated by electromagnetic signals, enabling remote or electronic operation instead of conventional mechanical key turning. It applies to fixed-construction locks whose control is achieved through non-mechanical means such as RFID, Bluetooth or other electronic interfaces.
[E05B 47/0615] operated by handles, e.g. by knobs -- E05B 47/0615 covers fixed lock constructions that are opened or closed by a handle such as a knob rather than a traditional key or latch. The classification applies to mechanisms where turning the handle directly moves the locking element, allowing manual control without additional mechanical linkage. It is used for doors, containers or similar assemblies where a simple rotary handle provides the actuation.
[E05B 47/0619] by blocking the rotor -- The code covers lock actuation in which the rotor's movement is prevented rather than driven, using non-mechanical means such as electromagnetic, magnetic or electronic signals to block rotation and thereby lock or unlock a device. It pertains to fixed-construction locks where control is exercised remotely or without direct mechanical force.
[E05B 47/0623] axially, i.e. with an axially engaging blocking element -- The CPC subclass E05B 47/0623 relates to locks that are operated or controlled by non-mechanical means, such as remote activation, within the broader group of fixed constructions. It specifically covers designs where the blocking element engages the lock in an axial direction, i.e. axially.
[E05B 47/0626] radially
[E05B 47/063] with a rectilinearly moveable blocking element -- E05B 47/063 covers lock systems that are operated or controlled by non-mechanical means, such as remote or electronic signals, and that use a blocking element moving in a straight line to engage or release the lock. The classification is applied to devices like remotely actuated deadbolts, electronic safes and other fixed-construction locking mechanisms.
[E05B 47/0634] with a pivotally moveable blocking element -- This CPC subclass (E05B 47/0634) covers lock systems that are operated or controlled by non-mechanical means-such as remote or electronic activation-and that incorporate a blocking element which can pivot to engage or disengage the lock. The pivotally moveable component provides a means to obstruct the locking mechanism without direct mechanical actuation.
[E05B 47/0638] by disconnecting the rotor -- The subclass concerns lock mechanisms that are operated by disconnecting the rotor, allowing the lock to be released or altered without direct mechanical action. It falls under non-mechanical control of fixed lock constructions.
[E05B 47/0642] axially, i.e. with an axially disengaging coupling element -- The entry E05B 47/0642 covers lock systems that are operated or controlled without direct mechanical actuation, using a coupling element that disengages along the lock's axis. Such axial disengagement allows the lock to be opened or closed from a distance or by non-mechanical signals, integrating electronic, hydraulic or pneumatic actuation into the locking mechanism.
[E05B 47/0646] radially
[E05B 47/0649] with a rectilinearly moveable coupling element -- E05B 47/0649 relates to remotely operated lock mechanisms where the actuation is achieved by non-mechanical means such as electric, magnetic, hydraulic or pneumatic signals, and the lock includes a coupling element that moves linearly to engage or release the locking device. The classification captures designs in which a straight-line moving part transmits the control action from a distance to secure or unlock the mechanism.
[E05B 47/0653] with a pivotally moveable coupling element -- The code E05B 47/0653 covers lock devices that are operated or controlled by non-mechanical means such as remote or electronic signals, where the coupling element linking the control source to the lock can pivot. The pivotable coupling allows the transmission of the actuation force or signal despite changes in alignment or movement of the lock components.
