[E05B 47/0657] by locking the handle, spindle, follower or the like -- This subclass covers lock systems where the unlocking or locking action is achieved by securing components such as the handle, spindle or follower, rather than by manually turning a key. It includes mechanisms that lock these parts directly, often as part of remote or electronic control of a lock.
[E05B 47/0661] axially, i.e. with an axially engaging blocking element -- This subclass deals with locks that are operated by non-mechanical means such as remote or electronic control, where the blocking element engages along the lock's axis. The axial engaging component moves linearly to lock or unlock the mechanism.
[E05B 47/0665] radially
[E05B 47/0669] with a pivotally moveable blocking element -- This subclass covers locks that are actuated by non-mechanical means such as remote signals and incorporate a blocking component that pivots to engage or disengage the locking mechanism. The pivotally movable element adds an extra safety or control feature within the remotely operated lock system.
[E05B 47/0673] with a rectilinearly moveable blocking element -- The code E05B 47/0673 covers fixed lock constructions that are operated without direct mechanical action, for example by electronic, magnetic or remote control, and that employ a blocking element that moves in a straight line to engage or release the lock.
[E05B 47/0676] by disconnecting the handle -- This subclass covers lock devices that are released by electrically or otherwise disconnecting the handle, enabling the lock to be operated without direct mechanical movement of the handle, such as by remote or automated control.
[E05B 47/068] axially, i.e. with an axially disengaging coupling element -- E05B 47/068 defines lock systems that are operated or controlled without direct mechanical action, using an axially disengaging coupling element to transmit the actuation along the lock's axis. The element moves in line with the lock shaft, allowing remote or electronic activation while maintaining a sealed or fixed construction.
[E05B 47/0684] radially
[E05B 47/0688] with a pivotally moveable coupling element -- E05B 47/0688 covers lock devices that are operated or controlled by non-mechanical means, such as remote or electronic signals, and that incorporate a coupling element capable of pivoting to connect or disconnect the locking mechanism. The pivotable coupling enables actuation without direct mechanical contact.
[E05B 47/0692] with a rectilinearly moveable coupling element -- E05B 47/0692 covers lock actuation methods that use a straight-line moving coupling element to transmit a non-mechanical control signal, such as electrical or magnetic energy, from a remote location to the locking mechanism.
[E05B 47/0696] locking the bolt by an electromagnet in the striker -- The entry pertains to locks in which the bolt is held closed by an electromagnet placed in the striker, allowing the lock to be engaged by electromagnetic force rather than by a purely mechanical action. It classifies devices that achieve lock operation through non-mechanical, distance-controlled means within the fixed-construction lock category.
[E05B 49/00] Electric permutation locks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electric permutation locks and mechanical keys therefor. It has the following main structure:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Electric operated or controlled locks -> E05B47/00 - Electronic locks actuated by non mechanical keys -> G07C9/00 - Non-mechanical keys for electronic locks -> G07C9/00944
[E05B 49/002] Keys with mechanical characteristics, e.g. notches, perforations, opaque marks -- E05B 49/002 covers keys that incorporate mechanical features such as notches, perforations or opaque markings to interact with electric permutation lock mechanisms. The classification addresses the design of these key elements that provide a physical reference for electronic lock operation, enabling secure access without relying solely on digital encoding.
[E05B 49/004] actuating mechanical switches -- The E05B 49/004 class covers mechanisms that convert an electrical signal into mechanical motion to operate a lock, such as solenoid-driven or motor-driven actuators that move levers, bolts or cams in electric permutation locks. It includes the design of the linkage, cams and release devices that translate the electrical input into the physical actuation required to lock or unlock a door.
[E05B 49/006] actuating opto-electronic devices -- This subclass covers devices that use light or electronic signals to control the actuation of a lock, such as photo-sensors, LEDs or other opto-electronic components that trigger the lock mechanism. It includes systems in which the lock is opened or closed by detecting optical signals.
[E05B 49/008] by alignment of movable perforated parts, e.g. perforated tumblers -- The code E05B 49/008 refers to electric permutation locks that secure by aligning movable perforated parts such as perforated tumblers; the lock opens when the electrically controlled tumblers are positioned so that their holes line up, creating a passage for a bolt or latch. This subclass falls under fixed constructions, specifically the group of electric locks that use such perforated-part alignment.
[E05B 5/00] Handles completely let into the surface of the wing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Handles which are flush when mounted to a wing and pop-out handles.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Handles for vehicles -> E05B85/10 - E05B85/18
[E05B 5/003] Pop-out handles, e.g. sliding outwardly before rotation -- E05B 5/003 classifies lock handles that are fully recessed in the wing surface and can be slid outward before being rotated to operate the lock; it covers pop-out or sliding-out handles used in fixed-construction wing locks.
[E05B 5/006] essentially defining a completely closed surface together with the wing -- E05B 5/006 classifies lock arrangements where the operating handle is entirely recessed into the plane of the door or window wing, creating a flush, continuous surface that is effectively closed around the handle. This subclass covers designs that integrate the handle into the wing so that no protruding parts remain, enhancing both aesthetics and sealing performance.
[E05B 51/00] Operating or controlling locks or other fastening devices by other non-mechanical means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Operating or controlling locks or other fastening devices by other non-purely mechanical means, such as e.g. by bimetallic or memory-shaped elements, or by pneumatic or hydraulic means.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Opening a panic door under force or pressure on the surface of the door -> E05B65/102 - Locking several vehicle wings simultaneously by pneumatic or hydraulic means -> E05B77/50 - Pneumatic or hydraulic driven locks for vehicles and actuators or circuits therefor -> E05B81/10 - Hydraulic power locks for wing closers -> E05F3/223 - Power operated mechanism for wings with pressure medium -> E05F15/50
[E05B 51/005] by a bimetallic or memory-shape element  heated by electricity  E05B47/0009 ; for panic door actuation  E05B65/104 -- This subcategory E05B 51/005 relates to locks that are opened or closed by an electrically heated bimetallic or shape-memory element, providing a non-mechanical means of actuation and is also applied to panic-door release mechanisms.
[E05B 51/02] by pneumatic or hydraulic means -- E05B 51/02 covers lock mechanisms that are actuated by fluid pressure, such as pneumatic or hydraulic systems, to engage or release a fastening device. The classification applies to fixed constructions where the lock is controlled without direct mechanical linkage, using compressed air or fluid to move the locking elements.
[E05B 51/023] actuated in response to external pressure, blast or explosion  E05B65/102  takes precedence -- E05B 51/023 relates to locks or fastening devices that are automatically triggered by external pressure, blast or explosion instead of manual or mechanical operation. It covers systems that open, close or change state in response to shock waves or rapid pressure changes, such as safety-release mechanisms in aircraft doors, protective enclosures or emergency escape routes.
[E05B 53/00] Operation or control of locks by mechanical transmissions, e.g. from a distance -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks operated by mechanical transmission including foot-operated locks and locks operated by flexible means, e.g. cables or Bowden cables.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Flexible connections between lock parts in vehicles -> E05B79/20 - Mechanical operating mechanisms for wings -> E05F9/00 , E05F11/00
[E05B 53/001] Foot-operation -- E05B 53/001 covers mechanisms that actuate a lock by foot pressure, using a mechanical transmission such as a pedal or lever to engage or release the locking device. The classification applies to foot-operated locking systems for doors, cabinets, containers and similar enclosures.
[E05B 53/003] flexible -- The CPC subclass E05B 53/003 covers flexible mechanical means used to operate or control locks from a distance. It includes devices such as cables, flexible shafts, or other pliable transmission elements that convey motion to a lock mechanism. The focus is on the flexibility of the transmission rather than the lock itself.
[E05B 53/005] Bowden -- The E05B 53/005 Bowden classification covers devices that transmit actuation forces to a lock by means of a flexible mechanical transmission such as a Bowden cable, enabling remote operation or control of the locking mechanism. It is placed within the fixed constructions - locks hierarchy for mechanical transmission of lock operation.
[E05B 53/006] of ball-train type -- The subclass E05B 53/006 covers lock devices whose actuation is transferred by a ball-train mechanism, enabling remote or mechanically transmitted operation of the lock. It belongs to fixed constructions, specifically the control of locks via mechanical transmissions.
[E05B 53/008] by planetary gears -- The subclass E05B 53/008 covers mechanisms that operate or control a lock through a mechanical transmission system using planetary gears, enabling remote or indirect actuation of the locking device. This includes designs where the gear train converts motion from a distant source into the movement required to engage or disengage the lock.
[E05B 55/00] Locks with provision for latching -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks in which a sliding latch is used also as a locking bolt, e.g. cylindrical or tubular locks. It includes also locks in which the handle can be disconnected or locks in which the bolt is secured by the action of a trigger member cooperating with the frame.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Electrically disconnecting the handle -> E05B47/0676 - A latch bolt being initially retained in an intermediate position and subsequently projected to its full extend when the wing is closed -> E05B63/202
[E05B 55/005] Cylindrical or tubular locks -- Cylindrical or tubular locks are a subclass of latching locks in which the locking mechanism is contained within a round cylinder or tube, commonly actuated by a key, thumbturn, or push-button. The design allows the bolt or latch to be moved by rotation of the core, providing secure fastening for doors, cabinets, and similar enclosures.
[E05B 55/06] the handle being disconnected -- The subclass E05B 55/06 covers lock constructions in which the operating handle can be disconnected from the latching mechanism, allowing the handle to be removed or separated for security, maintenance or replacement. This includes designs where the handle is detachable, pivoting away, or otherwise disengaged from the bolt or latch that secures the door.
[E05B 55/12] the bolt being secured by the operation of a hidden parallel member -- E05B 55/12 pertains to locks where the bolt is secured by the action of a concealed parallel member that moves into position to latch the bolt, providing a hidden securing mechanism.
[E05B 57/00] Locks in which a pivoted latch is used also as locking means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks in which a pivoted latch is also can be dead locked.  AU556425
[E05B 59/00] Locks with latches separate from the lock-bolts or with a plurality of latches or lock-bolts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks with latches in addition to lock-bolts or dead bolts.  EP0792980
[E05B 61/00] Other locks with provision for latching -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks which provision of latching not covered by the groups E05B55/00 , E05B57/00 or E05B59/00 .
[E05B 63/00] Locks with special structural characteristics or for special use -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks or fastenings with special structural characteristics
[E05B 63/0004] Additional locks added to existing lock arrangements -- E05B 63/0004 covers devices in which one or more secondary locks are incorporated into an existing lock system to enhance security or provide specialised functions. It includes retro-fitted deadbolts, latch-bolts, electronic add-ons or mechanical supplements that work together with the primary lock.
[E05B 63/0008] Mounted on the hinged side of the wing -- The CPC subclass E05B 63/0008 covers locks that are installed on the hinged side of a wing, such as a door or gate leaf, where the locking mechanism is integrated into the moving part rather than the frame. It falls under fixed constructions → locks → locks with special structural characteristics or for special use, specifying the mounting position on the hinged leaf.
[E05B 63/0013] Locks with rotary bolt without provision for latching -- E05B 63/0013 classifies locks that use a rotary bolt as the securing element but do not include a separate latching mechanism, relying on the bolt's rotation alone to hold the door or barrier in place.
[E05B 63/0017] Locks with sliding bolt without provision for latching -- This subclass covers locks that secure by means of a sliding bolt that moves directly into a locked position without any separate latching device, providing a simple bolt-only securing action. It is part of the Fixed Constructions → Locks hierarchy.
[E05B 63/0021] the bolt being shot over an increased length by a single turning operation of the key -- The entry covers a lock where a single key rotation moves the bolt a longer distance than normal, a "shot" stroke that extends the bolt travel in one turn. This design is used for special-purpose or structurally unique locks that require a greater bolt movement without multiple key rotations.
[E05B 63/003] with key ejection means -- The CPC subclass E05B 63/003 covers locks that incorporate a key-ejection mechanism, where the key is automatically released from the lock after use. This feature is found in fixed-construction locks designed for special applications that require rapid key removal for safety or convenience.
[E05B 63/0034] Locks for use instead of cylinder locks, e.g. locks with cylinder lock profile and a low security operating mechanism -- This subclass covers locks that replace conventional cylinder locks, featuring a cylinder-shaped profile but employing a low-security actuation method such as a simple latch or magnetic mechanism. They are intended for applications where ease of manufacture or limited security is acceptable and may be found in furniture, cabinets or temporary enclosures.
[E05B 63/0043] Dummy or simulated locks; Cover elements without lock function -- This class covers lock assemblies that are purely decorative or non-functional, including dummy or simulated locks and cover components that look like a lock but provide no securing action. Such elements are used for visual consistency, compliance with design standards, or to conceal other hardware without serving a locking purpose.
[E05B 63/0047] Locks having a key withdrawable in different positions  cylinder locks  E05B27/0085 ,  E05B29/0073 -- The class E05B 63/0047 covers cylinder locks that allow the key to be withdrawn from more than one position, such as from different rotational or axial orientations. It includes designs where the key can be removed without fully aligning the bolt, enabling special operational or safety features.
[E05B 63/0052] Locks mounted on the "frame" cooperating with means on the "wing"  E05B47/0046 ,  E05B65/0829 ,  E05B65/0847 ,  E05B65/0882  take precedence -- E05B 63/0052 covers locks that are fitted to the stationary frame and work together with a locking element mounted on the moving wing or leaf of a door, window or similar opening. The design links a frame-borne mechanism to a cooperating component on the swing part, and it takes precedence over the related subclasses E05B47/0046, E05B65/0829, E05B65/0847 and E05B65/0882.
[E05B 63/0056] Locks with adjustable or exchangeable lock parts  adjustable strikers  E05B15/024 ; adjustable to different keys  E05B35/083 ; adjustable bolt or backset  E05B63/06 ; adjustable coupling means for bars, rods, cables  E05C9/20 -- The CPC subclass E05B 63/0056 relates to locks that incorporate adjustable or exchangeable components to suit different security requirements. It covers designs with adjustable strikers, mechanisms that can be set for different keys, variable bolt or backset dimensions, and couplings that can be altered for bars, rods or cables.
[E05B 63/006] for different door thicknesses  E05B9/045  takes precedence; for fastening handles to lock or latch parts  E05B3/00
[E05B 63/0065] Operating modes; Transformable to different operating modes -- The classification covers locks that can be switched between different operating modes, such as moving from a purely mechanical operation to an electronic or electro-mechanical mode, or altering the locking principle (for example, from a latch to a bolt) while maintaining the same basic housing.
[E05B 63/0069] Override systems, e.g. allowing opening from inside without the key, even when locked from outside  E05B65/1086 ,  E05B9/105  take precedence; by movable striker  E05B63/246
[E05B 63/0073] by withdrawal of the entire lock unit -- E05B 63/0073 covers locks in which the complete lock unit can be removed as a single piece, typically for ease of maintenance, replacement, or reconfiguration, rather than extracting individual components. This design feature is used in specialized or modular lock systems where the entire assembly is withdrawn from the door or frame.
[E05B 63/0078] allowing opening with key from outside, even with key inserted from inside  E05B9/10  takes precedence -- E05B 63/0078 covers lock designs that permit a door to be opened with a key inserted from the interior while the key is turned from the exterior, a feature used for safety or convenience. This falls under the broader category of fixed constructions, locks with special structural characteristics, and is superseded by the more specific subclass E05B9/10.
[E05B 63/02] without springs -- E05B 63/02 covers locks that operate without the use of springs, such as dead-bolt or latch mechanisms that rely on manual force or alternative retaining elements. These designs are used where spring-free operation is required for safety, durability, or specific environmental conditions.
[E05B 63/04] for alternative use on the right-hand or left-hand side of wings -- E05B 63/04 designates locks that can be installed or operated on either the right-hand or left-hand side of winged doors or panels, providing interchangeable or mirrored mounting options for special structural configurations.
[E05B 63/042] constructed symmetrically -- A lock in which the functional components are arranged as mirror images on either side of a central axis, giving the mechanism a balanced, symmetric form. This design often simplifies manufacturing and can provide uniform torque distribution, making it suitable for applications where consistent performance and aesthetic uniformity are desired.
[E05B 63/044] with reversible bolt or bolt head -- The subclass covers locking devices whose bolt or the portion that drives it can be reversed, meaning the bolt can be moved in opposite directions or rotated to alternate between locked and unlocked positions, often allowing operation from more than one side or providing a fallback locking position.
[E05B 63/046] with double-bevelled latch or the like -- This subclass covers lock mechanisms that use a double-bevelled latch or a similar latch design, where the latch has two angled surfaces that engage the strike plate for improved security or alignment. It applies to locks with special structural characteristics or for special use such as doors that require precise sealing.
[E05B 63/048] with bolt heads at both sides of the lock -- A lock classified under E05B 63/048 features bolt heads that extend from both sides of the lock body, enabling the lock to be operated from either side of a door or panel. This design is used where double-sided access or release is required, such as in passage doors or security enclosures, and forms a specialized type of fixed-construction lock.
[E05B 63/06] with lengthwise-adjustable bolts -- This subclass covers locking devices whose bolt can be moved lengthwise-slid, screwed or otherwise adjusted-to vary the projecting length of the bolt. The adjustable bolt allows the lock to engage at different depths, useful for doors, gates or containers where variable security, sealing or alignment is required. It is applied to locks with special structural characteristics or for special uses.
[E05B 63/08] Mortise locks -- Mortise locks are installed in a recess cut into the edge or face of a door and consist of a lock body that houses a latch or deadbolt operating from within the pocket. They provide robust security and are commonly used in residential, commercial and institutional doors.
[E05B 63/10] requiring only two cylindrical holes in the wing -- A lock of this type is designed so that the wing contains just two cylindrical holes, which serve as the only points of interaction for the locking mechanism, allowing a simple and compact construction.
[E05B 63/12] with means carried by the bolt for interlocking with the keeper -- This subclass concerns locks whose bolt includes a device that engages directly with a keeper to form an interlock, providing additional holding or safety features. It applies to lock designs where the interlocking means are integral to the bolt rather than to separate components, and is used for special structural or functional purposes.
[E05B 63/121] using balls or the like cooperating with notches -- Locks that employ balls, rollers or similar elements that engage with notches or recesses to retain or release a movable part, providing a simple detent or locking function. This arrangement is used in devices such as latches, hinges, or cam mechanisms where a spring-biased ball snaps into a notch to hold the component in position.
[E05B 63/122] with transverse, i.e. vertically movable bolt or dropbolt  E05B63/126  takes precedence -- The CPC subclass E05B 63/122 covers locks that incorporate a transverse, vertically movable bolt or dropbolt as part of their securing mechanism. These devices are designed for special structural or functional requirements, such as space-saving or specific directional actuation. For detailed features, subclass E05B 63/126 is taken as the more specific reference.
[E05B 63/123] with circular bolts -- The subclass E05B 63/123 includes locks that employ circular bolts-bolts shaped as a cylinder or disc that rotate or turn to secure a door, gate or similar opening. These bolts differ from conventional rectangular or wedge bolts and are used where a rotating locking element is required.
[E05B 63/125] with a sliding bolt rotating about its axis, i.e. of bayonet type -- This subclass covers locks in which the bolt slides while rotating about its own axis, as found in bayonet-type mechanisms. The bolt is moved linearly into position and then turned to lock, providing rapid engagement and release.
[E05B 63/126] the bolt having an additional sliding bolt or movement  E05B63/121  takes precedence
[E05B 63/127] the bolt having an additional rotating bolt or movement -- E05B 63/127 refers to lock bolts that include an additional rotating bolt or movable element, providing an extra locking action or adjustable security feature, often used in specialist or high-security constructions such as safes, vault doors or other fixed installations.
[E05B 63/128] with additional bolt in the striker  electric  E05B47/0696
[E05B 63/14] Arrangement of several locks or locks with several bolts, e.g. arranged one behind the other -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Bolts with multiple head -> E05B15/108 - With provision for latching -> E05B59/00 , E05B61/00
[E05B 63/143] Arrangement of several locks, e.g. in parallel or series, on one or more wings -- E05B 63/143 relates to the layout of multiple locks on one or more lock wings, describing configurations such as parallel or series arrangements that enable coordinated operation or greater capacity for water level control and vessel passage.
[E05B 63/146] locks with two or more bolts, each bolt itself being a tumbler -- Locks with two or more bolts in which each bolt functions as an individual tumbler, providing multiple independent locking points for enhanced security in special-purpose or high-security applications. This design is classified under the cooperative patent system for fixed constructions.
[E05B 63/16] with the handles on opposite sides moving independently -- E05B 63/16 pertains to fixed locks whose operating handles are positioned on opposite sides of the mechanism and can be moved independently, allowing separate actuation of each side. It is a subcategory of locks with special structural characteristics or for special use.
[E05B 63/18] with arrangements independent of the locking mechanism for retaining the bolt in the retracted position -- This class covers lock designs where the bolt is held back by a separate device that does not rely on the lock's own mechanism. Such arrangements may use springs, catches, or external latches to keep the bolt retracted until the lock is deliberately released. It is used for special-purpose or safety-critical applications where the bolt must stay out of the locked position independently.
[E05B 63/185] Preventing actuation of a bolt when the wing is open  E05B63/20  takes precedence, similar safety devices  E05D15/526 -- The subclass E05B 63/185 covers safety arrangements that block the movement of a lock bolt while a hinged wing, such as a door or flap, is open, ensuring the bolt cannot be actuated in that position. It falls under fixed-construction locks with special structural or usage features.
[E05B 63/20] released automatically when the wing is closed -- This subclass covers locks that automatically disengage when the door wing is brought into the closed position, allowing the latch to release without manual operation. It applies to mechanisms where the closing motion itself triggers the release, typical in swinging doors and similar closures.
[E05B 63/202] a latch bolt being initially retained in an intermediate position and subsequently projected to its full extent when the wing is closed -- E05B 63/202 covers a type of latch bolt that is held in an intermediate position after being set and then moves to its full projected position when the lock's wing is closed, providing a secure fastening that engages only at the final closing step. This mechanism is used in locks with special structural or functional requirements.
[E05B 63/205] by a trigger at the hinged edge of the wing -- E05B 63/205 classifies locks that are released by a trigger positioned at the hinged edge of a wing, such as a door or panel, allowing the lock to open when the wing is moved. This design is used in fixed constructions where the locking mechanism is integrated into the hinge area for special-purpose applications.
[E05B 63/22] operated by a pulling or pushing action perpendicular to the front plate, -- E05B 63/22 pertains to fixed-construction locks that are engaged by a pulling or pushing force applied perpendicular to the front plate. The classification covers devices with special structural features designed for such linear actuation, typically used where a direct forward or rearward motion opens or secures the lock.
[E05B 63/24] Arrangements in which the fastening members which engage one another are mounted respectively on the wing and the frame and are both movable, e.g. for release by moving either of them -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Operating or controlling locks using electric or magnetic means in the striker or on the frame -> E05B47/0046 - Adaptation of locks - locking the bolt by an electromagnet in the striker -> E05B47/0696 - Locks with means carried by the bolt for interlocking with the keeper and having additional bolt in the striker -> E05B63/128 - Hasp locks -> E05B65/48
[E05B 63/242] Auxiliary bolts on the frame, actuated by bolts on the wing, or  vice versa -- E05B 63/242 covers lock mechanisms that employ auxiliary bolts fixed to the frame and operated by corresponding bolts on the wing, or the reverse arrangement, providing added security or specific functional features in locks with special structural characteristics. This falls under the fixed-construction locks category.
[E05B 63/244] the striker being movable for latching, the bolt for unlatching, or  vice versa -- E05B 63/244 classifies locks whose striker moves to latch while the bolt moves to release, or the reverse arrangement, a special structural feature used in fixed constructions.
[E05B 63/246] Emergency release from inside by releasing the striker -- This subclass refers to fixed-construction locks that provide an emergency release operated from the interior by disengaging the striker. The mechanism lets occupants open the door quickly without a key or external tool, and is typical for safety-critical doors such as fire exits.
[E05B 63/248] the striker being movable for latching, and pushed back by a member on the wing for unlatching, or  vice versa -- A lock in which the striker moves to engage a latch and is returned to its original position by a component on the wing when the latch is released, or the reverse arrangement, is covered by CPC code E05B 63/248. This falls under fixed constructions, specifically locks designed with special structural features for particular functions.
[E05B 65/00] Locks for special use -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks or fastenings for special use.
[E05B 65/0003] for locking a plurality of wings, e.g. simultaneously  E05B65/0017 ,  E05B77/46 ,  E05B65/462 ,  E05C7/00  take precedence -- A subclass of fixed-construction locks that secures several hinged or pivoting wings together in a single action, such as multi-wing doors, shutters or panels. The mechanism is designed to engage all wings simultaneously, providing coordinated locking for special-use applications where multiple moving elements must be restrained at once.
[E05B 65/0007] for gates  for wire gates  E06B11/021 -- E05B 65/0007 pertains to lock mechanisms specifically adapted for gates, especially wire or mesh gates, within the broader category of fixed-construction special-use locks. It addresses the design and securing features required for these gate types.
[E05B 65/001] for gas- or watertight wings -- A lock for gas- or watertight wings is a sealing device incorporated into fixed structures such as aircraft, ship or vehicle wings to prevent the passage of air or water. The mechanism provides an airtight or watertight closure, ensuring the wing interior remains sealed during operation or when the vehicle is at rest.
[E05B 65/0014] to prevent opening by children  E05B13/00  takes precedence; child-proof container closures  B65D50/00 -- This subclass of E05B covers fixed lock constructions specifically engineered to deter children from opening a device, such as child-proof closures on containers. It falls under the broader category of locks for special use (E05B13/00), which includes various safety mechanisms for preventing accidental access by minors.
[E05B 65/0017] Jail locks -- E05B 65/0017 covers jail locks, a subclass of special-use locks designed to secure prison cells, detention facilities and related structures. These locks typically incorporate high-security features such as reinforced bolts, multiple locking points, and tamper-resistant components to prevent unauthorized opening.
[E05B 65/0021] for overhead or roll-up doors, e.g. garage doors -- This CPC subclass E05B 65/0021 covers locking devices designed specifically for overhead or roll-up doors such as garage doors. It includes mechanisms that secure the door in its closed position and may feature manual, automatic or remote actuation, falling under the broader group of locks for special use in fixed constructions.
[E05B 65/0025] for glass wings -- E05B 65/0025 concerns locks specifically adapted for glass wings, that is, locking mechanisms designed to secure hinged or folding glass panels used as doors or partitions in storefronts, exhibition halls or similar settings. It falls under the fixed-construction group of special-use locks for glass elements.
[E05B 65/0028] for narrow-stile wings -- E05B 65/0028 classifies locks that are intended for use on narrow-stile wings of doors, where the side panel of the door is unusually thin. These devices are a subset of special-use lock constructions and address the specific geometry and load requirements of narrow door sections.
[E05B 65/0032] for hotel rooms  electronic  G07C9/00904
[E05B 65/0035] for privacy rooms, e.g. bathrooms -- E05B 65/0035 designates locks intended for privacy rooms such as bathrooms, falling under the special-use category of fixed-construction locks. These devices provide temporary securing of spaces where short-term privacy is required.
[E05B 65/0042] For refrigerators or cold rooms  E05B17/0075  takes precedence; magnetic  E05C19/16 -- E05B 65/0042 defines lock mechanisms specially adapted for refrigerators or cold rooms, covering designs such as magnetic closures and other secure fastening methods used in temperature-controlled environments; it falls under the "locks for special use" branch of Fixed Constructions and is subordinated to the broader class E05B17/0075.
[E05B 65/0046] with a bifurcated bolt -- A bifurcated-bolt lock uses a bolt split into two parallel tongues that engage separate strike plates, providing enhanced holding security and alignment for special-use doors or panels. This design is classified under fixed constructions for locks intended for specialized applications.
[E05B 65/005] with sliding bolt -- The classification E05B 65/005 covers locks that are designed for special-use applications and incorporate a sliding bolt as the securing element. It falls within the broader group of fixed constructions for locks, specifying mechanisms where the bolt moves linearly to engage or release the lock.
[E05B 65/0053] with safety release from inside  by releasing the striker  E05B63/246 ; similar means for safe doors  E05B65/0078 -- A lock design that provides an interior safety-release function by disengaging the striker, enabling the door to be opened from inside, similar to the release mechanisms used on safe doors.

[E05B 65/0067] for portable computers, e.g. for locking the screen panel to the keyboard panel -- This subclass covers locking devices designed specifically for portable computers, such as mechanisms that secure the screen panel to the keyboard panel, ensuring the two parts remain fixed together when the device is in use.
[E05B 65/0071] for revolving doors -- The subclass E05B 65/0071 relates to locking devices specifically designed for revolving doors, covering mechanisms that secure multi-leaf or pivoted door systems used in fixed constructions. [E05B 65/0082] with additional locking responsive to attack, e.g. to heat, explosion  in general  E05B17/2092 -- This subclass covers fixed lock constructions intended for special use that incorporate an additional locking mechanism which activates in response to attacks such as heat or explosion; the extra feature engages when the primary lock is compromised, providing secondary security.
[E05B 65/0085] for folding wings, e.g. bi-fold wings -- E05B 65/0085 covers locking devices designed for folding or bi-fold wings of fixed constructions such as doors, shutters or panels that pivot or fold. [E05B 65/0089] for utility meters or the like -- A lock classified under E05B 65/0089 is a fixed-construction device specifically designed for securing utility meters or similar equipment. It is intended for special-use applications where the lock must protect metering installations from tampering, removal or unauthorized access.
[E05B 65/0092] for wardrobe trunks -- This CPC subgroup covers locks specifically designed for wardrobe trunks, a type of furniture storage where the lock must accommodate the trunk's dimensions and motion. Such locks are intended for special-use applications within the broader category of fixed construction locks.
[E05B 65/02] for thin, hollow, or thin-metal wings -- E05B 65/02 pertains to lock devices specifically adapted for thin, hollow or thin-metal wings, such as shutters, doors or panel sections where the wing material lacks the thickness to accommodate conventional lock hardware. The classification addresses designs that secure these lightweight elements without damaging or deforming them.
[E05B 65/025] for lockers -- E05B 65/025 pertains to lockers, which are enclosed storage compartments equipped with a door and a locking mechanism for secure, temporary holding of personal items. The code applies to lockers used in settings such as schools, gyms, workplaces or public venues, classifying them as a special-use form of lock within fixed constructions.
[E05B 65/06] for swing doors -- E05B 65/06 designates locking devices specifically intended for swing-type doors, covering mechanisms that secure doors that open inward or outward on a hinge. It falls under the fixed constructions group, within the locks for special use category.
[E05B 65/08] for sliding wings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks or fastenings for sliding wings.
[E05B 65/0811] the bolts pivoting about an axis perpendicular to the wings -- This subclass defines lock mechanisms where the securing bolts are mounted to pivot around an axis that is perpendicular to the plane of their wing-like extensions. The design is used in special-purpose locks that move the bolt by rotating the wings rather than by straight sliding.
[E05B 65/0817] with additional movement, e.g. toggle, overcenter, excentric -- This subgroup covers locks that incorporate extra mechanical motions such as toggle levers, over-center mechanisms or eccentric actions to engage or release the lock. They are used in specialized applications where a secondary motion improves security or ergonomics.
[E05B 65/0823] cooperating with the slide guide, e.g. the rail -- E05B 65/0823 defines lock mechanisms that work in cooperation with a slide guide such as a rail, typically for specialised applications where the locking element moves along a guided track. The design integrates the guide function to ensure precise alignment and secure operation.
[E05B 65/0829] mounted on the slide guide, e.g. the rail -- This subclass covers locks that are installed directly on a slide guide or rail, typically used for sliding doors, windows or other movable panels. The mounting integrates the locking mechanism with the guide, providing secure closure while preserving the sliding function.
[E05B 65/0835] the bolts pivoting about an axis parallel to the wings -- This subclass covers lock mechanisms in which the securing bolt pivots about an axis that runs parallel to the lock's wings, a configuration commonly used in special-use locks such as deadbolts or latch devices where the bolt swings rather than slides.
[E05B 65/0841] and parallel to the sliding direction of the wings -- This subclass concerns special-use locks in which the locking element or actuation mechanism is arranged parallel to the sliding direction of the lock's wings, typical of fixed constructions where the moving parts follow the wing's slide path.
[E05B 65/0847] mounted on the slide guide, e.g. the rail  E05B65/0841  takes precedence -- E05B 65/0847 covers locks that are attached directly to the slide guide or rail of a movable element such as a drawer or sliding door. The subclass applies to special-use fastening devices where the locking mechanism is mounted on the guide component to provide secure positioning.
[E05B 65/0852] with a bolt under compression force -- The CPC subclass E05B 65/0852 covers locks designed for special use that employ a bolt kept in place by a compression force, typically to enhance security or safety in applications such as fire-resistant doors, armored safes or other high-security installations.
[E05B 65/0858] comprising simultaneously pivoting double hook-like locking members -- This subclass of the Fixed Constructions - Locks group covers lock mechanisms that use two hook-shaped members which pivot at the same time to engage and secure a door or panel. The simultaneous movement of the double hooks provides a locking action suited for special-use applications such as safety doors or secure containers.
[E05B 65/0864] the bolts sliding perpendicular to the wings -- This CPC subclass covers fixed-construction locks for special use in which the locking bolts move by sliding at right angles to the lock's wing plates, providing a perpendicular bolt motion within the mechanism.
[E05B 65/087] the bolts sliding parallel to the wings -- E05B 65/087 covers lock arrangements in which the fastening bolts move in a path parallel to adjoining wing-like members, typically used in specialised locking devices such as safety doors, vehicle hatches or aerospace panels where the bolt slides alongside the wing to secure the closure.
[E05B 65/0876] cooperating with the slide guide, e.g. the rail -- This subclass concerns locks for special use that work together with a slide guide such as a rail, providing guided movement and secure positioning of sliding components. It covers arrangements where the lock's action is coordinated with the rail to control or restrain the sliding element, as found in sliding doors, panels or similar structures.
[E05B 65/0882] mounted on the slide guide, e.g. the rail -- E05B 65/0882 pertains to locks that are mounted on a slide guide such as a rail, used in special-purpose locking arrangements. These devices belong to the fixed-construction group for locks designed for particular applications.
[E05B 65/0888] Locking bars -- E05B 65/0888 covers locking bars, rigid members that engage a door, gate or other opening to prevent movement. These devices are installed in fixed constructions and are used for special applications such as bars that slide, swing or drop into place to provide additional security.
[E05B 65/0894] Portable or removable locks  E05B65/0864 ,  E05B65/0888  take precedence -- E05B 65/0894 covers portable or removable locks designed for special-use applications, such as temporary or mobile security devices that can be attached and detached without permanent alteration of the protected object. It falls under Fixed Constructions → Locks → Locks for special use, and it is subordinate to the broader groups E05B65/0864 and E05B65/0888.
[E05B 65/10] for panic or emergency doors -- E05B 65/10 covers lock mechanisms for panic or emergency doors, allowing rapid egress while preserving security, and typically includes fail-safe or fail-open features that automatically release the lock when pressure, loss of power, or another emergency condition is detected.
[E05B 65/1006] of the vertical rod type -- A lock of the vertical-rod type is a special-use fastening device in which a rod moves vertically to engage or release a latch, typically applied to doors, gates or other fixed constructions where space or layout favours a straight up-and-down motion. It falls under the fixed-construction category of locks designed for particular applications.
[E05B 65/1013] Trigger means for holding the bolt in the retracted position and releasing the bolt when the door is closed
[E05B 65/102] opening under pressure on the surface of the door itself  E05C19/02  takes precedence -- E05B 65/102 covers doors that are opened by applying pressure directly to the door's surface, such as push-to-open or surface-actuated mechanisms, typically used where conventional handles are unsuitable. It falls under fixed constructions → locks → locks for special use, and is superseded by the more specific E05C19/02.
[E05B 65/1026] by a yieldable or breakable bolt or striker -- E05B 65/1026 classifies lock assemblies that use a bolt or striker designed to yield, deform or break when excessive force is applied, providing a controlled release for special-use applications such as safes, tamper-resistant devices or emergency-exit hardware.
[E05B 65/1033] emergency release of windows, window grills, escape hatches or the like -- E05B 65/1033 classifies devices that provide an emergency release for windows, window grills, escape hatches or similar openings, allowing rapid egress in case of fire or other urgent situations. These mechanisms fall within the fixed-constructions group and the special-use locks subclass.
[E05B 65/104] actuated in response to heat, e.g. with fusible element, bimetal, memory shape or swelling material -- E05B 65/104 covers fixed construction locks designed for special applications that automatically engage or release when heated. The actuation may rely on fusible elements, bimetallic strips, shape-memory alloys, or materials that swell with temperature, enabling a heat-responsive security mechanism.
[E05B 65/1046] Panic bars -- Panic bars are fixed-construction door hardware that allows rapid outward opening by pressing a horizontal bar, providing a simple release mechanism for emergency egress. They are classified under special-use locks for situations where quick, unobstructed exit is required.
[E05B 65/1053] sliding towards and away form the door -- This subclass covers lock assemblies in which the locking element moves linearly toward and away from the door, such as sliding bolts and other special-use mechanisms that engage or release by a straight-line motion rather than rotation. It is part of the fixed-construction locks group in the CPC.
[E05B 65/106] pivoting -- E05B 65/106 covers pivoting locks designed for special applications where the locking bolt or latch pivots about an axis to secure a door, panel or window. These mechanisms are a subset of fixed construction locks, providing adjustable or swing-type engagement for restricted or high-security use.
[E05B 65/1066] the pivot axis being substantially parallel to the longitudinal axis of the bar -- The entry E05B 65/1066 classifies lock designs in which the pivot axis of the moving component is essentially parallel to the longitudinal axis of the bar, a configuration found in special-use fixed-construction locks. It refines the broader categories of fixed constructions, locks, and locks for special use.
[E05B 65/1073] the pivot axis being substantially pependicular to the longitudinal axis of the bar -- This CPC subgroup covers locks designed for special applications in which the pivot axis of the locking element is arranged substantially perpendicular to the longitudinal axis of the bar that moves or swings, defining a distinct locking geometry.
[E05B 65/108] Electronically controlled emergency exits -- The CPC code E05B 65/108 designates emergency exit doors that are operated by electronic control systems. It includes mechanisms such as sensor-triggered opening, remote activation, or integration with fire-alarm networks, intended for rapid egress in special-use buildings or confined spaces. This falls under the broader category of locks for special use within fixed constructions.
[E05B 65/1086] Locks with panic function, e.g. allowing opening from the inside without a ley even when locked from the outside -- This subclass covers fixed locks designed for emergency egress, where the mechanism permits the door to be opened from the interior without a key even though it is locked from the outside. Such panic-function locks are used in public buildings and other spaces requiring rapid, unobstructed exit.
[E05B 65/1093] Dogging means for holding the actuation means, e.g. the actuating handle -- E05B 65/1093 relates to mechanisms that lock or hold the moving part of a lock, such as the handle or lever, in position when the lock is engaged. These dogging means prevent the actuation element from moving unintentionally and are used in safety-critical or special-use locking devices.
[E05B 65/44] for furniture -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks for furniture.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Locks for drawers -> E05B65/46
[E05B 65/46] for drawers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks for drawers.
[E05B 65/461] for cash drawers -- E05B 65/461 covers lock mechanisms designed specifically for cash drawers, including the locking devices and hardware that secure the drawer in retail or banking settings. These locks belong to the fixed constructions group and are intended for special-use applications where controlled, rapid access is required.
[E05B 65/462] for two or more drawers -- The CPC subclass E05B 65/462 covers locking mechanisms that secure two or more adjacent drawers as a single unit, preventing individual opening without unlocking the whole set. These locks are used in filing cabinets, kitchen units and similar furniture where grouped drawer protection is required.
[E05B 65/463] Drawer interlock or anti-tilt mechanisms, i.e. when one drawer is open, at least one of the remaining drawers is locked -- E05B 65/463 relates to drawer interlock or anti-tilt mechanisms that secure the remaining drawers when one drawer is opened, preventing their movement and enhancing safety and stability in furniture or storage units.

Such arrangements are used in special-purpose locks where the elements work together to secure a door or device.
[E05B 65/465] with rotary locking bars -- A lock that secures a door, gate or similar opening by means of one or more bars that rotate about an axis to engage a strike or bolt, rather than sliding or latching straight. It falls under the fixed-construction group for locks with special use.
[E05B 65/466] with tensionable or flexible elements, e.g. cables, bands, chains or ropes -- This subcategory covers lock mechanisms for special use that employ a tensionable or flexible element such as a cable, band, chain or rope to secure or restrain an object, with the flexible component forming an integral part of the locking action.
[E05B 65/467] Locking bars secured in front of the drawers -- E05B 65/467 covers locking bars that are mounted on the front of drawers to secure them against opening; the bars are fixed constructions used in special-purpose lockers or cabinets.
[E05B 65/468] using rotary locking bars -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks for two or more drawers using rotary locking bars.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Drawer interlock or anti-tilt mechanisms with rotary locking bars -> E05B65/465 - Locking bars secured in front of the drawers -> E05B65/467
[E05B 65/48] Hasp locks -- Hasp locks are simple mechanical fasteners consisting of a hinged metal plate (the hasp) that closes over a staple or loop and is secured with a pin or padlock, providing a detachable latch for doors, gates, cabinets and similar enclosures, often used where quick release or limited security is required.
[E05B 65/50] for briefcases -- E05B 65/50 covers locks designed specifically for briefcases, fitting within the broader category of fixed constructions and the sub-category of special-use locks. The classification addresses mechanisms that secure briefcase compartments, including latch types, combination devices and other locking arrangements tailored to portable cases.
[E05B 65/52] Other locks for chests, boxes, trunks, baskets, travelling bags, or the like -- E05B 65/52 covers lock devices intended for securing chests, boxes, trunks, baskets, travelling bags and similar containers. It includes any non-specific locking arrangements used to protect the contents during transport or storage, such as latch-type or cam locks, but excludes specialized mechanisms classified elsewhere.
[E05B 65/5207] characterised by bolt movement -- E05B 65/5207 classifies lock mechanisms that are defined by the movement of a bolt, typically sliding or retractable bolts used in specialized or high-security installations.
[E05B 65/5215] sliding -- The CPC subclass E05B 65/5215 refers to sliding locks used for special-purpose applications, such as doors, windows or panels that move linearly. It covers lock mechanisms that secure the element in the closed position while permitting controlled sliding motion.
[E05B 65/5223] perpendicular to the surface on which the lock is mounted -- The subclass E05B 65/5223 covers locks that are installed so the locking mechanism extends perpendicular to the surface on which the lock is mounted, typically used in special-use applications where the lock operates outward from the plane of a door, panel or other fixed construction.
[E05B 65/523] parallel to the surface on which the lock is mounted -- This subclass covers locks whose operative parts are arranged parallel to the surface on which they are mounted, such as flush-mounted or surface-mounted locks where the bolt or latch moves in a plane parallel to the door or panel. It includes designs where the lock body lies in the same plane as the mounting surface.
[E05B 65/5238] parallel to the wing edge -- E05B 65/5238 refers to a lock mechanism for special-use fixed constructions where the locking element is positioned parallel to the wing edge of the lock housing. This arrangement is used in doors or panels that need a low-profile, side-aligned bolt or latch.
[E05B 65/5246] rotating -- A rotating lock is a type of fixed-construction lock in which the locking element turns around an axis to engage or release the lock, often used where a simple twist action secures a door, drawer, or other enclosure. This design is classified under special-use locks in the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[E05B 65/5253] about an axis perpendicular to the surface on which the lock is mounted -- The classification concerns locks whose operating axis is oriented perpendicular to the surface on which the lock is installed, such as mechanisms that move out of the plane of a door or panel to engage or release. It applies to special-use fixed constructions where the lock's motion is along an axis normal to the mounting face.
[E05B 65/5261] with simultaneously operating double bolts -- A lock classified under E05B 65/5261 features two bolts that are driven to engage or retract at the same time, providing a unified locking action often used in heavy-duty or secure enclosures where rapid, simultaneous fastening of multiple points is required.
[E05B 65/5269] about an axis parallel to the surface on which the lock is mounted -- E05B 65/5269 designates locks whose operating axis runs parallel to the surface to which the lock is attached, a feature used in special-purpose fixed constructions such as recessed or surface-mounted security devices. The classification covers mechanisms where the rotational movement is aligned with the mounting plane rather than perpendicular.
[E05B 65/5276] parallel to the wing edge -- E05B 65/5276 classifies special-use fixed locks whose mechanism is arranged parallel to the edge of a wing, such as the side of a door or panel, rather than perpendicular. It covers lock designs that run along the wing edge to save space or meet specific functional requirements.
[E05B 65/5284] in which a movable latch is passed through a staple loop  similar fasteners for buckles  A44B11/00 -- This CPC subclass E05B 65/5284 relates to fixed-construction locks for special use in which a movable latch is inserted through a loop or staple, functioning similarly to buckle fasteners. The design provides a secure fastening method for items such as luggage, equipment cases, or safety devices.
[E05B 65/5292] Gravity - or orientation sensitive mechanisms, e.g. to prevent opening when wrongside-up -- A gravity- or orientation-sensitive device that stops a lock from opening when it is installed the wrong way round, using the position of the mechanism relative to gravity to block operation. It is classified under fixed-construction locks designed for special-use applications.
[E05B 67/00] Padlocks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Padlocks; Details thereof  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Permutation locks -> E05B37/00
[E05B 67/003] Chain, wire or cable locks  alarms therefor  E05B45/005 ;  E05B69/006  takes precedence -- E05B 67/003 covers locks that secure a chain, wire or cable, including the associated alarm devices that detect tampering or unauthorized removal. [E05B 67/006] with retraction of the cable for storage  E05B73/0029  takes precedence; for devices mounted to a fixed surface or a portable object  E05B73/0011
[E05B 67/02] Cases -- These are protective housings or enclosures that contain the internal mechanism of a padlock, often made of metal and designed to receive a shackle or bolt. They provide structural support, security against tampering and facilitate mounting or fastening of the lock.
[E05B 67/04] Armoured cases -- The CPC subclass E05B 67/04 pertains to armoured cases for padlocks, i.e., protective housings or enclosures that shield the lock mechanism against forced entry, impact or tampering. It includes designs such as steel or composite shells, reinforced covers and mounting brackets that form a durable outer shell around a padlock.
[E05B 67/06] Shackles -- E05B 67/06 designates shackles, the metal links that form the movable hasp or bolt of a padlock and provide a secure attachment point for locking. The class lies within fixed constructions, locks, padlocks.
[E05B 67/063] Padlocks with removable shackles -- Padlocks that feature a shackle which can be detached from the lock body, allowing the shackle to be removed for security, maintenance, or storage purposes, are classified under E05B 67/063. This design enables the lock to be used with a separate, removable fastening element.
[E05B 67/08] Padlocks with shackles hinged on the case -- Padlocks classified under E05B 67/08 are those whose shackle is mounted on a hinge attached to the lock case, allowing the shackle to pivot open for removal or replacement. This hinged-shackle arrangement is used to facilitate easy engagement while keeping the lock body fixed.
[E05B 67/10] with devices for securing the free end of the shackle -- E05B 67/10 is a Cooperative Patent Classification subcategory for padlocks that include mechanisms to secure the free end of the shackle, such as bars, hooks, or other locking members that engage the shackle when the lock is closed.
[E05B 67/14] with devices for securing the hinged end of the shackle -- The class E05B 67/14 covers padlocks whose shackle is hinged and includes a specific device that locks or otherwise secures the hinged end when the lock is closed. This sub-category addresses mechanisms such as latches, pins or spring-loaded catches that prevent the hinge from opening, thereby enhancing the overall security of the padlock.
[E05B 67/18] with devices for securing both ends of the shackle -- E05B 67/18 pertains to padlocks that include features securing both ends of the shackle, such as a mechanism that locks the free end as well as the hinge side, thereby enhancing resistance to forced opening.
[E05B 67/22] Padlocks with sliding shackles, with or without rotary or pivotal movement -- The subclass E05B 67/22 pertains to padlocks whose shackle slides into the lock body, optionally employing rotary or pivotal motion, allowing the shackle to be withdrawn or retracted for opening. This design is classified under Fixed Constructions → Locks → Padlocks.
[E05B 67/24] with built- in cylinder locks -- The code E05B 67/24 covers padlocks that incorporate a cylinder lock mechanism within the body of the device. It is used for fixed-construction locking systems where the key-operated cylinder is an integral part of the padlock rather than an attached accessory.
[E05B 67/26] with screw action, with or without the shackle being moved by turning the key -- The subclass E05B 67/26 comprises padlocks whose locking and unlocking are achieved by a screw-type action driven by the key, allowing the shackle to be engaged or released either through direct screw movement or by the shackle being moved as the key turns. This covers designs where the key rotates a threaded element that either pushes the shackle into a locked position or pulls it free without additional mechanisms.
[E05B 67/28] Padlocks with shackles forming a circle -- E05B 67/28 classifies padlocks whose shackle is formed as a continuous circle, creating a closed loop around the lock body. The circular shackle design offers increased resistance to cutting and pulling forces compared with conventional straight-shank padlocks.
[E05B 67/32] Padlocks with pincer- like shackles -- E05B 67/32 classifies padlocks whose shackle is formed as a pincer-like mechanism, typically with two hinged arms that close together to secure the lock. The design provides a reversible opening motion and is used where a quick release or rapid engagement of the shackle is required, distinguishing it from straight-stem or looped shackle types.
[E05B 67/36] Padlocks with closing means other than shackles -- The CPC subclass E05B 67/36 refers to padlocks whose closing mechanism is not a traditional shackle, encompassing designs that use integral latches, lever or other non-shackle closure types. This entry is nested under the broader categories of fixed constructions and locks, specifically within the padlock group.
[E05B 67/365] with locking means in the form of balls or rollers -- A padlock design in which the bolt is held by spherical or cylindrical locking elements such as balls or rollers instead of traditional pins or levers. The balls or rollers rotate into recesses when the key is turned, providing a compact and often more resistant locking mechanism.
[E05B 67/38] Auxiliary or protective devices -- The entry E05B 67/38 refers to auxiliary or protective devices used with padlocks in fixed-construction lock systems. It covers components that enhance security or durability, such as protective shrouds, covers, anti-pick or anti-cut attachments, and other supplemental elements that safeguard the lock body or its mechanism.
[E05B 67/383] Staples or the like for padlocks; Lock slings; Arrangements on locks to cooperate with padlocks -- The E05B 67/383 class covers components that enable a padlock to be attached to a fixed structure, such as staples, lock slings, or other arrangements that cooperate with a padlock. It includes devices that provide a secure point for the shackle to engage, allowing the lock to be fastened to rails, bars, or similar fittings.
[E05B 69/00] Locking devices for clothing, sticks, umbrellas, or cycles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for locking clothing, shoes, lockable hangers or hanger racks and lockable clothing hooks.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Chain cables or padlocks with alarms -> E05B45/005 - Anti-theft tags or monitors -> E05B73/0017
[E05B 69/003] for locking shoes against removal -- A shoe-locking device that prevents the footwear from being taken off without releasing a built-in mechanism, such as a clasp, latch or strap that secures the shoe to the foot or to an external holder. It is classified under fixed constructions for clothing, specifically locks for shoes.
[E05B 69/006] Lockable hangers or hanger racks -- E05B 69/006 pertains to lockable hangers or hanger racks designed to secure items such as clothing, sticks, umbrellas or bicycles. The classification covers fixed-construction devices that incorporate a locking mechanism to prevent removal or tampering with the hung objects.
[E05B 69/02] Lockable clothing hooks -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - For means for safe-keeping of property, left temporarily, e.g. by fastening the property -> G07F17/10
[E05B 7/00] Handles pivoted about an axis parallel to the wing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Handles pivoted about an axis parallel to the wing  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Handles for vehicles -> E05B85/10 - E05B85/18
[E05B 71/00] Locks specially adapted for bicycles, other than padlocks -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks specially adapted for bicycles, other than padlocks  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Locks integral with holders for parking bicycles -> B62H3/00 - Locks integral with cycles -> B62H5/00
[E05B 73/00] Devices for locking portable objects against unauthorised removal -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for locking portable objects against unauthorised removal; Miscellaneous locking devices  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Locks specially adapted for bicycles -> E05B71/00 - Anti-theft arrangements for helmets -> A42B3/0413 - Devices for storing or displaying riffles or guns -> A47B81/005 - Show cases with theft protection -> A47F3/002 - Anti-theft means for display panels -> A47F5/0861 - Show stands with provision for preventing unauthorised removal -> A47F7/024 - Anti-theft devices for skis or ski equipment -> A63C11/004 - Covers cups or guards for traction coupling, hitches -> B60D1/60 - Supporting devices for attaching articles to cycles -> B62J11/00
[E05B 73/0005] using chains, cables or the like  chain- or cable-locks of the padlock type  E05B67/003 ; lockable clothing hangers or hanger racks  E05B69/006 -- This class covers devices that secure portable items with chains, cables or similar, including padlock-type chain or cable locks and lockable clothing hangers or hanger racks. It falls under fixed constructions - locks - devices for locking portable objects against unauthorised removal.
[E05B 73/0011] with retraction of the flexible element for storage  E05B73/0029  takes precedence; for padlocks  E05B67/006 -- E05B 73/0011 covers devices that lock portable objects using a flexible element-such as a cable, chain or strap-that can be retracted into the lock for compact storage. The retractable mechanism is intended to protect the flexible component and simplify handling, and it is subordinate to the broader class E05B 73/0029 while excluding padlock-specific designs covered in E05B 67/006.
[E05B 73/0017] Anti-theft devices, e.g. tags or monitors, fixed to articles, e.g. clothes, and to be removed at the check-out of shops -- The entry E05B 73/0017 covers anti-theft devices that are affixed to portable items such as clothing, tags or monitors, and are intended to be removed when the item is purchased. These fixed constructions deter unauthorized removal of the article while it is in a retail environment.
[E05B 73/0023] Containers, boxes, cases or the like, e.g. for compact discs or video-cassettes, specially adapted therefor -- The entry E05B 73/0023 covers containers, boxes, cases or similar housings that are specially adapted to lock portable items such as compact discs, video-cassettes or other media, preventing unauthorised removal. It falls under the fixed-construction locks category for devices that secure removable objects.
[E05B 73/0029] Tags wrapped around the protected product using cables, wires or the like, e.g. with cable retraction for tensioning -- The entry pertains to devices that secure a portable item by wrapping a tag around it and fastening the tag with cables, wires or similar means. It often includes a cable-retraction system that keeps the tension high to prevent unauthorized removal.
[E05B 73/0035] for eyeglasses or spectacles -- E05B 73/0035 covers locking mechanisms specifically designed for eyeglasses or spectacles, enabling the frames to be secured to a surface or holder to deter unauthorized removal. Such devices may include brackets, clamps, or attachment systems that fasten the glasses in place while allowing convenient release by the owner.
[E05B 73/0041] for essentially round objects, e.g. bottles or racket handles -- E05B 73/0041 relates to locking devices specifically adapted for essentially round portable items, such as bottles or racket handles, to prevent unauthorized removal. It falls under fixed-construction locks that secure cylindrical objects.
[E05B 73/0047] Unlocking tools; Decouplers -- This class covers tools and devices used to open or disengage locking mechanisms on portable items such as safes, lockers or luggage, including pick sets, key cutters and release mechanisms designed to overcome unauthorized removal restraints. It also includes decouplers that separate linked lock components.
[E05B 73/0052] of the magnetic type -- E05B 73/0052 classifies magnetic locking devices used to secure portable items such as luggage, containers or toolboxes against unauthorized removal. These systems employ magnetic attraction to hold a lock closed and release it when a magnetic key or de-energised coil is applied, fitting within the broader group of fixed construction locks for portable objects.
[E05B 73/0058] for essentially box-shaped devices -- E05B 73/0058 covers fixed-construction locks designed to secure portable items, specifically those that are essentially box-shaped, such as cases or containers, using mechanisms that prevent unauthorized removal. This subclass falls under the broader category of locks for portable objects within the Cooperative Patent Classification system.
[E05B 73/0064] of the mechanical type -- This CPC subclass covers mechanical devices used to secure portable items against theft or loss. It includes lock mechanisms that rely on physical components such as tumblers, levers or cams rather than electronic or magnetic means. The classification applies to locks fitted to luggage, containers, tools and similar objects.
[E05B 73/007] for boats, surfboards or parts or accessories thereof -- The CPC entry E05B 73/007 covers locking devices specifically intended to secure boats, surfboards or their parts and accessories against unauthorised removal. It includes mechanisms integrated into hulls, deck fittings, storage racks or other fixed constructions that prevent theft or accidental loss of these portable watercraft and related components.
[E05B 73/0076] outboard motors or propellers -- The code E05B 73/0076 covers locking devices designed specifically for outboard motors or propellers, preventing their unauthorised removal from a vessel. These mechanisms are classified under fixed constructions for securing portable objects, providing a means to deter theft of marine propulsion equipment.
[E05B 73/0082] for office machines, e.g. PC's, portable computers, typewriters, calculators -- A device for securing portable office equipment-such as personal computers, laptops, typewriters or calculators-against theft or unauthorised removal, classified under the fixed-construction locks for portable objects (E05B 73/0082).
[E05B 73/0094] for skate-boards -- E05B 73/0094 designates locking devices specifically adapted to secure skate-boards against unauthorized removal. These are fixed-construction mechanisms that attach to or encircle a skateboard, providing a physical barrier to deter theft while allowing the board to remain portable.
[E05B 73/02] for walking-sticks or umbrellas -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Locks integral with walking sticks -> A45B1/04
[E05B 75/00] Handcuffs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Handcuffs; Finger cuffs; Leg irons; Handcuff holsters; Means for locking prisoners in automobiles
[E05B 75/005] Handcuff holders, e.g. for storage -- Handcuff holders are devices designed to retain handcuffs in a fixed position for convenient storage and quick access, often incorporated into police equipment racks or vehicle interiors. They fall under the fixed constructions group of the Cooperative Patent Classification, specifically within the locks subclass, and are identified by code E05B 75/005.
[E05B 77/00] Locks for vehicles other than bicycles -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Locks specially adapted for bicycles -> E05B71/00 - Locking arrangements for nonfixed vehicle roofs -> B60J7/185  **Glossary:** - latching/unlatching: holding/release of a bolt element that a wing or door can be opened. A typical vehicle lock is unlatched when a detent, pawl or ratchet releases the fork shaped bolt - locking/unlocking: preventing/allowing an unlatching action. In the locked state an unlatching action, normally done by actuating a handle or grip, is prevented. A locked door or wing is at least prevented from being unlatched from outside of the vehicle
[E05B 77/02] for accident situations -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks designed to perform or prevent certain functions in case of accidents
[E05B 77/04] Preventing unwanted lock actuation, e.g. unlatching, at the moment of collision -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks, where an unwanted unlatching is prevented in case of an accident. One particular example is that a door handle could be accidentally actuated in case of a collision against the side door of a vehicle.  One way of preventing such an unwanted actuation is the provision of an extra long bowden cable. The extra length thereby prevents unwanted actuation, if a vehicle part is deformed.  The figure shows another construction, which should avoid actuation at the moment of a collision. Two parallel connecting rods (10, 11) are provided, such that if one of the rods (10,11) would be deformed during an accident, then it could not cause the lever (9) to turn since the second lever (11) would resist this by an equal force acting to turn the lever (9) in the opposite direction.
[E05B 77/06] by means of inertial forces -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for preventing actuation caused by inertial forces acting on lock parts in case of an accident. Typically, a weight arrangement is used, which is actuated in order to block a handle against movement at the moment of collision.  The figure shows an example whereby at the moment of a collision a force (29b) would cause a mass-lever (30) to rotate counterclockwise. A blocking member (34) of the mass-lever (30) thereby prevents the handle (37) to be actuated by an inertial force (32b).
[E05B 77/08] Arrangements for protection of pedestrians -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks designed for pedestrian protection. In practice, it almost always concerns engine compartment locks ( E05B83/24 )  The example in the figure concerns an active front hood system, whereby both the hinge part (41) and the lock (25) are designed such that they are automatically raising the hood (13) in case of a collision with a pedestrian. This allows the front zone of the vehicle to deform easier.
[E05B 77/10] Allowing opening in case of deformed bodywork, e.g. by preventing deformation of lock parts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The general problem that a wing/door is frequently deformed at an accident. In this subgroup the lock is protected in such a way that it can be unlatched/unlocked even if the door is deformed. This is often achieved by protective plates, shields and reinforcements.  Another alternative is to design lock parts in order to be deformed in a controlled way, such that they can still function after an accident. In the figure the bolt (8) and the detent (13) are provided with deformation zones (17, 25, 26). In case of an accident the bolt and the detent will not break. They will rather be deformed plastically such that they will still function properly after the collision.
[E05B 77/12] Automatic locking or unlocking at the moment of collision -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements whereby a particular action is forced to take place in case of an accident. The situation is more or less the opposite of E05B77/04 in which a particular action is prevented.  A typical example is that a locking or an unlocking action is initiated at the accident. The choice of action depends on the security philosophy. Sometimes an unlocked door, which allows easy escape, is preferred and sometimes the preference is a locked door, which prevents (unwanted) opening of the door.  Another frequent example from this class is locks which after an accident remain locked for a certain time and then automatically move to an unlocked condition.  The example of the figure shows a system whereby a sensing means (50) is designed to pivot in case of an accident. This releases the bolt element (26) which is moved by a spring (52) into a striker (56). The door is therefore automatically locked at the moment of an accident.
[E05B 77/14] Specially controlled locking actions in case of open doors or in case of doors moved from an open to a closed position, e.g. lock-out prevention or self-cancelling -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks which usually concern the problem that vehicle keys can be left inside the vehicle, if the door would be accidentally closed in a locked condition.
[E05B 77/16] Preventing locking with the bolt in the unlatched position, i.e. when the door is open -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks in which the bolt position is sensed e.g. mechanically, magnetically or electrically. An unlatched bolt should thereby indicate an open door, and in this situation the locking system is blocked in order to make it possible to lock the door only after it has been closed.  The example of the figure illustrates this by a detent (20) having a cam (46) and a locking lever (24) having a shoulder (48).  When the bolt (14) is in its open position, the locking lever (24) cannot move to the locked position, since the shoulder (48) of the locking lever (24) is blocked by the cam (46) on the detent.
[E05B 77/18] Keyless locking with self-cancellation, e.g. resulting in an unlocking action when the door is being closed -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks where the closing of the door always results in an unlocking action. The lock will thus never be in a locked state after having closed the door. This means that the door can always be unlatched after closing.
[E05B 77/20] Override of self-cancellation, e.g. by actuation of the handle while the door is being closed -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements whereby the automatic unlocking action can be prevented by overriding means, which require an additional action by the user, e.g. that the door handle is actuated during the closing of the door.  The user can thus decide, that the door should remain locked after closing, but this choice requires that a certain action is carried out during the closing. This makes it less likely, that the door is by mistake closed in a locked state.  According to the exemplifying figures, a lever (60) is actuated by a handle and can be used for unlatching the lock, if a release lever (64) can hit a pin (42) on the detent.  A locking lever (66) can move the release lever (64) to a locked position in which it will not hit the pin (42) as shown in the lower figure. A cam element (92a, 92b) rotates together with the actual latch bolt.
