If the lock is brought to the locked position when the door and thereby also the bolt are in the open position, then the closing of the door will rotate the bolt to the latched position. The cam (92b) will therefore act on an intermediate element (94), which brings the locking lever (66) back to the unlocked position.  If however the handle lever (60) is actuated during the closing of the door, then the cam (92b) will not be able to contact the intermediate3 element (94) as shown in the right-hand figure. The lock will thereby stay in the locked position.
[E05B 77/22] Functions related to actuation of locks from the passenger compartment of the vehicle -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Certain functions linked to the inside actuation of locks.
[E05B 77/24] preventing use of an inner door handle, sill button, lock knob or the like -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Essentially the subjects "child safety" and "super-locking/double-locking". Even though the general purpose of preventing an inside handle from being used is the same for these both functions, there is one fundamental difference.  Child safety prevents the use of an inside handle at any time, i.e. the inside handle can never be used regardless if the door is unlocked or locked from the outside. A double-locked/super-locked door has an inside handle, which cannot be used, when the door is locked from the outside.
[E05B 77/245] by blocking the movement of a movable element -- The CPC group E05B 77/245 relates to locks for vehicles other than bicycles that secure a component by physically obstructing its movement. It covers mechanisms such as dead-bolts, latch blocks or other devices that lock by preventing a movable element from being displaced.
[E05B 77/26] specially adapted for child safety -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks which prevent all use of an inside handle, when the child safety is activated. This means that the inside handle cannot be used regardless if the door is unlocked or locked from the outside.  The figure shows a child safety arrangement with an actuation button (7) of the push-push type. Every time the button (7) is pushed, the system changes between an active and an inactive state.
[E05B 77/265] hand actuated, e.g. by a lever at the edge of the door -- The code E05B 77/265 covers hand-actuated locking devices for vehicle doors other than bicycles that are operated by a lever mounted at the edge of the door. Such mechanisms are engaged or released by pulling or pushing the lever.
[E05B 77/28] for anti-theft purposes, e.g. double-locking or super-locking -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks having an inside handle, which cannot be used, when the door is locked from the outside. It is therefore not possible to open the door by breaking a window in order to reach the inside handle.  The double-locking can be achieved either by uncoupling or blocking of the inside lock system and inside handle.  In the figure, an example of a blocking system is shown. A double-lock arrangement (9, 12) prevents any actuation of an inside lock knob rod (7).
[E05B 77/283] initiated by hand actuation, e.g. by using a mechanical key -- The subclass E05B 77/283 covers vehicle locks (excluding bicycles) that are opened or closed by a manual action, such as turning a mechanical key or other hand-actuated device. It classifies lock mechanisms where the user's hand directly initiates the locking or unlocking motion.
[E05B 77/287] Prevention or overriding of a super-lock condition, e.g. for preventing being trapped in the vehicle -- A super-lock condition occurs when a vehicle's locking system holds all doors closed, risking occupants being trapped. This subclass covers devices that detect such a condition and automatically release or override the lock, ensuring safe egress in emergencies. It applies to locks on cars, trucks, and other motor vehicles rather than bicycles.
[E05B 77/30] allowing opening by means of an inner door handle, even if the door is locked -- **Definition:** This place covers:  A combined unlocking and unlatching function of the inside door handle. This is sometimes achieved by one single actuation of the handle. Another possibility is that a first actuation of the handle unlocks the door and that the second actuation unlatches the door.  The figure shows an example of an inside handle (7), which can be moved from a neutral position (A) to an unlatching position (B). If the door would be locked, then the same handle can be moved from the neutral position (A) to an intermediate position (C) and back to the neutral position (A) in order to unlock the lock. The handle can thereafter be actuated one more time to position (C) for unlatching.
[E05B 77/32] allowing simultaneous actuation of locking or unlocking elements and a handle, e.g. preventing interference between an unlocking and an unlatching action -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements addressing the well known problem that an unlocking (or locking) action can fail, if the handle of the door is actuated at the same time.  Often a second unlocking action is required. This problem is normally avoided by an energy accumulation arrangement of springs.
[E05B 77/34] Protection against weather or dirt, e.g. against water ingress -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means offering protection against weather influence. This can for example be achieved by a totally closed lock housing or by arranging some parts of the lock in a "wet compartment" and other parts in a "dry compartment". Another type of weather protection concerns means preventing freezing of the lock in cold conditions.  In the figure, a "wet area" (N) of the lock comprises the main lock elements, i.e. the bolt (3a) and the detent (3b). A sensing arrangement (7a, 7b) for determination of the detent position is housed in a "dry area" (T) o the lock. A single thin link rod (50) extends from the "wet area" to the "dry area" through a small opening (80).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Closures or guards for keyholes -> E05B17/14
[E05B 77/36] Noise prevention -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ways of reducing noise and vibrations in locks. The example in the figure shows a lever element (8) of a lock. The end positions of the lever are defined by stretching of cord elements (3, 3a). The use of cords for this purpose reduces the noise of the lever (8) reaching its end position.
[E05B 77/38] Cushion elements, elastic guiding elements or holding elements, e.g. for cushioning or damping the impact of the bolt against the striker during closing of the wing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Elastic blocks, elastic guiding elements etc. which are typically placed in the zone of engagement between the bolt and the striker. One function of such blocks can thus be to make an impact between moving elements softer.  Another purpose can be to hold for example a striker firmly fixed in the latched position. This prevents noise due to rattling.  In the figure, an elastic cushion means (30, 40) defines a seat for firmly holding the striker (2).
[E05B 77/40] Lock elements covered by silencing layers, e.g. coatings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Bolts, strikers, detents etc. which are covered by a (thin) anti-noise coating/layer. One rather typical example is a metal bolt with a plastic coating.  According to the exemplifying figure, a coating (16) of elastically deformable material allows any direct contact between the base plate (2) of the striker and the bodywork of the vehicle. The noise produced during closing of the lock is thereby reduced.
[E05B 77/42] Means for damping the movement of lock parts, e.g. slowing down the return movement of a handle -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements for damping of movements. This class does however not cover elastic blocks/guides ( E05B77/38 ). It is rather directed to dampers, for example of the fluid type. A typical example is a damper, which slows down the return movement of a handle.  The return movement of the handle in the figure is slowed down by a fluid damper (30).
[E05B 77/44] Burglar prevention, e.g. protecting against opening by unauthorised tools -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks where an arrangement has been provided, which prevents manipulation by burglar tools. Typical examples are protective shields, completely closed casings, or plates which deflect tools away from the essential lock parts.  The figure shows a hollow shield (34) which covers and protects the locking lever (14) and the linkage (20,24) between the locking lever (14) and the lock cylinder (30).
[E05B 77/46] Locking several wings simultaneously -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Central locking arrangements. The lower subgroups concern the main families of such arrangements, and the example in the figure therefore shows a more unusual central lock configuration.  A mechanical central lock arrangement uses actuation cables (110,112) for locking/unlocking two doors by a single action performed at one of the two doors.
[E05B 77/465] Automatic locking when all passengers have left the vehicle -- E05B 77/465 covers systems that automatically lock a vehicle's doors after the last occupant exits. The technology typically uses weight, motion or proximity sensors to detect an empty cabin and triggers the lock mechanism without driver action. It is classified under fixed constructions, specifically locks for non-bicycle vehicles.
[E05B 77/48] by electrical means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electrical circuits for control of several locks in a central locking system. This subgroup is essentially directed to the overall electric/electronic circuits for the control of a central door lock system.  In the example each door has an electrically controlled lock module (2), and these four lock module are connected (19) to a central processing and control unit (18)
[E05B 77/50] by pneumatic or hydraulic means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pneumatic or hydraulic circuits for control of several locks in a central locking system. This subgroup is essentially directed to the overall circuits, as exemplified by the figure wherein a number of actuators (1-4) is controlled by a central control system.
[E05B 77/52] Locking one wing by shutting another -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Sequential lock control of a vehicle door system. As shown in the figure, when a first door (A) is unlatched a second door (B) is unlocked, and when the first door (A) is closed and its bolt moves to a latched position, then the bolt of the second door (B) is automatically locked.
[E05B 77/54] Automatic securing or unlocking of bolts triggered by certain vehicle parameters, e.g. exceeding a speed threshold -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locking of vehicle doors, based on the state of the vehicle systems in general. The state of the door locks could thereby be decided based on for example the speed of the vehicle, the status of the ignition lock, the selected gear or the actuation of the vehicle brakes.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Triggered by vehicle collision -> E05B77/12
[E05B 79/00] Mounting or connecting vehicle locks or parts thereof -- E05B 79/00 classifies the ways vehicle lock mechanisms or their components are mounted, secured or linked to a vehicle structure, including brackets, housings, fasteners and other connecting devices. It covers the integration of locks with doors, hoods, trunks or other parts of a vehicle.
[E05B 79/02] Mounting of vehicle locks or parts thereof -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Various aspects of the mounting of lock elements or mounting of the lock itself. It is to be noted, that this subgroup does not cover the connection of movable parts to each other ( E05B79/10 ).
[E05B 79/04] Mounting of lock casings to the vehicle, e.g. to the wing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ways of attaching the lock casing to the vehicle. The casing is thereby often mounted to the wing, but it may also concern the mounting of casings on the fixed frame.  According to the example of the figure, bolt elements (36) are used as axis for e.g. the bolt and the detent of the lock. At the same time these bolt elements (36) have threaded parts (50) which are used for mounting the lock casing (22) to the vehicle.
[E05B 79/06] Mounting of handles, e.g. to the wing or to the lock -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ways of mounting the handles specific elements in the lock. The figure shows a system using two different screw arrangements (6, 7) for attaching the handle to the wing.
[E05B 79/08] Mounting of individual lock elements in the lock, e.g. levers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ways of mounting specific elements in the lock. According to the example in the figure, a specific type of pivot axis (32) is used for mounting the bolt (12) and the detent (14).
[E05B 79/10] Connections between movable lock parts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Various ways of connecting movable lock parts to each other. The lower subgroups cover he most frequently represented connection aspects.
[E05B 79/12] using connecting rods -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Elongated connection elements. According to the example shown in the figure a connecting rod (2) is provided with an extension (3) having a hooked end (4). If the door and the rod would be deformed, e.g. due to a collision, then the hooked extension of the rod can cooperate with the edge of an opening (6) such that unwanted operation of the lock occurs.
[E05B 79/14] the rods being linked to each other -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ways of connecting rods to each other, as exemplified by a connecting element (40), which links two operating rods (31, 32) to each other.
[E05B 79/16] characterised by the means for linking the rods to other lock parts, e.g. to levers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of how connecting rods are fixed to other lock elements. This would usually be the connection to a handle element or to a lever element in the lock housing. It can also concern the details of how a rod is connected to the output member of a powered actuator.  According to the example, a rod clip has one part (23), which is connected to a lever of the lock mechanism, while another part (22,32) connects to an operating rod (40).
[E05B 79/18] Rod guides -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for guiding long rod elements. In the case of fairly long rod elements it might be necessary to provide guiding means along the length of the rod. This is of particular importance in order to prevent bending of the rod, when it is compressed during a pushing action.  A typical example of such a rod guide (7) is shown in the figure.
[E05B 79/20] using flexible connections, e.g. Bowden cables -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Non-rigid connections between lock parts. Bowden cables are frequently used in these situations, but also wires, ropes or the use of springs as connection means are covered by this subgroup.  The figure shows a specific type of Bowden-cable connection, whereby a cable (32) is fixed at one end (36). Tensioning the cable (36) will therefore cause the sheath to be straightened and thereby push on a lever (12) of the lock.
[E05B 79/22] Operative connections between handles, sill buttons or lock knobs and the lock unit -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of how hand actuated elements are connected to lock elements. It is to be noted that E05B79/12 and E05B79/20 quite often will cover the connection of handles to a lock situated at a distance more or less far away.  This subgroup will consequently for example cover operative, i.e. movable, connections where a handle is more or less directly fixed to the lock. Other elongated connections than rods or flexible elements will also be covered by this subgroup.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Mounting of the non-movable base elements of a handle to a lock -> E05B79/06
[E05B 81/00] Power-actuated vehicle locks -- **Glossary:** - latching/unlatching: holding/release of a bolt element that a wing or door can be opened. A typical vehicle lock is unlatched when a detent, pawl or ratchet releases the fork shaped bolt - locking/unlocking: preventing/allowing an unlatching action. In the locked state an unlatching action, normally done by actuating a handle or grip, is prevented. A locked door or wing is at least prevented from being unlatched from outside of the vehicle
[E05B 81/01] Comfort closing, i.e. the locking action for a wing is combined with actuation of other vehicle functions, e.g. locking the doors and closing the windows simultaneously -- E05B 81/01 covers comfort-closing systems in motor vehicles where the act of closing a wing (such as a door) is linked to additional functions, for example automatically locking the doors and lowering windows at the same time.
[E05B 81/02] characterised by the type of actuators used -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Various types of actuators, whereby the most common alternatives are covered by the lower subgroups.  One example of a less common power driving means is shown in the figure, which shows a pyrotechnic charge (10) provided for bringing a bolt (4) or a connecting rod (24) to an unlocked position in case of an accident.
[E05B 81/04] Electrical -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Actuators driven by electricity. The figure shows a lock with a handle (3), which can release a bolt (2) from a striker (6). The bolt (2) can also be released by electrically heating a shape memory alloy actuator wire (9). This heating shrinks the actuator wire, and the bolt (2) will consequently be released from the striker (6).  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Electrical circuits -> E05B81/54
[E05B 81/05] moving in one direction only -- A power-actuated vehicle lock that is designed to move only in a single direction when engaged, preventing any reversal of motion during operation. This ensures a one-way actuation of door, trunk or other vehicle access points.
[E05B 81/06] using rotary motors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electrically driven motors, as exemplified by the motor (2) of the figure.
[E05B 81/08] using electromagnets or solenoids -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electromagnets or solenoids, most often of the linear type. However, as the figure exemplifies, also rotary electromagnets (10) are used for vehicle lock actuation.
[E05B 81/10] Hydraulic or pneumatic -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Actuators of the hydraulic or pneumatic type. In the figure, a pneumatic actuator (8) is used for powered actuation of a boot lid lock.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Hydraulic or pneumatic circuits -> E05B81/52
[E05B 81/12] characterised by the function or purpose of the powered actuators -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Various ways of using an actuator in a vehicle lock. These uses are thereby in principal defined depending on which lock part(s) the actuator is connected to.
[E05B 81/13] a single actuator for driving a lock and additional vehicle components, e.g. window wipers or window lifters -- The classification covers vehicle lock mechanisms that use a single power-actuated unit to operate the lock together with other components such as window wipers or window lifters, integrating multiple functions into one actuator. This design simplifies wiring and control while providing coordinated movement of the lock and ancillary systems.
[E05B 81/14] operating on bolt detents, e.g. for unlatching the bolt -- **Definition:** This place covers:  A typical vehicle lock comprises a fork-shaped rotary latch bolt, which is kept in its latched position by a detent. In this subgroup the power actuator releases the detent of the bolt and consequently an unlatching takes place. The result of the powered actuation is thus that the door is free to be opened.  The figure shows an actuator (12), which drives cam elements (17) through a geared transmission. The cam elements (17) act on a detent (2) in order to unlatch the bolt (1).
[E05B 81/15] with means preventing the detent to return to its latching position before the bolt has moved to the unlatched position -- The CPC subclass E05B 81/15 covers power-actuated vehicle locks that include a safety feature preventing the detent from re-engaging its latching position until the lock bolt has fully moved to the unlatched position, ensuring the lock cannot accidentally re-lock while the door is still opening.
[E05B 81/16] operating on locking elements for locking or unlocking action -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Actuators which achieve the movement of a locking element (typically a locking lever) between a locked and an unlocked position.  The actuator consequently moves the lock between an unlocked state wherein the bolt is allowed to move and a locked state in which the movement of the bolt is prevented.  The movement of the bolt is thus not directly influenced by the actuator. A very common example in this subgroup is the powered locking/unlocking of central-locking arrangements.  According to the figure, a locking lever (10) is pivotally mounted on the output axis (12) of an actuator (14). The locking lever (10) can be moved between a locked and an unlocked position by means of the actuator (14).
[E05B 81/18] to effect movement of a bolt or bolts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Actuator acting on the bolt in order to move it, normally from a position in which it holds the door closed to a position in which the door can be opened, or vice-versa. It could thereby concern both latch bolts and dead bolts.  The figure shows an actuator (108), which drives a lock bolt (124) between a locked and unlocked position.
[E05B 81/20] for assisting final closing or for initiating opening -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pulling the door tight or pushing the door slightly open. It is to be noted, that this subgroup covers the cases where these (small) movements of the door are achieved by the lock. This should not be confused with the powered closing /opening of for example E05F15/00 , where the wing is moved from a (more or less) open position to a (more or less) closed position.  The example illustrates this by an actuator (21), which uses a pushing element (22) in order to assist movement of a bolt from a half-latched to a fully-latched position.
[E05B 81/21] with means preventing or detecting pinching of objects or body parts -- E05B 81/21 designates power-actuated vehicle locking mechanisms equipped with safety devices that prevent or detect the pinching of objects or body parts, such as sensors or retractable elements that stop the lock when resistance is sensed.
[E05B 81/22] by movement of the striker -- **Definition:** This place covers:  A movable striker for achieving the final closing or the initiated opening. A typical example is that after a first engagement between a striker and a bolt, the striker is moved by an actuator in order to further pull the wing tight.  The exemplifying figure shows an actuator in the form of an electric motor (8). When an axis (9) is turned by the motor (8), a pin (6) moves in an inclined slot (5) of a striker base (2). The actual striker (3) can thereby be driven by the motor (8) for assisting the movement of the door to a fully closed position.
[E05B 81/24] characterised by constructional features of the actuator or the power transmission -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Various individual aspects of actuator details and transmission constructions.
[E05B 81/25] Actuators mounted separately from the lock and controlling the lock functions through mechanical connections -- Covers mechanisms where the actuation unit is situated away from the lock itself and transmits motion through rods, levers, cables or gears to operate vehicle door or compartment locks. It applies to power-actuated locks whose controller is mechanically linked rather than integrated.
[E05B 81/26] Output elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Different types of actuator output elements. It thereby essentially concerns the final element in the kinematic chain of the actuator device. Such an output element is then typically connected to elements of a vehicle lock, either directly or via further connecting means. The lower subgroups E05B81/28 and E05B81/30 relate to the two most common types of output members.
[E05B 81/28] Linearly reciprocating elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Output elements which move linearly, as exemplified by the sliding output member (105) in the figure.
[E05B 81/30] Rotary elements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Output elements which rotate, as exemplified by rotatable output members (10,12) which both rotate around axis (13) in the figure.
[E05B 81/32] Details of the actuator transmission -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Various elements in the kinematic chain before the actuator output member. Different typical kinematic elements are the subject of the lower subgroups E05B81/34 – E05B81/46 .
[E05B 81/34] of geared transmissions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Geared elements in the kinematic chain of the actuator, as exemplified by the toothed gear elements (8) which are driven by the worm-screw (19).
[E05B 81/36] Geared sectors, e.g. fan-shaped gears -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Elements in the shape of a sector with gears on its outer rim. An example of such a sector (10) is shown in the figure.
[E05B 81/38] Planetary gears -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The use of planetary gear transmission in actuators, as exemplified by the figure.
[E05B 81/40] Nuts or nut-like elements moving along a driven threaded axle -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Transmissions of the type where a nut-like element (6) slides along a threaded rotating axle (4) as exemplified by the figure.
[E05B 81/42] Cams -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cam-like transmission elements, for example a motor driven cam (34), which is used for transmitting a movement to elements of the actual lock.
[E05B 81/44] in the form of grooves -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The use of the contour of a groove as cam element for transmission of a movement, as exemplified by the groove (3) in the figure.
[E05B 81/46] Clutches -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Transmissions comprising some kind of engagable/releasable clutch arrangement. In the figure, a clutch element (23) engages a groove (21) in a driven element (13), when a motor driven element (9) starts rotating.
[E05B 81/48] Actuators being driven in a single direction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Actuators moving only in one direction. From a starting position, the actuator moves continuously in one step or in several steps, always in the same direction, until it arrives back at the starting position again.  One example can be seen in the figure, where the motor (2) and the actuating element (8) can only turn in one direction (3,9). An electric arrangement (17) is provided for minimizing the back-drive when the drive pin (10) hits an abutment stop (12). When the detent pawl (11) has moved further clock-wise, then the actuator can continue to move in its single driving direction (3,9).  It is to be noted, that essentially the whole movement cycle should be power actuated. If for example a significant part of the movement is achieved by e.g. a mechanical spring, then the subgroup E05B81/50 takes precedence.
[E05B 81/50] Powered actuators with automatic return to the neutral position by non-powered means, e.g. by springs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Actuators which are power driven to move from a neutral position to an active position, and the return movement to the neutral position is provided by non-powered means.  Examples of such arrangements can for example be a linearly moving solenoid, whereby an output member is moved electro-magnetically from a neutral position to an active position. When the power later is cut, a spring is arranged for moving the output member.  In the figure a geared transmission is moved by powered means to an active position. At the same time a geared rack (130) is moved, and this compresses spring (146). When the power is cut, the accumulated energy of the compressed spring (146) moves the rack (130) in the opposite direction such that the actuator returns to its normal position.
[E05B 81/52] Pneumatic or hydraulic circuits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hydraulic or pneumatic circuits of vehicle locks. This subgroup concerns the overall circuits (pipes, valves pumps etc.) providing pressurized fluid to the actuators, while the structural elements of hydraulic/pneumatic actuators can be found in E05B81/10 .  In the figure a typical example of a circuit in the sense of the present subgroup can be seen.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - For locking several wings simultaneously -> E05B77/50
[E05B 81/54] Electrical circuits -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Electric circuit schemes and individual circuit components. The lower subgroups concern a variety of specific control methods, monitoring, sensing and energy supply. This subgroup will consequently contain e.g. electrical circuits not covered by the various lower subgroups.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Electric circuits for controlling plurality of locks on different doors -> E05B77/48
[E05B 81/56] Control of actuators -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Various ways of controlling the way in which an actuator works. The most common control methods can be found in the respective lower subgroup. This means, that this subgroup will for example contain electrical circuits in cases which are not covered by one of the specific lower subgroups. An example of such a circuit is given in the figure, whereby an electrical control circuit (6) is provided in order to control the power input to the motor connectors (9,10).
[E05B 81/58] including time control, e.g. for controlling run-time of electric motors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The use of time data for the control of actuators. In the example of the figure, a switch indicates when 50% of the actuator stroke has been completed. The time needed for this first half of the stroke is measured and used in order to calculate how much run-time will be needed for achieving the second half of the stroke.
[E05B 81/60] using pulse control, e.g. pulse-width modulation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of actuators by pulses e.g. of varying length and frequency. One way of providing such control is shown in the figure, whereby the pulse-width for operating the actuator to release the detent (4) from the bolt (1) is different from the pulse-width used for bringing the detent (4) back to the initial position.
[E05B 81/62] for opening or closing of a circuit depending on electrical parameters, e.g. increase of motor current -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The use of electrical parameters for actuator control. A typical example of such a controlling method is to detect an increase of the current when an end position is reached, as exemplified by the figure wherein a current increase (124) is detected for stopping the movement of an actuator in one direction and a subsequent current increase (134) causes the stopping of the movement in the opposite direction.
[E05B 81/64] Monitoring or sensing, e.g. by using switches or sensors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Detection of lock states or user input by sensing/monitoring positions of various lock elements.
[E05B 81/66] the bolt position, i.e. the latching status -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Monitoring of the lock status by sensing the bolt position. In the exemplifying figure two switches (55,57) are used for indicating the position of the bolt (17.
[E05B 81/68] by sensing the position of the detent -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Control of the lock status by sensing the detent position. In the figure, a sensor (10) detects the position of the bolt as well as the position of the detent.
[E05B 81/70] the wing position -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Indication of the closure status by sensing the door position. It should be noted, that the sensors for door position should be part of the lock or at least related to the function of the lock. An example of a lock monitoring system, which uses the detection of the door position is shown in the figure.
[E05B 81/72] the lock status, i.e. locked or unlocked condition -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Lock monitoring by sensing a locked/unlocked condition. In the figure, such a monitoring is performed by a switch (7).
[E05B 81/74] by sensing the state of the actuator -- **Definition:** This place covers:  A special case of sensing the lock status via the state of the actuator. The figure shows such an arrangement, whereby a protrusion (32) comes into contact either of the two plates (36,38). This closes an electrical circuit, which indicates the state of the actuator and this is then used for indication of the lock status.
[E05B 81/76] Detection of handle operation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Handles with some kind of electrical function. It first of all concerns handles working more or less as an electrical switch. The handle does thus not perform any mechanical action on the lock. It is simply a switch for sending a signal (e.g. by closing a circuit) to the lock-electronics.  This subgroup also covers handles, which perform both a mechanical function as well as an electric function.  An example of a switch- type handle is given in the figure. This shows an opening switch for vehicle doors or trunks, which is actuated by pressing the cover element (3) in the direction of the arrow.
[E05B 81/77] comprising sensors detecting the presence of the hand of a user -- This CPC subclass E05B 81/77 falls under fixed constructions for power-actuated vehicle locks and defines lock mechanisms that incorporate sensors to determine when a user's hand is present. The sensors trigger the lock's operation, enabling hands-free or assisted activation in vehicles.
[E05B 81/78] as part of a hands-free locking or unlocking operation -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Handles used for the initiation of a hands-free opening. A sensor or switch in or near the handle starts the data transfer. This could be done either by moving the handle in a predetermined way or by a proximity sensor, which registers the approach of a hand towards the handle.  It should be noted that this class does not concern the details of the coded data transmission between the lock and a transponder key.  According to the example in the figure, two switches (28,29) can be sensed/actuated by different actions of a user's hand for example in order initiate a hands-free opening process.
[E05B 81/80] characterised by the power supply -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Various kinds of energy sources (other than the vehicle main battery) and spare-power devices.  If the main power source is defect (for example because the main battery of the vehicle is empty), then these power sources can provide a small amount of power for an emergency opening of the vehicle. This section also covers cases where the primary energy source is not the vehicle main battery. This would for example sometimes be the case for certain types of battery charged locks.
[E05B 81/82] using batteries other than the vehicle main battery -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Additional batteries or back up batteries used for example in case of failure of energy supply from vehicle main battery.
[E05B 81/84] using manually operated generator means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Generators operated by the vehicle user. Such generators can be adapted for supply of energy for example by handle movement, opening/closing of the door or introduction of the key into the keyhole. The figure shows a generator (22), which supplies energy for the operation of the actuator (17) when the voltage of the main battery is below a certain level.
[E05B 81/86] using capacitors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The use of capacitors for provision of energy for example when the main battery has been discharged. The figure shows a number of supercapacitors (30) which are available as a back-up energy source. A diode (32) or similar component prevents discharge of the supercapacitors (30) into the main power supply means.
[E05B 81/88] using  inductive energy transmission -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The use of induction for provision of energy. This can be used in situations when it is desired to avoid cables and contacts. One example of this type of arrangement is the provision of a coil in the key another coil in the lock for non-contact supply of emergency power to the lock.
[E05B 81/90] Manual override in case of power failure -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Purely mechanical manual override means. One example would be a door handle, which in normal use electrically activates an actuator. In case of a power failure this handle can be actuated further or in another direction in order to actuate the lock mechanically.  This subgroup also covers cylinder locks in normally not visible, concealed positions. In the figure, a cylinder lock (9) is for example mounted behind the rear-view mirror (1).  It is to be noted, that this subgroup does not cover normally visible, non concealed cylinder locks mounted in or near the door handle.
[E05B 83/00] Vehicle locks specially adapted for particular types of wing or vehicle -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Locks specially adapted for bicycles -> E05B71/00 - Locking arrangements for non-fixed roofs -> B60J7/185 - Latching means for sideboards or tailgates of open load compartments -> B62D33/037
[E05B 83/01] Locks for military or armoured vehicles -- A lock specifically designed for military or armoured vehicles, providing enhanced security and resistance to forced entry under combat conditions. It falls within the vehicle-lock subclass for specially adapted wing or vehicle applications.
[E05B 83/02] Locks for railway freight-cars, freight containers or the like -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks for commercially used vehicles.  One specific subject, covered by this and the lower subgroups, is locks for (standardized) freight containers for use on trucks and trains, but also on ships or in aircrafts.  This subgroup consequently covers locks both for load compartments that form an integral part of the vehicle as well as freight containers forming a removable part which can be separated from the vehicle.  The figure shows a lockable structure applied to a container (1). In the locked position three bars (7, 8, 9) form a cross-bar construction preventing the doors (4, 5) of the container (1) to be opened.
[E05B 83/04] for sliding wings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks for sliding wings on commercially used vehicles. The figure shows an example of a hook bolt (34) for locking a sliding roll-up door (16).
[E05B 83/06] of railway freight-cars -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locking arrangements for railway freight cars, as exemplified by the figure where the locking device (20) keeps the sliding door (12) closed.
[E05B 83/08] with elongated bars for actuating the fastening means -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locking devices with actuation bars which usually connect actuation means, for example a handle, with a fastening means. The figure illustrates this by showing a central actuation unit (10) connected to fastening means (22) by sliding bars (16).
[E05B 83/10] Rotary bars -- **Definition:** This place covers:  A very common locking arrangement for cargo vehicles with rotary bars connecting for example a handle and lock bolt(s).  In the figure the rotary bars (50a, 50b) are actuated by handles (64a, 64b) in order to move fastening means (66,68) between a locked and unlocked position.
[E05B 83/12] for back doors of vans -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Smaller locks for vans (e.g. delivery vans, plumber's van, locksmith's van). These vans usually have doors with locks, which are similar to locks for passenger doors, but quite often adapted to the special requirements needed for the various commercial purposes of the van.  The example of the figure discloses a lock for use on a door between the driver compartment and the cargo compartment.
[E05B 83/14] with provisions for sealing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Lock devices with means indicating authorized or unauthorized actuation of the lock device.  In the figure, the handle (143) of a hook bolt locking device is sealed by a wire (189) which has to be broken when the handle is to be actuated.
[E05B 83/16] Locks for luggage compartments, car boot lids or car bonnets -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Lock devices for non-passenger compartments essentially located at the outside of the vehicle.  In most of the cases the rear compartments are used for load purposes, e.g. for luggage. This also covers locks for luggage compartments of hatchback/station (family) cars.  At the front of the car it usually concerns a hood for the engine compartment.
[E05B 83/18] for car boot lids or rear luggage compartments -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locking arrangements for boot lids and luggage compartments, normally at the back of a car. A typical example of this subgroup is shown in the figure, whereby a lock (10) cooperates with a striker (20) for holding the boot lid (2) closed.
[E05B 83/20] with two or more wings, which together close a single compartment -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locking arrangements for luggage compartments with more than one wing. One example of such a lock system is shown in the figure.
[E05B 83/22] for luggage compartments at the side of the vehicle, e.g. of buses or camper vans -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locking arrangements for luggage compartments at the side of the vehicle, as exemplified by the figure, which shows a recreational vehicle with a central actuator (160) for locking and unlocking of several luggage compartment doors at the vehicle side.
[E05B 83/24] for car bonnets -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks for compartments at the front of a vehicle. These locks are normally provided with a primary and secondary bolt in order to obtain a double security. This assures that the wing stays closed even if the primary bolt fails.  In the figure a striker (4) is held in the latched position by a primary bolt (8). A secondary bolt (5) catches the striker (4), if the primary bolt (8) would fail.
[E05B 83/243] Hood clamps, i.e. individually actuated, usually yielding hooks -- Hood clamps are mechanisms mounted on a vehicle's hood to fasten it in place, typically employing individually actuated hooks that can yield or release under load to allow controlled opening. They fall within the fixed-construction lock category, specifically for vehicle locks adapted to particular wing or vehicle types.
[E05B 83/247] Simultaneously actuated fasteners at separated positions -- E05B 83/247 covers vehicle lock systems where two or more fasteners that are physically separated are released or engaged by a single actuation mechanism. The design enables simultaneous operation of lock points such as wing doors, trunk lids or roof panels, improving security and convenience, and is used for specialized automotive or aerospace applications that require coordinated locking of multiple sections.
[E05B 83/26] Emergency opening means for persons trapped in the luggage compartment -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements for allowing escape from the inside of a boot. There are normally no means for actuating the boot lock from the inside.  This subgroup concerns means allowing operation from the inside in case of emergency. In the figure a handle (12) is provided for emergency opening.
[E05B 83/28] Locks for glove compartments, console boxes, fuel inlet covers or the like -- E05B 83/28 covers vehicle locks that are integrated into interior fittings such as glove compartments, console boxes, fuel-inlet covers and similar enclosures, providing secure closure for these specific compartments. It applies to fixed constructions specially adapted to particular vehicle types.
[E05B 83/30] for glove compartments -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks for storage compartments located in the dashboard area in front of the passenger seat. An example of such a device can be seen in the figure.
[E05B 83/32] for console boxes, e.g. between passenger seats -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locking arrangements for storage compartments normally placed between passenger seats as exemplified by the figure.
[E05B 83/34] for fuel inlet covers essentially flush with the vehicle surface -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks for fuel inlet covers, which essentially lie in the plane of the vehicle surface, when closed. In the figure a fuel inlet (9) is covered by a lid (4). An actuator (20) drives a bolt (16) between a locked and unlocked position. The caps which actually close the inlet pipe in a fluid-tight way (with or without lock) and the flush cover flaps, per se, are rather covered by B60K15/04 and B60K15/05
[E05B 83/36] Locks for passenger or like doors -- E05B 83/36 covers locking mechanisms designed specifically for passenger doors or similar openings on vehicles such as cars, buses and vans. The classification includes devices that secure the door against unauthorized entry, including latch bolts, deadbolts, electronic actuators and combinations thereof, adapted to the geometry of the door frame.
[E05B 83/363] for railway vehicles  E05B77/54  takes precedence
[E05B 83/367] with centering alignment pin -- The code E05B 83/367 designates vehicle locks that are specially adapted for particular wing or vehicle types and incorporate a centering alignment pin to guide and secure the locking mechanism in the correct position. This falls within the broader group of fixed constructions for locks.
[E05B 83/38] for pillar-less vehicles, i.e. vehicles where a front and a back door engage each other in the closed position -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks for door arrangements without a central post between the front and the back door. According to the exemplifying figure a pivoting door (PB) and a sliding door (PC) are kept in a closed position by a system comprising four different locks (DS1, DS2, TS, PS).
[E05B 83/40] for sliding doors -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locking devices for sliding passenger doors. The example in the figure shows a lock system for a sliding door (D). It comprises a rear latch mechanism (11), a front latch mechanism (13) and a lower latch mechanism (17).
[E05B 83/42] for large commercial vehicles, e.g. trucks, construction vehicles or vehicles for mass transport -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks for passenger compartments of large commercially used vehicles. The figure shows a cabin (1) of a working vehicle. On the inside of the cabin door a handle (24) can be operated for releasing a door lock mechanism (22).
[E05B 83/44] for recreational vehicles, e.g. caravans or camper vans -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks for campers, caravans and similar. One commonly addressed problem is to secure the doors from the inside during night.  The figure is directed to a lock for a caravan, which can be locked and unlocked with a remote control (82).
[E05B 85/00] Details of vehicle locks not provided for in groups -- E05B 85/00 covers miscellaneous details of vehicle locks that are not covered in other more specific groups, such as unusual lock parts, auxiliary mechanisms, or special security features. It is placed under the hierarchy Fixed constructions → Locks and serves to classify lock elements that lack a dedicated subclass elsewhere.
[E05B 85/01] Mechanical arrangements specially adapted for hands-free locking or unlocking  handles with means for initiation of a hands-free locking or unlocking process  E05B81/78 -- Mechanical systems designed to enable vehicle door handles to lock or unlock without the user touching a manual control, using sensors, actuators or other means that start the locking or unlocking action automatically. The arrangement covers any configuration that initiates a hands-free locking or unlocking process.
[E05B 85/02] Lock casings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Housing structures for vehicle locks as exemplified by the figure, which shows a housing (6) composed of a plastic shell (7) and a metallic plate (9).
[E05B 85/04] Strikers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Elements cooperating with the bolt for holding a wing closed. These striker elements are also called keepers. They are normally constructed as a pin or a staple for cooperation with a bifurcated latch bolt. A typical example is shown in the figure.
[E05B 85/045] for bifurcated bolts -- The subclass E05B 85/045 covers vehicle locking devices that employ bifurcated bolts, i.e., bolts split into two arms that engage opposite sides of a door, panel or hatch. It is used for lock designs that are not covered by the more general vehicle-lock groups and falls under the hierarchy FIXED CONSTRUCTIONS → LOCKS → DETAILS OF VEHICLE LOCKS NOT PROVIDED FOR IN GROUPS.
[E05B 85/06] Lock cylinder arrangements -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements of lock cylinders in vehicle lock devices. A usual subject of this subgroup is the connection between a lock cylinder and the lock mechanism in a lock housing. Such a construction is disclosed in the figure, wherein a lock cylinder (30) is connected to a lock mechanism (20) by means of a particular rod (4).
[E05B 85/08] Sill-buttons, garnish buttons or inner door lock knobs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Buttons or knobs on the inside of the door for locking/unlocking of the door. It usually concerns either a sill-button placed on the door trim near the window or a knob/button placed near or in the door handle unit. The figure gives an example of a sill/garnish button (11) arranged for actuation of a locking lever (12).
[E05B 85/085] Manipulators therefor  E05B19/20  takes precedence -- The CPC class E05B 85/085 covers manipulators used with vehicle locking systems whose specific details are not covered elsewhere in the lock categories. It includes mechanical or electronic devices that operate, adjust or engage locks on cars, trucks, buses and similar vehicles and therefore fall outside the more specific groups. The entry is subordinate to E05B 19/20, which has precedence.
[E05B 85/10] Handles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Various types of vehicle door handles for actuation of locks. The lower subgroups concern the most common groups of such handles.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Mounting of handles -> E05B79/06 - Operative connections of handles -> E05B79/22
[E05B 85/103] creating a completely closed wing surface -- E05B 85/103 covers the design or manufacturing of a vehicle lock element in which the wing component is formed as a completely closed surface, eliminating any openings or gaps that could affect security or aerodynamic performance. It includes methods that produce a solid, uninterrupted wing shape for locks whose detailed features are not covered elsewhere in the classification.
[E05B 85/107] Pop-out handles, e.g. sliding outwardly before rotation -- E05B 85/107 defines vehicle lock mechanisms that feature pop-out handles which slide outward before being rotated to operate the lock. This subcategory falls under fixed constructions and locks, covering handle designs not detailed in other groups.
[E05B 85/12] Inner door handles -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Handles on the inside of the door as exemplified by the figure, in which a door handle (32) is provided for actuation of the door lock.
[E05B 85/13] with a locking knob forming part of the inside door handle unit -- E05B 85/13 covers vehicle door lock arrangements in which the locking knob is incorporated into the interior door-handle unit, forming part of a single handle assembly rather than a separate lock component. It falls under Fixed Constructions → Locks → Locks for details of vehicle locks not covered in other groups.
[E05B 85/14] Handles pivoted about an axis parallel to the wing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Pivoting handle constructions with an axis essentially parallel to the plane of the wing. This is a very common type of handle movement, and the lower subgroups concern the most usual basic concepts. The example figure of this subgroup therefore discloses another type of such a pivoting handle.
[E05B 85/16] a longitudinal grip part being pivoted at one end about an axis perpendicular to the longitudinal axis of the grip part -- **Definition:** This place covers:  One of the two most commonly used vehicle handle constructions. The figure illustrates how a longitudinal handle (1) pivots at one end (4) around an axis perpendicular to the longitudinal direction of the handle.
[E05B 85/18] a longitudinal grip part being pivoted about an axis parallel to the longitudinal axis of the grip part -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The second of the two most commonly used vehicle handle constructions. The figure illustrates how a longitudinal handle (16) pivots around an axis (27) parallel to the longitudinal direction of the handle.
[E05B 85/20] Bolts or detents -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Details of locking elements in the form of e.g. latch-bolts or dead-bolts and detents for such bolts. A vehicle front hood lock comprising a bolt (2) with a combined rotational and sliding movement, as illustrated by the figure, would belong in this subgroup.
[E05B 85/22] Rectilinearly moving bolts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Vehicle locks with sliding bolts as illustrated by the figure, in which an actuator (18) causes a sliding movement of a bolt (22).
[E05B 85/24] Bolts rotating about an axis -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Vehicle locks with pivoting bolts. The vast majority of pivoting bolts in vehicle locks is of the bifurcated type. The example of the figure therefore shows a more unusual form of pivoting bolt. The striker (5) is guided onto a centering pin of the lock housing, when the door is closed. A hook element (3) of the bolt (2) will thereby come in contact with an edge (4) of the striker (5). The pivoting bolt (2) is held in the latched position by two detents (6, 10).
[E05B 85/243] with a bifurcated bolt -- E05B 85/243 designates lock arrangements for vehicles that employ a bifurcated bolt, where the bolt is split into two parallel arms that engage complementary recesses to secure a door or hatch. This subcategory falls under Fixed Constructions - Locks and covers vehicle lock details not provided for in other groups.
[E05B 85/245] with a pair of bifurcated bolts -- The CPC subclass E05B 85/245 covers vehicle lock mechanisms that employ a pair of bifurcated bolts as the securing elements. It falls under the broader group of vehicle locks that are not detailed in other specific lock categories. This design typically uses two split bolts that engage simultaneously to lock doors or compartments.
[E05B 85/247] about a vertical axis -- E05B 85/247 addresses vehicle locking devices whose design is not otherwise classified, specifically mechanisms that engage or release by rotating around a vertical axis such as steering-column or door lock assemblies that turn vertically.
[E05B 85/26] Cooperation between bolts and detents -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cases where the detent, the bolt or the way in which they cooperate is described. In the figure a bolt (2) and a detent (3) are provided with contact surfaces with specifically chosen curvatures.
[E05B 85/28] in which the member engaging the keeper is shaped as a toothed wheel or the like -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Bolt members shaped as toothed wheels. This used to be a very common bolt/striker construction but is rather unusual nowadays.  The lock in the figure has a wheel shaped bolt (21) with toothed elements (14) which cooperate with a protrusion (35) on the striker for holding the wing closed.
[E05B 9/00] Lock casings or latch-mechanism casings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Lock or latch mechanism casings of latch bolt locks, of dead bolt locks and of cylinder locks;  Face plates;  Fastening of locks or parts thereof to the wing;  Coupling devices for the two halves of a double cylinder lock.  This group has the following structure:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Padlock casings -> E05B67/02 - Mounting of lock casings to the vehicles -> E05B79/04 - Mounting of lock casings to vehicles -> E05B85/02
[E05B 9/002] Faceplates or front plates  E05C9/004  takes precedence -- E05B 9/002 covers the protective or decorative casings that surround the external part of a lock or latch mechanism, commonly called faceplates or front plates, which may be attached to the lock body to shield internal components or provide a mounting surface. When a lock's front plate also functions as a hinge or pivot element, the more specific subclass E05C9/004 takes precedence. This subclass is used for devices where the faceplate is integral to the lock's operating movement.
[E05B 9/02] of latch-bolt locks -- E05B 9/02 concerns the housings that contain the latch-bolt mechanism of a lock, including the outer shell or casing that protects and supports the latch-bolt assembly. It covers design features of the enclosure rather than the bolt itself.
[E05B 9/04] of cylinder locks -- E05B 9/04 classifies the housings or casings that encase cylinder-type locks, covering the structural shells that hold the rotating core and associated latch mechanism within fixed-construction lock systems.
[E05B 9/041] Double cylinder locks  E05B9/045 ,  E05B9/048  take precedence -- The E05B 9/041 class covers double-cylinder lock casings, where a key is required to operate the lock from both the interior and exterior of a door. These fixed-construction devices are used where extra security is needed, for example in entry doors that must be locked from either side. Sub-groups E05B 9/045 and E05B 9/048 take precedence over this entry.
[E05B 9/042] Stators consisting of multiple parts being assembled together  E05B9/045  takes precedence
[E05B 9/045] Modular casings for adjusting the length of cylinder locks -- Modular casings are interchangeable housings designed to change the projected length of a cylinder lock without replacing the lock itself. By selecting different casing modules, the lock can be adapted to various door thicknesses or security requirements, simplifying installation and maintenance. This solution falls within the fixed-construction lock category under the CPC code E05B 9/045.
[E05B 9/048] Stator reinforcements  for drill protection  E05B15/1614 -- E05B 9/048 covers reinforced stator elements built into lock or latch casings to protect the internal mechanism from drilling attacks. It includes material or structural modifications that strengthen the stator against forced entry attempts.
[E05B 9/08] Fastening the casings of latch-bolt locks or cylinder locks to the wing -- This subclass covers methods and devices for securing the outer casing of latch-bolt or cylinder lock mechanisms to the lock's wing or escutcheon. It includes fastening techniques such as screws, rivets, adhesives, or interlocking geometries that bind the casing to the rotating or fixed wing part.
[E05B 9/082] with concealed screws -- E05B 9/082 covers lock or latch casings in which the fastening screws are hidden from view, typically by being enclosed within the body, covered by decorative elements or integrated into the surrounding structure, so the screws are not externally observable.
[E05B 9/084] Fastening of lock cylinders, plugs or cores -- E05B 9/084 covers the means for securing the moving core of a lock - such as cylinders, plugs or cores - to the surrounding lock or latch casing. It includes fastening elements like screws, bolts, pins, adhesives or other retaining devices that hold the core in place during operation.
[E05B 9/086] Fastening of rotors, plugs or cores to an outer stator -- E05B 9/086 covers methods for securing rotors, plugs or magnetic cores to the surrounding stationary housing of an electric machine. It addresses fastening techniques that hold these inner components in place within the outer stator to provide mechanical stability and proper alignment.
[E05B 9/088] Means for preventing loss of tumblers during mounting or removal -- A device built into a lock casing that secures the tumblers while the lock is being installed or taken out, preventing them from falling out. Typical solutions include retaining clips, plates or magnetic holders that hold the tumblers in place during mounting or removal.
[E05B 9/10] Coupling devices for the two halves of double cylinder locks -- This subclass covers mechanisms that join the two separate cylinders of a double-cylinder lock so they operate together within the lock or latch casing. Typical examples are linking rods, gears, interlocking levers or other devices that transmit motion from one cylinder to the other.
[E05B 9/105] including disengagement means, e.g. opening from one side being still possible even if the key is inserted from the other side -- E05B 9/105 relates to lock casings or latch-mechanism housings that include disengagement means, enabling the lock to be opened from one side even when a key is inserted and engaged on the opposite side, providing an emergency release or convenient access without removing the key.
[E05C] BOLTS OR FASTENING DEVICES FOR WINGS, SPECIALLY FOR DOORS OR WINDOWS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Bolts or fastening devices for swingable, slidable, or otherwise movable wings, e.g. doors or windows. This subclass has the following structure:  E05C1/00 - E05C5/00  Fastening devices characterised by the way the bolt is moved.  E05C7/00  Fastening devices specially adapted for two wings.  E05C9/00  Arrangements of simultaneously actuated bolts or other securing devices at well separated positions on the same wing.  E05C17/00  Fastening devices for holding wings open.  E05C19/00 , E05C21/00  Other devices specially designed for securing wings.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Buckles; Similar fasteners for interconnecting straps or the like, e.g. for safety belts -> A44B11/00 - Hinges or pivots -> E05D1/00 - E05D7/00 - Accessories for sliding or lifting wings -> E05D13/00 - Suspension arrangements for wings -> E05D15/00 - Closers or openers for wings, braking devices -> E05F1/00 - E05F7/00 - Operating mechanisms for wings -> E05F9/00 - E05F17/00 - Sealing arrangements for wings -> E06B7/16 - Devices for fastening or securing constructional elements or machine parts together -> F16B  **Glossary:** - Wing: General term for swingable, slidable, or otherwise movable doors or windows. This term also includes other movable structures such as drawers, lids of chests, car boots, or car bonnets, to which the operating, mounting, latching, or locking means covered by this class may be applied. - Frame: Any member to which a wing may be held by a fastening device. It does not include a framework forming part of the wing, but it may be another wing. - Lock: Primarily a device for releasing or securing any member, which requires a key or a permutation mechanism for release. - Bolt: A sliding, pivoted, or otherwise movable member such as is normally carried by a door to hold it shut by engagement with a keeper on the frame. It may be operated by hand directly or through mechanism or by a key; it may be a latch (see below); - Latch: A bolt arranged to be moved to the releasing position against the force of a spring, or some other returning force, when a wing meets the frame on closing, so that it does not have to be operated by hand to secure the wing, but only to open it. - Hasp: A member hinged to the frame or wing so that it can be moved towards the face of the wing or frame and secured thereto, e.g. by a turn-button, by a padlock and staple.
[E05C 1/00] Bolts, latches, or equivalent wing-fastening devices, characterised by special way of movement, e.g. moving rectilinearly, pivotally or rotatively -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fastening devices comprising a rectilinearly movable bolt. The bolt can have either latching or non latching action. The bolt can be moved with an operating handle or equivalent member moving rigidly or non rigidly with the bolt. This group also cover cylindrical or tubular latches.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Arrangements of simultaneously actuated bolts at well-separated positions on the same frame -> E05C9/00 - Devices for holding a wing open comprising a sliding securing member -> E05C17/48 - Automatic catches with sliding bolts -> E05C19/028 - Locks in which a sliding latch is used also as a locking bolt -> E05B55/00 - Lock with a sliding bolt without provision of latching -> E05B63/0017 - Vehicle locks with a rectilinearly movable bolt -> E05B85/22
[E05C 1/002] perpendicular to the surface on which the fastener is mounted -- E05C 1/002 covers bolts, latches or similar wing-fastening devices that are installed so that their motion is perpendicular to the surface they are attached to. It applies to fixed constructions such as doors or windows where the fastener moves straight out from the panel, pivoting or rotating in a vertical direction.
[E05C 1/004] parallel to the surface on which the fastener is mounted -- This CPC subclass defines bolts, latches or similar wing-fastening devices whose actuation moves parallel to the surface they are mounted on rather than pivoting or rotating. It applies to doors or windows where the fastening element slides or translates along the plane of the panel.
[E05C 1/006] parallel to the wing edge -- The E05C 1/006 class covers fastening devices such as bolts, latches or similar mechanisms used on aircraft wings, especially for doors or windows, whose movement is guided parallel to the wing edge. These devices feature a distinctive linear, pivoting or rotating motion that differs from conventional wing-fasteners.
[E05C 1/02] without latching action -- The subclass E05C 1/02 covers bolts, latches or similar wing-fastening devices for doors or windows that secure by a simple movement such as sliding, pivoting or rotating and do not provide a latching or retaining action. It falls under fixed constructions, locks and bolts or fastening devices for wings.
[E05C 1/04] with operating handle or equivalent member rigid with the bolt -- Bolts, latches, or similar wing-fastening devices for doors or windows that incorporate an operating handle or equivalent member rigidly attached to the bolt, so the handle moves together with the bolt during rectilinear, pivoting or rotating actuation. This subclass groups mechanisms where the handle is not separate but forms a fixed extension of the fastening element.
[E05C 1/06] with operating handle or equivalent member moving otherwise than rigidly with the bolt -- This subclass covers bolts, latches or equivalent wing-fastening devices for aircraft doors or windows where the operating handle or actuation member moves in a non-rigid way relative to the bolt, such as sliding, pivoting or rotating. It applies to mechanisms that secure doors or windows on wings and where the handle follows a different motion path than the bolt itself.
[E05C 1/065] flush -- The E05C 1/065 "flush" subclass covers bolts, latches or similar wing-fastening devices that are installed so that the operating parts lie flush with the surrounding surface, typically moving linearly, pivotally or rotatively to secure aircraft doors or windows.
[E05C 1/08] with latching action -- The subclass E05C 1/08 includes bolts, latches or equivalent wing-fastening devices for aircraft doors or windows that operate with a latching action, such as moving rectilinearly, pivotally or rotatively, to secure the wing sections.
[E05C 1/085] With means for assisting depression of the latch bolt during latching -- This CPC subclass (E05C 1/085) pertains to wing bolts, latches or equivalent fastening devices for doors or windows that incorporate a mechanism - such as a spring, cam or lever - to assist the depression of the latch bolt during the latching action.
[E05C 1/10] with operating handle or equivalent member rigid with the latch -- This subclass covers bolts, latches or similar wing-fastening devices for doors or windows that are operated by a handle or a rigid member fixed to the latch, allowing the fastening element to be moved by pushing or pulling the handle.
[E05C 1/12] with operating handle or equivalent member moving otherwise than rigidly with the latch -- This subclass covers bolts, latches or similar wing-fastening devices that are operated by a handle or equivalent member that does not move rigidly with the latch, such as mechanisms that translate, pivot, or rotate relative to the latch. The classification is used for door or window fastening devices on aircraft wings where the actuation involves a non-rigid movement of the control element.
[E05C 1/14] the handle or member moving essentially towards or away from the plane of the wing or frame -- A fastening device for aircraft doors or windows whose handle or operating member moves essentially straight outward or inward, perpendicular to the wing or frame surface. This includes bolts, latches or similar mechanisms that translate linearly to open or close the opening. The classification covers designs where the motion is chiefly toward or away from the wing plane.
[E05C 1/145] flush -- The E05C 1/145 "flush" subclass covers bolts, latches or similar wing-fastening devices that are installed flush with the surface of a door or window and operate by a special movement such as straight, pivoting or rotating motion. This includes fasteners specifically designed for wings of doors or windows where the hardware remains level with the surrounding structure.
[E05C 1/16] the handle or member moving essentially in a plane substantially parallel to the wing or frame -- E05C 1/16 defines bolts, latches or similar wing-fastening devices whose handle or actuating member moves essentially in a plane substantially parallel to the wing or frame, such as sliding or rotating mechanisms.
[E05C 1/163] Cylindrical or tubular latches -- The CPC subclass E05C 1/163 covers cylindrical or tubular latches used to fasten aircraft or vehicle wings, especially doors or windows. These devices are bolts, latches or equivalents characterized by a straight-line, pivoting or rotating movement to secure the wing opening.
[E05C 1/166] with sliding handle -- E05C 1/166 covers wing-fastening bolts, latches or similar devices that are operated by a handle moving linearly along a track. The sliding handle provides straight-line motion for opening or closing doors or windows on aircraft or other vehicles.
[E05C 17/00] Devices for holding wings open -- The E05C 17/00 class comprises devices that secure hinged panels such as wings, doors or windows in an open position, typically using bolts, brackets or similar hardware. These mechanisms are intended to hold the wing-like elements apart for ventilation, loading or access and are a subset of fixed-construction locking systems.
[E05C 17/003] Power-actuated devices for limiting the opening of vehicle doors -- E05C 17/003 covers power-actuated mechanisms that restrict how far vehicle doors can open, such as electronically or hydraulically controlled stoppers that prevent doors from swinging beyond a set angle. These devices belong to the broader class of fixed constructions, locks, and fastening bolts used on wings, doors, and windows.
[E05C 17/006] with means for detecting obstacles outside the doors -- E05C 17/006 covers devices that keep aircraft wing sections, such as doors or windows, open and incorporate a system to sense external obstacles. The obstacle-detection means can trigger automatic retraction or alert the crew to prevent damage or injury. This subclass falls under bolts or fastening devices for wings within the broader fixed constructions category.
[E05C 17/02] by mechanical means -- E05C 17/02 classifies mechanical devices that hold aircraft wings, doors or windows open, such as bolts or other fasteners designed to lock these components in position.
[E05C 17/025] Means acting between hinged edge and frame  E05C17/203  takes precedence -- The subgroup covers devices that connect a hinged edge of a door, window or similar wing to its surrounding frame in order to keep the wing open, such as catches, latches, stays or similar fastening means. It is situated within the family of bolts or fastening devices for wings, especially for doors or windows, under the broader category of fixed constructions.
[E05C 17/04] with a movable bar or equivalent member extending between frame and wing -- E05C 17/04 covers devices that keep a door, window or similar wing open by using a movable bar or equivalent member that spans between the fixed frame and the wing itself. The bar can be adjusted or released to allow the wing to be held at a chosen angle. This subclass falls under fixed constructions, locks, and bolts or fastening devices for wings.
[E05C 17/042] for anchoring the trunk lid of a car while carrying oversize objects  E05C17/36  takes precedence -- E05C 17/042 covers devices that secure a car's trunk lid in an anchored or open position while transporting oversized loads, using bolts, locks or other fastening means designed for wing or door sections. It falls under Fixed Constructions → Locks → Bolts or fastening devices for wings, especially doors or windows, and is subordinated to the broader class E05C 17/36.
[E05C 17/045] Hinges for the movable bar  E05C17/163 ,  E05C17/26 ,  E05C17/345  take precedence; hinges in general  F16C11/04 ,  E05D
[E05C 17/047] Portable bars or the like, i.e. completely removable  E05C17/042  takes precedence -- E05C 17/047 classifies completely removable portable bars or similar devices used to hold aircraft wings, doors or windows in an open position. It falls under fixed constructions and locking or fastening mechanisms for wings, and is subordinate to the broader subclass E05C 17/042, which has precedence.
[E05C 17/08] with special means for release, e.g. automatic release by further opening -- The subclass E05C 17/08 covers devices that hold aircraft wing sections, such as doors or windows, in an open position and provide a special means for release, for example an automatic release triggered by further opening of the wing. It falls under fixed constructions, locks, bolts or fastening devices for wings, specially for doors or windows.
[E05C 17/085] automatic release by further opening -- The subclass E05C 17/085 covers devices that automatically release a wing, door or window when it is opened further, allowing the fastening element to disengage without manual action. It falls under bolts or fastening devices for wings within the fixed-construction category.
[E05C 17/12] consisting of a single rod -- E05C 17/12 pertains to wing-holding devices that consist of a single rod, used to keep doors, windows or other movable wing sections in the open position, typically as a simple bolt or latch mechanism.
[E05C 17/14] Hook and eye, or equivalent -- A hook-and-eye or equivalent fastener is a simple mechanical device comprising a hooked prong that engages a loop or eye to keep a wing, door or window held open. It may incorporate springs or latches to provide secure retention and easy release, functioning as a low-tech locking or holding mechanism within the wing-opening category.
[E05C 17/16] pivoted only at one end and having an elongated slot -- A wing-holding device that is hinged at a single end and incorporates an elongated slot, allowing the wing to be positioned at various angles while remaining secured, typically used for doors or windows.
[E05C 17/163] with clamping or securing means at the pivot, e.g. friction hinge -- This subclass covers mechanisms that keep a wing, such as a door or window, in an open position by using clamping or securing elements located at the pivot point, for example a friction hinge. It falls under fixed constructions, locks, and bolts or fastening devices for wings, especially for doors or windows, and is coded E05C 17/163.
[E05C 17/166] Security devices -- E05C 17/166 covers security devices that lock or hold a wing, door or window open, such as bolts, latches or other fastening mechanisms designed to prevent unintended closing. These devices are used in fixed constructions where reliable retention of a movable panel is required. The classification groups them under locks for wings, especially for doors or windows.
[E05C 17/18] pivoted only at one end having a row of holes, notches, or pins -- A device for keeping a wing, door or window open that pivots on a single end and features a series of holes, notches or pins along its length to provide adjustable attachment points. This design allows the wing to be secured in various positions while remaining hinged at one side.
[E05C 17/20] sliding through a guide -- A sliding guide device for holding a wing, door or window open uses a bolt or fastening element that moves along a track to keep the opening secured in the open position.
[E05C 17/203] concealed, e.g. for vehicles -- The E05C 17/203 subclass refers to concealed devices that securely hold vehicle wings-such as doors, hatches or windows-open. These mechanisms are integrated within the bodywork so they are invisible when the wing is closed, often using spring-loaded or latch-type arrangements.
[E05C 17/206] with elastomeric springs to hold wing open -- Devices that use elastomeric springs to keep a wing, such as a door or window, opened, falling under fixed-construction fasteners for wings. The classification covers spring-loaded bolts or similar mechanisms designed specifically to hold the wing in the open position.
[E05C 17/22] with braking, clamping or securing means in the guide -- E05C 17/22 covers fastening devices for wings, especially doors or windows, that incorporate braking, clamping or other securing elements in the guide to hold the wing open. The mechanisms prevent unintended movement and keep the wing stably positioned during operation.
[E05C 17/24] pivoted at one end, and with the other end running along a guide member -- The CPC subclass E05C 17/24 covers wing-holding devices that are pivoted at one end while the opposite end slides along a guide member, enabling a wing, door or window to stay open in a controlled position. This design is used primarily in aircraft or vehicle applications where secure, adjustable opening of wing-type components is required.
[E05C 17/26] with braking, clamping or securing means at the pivot of the rod -- E05C 17/26 covers devices that keep wings such as doors or windows open by using a braking, clamping or securing mechanism located at the pivot point of the operating rod. The classification falls under fixed constructions, locks and bolts or fastening devices for wings, specifically for doors or windows.
[E05C 17/28] with braking, clamping or securing means at the connection to the guide member -- E05C 17/28 covers devices that secure wing sections-such as aircraft doors or windows-by incorporating braking, clamping or other retaining mechanisms at the point where the wing connects to its guide member.
