These fastening solutions are designed to keep the wing open or locked in position, often using bolts or similar hardware with added locking or braking features.
[E05C 17/30] of extensible, e.g. telescopic, construction -- E05C 17/30 refers to fastening devices for wings, doors or windows that have an extensible, for example telescopic, construction. It includes bolts, locks or other mechanisms designed to hold a wing or similar panel open by extending their length to secure the position. The subclass groups all such adjustable or retractable devices used in fixed construction applications.
[E05C 17/305] with hydraulic locks -- E05C 17/305 classifies hydraulic locks used to secure wings, doors or windows in fixed constructions. The code covers bolts, fastening devices or other mechanisms that employ hydraulic pressure to hold a wing or opening in the locked position, facilitating controlled opening and closing.
[E05C 17/32] consisting of two or more pivoted rods -- E05C 17/32 covers devices that keep wings, doors or windows open and are built from two or more pivoted rods. They fall under fixed constructions, specifically locks and bolts for wing openings.
[E05C 17/34] with means for holding in more than one position -- This class includes mechanisms that secure wings, doors or windows in more than one preset position, allowing the panel to be held open at various angles. The devices typically use bolts, clamps or other fastening elements to lock the component in multiple positions, providing adjustable opening for ventilation or access. It falls under fixed constructions, locks, and fastening devices for wings, especially for doors or windows.
[E05C 17/345] using friction, e.g. friction hinge -- E05C 17/345 covers wing-holding devices that rely on friction to keep doors, windows or similar panels open, such as friction hinges, rather than springs or latches. It falls under fixed constructions, specifically the lock and bolt category for wing fastening.
[E05C 17/36] comprising a flexible member, e.g. chains -- E05C 17/36 relates to devices that keep aircraft or building wings open, using a flexible element such as a chain to lock the wing in position. These are a subset of bolts or fastening devices for wings, especially for doors or windows, within the Fixed Constructions - Locks hierarchy.
[E05C 17/365] Security chains -- Security chains are fixed fastening devices mounted on doors or windows to restrict their opening angle, allowing ventilation while preventing forced entry. They consist of a metal link attached to the door and a hinged bracket on the frame, limiting how far the wing can swing.
[E05C 17/38] with a curved rail rigid with the frame for engagement with means on the wing, or -- This subclass covers devices that keep aircraft wings, particularly door or window wings, open by using a curved rail that is rigidly attached to the aircraft frame and engages with a corresponding element on the wing. The rail's curvature and fixed position provide secure retention of the wing in the opened position.
[E05C 17/40] Bars or like parts connecting a right wing with a left wing which move against each other when being closed -- The classification covers bars or similar components that join a right wing to a left wing, such as door or window panels, and move against each other when the wings are closed. These devices provide a secure, aligned connection by sliding or pivoting the bar as the wing is shut.
[E05C 17/42] connecting exterior and interior wings -- E05C 17/42 covers devices that connect an exterior wing, such as a door or window, to an interior wing, providing a means to hold the wing open or secure it in position. These are fixed-construction fasteners classified under locks and bolts for wings.
[E05C 17/44] with a device carried on the wing for frictional or like engagement with a fixed flat surface, e.g. retractable feet -- A device mounted on a wing that engages a fixed flat surface by friction or a similar action, such as retractable feet, to hold the wing open or in a set position. This includes mechanisms that press against the surface to secure doors, windows or other wing-mounted components.
[E05C 17/443] of the pivoted lever or eccentric type, e.g. for sliding windows -- This subclass covers devices that use a pivoted lever or eccentric mechanism to hold a wing, such as a door or sliding window, in its open position. The lever typically engages a latch or bolt to prevent the wing from closing unintentionally.
[E05C 17/446] of the retractable sliding feet type  similar devices for sliding wings  E05C17/64 -- E05C 17/446 covers retractable sliding-foot devices used to hold aircraft doors or window wings open. The mechanism slides along a fixed track to secure the wing in the open position and can be pulled back into the structure when the opening is closed.
[E05C 17/46] in which the wing or a member fixed thereon is engaged by a movable fastening member in a fixed position -- The classification covers mechanisms that hold a wing-such as a door or window-open by engaging it with a movable fastening member that locks the wing in a fixed position. These devices belong to the fixed-construction group of locks and bolts specifically intended for wing elements.
[E05C 17/48] comprising a sliding securing member -- The E05C 17/48 subclass of the Cooperative Patent Classification covers devices that secure a wing, such as a door or window, using a sliding member. It falls under fixed constructions, locks, and bolts or fastening devices for wings, specifically for doors or windows, and includes mechanisms that hold the wing open.
[E05C 17/50] comprising a single pivoted securing member -- A single-pivoted latch or bolt that secures a wing-typically a door or window-open, forming a simple fastening device that locks the wing in the open position.
[E05C 17/505] acting directly on the knob or handle -- The subclass E05C 17/505 covers devices that keep a wing-such as a door or window-open by acting directly on its knob or handle. It includes mechanisms that engage, push or rotate the handle itself to lock the wing in the open position, rather than using separate latches or brackets. This approach provides a simple, direct means of securing the opening without additional fastening hardware.
[E05C 17/52] comprising a snap, catch, or the like -- E05C 17/52 concerns devices that secure a wing, such as a door or window, in the open position by using a snap, catch, or similar mechanism. These fasteners lock the wing without a separate bolt, allowing easy release when the wing is to be closed.
[E05C 17/525] comprising a suction cup -- This classification covers devices for holding wings, such as doors or windows, open by using a suction cup, forming part of fixed-construction locking or fastening mechanisms.
[E05C 17/54] Portable devices, e.g. wedges -- E05C 17/54 covers portable devices such as wedges that are used to hold wing sections, doors or windows open. These simple removable fasteners can be placed temporarily to keep the opening in a desired position.
[E05C 17/56] by magnetic or electromagnetic attraction ; -- This subclass covers devices that hold aircraft wings, doors or windows open by using magnetic or electromagnetic attraction. The fastening elements are designed as bolts or other fixed constructions that secure the wing in an open position without a mechanical latch.
[E05C 17/58] operated or controlled from a distance -- E05C 17/58 covers devices that hold aircraft wing doors or windows open and can be operated or controlled from a distance, such as remote-actuated, pneumatic, hydraulic or electrically driven mechanisms that lock or release the wing opening without manual effort.
[E05C 17/60] holding sliding wings open -- E05C 17/60 covers devices that lock or secure sliding wing sections, such as doors or windows, in an open position. The classification falls under fixed constructions, locks, and bolts or fastening devices for wings, focusing on mechanisms that keep the wing extended rather than closed.
[E05C 17/62] using notches -- The subgroup defines fastening devices that hold a wing, door or window open by using notches cut into the wing or its frame, into which a bolt or similar element engages to prevent the wing from closing.
[E05C 17/64] by friction -- E05C 17/64 pertains to devices that keep aircraft wings, doors or windows open by applying friction, rather than a latch or hinge. It is classified under fixed constructions, locks, and bolts or fastening devices specifically designed for wing openings.
[E05C 19/00] Other devices specially designed for securing wings, -- This subclass includes any auxiliary mechanisms other than standard bolts or fasteners that are intended to lock or secure aircraft wings, wing doors or windows. It covers custom latches, safety pins, protective clips or similar devices designed specifically for keeping wing components closed during operation.
[E05C 19/001] with bolts extending over a considerable extent, e.g. nearly along the whole length of at least one side of the wing  movable sealing strips  E06B7/18 -- E05C 19/001 covers fixed-construction lock bolts for wings such as doors or windows where the bolt extends over most of the wing length, often incorporating moving sealing strips. This design secures wing edges and is cross-referenced to E06B7/18.
[E05C 19/002] Rotating about a longitudinal axis -- This CPC subclass (E05C 19/002) covers devices that rotate about a longitudinal axis to lock or secure aircraft wings, doors or windows. It includes mechanisms that pivot to engage locking elements, such as flaps or fasteners, specifically designed for wing sections and related openings.
[E05C 19/003] Locking bars, cross bars, security bars  for sliding wings  E05B65/0888 ; for a plurality of drawers  E05B65/467 -- The entry E05C 19/003 covers locking, cross-bars and security bars designed to secure sliding wing elements such as doors, windows or other movable panels, including devices that lock multiple wings or a series of drawers. These are fixed construction components within the broader locks category.
[E05C 19/004] at an angle between door and floor or wall
[E05C 19/005] pivoted about an axis on the wing, perpendicular to the plane of the wing -- This group covers locking or fastening devices fixed to a wing that operate by rotating about an axis that is perpendicular to the wing surface. The pivoted mechanism secures doors, windows or other wing openings, often using a bolt or latch that swings out of the plane to engage a keeper. It is intended for aircraft or similar structures where the locking axis is orthogonal to the wing.
[E05C 19/006] by displacement of the wing substantially in its own plane  E05F7/02  takes precedence
[E05C 19/007] Latches with wedging action  wedges between wing itself and fixed surface  E05C17/54
[E05C 19/008] Tilt-plate latches -- E05C 19/008 designates tilt-plate latches, a type of lock used on aircraft wings, doors or windows where a plate rotates or tilts into a locked position to secure the opening. These devices belong to the broader group of fixed constructions and fastening devices for wings.
[E05C 19/009] Latches with floating bolts, e.g. rings, balls -- The classification E05C 19/009 covers latches that incorporate a floating bolt, such as a ring or ball, which can move independently of the latch body to engage a locking point. These devices are used on fixed constructions like aircraft wings, doors or windows to provide a secure but releasable fastening.
[E05C 19/02] Automatic catches, i.e. released by pull or pressure on the wing -- E05C 19/02 is a CPC class covering automatic catches for wing structures such as aircraft doors or windows, designed to release when a pull or pressure is applied to the wing. It falls under Fixed Constructions → Locks → Bolts or fastening devices for wings, especially for doors or windows, and includes any specially designed devices that secure wings by this automatic mechanism.
[E05C 19/022] Released by pushing in the closing direction -- A device that secures a wing, door or window and can be released by pushing it in the direction of closing. It is a type of bolt or fastening mechanism designed for quick opening of aircraft or vehicle wings where the closing motion disengages the lock.
[E05C 19/024] with a bifurcated latch -- E05C 19/024 covers fastening devices for building wings such as doors or windows that incorporate a bifurcated latch, a two-pronged locking element that engages a corresponding receptacle to secure the opening.
[E05C 19/026] with a keeper caught between two pivoting bolts -- This subclass covers locking arrangements for doors or windows where a keeper element is held in place by two bolts that swing about pivots to engage the keeper, providing a secure fastening for wing panels. The mechanism is used in fixed constructions to retain wing sections such as aircraft or building openings.
[E05C 19/028] with sliding bolt(s) -- E05C 19/028 covers devices that secure doors, windows or other wings by using sliding bolts. The classification applies to fixed-construction locking mechanisms where a bolt moves linearly to engage a latch or strike plate, providing a simple, manual means of holding the wing closed. This entry is used for any specially designed sliding-bolt system for wing closures.
[E05C 19/04] Ball or roller catches -- The CPC subclass E05C 19/04 covers ball or roller catches, which are spring-loaded locking mechanisms that latch by allowing a spherical or cylindrical element to rotate into a recess. These devices are used particularly on movable wing sections such as doors or windows to secure them in the closed position.
[E05C 19/06] in which the securing part if formed or carried by a spring and moves only by distortion of the spring, e.g. snaps -- E05C 19/06 classifies fastening devices for aircraft wing doors or windows where the securing element is a spring that locks by being distorted, such as snap-type latches. The spring carries the locking part and moves only through its own deformation.
[E05C 19/063] Released by pull or pressure on the wing  E05C19/022  takes precedence -- This subclass covers devices that unlock or open a wing such as a door or window by means of a pulling action or by applying pressure to the wing itself. It is found under fixed constructions, locks and bolts for wings and is more specific than the broader group E05C19/022, which therefore takes precedence.
[E05C 19/066] made of plastics, e.g. hook-and-loop type fastener -- This subclass concerns plastic fasteners used to secure aircraft wings, such as hook-and-loop type devices for doors or windows. It falls under bolts or fastening devices for wings within the fixed-construction category.
[E05C 19/08] Hasps -- Hasps are fastening devices used to secure doors, windows or other wing sections, typically comprising a metal plate with a slot that receives a latch or padlock. In the CPC hierarchy they fall under fixed constructions, locks and bolts or fastening devices for wings, specifically for doors or windows. This entry is designated E05C 19/08.
[E05C 19/10] Hook fastenings -- This CPC entry E05C 19/10 covers hook fastenings used as part of devices for securing aircraft wings, doors or windows. It falls under fixed constructions, locks, bolts or fastening devices for wings, especially for doors or windows, and includes other specially designed wing-securing devices.
[E05C 19/105] Butterfly latches -- Butterfly latches are quick-release fastening devices mounted on aircraft wings, doors or windows to secure panels in place. They consist of a pivoting lever that locks over a catch when closed and can be opened with a simple manual motion, providing fast and reliable securing of wing sections.
[E05C 19/12] pivotally mounted -- E05C 19/12 pertains to pivotally mounted devices that secure wing sections, such as doors or windows, using bolts or other fastening elements that rotate to allow opening or closing while keeping the wing fixed in place.
[E05C 19/14] with toggle action -- This subclass covers fastening devices for aircraft or vehicle wings that operate with a toggle mechanism, such as bolts or locks that lock or unlock by moving a lever that snaps into position. It is used for doors or windows on wings.
[E05C 19/145] flush -- A flush bolt is a low-profile fastening device mounted within the edge of a door, window or aircraft wing so that, when engaged, the bolt sits level with the surrounding surface. It provides a concealed securing method for wings, allowing the closure to appear seamless while still being locked.
[E05C 19/16] Devices holding the wing by magnetic or electromagnetic attraction -- The code E05C 19/16 classifies devices that retain an aircraft wing, door or window by magnetic or electromagnetic attraction, providing a non-mechanical fastening method. It is placed under Fixed Constructions - Locks - Bolts or fastening devices for wings, especially for doors or windows.
[E05C 19/161] magnetic gaskets -- Magnetic gaskets are fastening devices that use the attractive force of permanent magnets to hold together wing sections, doors or windows. They provide both a sealing function and a releasable lock, often employed in aircraft, automotive or building applications where rapid, repeatable closure is required.
[E05C 19/163] a movable bolt being held in the striker by a permanent magnet -- A movable bolt that is retained in the striker by a permanent magnet, providing a magnetic hold for the locking position. This device is intended for securing aircraft wings, doors or windows where a quick-release, magnet-assisted fastening is required.
[E05C 19/165] released by pushing in the closing direction -- A device in the fixed-construction group that secures aircraft wings, doors or windows with a bolt-type lock that opens when a user pushes the component in the closing direction, allowing the wing or opening to release without a separate unlocking action.
[E05C 19/166] electromagnetic -- E05C 19/166 covers bolts, fasteners or locking devices for aircraft wings, doors or windows that are operated by electromagnetic means. It includes any electromagnetic actuation, release or holding system specially designed to secure wing openings, such as electromagnetically engaged bolts or latches. This subclass falls under Fixed Constructions → Locks → Bolts or fastening devices for wings, especially for doors or windows.
[E05C 19/168] a movable bolt being electromagnetically held in the striker by electromagnetic attraction -- E05C 19/168 covers a locking mechanism for aircraft wing doors or windows in which the bolt is free to move but is retained in the striker by an electromagnetic force, allowing the bolt to be released or engaged electrically rather than mechanically.
[E05C 19/18] Portable devices specially adapted for securing wings -- Portable devices designed to lock or fasten aircraft wings, doors or windows, allowing quick and temporary securing without permanent installation. These handheld or detachable mechanisms are typically used during ground handling, maintenance or emergency situations to prevent movement or opening of wing sections. They fall under the bolt or fastening devices subclass for wings in the cooperative patent classification.
[E05C 19/182] insertable in the gap between the wing and the frame or in the gap between a lock and its striker, e.g. for cooperation with the striker -- E05C 19/182 covers devices that are inserted into the gap between a wing (such as a door or window) and its frame, or between a lock and its striker, to cooperate with the striker for improved locking or sealing. These insertables are used in fixed-construction fastening systems for securing wings.
[E05C 19/184] a portable member cooperating with a fixed member or an opening on the wing or the frame, for locking the wing -- E05C 19/184 covers portable members that cooperate with a fixed part or an opening in a wing or its frame to provide a locking function, such as devices used to secure aircraft doors, windows or other wing openings.
[E05C 19/186] with a pair of hooks, which are movable towards each other for grasping of an element on the wing, respectively on the frame, or for grasping of an element on each of the wings forming a double door -- The code E05C 19/186 covers locking devices for wing or door constructions that use two hooks which can be moved toward each other to seize a fitting on the wing, on the frame, or on each wing of a double-door arrangement. This mechanism is used to secure windows, doors or similar openings in fixed constructions.
[E05C 19/188] Removably mounted securing devices, e.g. devices clamped to the wing or the frame  E05C19/182  -  E05C19/186  take precedence -- The E05C 19/188 class covers removable securing devices for aircraft wings, such as clamps or other fittings that can be attached to the wing or its frame to hold doors, windows or other panels in place. These devices are intended for easy installation and removal without permanent modification of the structure.
[E05C 2001/008] the axis of the bolt movement forming an oblique angle with the surface from which the bolt protrudes, e.g. the wing edge -- This entry relates to bolts whose axis is set at an oblique angle to the surface from which they protrude, such as when a bolt emerges from a wing edge at a non-perpendicular inclination.
[E05C 2003/126] Only part of the latch movable for latching -- This CPC subgroup designates latch mechanisms in which only a portion of the latch moves to achieve a latched condition, while the remainder of the device stays fixed. Examples include a swinging leaf or a spring-loaded hook that engages a strike plate without moving the whole assembly.
[E05C 2003/128] Pendulum-type bolt -- In CPC the code E05C 2003/128 designates pendulum-type bolts, which are fasteners that incorporate a swinging or weighted element attached to the bolt head or shank to produce a controlled oscillating motion during installation or operation, providing self-locking, impact-damping or precise positioning functions.
[E05C 2005/005] Pivoting bolts or catches being able to move in an additional direction, e.g. by sliding or by pivoting about an additional axis, in order to allow closing of the wing even if the bolt or catch is in its locked position -- The CPC subclass E05C 2005/005 covers aircraft wing fasteners, such as bolts or catches, that can shift in an extra direction-by sliding or pivoting about another axis-to permit wing closure even when the locking element remains engaged. This allows the wing to be secured while still providing the necessary movement for locking mechanisms.
[E05C 2007/007] for a double-wing sliding door or window, i.e. where both wings are slidable -- This group concerns double-wing doors or windows in which each wing can slide, usually on separate tracks, allowing the opening to be widened by moving either side. It includes mechanisms that guide, seal, and lock the two sliding leaves for building apertures.
[E05C 2009/1816] snap-mounted without screw fasteners -- E05C 2009/1816 deals with doors, windows or similar openings that are attached by snap-fit methods without using screw fasteners. It covers structures where the component is held in place by resilient elements that engage a seat, allowing installation and removal without screws. This subclass includes snap-mounted frames, panels and sealing elements.
[E05C 2009/1866] of the keyhole slot type -- E05C 2009/1866 classifies the particular shape and geometry of a keyhole slot used in locking mechanisms for doors, windows or shutters. It covers the design of the elongated opening that receives the lock bolt, including dimensions, positioning and methods of forming the slot in the material.
[E05C 21/00] Arrangements or combinations of wing fastening, securing, or holding devices, not covered by a single preceding main group; -- This class covers various arrangements or combinations of devices used to fasten, secure, or hold aircraft wings, doors, or windows when the solution does not fit a single preceding main group. It includes mixed or hybrid bolt, lock, and latch systems designed for wing fastening.
[E05C 21/005] Provisional arrangements between door and frame for holding vehicle doors closed or partially open during manufacturing or maintenance -- The E05C 21/005 subclass pertains to provisional arrangements between a vehicle door and its frame that hold the door closed or partly open during manufacturing, servicing or maintenance. It includes temporary fixtures, clamps or similar devices that are not part of the permanent fastening system.
[E05C 3/00] Fastening devices with bolts moving pivotally or rotatively -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fastening devices comprising a pivotally or rotatively movable bolt. The bolt can have either latching or non latching action. The bolt can be moved with an operating handle or equivalent member moving rigidly or non rigidly with the bolt.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Arrangements of simultaneously actuated bolts at well-separated positions on the same frame -> E05C9/00 - Devices for holding a wing open comprising a single pivoting securing member -> E05C17/50 - Latches with wedging action, tilt-plates -> E05C19/008 - Automatic catches with a bifurcated latch -> E05C19/024 - In which the securing part is formed or carried by a spring and moves only by distortion of the spring, e.g. snaps -> E05C19/06 - Locks in which a pivoted latch is used also as locking means -> E05B57/00 - Locks with rotary bolt without provision for latching -> E05B63/0013 - Locks for refrigerators with a bifurcated bolt -> E05B65/0046 - Locks for sliding wings with pivoting bolts -> E05B65/0811 , E05B65/0835 - Locks for sliding wings with double hook-like pivoting members -> E05B65/0858 - Locks for car boot lids -> E05B83/18 - Locks for car bonnets -> E05B83/24 - Vehicle locks with a bolt turning around an axis -> E05B85/24 - Vehicle locks in which the member engaging the keeper is shaped as a toothed wheel or the like -> E05B85/28
[E05C 3/002] sliding in an arcuate guide or the like  locks with circular bolts  E05B63/123
[E05C 3/004] about an axis perpendicular to the surface on which the fastener is mounted -- E05C 3/004 covers fastening devices in which the bolt rotates or pivots about an axis that is perpendicular to the mounting surface, such as hinges or latches for doors, windows or aircraft wings. The classification applies to mechanisms where the bolt's motion is driven by a shaft or arm that swings out of the plane of the surface, enabling opening and closing without axial translation.
[E05C 3/006] about an axis parallel to the surface on which the fastener is mounted -- E05C 3/006 refers to bolts or fastening devices whose rotating or pivoting axis is parallel to the surface they are mounted on, such as those used in wing, door or window locks where the bolt moves in a horizontal plane rather than vertically. This arrangement allows the fastener to slide or swing open and closed along the same plane as the surrounding panel.
[E05C 3/008] parallel to the wing edge -- E05C 3/008 concerns fastening devices whose bolts pivot or rotate and are positioned parallel to the edge of a wing, such as the wing of an aircraft, to secure doors or windows. The classification covers mechanisms that lock by moving a bolt along a line that runs alongside the wing surface.
[E05C 3/02] without latching action -- E05C 3/02 covers fastening devices such as bolts used in wing constructions like doors or windows that are operated by a pivot or rotary motion but do not incorporate any latching mechanism to hold the closure in place.
[E05C 3/04] with operating handle or equivalent member rigid with the bolt -- E05C 3/04 covers fastening devices for aircraft wing doors or windows where the bolt is operated by a handle or a rigid member that moves together with the bolt, providing a solid, pivoting or rotating actuation element. This sub-class falls under fixed constructions, locks, and bolts for wings, specifically those with an integral operating handle.
[E05C 3/041] rotating about an axis perpendicular to the surface on which the fastener is mounted -- The subclass E05C 3/041 relates to bolts or fastening devices for doors, windows or other wing sections that are operated by rotating about an axis that is perpendicular to the surface they are attached to. In this arrangement the bolt pivots or turns out of the plane of the door or window to engage or release the latch.
[E05C 3/042] the handle being at one side, the bolt at the other side or inside the wing -- This entry E05C 3/042 concerns fastening devices for wings such as doors or windows in which the handle is placed on one side while the bolt is positioned on the opposite side or concealed inside the wing, and the bolt moves pivotally or rotatively.
[E05C 3/043] the pivot being between bolt and handle  E05C3/042  takes precedence -- The CPC subclass E05C 3/043 covers fastening devices for wings, such as doors or windows, where the bolt pivots or rotates relative to the handle; the pivot point lies between the bolt and the handle. This subclass falls under Fixed Constructions → Locks → Bolts or fastening devices for wings. It is superseded by the more specific subclass E05C 3/042.
[E05C 3/044] the bolt and handle being at the same side of the pivot  E05C3/045 ,  E05C3/046  take precedence -- E05C 3/044 relates to wing-lock fastening devices where the bolt and its operating handle are located on the same side of the pivot point. It is a subcategory of bolts that move pivotally or rotatively in fixed constructions for doors or windows. The more specific groups E05C 3/045 and E05C 3/046 have priority over this entry.
[E05C 3/045] in the form of a hook  hook-like fastenings  E05C19/10 -- This CPC subclass covers fastening devices for aircraft wings, doors or windows where the bolt pivots or rotates and is shaped like a hook, providing a hook-type fastener that locks by moving into position.
[E05C 3/046] in the form of a crescent-shaped cam  hooks  E05C3/045
[E05C 3/047] rotating about an axis parallel to the surface on which the fastener is mounted -- E05C 3/047 relates to bolts or other fastening devices used in fixed constructions such as wing locks for doors or windows, where the bolt rotates about an axis that is parallel to the surface on which it is mounted. The classification covers pivoting or rotative bolts that move in a rotary motion rather than a linear one.
[E05C 3/048] parallel to the wing edge -- A fastening device for aircraft wing doors or windows in which the bolt pivots or rotates and is positioned parallel to the wing edge. It falls under the fixed-construction hierarchy of locks and bolts or fastening devices specially for wings.
[E05C 3/06] with operating handle or equivalent member moving otherwise than rigidly with the bolt -- This subclass covers bolts or other fastening devices for doors or windows that are operated by a handle or similar member that moves independently rather than rigidly with the bolt, such as a pivoting or rotating lever that transmits motion to the bolt.
[E05C 3/08] the handle or member moving essentially towards or away from the plane of the wing or frame -- E05C 3/08 refers to fastening devices for wings, especially doors or windows, where the handle or operative member moves essentially toward or away from the plane of the wing or frame. It includes handles that translate normal to the surface to open or close the opening, rather than rotating or pivoting within the plane. This subclass falls under fixed constructions, locks, and bolts or fastening devices for wings.
[E05C 3/10] the handle or member moving essentially in a plane substantially parallel to the wing -- This subclass covers handles or other operating members that move essentially in a plane parallel to the wing, used to actuate bolts or fastening devices for wing doors or windows in aircraft.
[E05C 3/12] with latching action -- E05C 3/12 relates to fastening devices for doors, windows or similar openings in which a bolt swings or rotates to secure the closure. The bolt moves pivotally or rotatively and engages in a latching position that holds the wing closed.
[E05C 3/122] flush -- E05C 3/122 covers flush bolts, a type of fastening device whose bolt pivots or rotates to lie level with the surface of a door, window or wing when engaged. These bolts are designed to be hidden within the surrounding material, providing a streamlined appearance while securing the opening. The classification pertains to fixed constructions where the bolt's motion keeps the hardware unobtrusive.
[E05C 3/124] with latch under compression force between its pivot and the striker  E05C3/122 ,  E05C3/14 ,  E05C3/16  and  E05B65/0852  take precedence -- E05C 3/124 covers fastening devices for aircraft wing doors or windows in which a bolt pivots or rotates and includes a latch that is kept under compression between the bolt's pivot point and the striker. The device is a fixed-construction lock for wings, and the definition builds on the broader groups E05C 3/122, 3/14, 3/16 and E05B65/0852, which take precedence.
[E05C 3/14] with operating handle or equivalent member rigid with the latch -- E05C 3/14 includes fastening devices for aircraft wings, doors or windows that use a bolt moved by an operating handle or a rigid member attached to the latch, providing a pivoting or rotating action.
[E05C 3/145] pivoting about an axis perpendicular to the wing -- This subclass concerns bolts or fastening devices for aircraft wings, particularly for doors or windows, that operate by rotating about an axis perpendicular to the wing surface, enabling the component to pivot open or closed. It is part of the broader group of fixed constructions and lock mechanisms for wing fastenings.
[E05C 3/16] with operating handle or equivalent member moving otherwise than rigidly with the latch -- E05C 3/16 covers fastening devices for wings such as doors or windows in which a bolt moves pivotally or rotatively and is operated by a handle or equivalent member that does not move rigidly with the latch. The classification applies to fixed-construction locks where the actuation mechanism provides a non-linear motion to secure the opening.
[E05C 3/162] the handle or member moving essentially towards or away of the plane of the wing or frame  E05C3/167  takes precedence
[E05C 3/165] the handle or member moving substantially parallel to the wing or frame  E05C3/167  takes precedence -- E05C 3/165 classifies fastening devices for wings, doors or windows whose bolts move pivotally or rotatively, specifically where the handle or actuating member travels substantially parallel to the wing or frame. The more detailed sub-group E05C 3/167 supersedes this entry for such arrangements.
[E05C 3/167] the latch pivoting about an axis perpendicular to the wing -- This class covers fastening devices for wing doors or windows in which a bolt-type latch is mounted to rotate about an axis that is perpendicular to the wing surface, allowing the latch to pivot for securing the opening.
[E05C 3/22] the bolt being spring controlled -- E05C 3/22 covers fastening devices for doors, windows or other winged openings in which the bolt's movement is controlled by a spring. The spring biases the bolt to lock or release automatically, and the bolt operates by pivoting or rotating within the fixed construction.
[E05C 3/24] in the form of a bifurcated member -- Fastening devices for doors, windows or wing panels that use a bolt shaped as a split or forked member, allowing the bolt to pivot or rotate while engaging the opening. The bifurcated form typically provides two prongs that spread apart to lock the element in place. This design falls under CPC class E05C 3/24.
[E05C 3/26] engaging a stud-like keeper -- This subclass covers fastening devices in which a bolt or other pivoting element engages a stud-like keeper to secure doors, windows or similar openings on wings. The mechanism typically allows the bolt to rotate or pivot into a fixed stud to lock the assembly, providing a simple, reversible retaining action.
[E05C 3/28] with simultaneously operating double bolts -- This subgroup covers fastening devices for doors or windows in which two bolts are driven or rotated together to lock simultaneously, providing a coordinated locking action. It belongs to the hierarchy of fixed constructions, locks, and bolts or fastening devices for wings, especially for doors or windows.
[E05C 3/30] in the form of a hook -- This subclass covers fastening devices for doors or windows where a bolt pivots or rotates and is shaped like a hook. The hook form engages a strike or keep to lock the opening. It falls under fixed constructions, locks, bolts or fastening devices for wings.
[E05C 3/34] with simultaneously operating double bolts -- This subclass covers fastening devices for doors or windows that employ a pair of bolts linked so that both engage or release at the same time, typically moving pivotally or rotatively. The mechanism ensures simultaneous operation of the two bolts to secure the opening.
[E05C 3/36] in the form of a rotary gear -- This CPC subclass covers fastening devices for aircraft wing doors or windows where the bolt functions as a rotary gear, moving by rotation rather than linear motion. It applies to lock mechanisms in which the bolt pivots or turns to secure the opening.
[E05C 3/38] with bolts engaging a hooked keeper -- Fastening devices for doors or windows that use bolts moving pivotally or rotatively and lock by inserting the bolt into a hooked keeper, providing a secure latch for wing-type openings.
[E05C 3/40] with bolts engaging a stud-like keeper -- This subclass pertains to fastening devices where a bolt can pivot or rotate and locks by engaging a stud-like keeper, commonly employed in fixed constructions such as door or window wing assemblies. It is used for bolts that secure doors, windows or similar panels by fitting into a keeper that resembles a stud.
[E05C 5/00] Fastening devices with bolts moving otherwise than only rectilinearly and only pivotally or rotatively -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fastening devices with bolts moving otherwise than only rectilinearly and only pivotally or rotatively, e.g. bolts moving axially and turning about their axis to secure the wing.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Espagnolette locks with a pivoting bolt on actuating bar -> E05C9/1883 - Latches with floating bolts, e.g. rings, ballls -> E05C19/009 - Hook fastenings with toggle action -> E05C19/14 - Fastening devices with means carried by the bolt for interlocking with the keeper comprising a sliding bolt rotating about its axis, i.e. of bayonet type -> E05B63/125 - Fastening devices with means carried by the bolt for interlocking with the keeper, the bolt having an additional sliding bolt or movement -> E05B63/126 - Fastening devices with means carried by the bolt for interlocking with the keeper, the bolt having an additional rotating bolt or movement -> E05B63/127 - Locks for sliding doors with a pivoting bolt having an additional movement -> E05B65/0817
[E05C 5/02] both moving axially and turning about their axis to secure the wing -- E05C 5/02 covers fastening devices for aircraft wings, such as door or window locks, whose bolts move both axially and rotate about their own axis to secure the wing. The class includes mechanisms that combine linear sliding with turning motion, rather than relying solely on straight or purely pivoting movement. This dual-action design provides a secure, space-efficient lock for wing openings.
[E05C 5/04] performing both movements simultaneously, e.g. screwing into a keeper -- E05C 5/04 classifies fastening devices whose bolt combines linear and rotational motion at the same time, such as a bolt that screws into a keeper while advancing. The entry applies to wing-type locks for doors or windows where the bolt performs both movements simultaneously.
[E05C 7/00] Fastening devices specially adapted for two wings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Fastening devices specially adapted for two wings. It includes locks for sluice doors, for doors having upper and lower wings, for doors which lie one behind the other when closed, and for doors which abut when closed.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Locking one wing by shutting another in vehicles -> E05B77/52 - Locks for pillar-less vehicles -> E05B83/38
[E05C 7/002] for sluice doors  for nuclear reactors  G21C13/0285
[E05C 7/005] for "Dutch doors", i.e. upper and lower wings -- E05C 7/005 covers bolts or other fastening devices designed specifically for doors with two hinged sections, such as "Dutch doors" that have an upper and a lower wing. These devices are a subset of lock mechanisms for fixed constructions, particularly for winged doors or windows.
[E05C 7/02] for wings which lie one behind the other when closed -- E05C 7/02 covers fastening devices specially adapted for wing sections that overlap, such as doors or windows where one wing lies behind the other when closed. These devices, including bolts, latches or locks, secure the rear wing to the front wing to provide a firm seal and easy operation.
[E05C 7/04] for wings which abut when closed -- E05C 7/04 covers fastening devices designed for paired wing sections such as double doors or sliding windows that meet and abut when closed, providing a secure seal and alignment; it includes bolts, latches and lock mechanisms specially adapted to hold the two wings together in the closed position.
[E05C 7/045] Sliding bolts mounted on or in the edge of a normally closed wing of a double-door or -window -- Sliding bolts that are integrated into, or attached to, the edge of one leaf of a normally closed double door or window, enabling the two wings to be locked together by a linear movement of the bolt across the joint. The device is designed specifically for securing the meeting edge of the two panels. It falls under the fixed-construction category for locks and fastening devices for wings.
[E05C 7/06] a fastening device for one wing being actuated or controlled by closing another wing -- The subclass E05C 7/06 covers fastening devices for a single wing that are actuated or controlled by the closing of a second wing, such as interlocking bolts used in doors or windows. It falls under the hierarchy of fixed constructions, locks, and bolts or fastening devices for wings, specifically for doors or windows.
[E05C 9/00] Arrangements of simultaneously actuated bolts or other securing devices at well-separated positions on the same wing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Locks with simultaneously actuated bolts, e.g. by means of transmission bars, at well separated positions on the same wing.  This group has the following main structure:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Bolts extending nearly along the whole length of the wing -> E05C19/001 - Locking bars, cross bars, security bars -> E05C19/003 - Face plates or front plates for non-espagnolette locks -> E05B9/002 - Striker plates for non-espagnolette locks -> E05B15/0205 - Operation of locks by flexible means, e.g. by cables -> E05B53/003 - Operation of locks by flexible means, e.g. by Bowden cables -> E05B53/005 - Arrangements of several locks or locks with several bolts -> E05B63/14 - Panic locks of the vertical rod type -> E05B65/1006 - Drawer interlock mechanism with rotary locking bars -> E05B65/465 - Locks for a plurality of drawers using a rotary lock bar -> E05B65/468 - Connecting vehicle lock parts using connecting rods -> E05B79/12 - Locks for vehicles with at least one elongated bar for actuating the fastening means -> E05B83/08 - Locks for vehicles with a rotary bar for actuating the fastening means -> E05B83/10 - Means for transmitting movements between vertical and horizontal sliding bars -> E05F7/08
[E05C 9/002] with arrangements allowing the wing to be slam-shut, e.g. by securing elements with latching action  E05B63/20 ,  E05B63/24  take precedence -- This subclass covers wing locking systems in which several bolts or fasteners positioned far apart on the same wing are actuated together to permit a rapid, slam-shut closure, typically using a latching action. The arrangement is designed for doors or windows and supersedes the more general E05B63/20 and E05B63/24 categories.
[E05C 9/004] Faceplates  for other locks or fasteners  E05B9/002 ; Fixing the faceplates to the wing
[E05C 9/006] Details of bars -- E05C 9/006 relates to the detailed design of bars used in arrangements where several bolts or other securing devices are actuated at the same time on widely spaced points of the same wing, such as doors or windows. It falls under fixed constructions → locks → bolts or fastening devices for wings, and addresses the geometry, connection features and reinforcing elements of the bars that transmit the simultaneous locking action.
[E05C 9/008] mounted in an elongate casing on the surface of the wing -- E05C 9/008 covers configurations where multiple bolts or other locking devices are actuated together but placed far apart on a single aircraft wing, the devices being installed in a long casing that runs along the wing surface. The classification falls under fixed constructions, locks and fastening devices for wing doors or windows.
[E05C 9/02] with one sliding bar for fastening when moved in one direction and unfastening when moved in opposite direction -- This group covers wing fastening arrangements that use a single sliding bar which secures the wing when pushed in one direction and releases it when pulled the opposite way. It applies to doors, windows or similar panels where the bolt or locking device is actuated by linear motion of the bar rather than rotation.
[E05C 9/021] with rack and pinion mechanism -- This CPC subclass E05C 9/021 covers configurations where multiple bolts or other securing devices on the same door or window wing are actuated together by a rack-and-pinion mechanism, enabling simultaneous movement of widely separated fasteners.
[E05C 9/023] between a lock cylinder and the bar -- E05C 9/023 covers arrangements where a lock cylinder is linked to a bar that simultaneously operates multiple bolts or fastening devices positioned far apart on a single wing, such as doors or windows, allowing coordinated locking.
[E05C 9/025] with pins engaging slots -- The CPC subclass E05C 9/025 covers configurations where multiple bolts or other securing devices are arranged at widely spaced points on the same wing, such as a door or window, and are operated together. It specifically relates to designs that use pins engaging slots to lock the wing simultaneously.
[E05C 9/026] comprising key-operated locks, e.g. a lock cylinder to drive auxiliary deadbolts or latch bolts  E05C9/023  takes precedence -- E05C 9/026 covers arrangements where multiple bolts or other securing devices are actuated at the same time and placed at widely spaced points on the same wing, such as a door or window. The design includes a key-operated lock cylinder that drives auxiliary deadbolts or latch bolts, providing coordinated locking of the wing.
[E05C 9/028] externally mounted on the wing, i.e. surface mounted  E05C9/008  takes precedence
[E05C 9/04] with two sliding bars moved in opposite directions when fastening or unfastening -- E05C 9/04 covers wing locks in which two sliding bars are moved in opposite directions to secure or release a door or window, providing simultaneous actuation of bolts positioned far apart on the same wing. The arrangement ensures that both bolts engage or disengage together, improving sealing and safety.
[E05C 9/041] with rack and pinion mechanism -- This subclass covers wing locking systems that use several bolts or other fastening devices placed far apart on the same wing and actuated simultaneously, typically for aircraft doors or windows, with the motion produced by a rack-and-pinion mechanism.
[E05C 9/042] with pins engaging slots -- The classification covers schemes where several bolts or other securing devices positioned far apart on the same door or window wing are operated at the same time, using pins that engage corresponding slots to lock or unlock the wing. This arrangement ensures simultaneous actuation of multiple fasteners.
[E05C 9/043] with crank pins and connecting rods -- The classification E05C 9/043 covers arrangements where several bolts or other fastening devices on the same wing are actuated together, even though they are spaced far apart, and the mechanism uses crank pins linked by connecting rods. This includes systems that turn a single motion into simultaneous movement of multiple securing elements, typically for doors or windows on aircraft or similar structures.
[E05C 9/045] with inclined surfaces, e.g. spiral or helicoidal -- E05C 9/045 concerns fastening devices for doors or windows in which the bolts or locking elements incorporate inclined, spiral or helicoidal surfaces that engage when actuated, allowing simultaneous locking at several widely spaced points on the same wing. This design provides a coordinated, multi-point securing action while the inclined surfaces guide the bolts into their locked positions.
[E05C 9/046] with two interconnected mechanisms each driving one rod -- This subclass deals with wing lock systems where two separate actuating mechanisms are mechanically linked so that each operates its own rod, allowing simultaneous movement of bolts at spaced positions on the same wing, typically for doors or windows.
[E05C 9/047] comprising key-operated locks, e.g. a lock cylinder to drive auxiliary deadbolts or latch bolts  E05C9/041  takes precedence
[E05C 9/048] externally mounted on the wing, i.e. surface mounted  E05C9/008  takes precedence -- This group concerns wings, such as aircraft doors or windows, that have externally surface-mounted bolts or locking devices arranged at well-separated positions and actuated simultaneously. It falls under fixed constructions for locks and fastening devices for wings, specifically where multiple securing devices are coordinated on the same wing.
[E05C 9/06] with three or more sliding bars -- E05C 9/06 pertains to fixed-construction locks for wings, especially doors or windows, in which three or more sliding bars are arranged at well-separated positions on the same wing and are actuated simultaneously to secure the opening.
[E05C 9/063] extending along three or more sides of the wing or frame  means for transmitting movements between vertical and horizontal bars  E05F7/08
[E05C 9/066] Locks for windows or doors specially adapted for tilt and turn -- This subclass covers locking mechanisms for tilt-and-turn windows or doors in which multiple bolts or other fasteners positioned at separate points on the same wing are operated together. The arrangement provides simultaneous actuation of spaced-apart securing devices to lock the opening securely.
[E05C 9/08] with a rotary bar for actuating the fastening means -- E05C 9/08 covers wing constructions such as doors or windows that use several bolts or other securing devices positioned far apart but operated together. The simultaneous actuation is achieved through a rotary bar that engages all the fastening means at once.
[E05C 9/085] pivoting about an axis perpendicular to the door  locking bars or the like pivoted about an axis perpendicular to the plane of the wing  E05C19/005
[E05C 9/10] Actuating mechanisms for bars -- The entry covers devices that actuate bars used as bolts or other securing elements on wing sections such as doors or windows, especially when several bolts are operated at the same time from widely spaced locations on the same wing. It includes mechanisms that synchronize the movement of these fastening devices.
[E05C 9/12] with rack and pinion mechanism -- E05C 9/12 covers wing locks where several bolts or similar securing devices spaced far apart on the same door or window are operated together by a rack-and-pinion mechanism, translating rotary motion into simultaneous linear movement of the bolts to lock or unlock the opening.
[E05C 9/14] with pins engaging slots -- E05C 9/14 covers arrangements in which several bolts on the same wing are actuated together and each bolt carries a pin that engages a corresponding slot to secure or release the wing, a solution commonly used for doors or windows on aircraft, vehicles or buildings.
[E05C 9/16] with crank pins and connecting rods -- This subgroup refers to fastening systems for wings, especially doors or windows, in which several bolts positioned far apart are operated at the same time by a network of crank pins and connecting rods that link the bolts together, enabling simultaneous movement of the securing devices.
[E05C 9/18] Details of fastening means or of fixed retaining means for the ends of bars -- The CPC entry E05C 9/18 covers the specific design details of fastening or fixed retaining devices applied to the ends of bars, particularly when multiple bolts or similar securing elements are arranged at widely spaced positions on the same wing such as a door or window and are actuated simultaneously. It focuses on the construction of these end-mounted securing means within fixed constructions and lock systems.
[E05C 9/1808] Keepers -- Keepers are components that retain or guide bolts or other securing devices on a wing, allowing several widely spaced bolts to be actuated at the same time, typically in doors or windows of aircraft or similar structures.
[E05C 9/1825] Fastening means -- A fastening arrangement that uses multiple bolts or other securing devices, operated at the same time, positioned far apart on the same wing-typically for doors or windows on aircraft or similar structures.
[E05C 9/1833] performing sliding movements -- The subclass covers fastening systems for wings such as doors or windows where several bolts or locks positioned far apart are actuated at the same time and move by sliding rather than rotating, enabling coordinated opening or closing of the wing.
[E05C 9/1841] perpendicular to actuating bar -- The code covers wing fastening systems where multiple bolts placed at widely separated points are linked to a single actuator whose operating bar is arranged so the bolts lie perpendicular to it, enabling simultaneous locking of doors or windows.
[E05C 9/185] parallel with actuating bar -- The code refers to a parallel actuating bar that simultaneously moves several bolts or other securing devices placed far apart on the same wing, such as on aircraft doors or windows. The bar links the devices so they operate together in unison, providing a coordinated locking action.
[E05C 9/1858] of the roller bolt type -- The subclass deals with arrangements where several roller-type bolts or comparable securing devices are actuated at the same time on the same wing, such as on aircraft doors or windows, and are positioned far apart from each other to provide simultaneous locking or fastening.
[E05C 9/1875] performing pivoting movements  E05C9/08  takes precedence -- This subclass covers arrangements where several bolts or other securing devices located at widely spaced points on the same door or window wing are actuated together, typically to cause the wing to pivot during opening or closing. It details coordinated mechanisms for simultaneous movement and falls under the broader group E05C9/08.
[E05C 9/1883] pivotally mounted on the actuation bar -- This subclass concerns wing locks in which several bolts or other securing devices positioned far apart on the same door or window wing are driven simultaneously from a single actuation bar, each bolt being pivotally mounted on that bar.
[E05C 9/1891] pivoting around an axis parallel to the bar -- A classification for fastening devices on wings such as doors or windows that involve several bolts or similar securing elements placed far apart but operated together. It specifically covers designs where the bolts pivot around an axis that runs parallel to the bar, enabling simultaneous actuation of the widely spaced devices.
[E05C 9/20] Coupling means for sliding bars, rods, or cables -- E05C 9/20 covers coupling mechanisms that connect sliding bars, rods or cables, enabling simultaneous actuation of bolts or other securing devices positioned far apart on the same wing, such as in doors or windows. These devices are used in fixed-construction lock systems to lock or fasten wing components.
[E05C 9/22] Guides for sliding bars, rods or cables -- E05C 9/22 covers guides that direct sliding bars, rods or cables used in fixed constructions such as wing locks for doors or windows. The class applies to arrangements where multiple bolts or securing devices are actuated together but positioned at widely spaced points on the same wing, providing controlled movement of the sliding elements.
[E05C 9/24] Means for transmitting movements between vertical and horizontal sliding bars, rods or cables , e.g. corner guides -- The subclass E05C 9/24 covers devices that transmit motion between vertical and horizontal sliding elements-such as rods, bars or cables-often realized as corner guides. These mechanisms are used in fixed-construction lock assemblies for doors or windows, enabling simultaneously actuated bolts or fasteners positioned far apart on the same wing.
[E05D] HINGES OR SUSPENSION DEVICES FOR DOORS, WINDOWS OR WINGS -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hinges and other suspension devices such as sliding suspensions, for wings in building construction and outfitting, fences, domestic appliances, vehicles and furniture.  Also covered are hinge parts such as pins, flaps and sleeves, furthermore hinges of special construction, such as adjustable hinges and hinges with devices for preventing movement between hinge parts, and finally accessories for hinges and for sliding or lifting wings.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Arrangements for water-tight doors in ships -> B63B43/24 - Heavy bearings in sluice gates -> E02B7/40 - Self-closing hinges -> E05F1/06 , E05F1/12 , E05F3/20 - Pivotal connections in general -> F16C11/00 - Hinges for glasses -> G02C5/22 - Hinges for folding parts of portable electronic devices -> G06F1/1615 , G06F1/1633 , H04M1/02  **Glossary:** - Carriage: Support element that supports the weight of a wing and transfers it directly to an underlying rail/track, on which it (the carriage) slides or rolls. - Flaps: Parts of hinges for attachment to wings or frames - Hinges: Devices with a varying number and type of pins and arms, for pivoting connection of wings to a fixed structure - Overhead wings: Specific type of wings, wherein the wing can move into an overhead position, e.g. a garage door. - Self-closing hinges: Gravity- or spring-operated hinges constructed to provide a closing force to wings over a substantial part of their movement; includes Devices for wing-counterbalancing - Substitutes for hinges: Devices, without the use of a hinge pin, for pivoting connection of wings to a fixed structure - Suspension arrangements: Arrangements to suspend or otherwise support wings other than by hinges; for wing motions other than simple pivoting, e.g. revolving, sliding, combined or alternative motions - Wings: Pivotable, slideable, or otherwise moveable part of doors, windows, flaps, covers etc. in building construction and outfitting, fences, domestic appliances, vehicles and furniture; the term also includes other movable structures such as drawers, lids of chests, car boots, or car bonnets
[E05D 1/00] Pinless hinges -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means, without the use of a hinge pin, for pivoting connection of doors, windows or other wings to a fixed structure; e.g. flexible strips, arched guide members.
[E05D 1/02] made of one piece -- E05D 1/02 relates to hinges that are formed as a single-piece structure without separate pins, providing a pivot for doors, windows or wings. The integral design simplifies assembly, eliminates pin wear and offers a clean, durable hinge solution.
[E05D 1/04] with guide members shaped as circular arcs -- E05D 1/04 covers pinless hinges that incorporate guide members formed as circular arcs, allowing smooth swinging motion without a central pin. The arcuate guides engage the hinge leaves to transmit motion while maintaining alignment, useful for doors, windows or wing panels.
[E05D 1/06] consisting of two easily-separable parts -- E05D 1/06 covers pinless hinges that are constructed from two parts that can be quickly separated without the use of pins. The design enables easy disassembly for maintenance or replacement while still providing a secure hinge function for doors, windows or wings.
[E05D 11/00] Additional features or accessories of hinges -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hinges or pivots constructed to provide additional hinge-specific features relating especially to features relevant during regular use of the hinge, e.g. anti-tamper devices, protective devices, electrical cable routing, lubricating, movement controlling/limiting devices.  Also covered are accessories of hinges such as covers and templates for marking the mounting position of fittings on wings or frames.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Self-closing hinges -> E05F1/06 , E05F1/12 , E05F3/20
[E05D 11/0009] Templates for marking the position of fittings on wings or frames  implements for making doors, windows or frames  E04F21/003 -- This subclass covers devices such as templates or gauges used to indicate where hinges, locks or other fittings should be placed on doors, windows or frames during manufacturing. It includes marking tools and auxiliary implements for accurate positioning of hardware on fixed constructions.
[E05D 11/0018] Anti-tamper devices -- E05D 11/0018 classifies anti-tamper devices that are fitted to hinges or suspension mechanisms for doors, windows or wings. These accessories are designed to prevent unauthorised opening, removal or manipulation of the hinge assembly, enhancing security and resistance to forced entry.
[E05D 11/0027] arranged on or near the hinge and comprising parts interlocking as the wing closes, e.g. security studs -- The code E05D 11/0027 covers accessories fitted to hinges that interlock as a door or window wing closes, such as security studs positioned on or near the hinge. These devices add extra locking or protective functions to the hinge assembly.
[E05D 11/0054] Covers, e.g. for protection -- E05D 11/0054 pertains to protective covers and guards designed for hinges and related hardware, including plates, shells or sleeves that shield the hinge components from wear, corrosion or damage. These accessories may be fitted to door, window or wing hinges to enhance durability and safety.
[E05D 11/0081] for transmitting energy, e.g. electrical cable routing -- E05D 11/0081 covers hinge accessories that incorporate means for transmitting energy such as electrical cables, power lines or fluid conduits, integrated with the hinge or its mounting hardware. It includes designs where the hinge itself serves as a conduit or provides built-in routing channels for cables and other energy-carrying lines.
[E05D 11/02] Lubricating arrangements -- E05D 11/02 covers devices or systems that provide lubrication for hinges, locks or other suspension mechanisms of doors, windows or wings, such as oil reservoirs, grease dispensers, self-lubricating materials or drip feeds incorporated into the hinge assembly to reduce friction and wear.
[E05D 11/04] relating to the use of free balls as bearing-surfaces -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Hinges specially constructed to allow tilting of the members -> E05D7/06
[E05D 11/06] Devices for limiting the opening movement of hinges -- E05D 11/06 covers devices fitted to hinges, such as stops or limiting mechanisms, that restrict how far a door, window or wing can open. These accessories are used to control the swing angle for safety, security or functional reasons and are classified under fixed constructions, locks and hinges or suspension devices.
[E05D 11/08] Friction devices between relatively-movable hinge parts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Friction devices in hinges for holding relatively-moveable hinge parts and consequently wings in a user-chosen position.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Braking devices structurally combined with a hinge comprising two spigots placed on opposite edges of the wing -> E05D7/086 - Self-closing hinges with friction braking devices -> E05F3/16  **Glossary:** - Self-closing hinge: Spring-operated hinge constructed to provide a closing force to wings over a substantial part of their movement; includes devices for wing-counterbalancing - Braking device: Devices constructed to slow down the movement of wings
[E05D 11/081] with both radial and axial friction, e.g. conical friction surfaces -- The classification E05D 11/081 covers hinges or suspension devices for doors, windows or wings that incorporate combined radial and axial friction mechanisms, such as conical friction surfaces, to control movement and locking. It includes designs where friction is generated both around the hinge axis and along the axis, providing adjustable resistance or self-locking action. This entry falls under the broader categories of fixed constructions, locks, and hinges with additional features.
[E05D 11/082] with substantially radial friction, e.g. cylindrical friction surfaces -- E05D 11/082 covers hinges that incorporate primarily radial friction mechanisms, such as cylindrical friction surfaces, to control the movement of doors, windows or wings. These features provide adjustable resistance and can be used to lock positions without separate locking hardware.
[E05D 11/084] the friction depending on direction of rotation or opening angle of the hinge -- E05D 11/084 designates hinges or suspension devices for doors, windows or wings that incorporate friction that changes with the direction of rotation or with the opening angle, providing angle- or direction-dependent resistance such as self-closing or anti-lift functions. This subclass covers additional elements that modify hinge friction according to how the hinge is moved.
[E05D 11/087] with substantially axial friction, e.g. friction disks -- The E05D 11/087 subclass covers accessories for hinges that create substantially axial friction, typically using friction disks or similar elements, to regulate or resist rotational movement of doors, windows or wing panels. This feature is applied in fixed-construction lock and hinge systems to provide controlled opening and closing behavior.
[E05D 11/10] Devices for preventing movement between relatively-movable hinge parts
[E05D 11/1007] with positive locking -- E05D 11/1007 covers hinges or suspension devices for doors, windows or wings that include additional components delivering a positive locking function, whereby a mechanical element engages when the wing is closed to keep it securely locked in place.
[E05D 11/1014] for maintaining the hinge in only one position, e.g. closed -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hinges with devices for preventing movement between relatively-moveable hinge parts by biasing said hinge parts into a single pre-determined, e.g. closed, position.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Self-closing hinges -> E05F1/06 , E05F1/12 , E05F3/20
[E05D 11/1021] the hinge having two or more pins and being specially adapted for cabinets or furniture -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Cabinet or furniture hinges with two or more pins and with devices for preventing movement between relatively-moveable hinge parts by biasing said hinge parts into a single pre-determined, e.g. closed, position.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Self-closing hinges -> E05F1/06 , E05F1/12 , E05F3/20
[E05D 11/1028] for maintaining the hinge in two or more positions, e.g. intermediate or fully open -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hinges with devices for preventing movement between relatively-moveable hinge parts by biasing said hinge parts into intermediate or open positions.
[E05D 11/1042] the maintaining means being a cam and a torsion bar, e.g. motor vehicle hinge mechanisms -- E05D 11/1042 covers hinge or suspension devices whose retaining or maintaining means combine a cam with a torsion bar, a configuration frequently employed in motor-vehicle hinge mechanisms. It is a subcategory of fixed constructions, locks and hinges for doors, windows or wings, and addresses additional hinge accessories that use this cam-and-torsion-bar arrangement.
[E05D 11/105] the maintaining means acting perpendicularly to the pivot axis -- The classification covers devices that secure a hinge by applying a force perpendicular to its pivot axis, such as pins, bolts or stops that lock the hinge leaves together and prevent unwanted rotation or sagging. These maintaining means are auxiliary components that ensure stable positioning of doors, windows or other hinged elements.
[E05D 11/1057] specially adapted for vehicles  E05D11/1064  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Hinges featuring coil springs acting perpendicularly to the pivot axis in order to maintain the hinge (and wing) in predetermined positions -> E05D11/1064
[E05D 11/1064] with a coil spring perpendicular to the pivot axis -- E05D 11/1064 covers hinges that incorporate a coil spring positioned perpendicular to the hinge's pivot axis, giving a self-closing or tensioning action. The spring is provided as an accessory to the hinge mechanism for doors, windows or similar openings.
[E05D 11/1071] specially adapted for vehicles -- This subgroup covers hinge accessories specially adapted for vehicle use, such as car doors, windows or roof panels, providing mounting, sealing or locking features that meet automotive requirements. It resides under fixed constructions, locks, hinges or suspension devices for doors, windows or wings, focusing on additional features or accessories of hinges.
[E05D 11/1078] the maintaining means acting parallel to the pivot -- E05D 11/1078 relates to hinge accessories that maintain the relative position of a door, window or wing by exerting a force parallel to the hinge pivot, such as friction plates, cams or tension members that limit unwanted movement. The feature is used to keep the hinge set in a desired angular position.
[E05D 11/1085] specially adapted for vehicles -- E05D 11/1085 covers accessories or additional features for hinges and suspension devices that are specially designed for use on vehicles, such as automotive doors, windows or wing panels, providing adaptations for the particular stresses, space constraints and environmental conditions encountered in vehicular applications.
[E05D 13/00] Accessories for sliding or lifting wings, e.g. pulleys, safety catches -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Accessories for sliding or lifting wings such as pulleys, anti-dropping devices, hold-open devices and weight counterbalance devices.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Accessories for hinges -> E05D11/00 - Stops and buffers for sliding wings -> E05F5/003 - Aligning devices for wings -> E05F7/005 - Devices to prevent rattling -> E05F7/04
[E05D 13/003] Anti-dropping devices  E05D13/1223 ,  E05D13/1246 ,  E05D13/1269 ,  E05D13/1292  take precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Spring safety catches in counterbalance devices for sliding or lifting wings -> E05D13/1223 , E05D13/1246 , E05D13/1269 , E05D13/1292
[E05D 13/006] fixed to the wing, i.e. safety catches -- E05D 13/006 covers safety catches that are permanently attached to the moving wing of a door, window or similar opening, forming part of the locking or hinge system for sliding or lifting constructions.
[E05D 13/04] Fasteners specially adapted for holding sliding wings open  for holding wings closed  E05C -- E05D 13/04 covers fasteners specially designed to keep sliding or lifting doors, windows or similar wings either open or closed, including related accessories such as pulleys and safety catches that assist in positioning the wings.
[E05D 13/06] with notches for vertically sliding wings -- E05D 13/06 covers accessories for vertically sliding doors or windows that incorporate notches to guide or retain the moving wing. The notches may be integrated in pulleys, tracks, safety catches or other suspension devices that aid in positioning, lifting or securing the wing during vertical travel.
[E05D 13/08] acting by friction for vertically sliding wings -- The code E05D 13/08 covers devices that employ friction to control the movement of vertically sliding doors, windows or wings. These accessories, such as friction rollers or dampers, are mounted on the wing to regulate sliding speed, provide holding torque and prevent undesired motion. They are part of the broader family of hinges, suspension and locking mechanisms for fixed constructions.
[E05D 13/10] Counterbalance devices -- A counterbalance device is an accessory for sliding or lifting doors, windows or wing sections that helps balance the weight of the moving element, often using pulleys, springs or safety catches. It is classified under Fixed Constructions → Locks → Hinges or suspension devices for doors, windows or wings → Accessories for sliding or lifting wings. The CPC code for this entry is E05D 13/10.
[E05D 13/12] with springs -- This subclass covers accessories for sliding or lifting door or window wings-such as pulleys, safety catches, and other hardware-that incorporate springs to provide tension, assist opening, or retain positions, distinguishing them from non-spring versions of similar devices.
[E05D 13/1207] with tension springs -- E05D 13/1207 relates to accessories for sliding or lifting door, window or wing panels that incorporate tension springs, such as pulleys or safety catches, to assist opening or closing. The devices belong to the hinge or suspension category of fixed constructions and are used to control the movement of moving wings.
[E05D 13/1215] specially adapted for overhead wings  E05D13/1223  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Spring safety catches in counterbalance devices for sliding or lifting wings -> E05D13/1223
[E05D 13/1223] Spring safety devices -- The CPC entry E05D 13/1223 covers spring-based safety devices that are fitted to sliding or lifting door, window or wing assemblies. These accessories, such as spring catches or dampers, help control or restrain movement, preventing accidental opening or closing and enhancing user safety. They are part of the broader group of hinges and suspension devices for doors and windows.
[E05D 13/123] with compression springs -- The code E05D 13/123 designates accessories for sliding or lifting doors, windows or wings that employ compression springs, such as spring-loaded pulleys, safety catches or other devices that use the stored energy of a compressed spring to assist opening, closing or holding the moving element.
[E05D 13/1238] specially adapted for overhead wings  E05D13/1246  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Spring safety catches in counterbalance devices for sliding or lifting wings -> E05D13/1246
[E05D 13/1246] Spring safety devices -- E05D 13/1246 relates to spring-actuated safety devices that are fitted as accessories to sliding or lifting doors, windows or wings, such as pulleys and safety catches designed to prevent unintended movement. These components belong to the fixed-construction group for hinges or suspension devices.
[E05D 13/1253] with canted-coil torsion springs -- This subclass defines accessories for sliding or lifting door, window or wing assemblies that use canted-coil torsion springs to provide tension, return or safety functions such as pulleys or catches.
[E05D 13/1261] specially adapted for overhead wings  E05D13/1269  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Spring safety catches in counterbalance devices for sliding or lifting wings -> E05D13/1269
[E05D 13/1269] Spring safety devices -- The code E05D 13/1269 covers spring-actuated safety devices that are mounted as accessories for sliding or lifting wing sections such as doors, windows or panels. These mechanisms, including spring-loaded pulleys and catches, are designed to hold the wing in a closed position and release it safely under controlled conditions.
[E05D 13/1276] with coiled ribbon springs, e.g. constant force springs  E05D13/1253  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Canted-coil torsion springs -> E05D13/1253
[E05D 13/1284] specially adapted for overhead wings  E05D13/1292  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Spring safety catches in counterbalance devices for sliding or lifting wings -> E05D13/1292
[E05D 13/1292] Spring safety devices -- E05D 13/1292 designates spring-operated safety devices that are fitted to sliding or lifting doors, windows or other movable wings. These devices, such as spring-loaded catches, hold the wing in place and release it under a predetermined load to prevent accidental opening. They are classified under fixed constructions, locks and hinges or suspension devices.
[E05D 13/14] with weights -- This CPC subclass covers devices that use weights as counterbalancing elements in accessories for sliding or lifting doors, windows or wing sections, such as weighted pulleys or safety catches. It falls under fixed constructions, locks, hinges or suspension devices for doors, windows or wings.
[E05D 13/145] specially adapted for overhead wings -- E05D 13/145 classifies accessories specially adapted for overhead wings, such as sliding or lifting doors and windows, and includes devices like pulleys, safety catches and other mechanisms that assist movement or secure the position of overhead sections.
[E05D 15/00] Suspension arrangements for wings -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Arrangements of doors, windows, flaps or other wings, characterised by the construction of the suspending or supporting means; suspending or supporting means for doors, windows, flaps or other wings producing wing motions other than simple pivoting, e.g. revolving, sliding, combined or alternative motions.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Hinges per se -> E05D3/00 , E05D7/00
[E05D 15/02] for revolving wings -- E05D 15/02 covers suspension arrangements for revolving wings, such as the mechanisms that support and guide rotating door or window wings. It includes guide tracks, rollers, carriers and other devices that enable the wings to turn smoothly while staying securely suspended. The classification falls under fixed constructions, locks, and hinges or suspension devices for doors, windows or wings.
[E05D 15/04] with arms fixed on the wing pivoting about an axis outside of the wing -- This subclass covers door, window or wing hinges where the connecting arms are permanently attached to the moving wing and swing around an axis that is located outside the wing itself, providing a fixed-pivot suspension arrangement.
[E05D 15/06] for wings sliding horizontally more or less in their own plane -- The E05D 15/06 subclass covers suspension arrangements that enable wings-such as door or window panels-to slide horizontally within their own plane. It pertains to mechanisms that guide and support the wing's motion while keeping the overall structure fixed.
