[E21B 33/072] for cable-operated tools -- E21B 33/072 covers sealing or packing of boreholes or wells using cable-operated tools. It belongs to the fixed-construction earth-drilling group, specifically for rock or earth drilling applications where a cable mechanism drives the sealing device.
[E21B 33/076] specially adapted for underwater installations -- The E21B 33/076 entry covers sealing or packing solutions for boreholes and wells that are specifically designed for underwater installations, such as subsea drilling or offshore wellheads. It includes methods, materials and devices that can withstand water pressure, corrosion and marine conditions to ensure a reliable seal in submerged environments.
[E21B 33/08] Wipers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Wipers, oil savers and stuffing boxes.
[E21B 33/085] Rotatable packing means, e.g. rotating blow-out preventers -- This CPC subclass covers devices that provide a rotating sealing mechanism for boreholes or wells, such as rotating blow-out preventers used in drilling operations. The technology enables continuous packing around the drill-string while allowing rotation to maintain well control. It is classified under fixed constructions for earth drilling.
[E21B 33/10] in the borehole -- E21B 33/10 covers methods and devices for sealing-or-packing a borehole during or after earth or rock drilling, such as using cement, polymers, or other materials to protect the borehole from collapse, fluid loss, or contamination. The classification applies to techniques used in wells, shafts, and similar underground openings.
[E21B 33/12] Packers -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Packers, tools lowered with a reduced diameter and expanded by various means to establish an annular seal between an outer tubular or wall and an inner tubular:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Bridging plugs for cementing -> E21B33/134 - Using plugs for isolating cement charge -> E21B33/16
[E21B 33/1204] permanent; drillable -- A permanent, drillable seal or packing used to close a borehole or well, which can later be re-opened by drilling. It covers materials, structures or methods that provide long-term isolation yet allow subsequent drilling without removing the seal. This classification is applied to technologies that combine sealing performance with drillability for earth-or-rock drilling applications.
[E21B 33/1208] characterised by the construction of the sealing or packing means  E21B33/1277  takes precedence -- E21B 33/1208 covers fixed constructions used in earth or rock drilling that focus on the design of sealing or packing means for boreholes or wells, such as packers, plugs, or other barrier devices. The subclass is subordinated to E21B 33/1277, which takes precedence.
[E21B 33/1212] including a metal-to-metal seal element -- This subgroup deals with sealing or packing boreholes or wells in earth-drilling operations where the seal includes a metal-to-metal element that mates with another metal surface to prevent fluid leakage. It applies to metal-based seals used in rock-drilling equipment for well integrity.
[E21B 33/1216] Anti-extrusion means, e.g. means to prevent cold flow of rubber packing -- This classification covers devices integrated into borehole or well seals that restrict the outward movement of rubber packing under pressure, preventing cold flow or extrusion of the material. Such anti-extrusion means may be metal or polymer inserts, ribs, or other restraining structures designed to maintain seal integrity during drilling operations.
[E21B 33/122] Multiple string packers -- Multiple string packers are devices used in wellbore operations to isolate several distinct zones simultaneously by setting a series of packer elements on a single completion string, allowing independent control of fluid flow and pressure in each section of the borehole. This technology is employed in drilling and completing boreholes or wells to improve zonal isolation and enhance production or injection efficiency.
[E21B 33/124] Units with longitudinally-spaced plugs for isolating the intermediate space -- Units comprising a series of plugs arranged at intervals along a tube or pipe, designed to seal off and isolate the space between them within a borehole or well. They are used in earth-drilling and rock-drilling operations to provide controlled, longitudinal sealing of intermediate sections of the drilling string.
[E21B 33/1243] with inflatable sleeves -- The CPC subclass E21B 33/1243 covers sealing or packing of boreholes or wells in earth or rock drilling by means of inflatable sleeves. It includes devices or methods that insert a sleeve into the borehole and expand it with fluid pressure to create a tight seal, useful for temporary or permanent well abandonment or protection.
[E21B 33/1246] inflated by down-hole pumping means operated by a pipe string -- This subclass of E21B 33/1246 covers methods for inflating sealing or packing elements within boreholes using a pumping device positioned downhole that is driven by a pipe string. The technique is used in earth drilling to create expandable packers or plugs that are activated once pump pressure is supplied through the pipe.
[E21B 33/126] with fluid-pressure-operated elastic cup or skirt -- E21B 33/126 covers methods and devices for sealing or packing boreholes or wells in earth-or-rock drilling, specifically using an elastic cup or skirt that expands under fluid pressure to form a tight seal around the drill string. This fluid-pressure-operated element provides adaptable sealing for varying borehole diameters.
[E21B 33/1265] with mechanical slips -- This subclass covers fixed-construction solutions for earth or rock drilling in which boreholes or wells are sealed by means of mechanical slips that expand or lock against the hole wall, such as slip-type packers or similar devices used to secure the seal during drilling operations.
[E21B 33/127] with inflatable sleeve -- E21B 33/127 relates to sealing or packing boreholes or wells in earth-or-rock drilling by means of an inflatable sleeve, describing devices or methods that expand a sleeve within the borehole to provide temporary or permanent isolation during construction or servicing.
[E21B 33/1272] inflated by down-hole pumping means operated by a pipe string -- E21B 33/1272 pertains to fixed earth-drilling constructions that seal or pack boreholes or wells by inflating a down-hole element. The inflation is achieved with a pumping system operated through a pipe string, allowing the sealing device to expand against the borehole wall for fluid containment or stability.
[E21B 33/1275] inflated by down-hole pumping means operated by a down-hole drive -- E21B 33/1275 covers devices that inflate a sealing or packing element in a borehole by means of a pump located down-hole and powered by a down-hole drive. The classification applies to inflated packers or similar formations used to seal or pack boreholes during earth or rock drilling operations. It includes the mechanisms for delivering the inflation fluid and the drive system that operates the pump within the drilled hole.
[E21B 33/1277] characterised by the construction or fixation of the sleeve -- The E21B 33/1277 subclass covers earth-or-rock drilling technologies that seal or pack boreholes or wells by means of a sleeve whose construction or fixation is the defining feature, detailing how the sleeve is attached, locked or otherwise secured within the borehole to provide a reliable seal. This falls under the broader hierarchy of fixed constructions for earth drilling.
[E21B 33/128] with a member expanded radially by axial pressure -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Multiple string packers -> E21B33/122 - Units with longitudinally-spaced plugs for isolating the intermediate space -> E21B33/124
[E21B 33/1285] by fluid pressure -- This classification covers methods of sealing or packing boreholes or wells using fluid pressure, a technique employed in fixed-construction earth drilling operations.
[E21B 33/129] with mechanical slips for hooking into the casing -- E21B 33/129 pertains to fixed earth-drilling constructions that seal or pack boreholes or wells by employing mechanical slips that hook onto the casing. The slips provide a removable, pressure-tight connection to secure packer or pipe sections during drilling or completion operations.
[E21B 33/1291] anchor set by wedge or cam in combination with frictional effect, using so-called drag-blocks  E21B33/1295  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass (E21B 33/1291) describes anchors that are set in boreholes using a wedge or cam together with a frictional effect, typically employing drag-blocks, to seal or pack earth-or-rock drilling wells; it takes precedence over the broader E21B33/1295 group.
[E21B 33/1292] with means for anchoring against downward and upward movement -- E21B 33/1292 pertains to earth-or-rock drilling and the sealing or packing of boreholes and wells, specifically using means that anchor the sealing material to prevent both downward and upward movement. This subclass falls under Fixed Constructions → Earth Drilling → Earth or Rock Drilling → Sealing or packing boreholes or wells.
[E21B 33/1293] with means for anchoring against downward and upward movement  E21B33/1291 ,  E21B33/1295  take precedence -- This CPC subclass deals with devices used to seal or pack boreholes or wells that include anchoring features preventing both downward and upward displacement. It falls under fixed constructions, earth drilling, and earth or rock drilling. The subclass supersedes the more general E21B33/1291 and E21B33/1295 categories.
[E21B 33/1294] characterised by a valve, e.g. a by-pass valve -- The subclass E21B 33/1294 pertains to earth-or-rock drilling equipment that includes a valve, such as a bypass valve, to control fluid flow or pressure during sealing or packing of boreholes or wells. It covers designs where the valve is integral to the sealing arrangement used in fixed-construction drilling systems.
[E21B 33/1295] actuated by fluid pressure -- This class covers fixed earth-drilling constructions in which sealing or packing of boreholes or wells is performed by fluid-pressure actuation, such as hydraulic pistons or pressure-activated packers that close or protect drilled holes. It is a subcategory of earth or rock drilling sealing methods within the E21B 33/12 series.
[E21B 33/12955] using drag blocks frictionally engaging the inner wall of the well -- The subclass E21B 33/12955 relates to earth-or-rock drilling techniques that seal or pack boreholes by inserting drag blocks which press against the well's inner wall, creating friction to hold the blocks in place and provide a sealing action. This method is used to control the flow of fluids and stabilize the wellbore during drilling or completion operations.
[E21B 33/13] Methods or devices for cementing, for plugging holes, crevices or the like -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Tools for plugging the bore or a well, and to methods for cementing parts of a wellbore, for cementing a casing in the wellbore and similar.  Illustrative example of the subject matter classified in this place:
[E21B 33/134] Bridging plugs -- E21B 33/134, Bridging plugs, pertains to devices used in sealing or packing boreholes or wells during earth drilling, specifically to temporarily or permanently bridge gaps in the drill string or wellbore to allow safe operations.
[E21B 33/136] Baskets, e.g. of umbrella type -- The entry covers basket-type devices, such as umbrella-shaped tools, used to seal or pack boreholes and wells during earth or rock drilling operations. These baskets are inserted into the drill hole and expanded to close gaps, support casing, or prevent fluid loss.
[E21B 33/138] Plastering the borehole wall -- E21B 33/138 covers the practice of plastering the wall of a borehole during earth or rock drilling, a method used to seal, protect, or stabilize the borehole after drilling. It falls under fixed constructions for sealing or packing boreholes or wells.
[E21B 33/14] for cementing casings into boreholes -- The CPC subclass E21B 33/14 covers methods and apparatus for sealing or packing boreholes by cementing casings, typically used in earth-drilling operations to secure the well lining and prevent fluid migration. It includes techniques for placing and hardening cement around the casing within the drilled hole. This subclass falls under fixed constructions for earth or rock drilling.
[E21B 33/143] for underwater installations -- The E21B 33/143 subclass covers sealing or packing techniques specifically designed for boreholes or wells that are part of underwater installations, such as subsea oil or gas drilling sites. It includes devices, materials and methods that ensure a watertight seal against external pressure and fluid ingress. This subclass falls under Fixed Constructions → Earth Drilling → Earth or Rock Drilling → Sealing or packing boreholes or wells.
[E21B 33/146] Stage cementing, i.e. discharging cement from casing at different levels -- E21B 33/146 designates stage cementing, the method of discharging cement from a casing at several predetermined depths to seal individual sections of a borehole or well. It is employed in earth-or-rock drilling to provide selective sealing or packing of the drilled hole.
[E21B 33/16] using plugs for isolating cement charge -- E21B 33/16 relates to using plugs to isolate a cement charge during earth or rock drilling, allowing the cement to be placed in a specific section of a borehole or well while preventing it from flowing into unwanted zones. The plugs are positioned in the drill string or borehole to create a sealed segment for controlled cementing operations.
[E21B 33/165] Cementing plugs specially adapted for being released down-hole  cementing heads  E21B33/05 -- The E21B 33/165 subclass covers cementing plugs that are specially designed to be released down-hole within cementing heads during earth or rock drilling operations. These plugs are used to seal or pack boreholes and wells, facilitating controlled cement placement.
[E21B 33/167] Cementing plugs provided with anti-rotation mechanisms, e.g. for easier drill-out -- E21B 33/167 pertains to cementing plugs that incorporate anti-rotation devices to facilitate removal after setting, typically used in sealing or packing boreholes or wells during earth drilling operations. The classification falls under fixed constructions for earth or rock drilling.
[E21B 34/00] Valve arrangements for boreholes or wells -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Constructive details of valves specifically adapted for use at the surface of a wellbore or in the interior of a wellbore.  Illustrative example of the subject matter classified in this place:
[E21B 34/02] in well heads -- This subclass covers valve arrangements that are built into well heads for earth or rock drilling, focusing on the control of fluid flow, pressure and safety at the surface connection. It includes designs for shut-off, circulation and injection valves integrated in the well-head assembly.
[E21B 34/025] Chokes or valves in wellheads and sub-sea wellheads for variably regulating fluid flow -- The entry covers choke and valve assemblies installed in wellheads and subsea wellheads that allow the flow of drilling fluids or production fluids to be varied during operation. These devices are used in earth-or-rock drilling contexts to control pressure, prevent surges and manage fluid rates in boreholes or wells.
[E21B 34/04] in underwater well heads -- A classification for valve arrangements installed in underwater well heads, covering devices that control flow and pressure at the seabed entry of drilling or production wells. It pertains to fixed constructions used in earth or rock drilling where the wellhead is submerged, ensuring safe operation under water.
[E21B 34/045] adapted to be lowered on a tubular string into position within a blow-out preventer stack, e.g. so-called test trees -- This subclass concerns valve assemblies for boreholes or wells that are designed to be lowered on a tubular string and placed inside a blow-out preventer stack, such as the test-tree arrangements used during drilling operations.
[E21B 34/06] in wells -- E21B 34/06 refers to valve arrangements installed in wells as part of fixed drilling constructions, covering devices that control fluid flow, pressure or circulation within boreholes during earth or rock drilling operations.
[E21B 34/063] Valve or closure with destructible element, e.g. frangible disc  E21B34/103  takes precedence -- E21B 34/063 relates to valve or closure devices fitted in boreholes or wells that contain a destructible component, such as a frangible disc, which ruptures at a set pressure to provide safety or pressure-release functions in fixed-construction earth-drilling equipment.
[E21B 34/066] electrically actuated -- E21B 34/066 refers to valve arrangements for boreholes or wells that are electrically actuated, used in fixed-construction earth-drilling equipment. It covers designs where electrical signals control opening, closing or throttling of drilling-fluid or cementing valves for precise operation.
[E21B 34/08] responsive to flow or pressure of the fluid obtained -- The E21B 34/08 subclass covers valve systems for boreholes or wells that automatically react to the flow rate or pressure of the extracted fluid, such as pressure-sensing or flow-controlled shut-off devices used in earth-drilling operations.
[E21B 34/085] with time-delay systems, e.g. hydraulic impedance mechanisms -- The code E21B 34/085 designates valve systems for boreholes or wells that incorporate time-delay mechanisms, such as hydraulic-impedance devices, to regulate fluid flow during drilling operations. It is a subcategory of fixed constructions for earth or rock drilling.
[E21B 34/10] operated by control fluid supplied from outside the borehole -- This subclass covers valve systems for boreholes or wells that are actuated by a control fluid introduced from outside the borehole rather than from within it. Such valves are used in drilling and production to open, close or modulate flow without requiring mechanical linkage downhole.
[E21B 34/101] with means for equalizing fluid pressure above and below the valve -- This subclass covers drilling rigs where the downhole valve includes a mechanism that balances the fluid pressure above and below the valve, preventing differential forces during operation. It is used in fixed-construction earth drilling equipment to stabilise flow and improve safety.
[E21B 34/102] with means for locking the closing element in open or closed position  E21B34/105  and  E21B34/108  take precedence -- A subclass of valve arrangements for boreholes or wells used in earth or rock drilling, this code covers valves that incorporate mechanisms to lock the closing element in either the open or closed position, ensuring secure operation during drilling and well servicing. It refines broader fixed-construction drilling categories by specifying locking features for improved safety and control.
[E21B 34/103] with a shear pin -- This subclass covers valve arrangements for boreholes or wells that incorporate a shear pin as a safety or release mechanism, providing a controlled breaking point to protect drilling equipment. It is situated under fixed constructions for earth drilling, specifically earth or rock drilling applications.
[E21B 34/105] retrievable, e.g. wire line retrievable, i.e. with an element which can be landed into a landing-nipple provided with a passage for control fluid -- E21B 34/105 covers valve arrangements for earth-drilling boreholes that are designed to be retrievable, such as by wire-line, using an element that can be seated in a landing nipple provided with a passage for control fluid.
[E21B 34/106] the retrievable element being a secondary control fluid actuated valve landed into the bore of a first inoperative control fluid actuated valve -- In CPC class E21B 34/106 the focus is on valve arrangements for boreholes or wells in earth drilling, specifically a secondary fluid-actuated valve that can be retrieved and installed within the bore of a first control-fluid valve that has become inoperative, enabling re-activation or replacement without removing the entire valve assembly.
[E21B 34/107] the retrievable element being an operating or controlling means retrievable separately from the closure member, e.g. pilot valve landed into a side pocket  E21B34/106  takes precedence -- This subclass covers valve arrangements in boreholes or wells where the operating or controlling component, such as a pilot valve, is designed to be removed separately from the main closure element, often being placed in a side pocket of the drilling equipment. The separate retrieval allows maintenance or replacement without disturbing the primary sealing device.
[E21B 34/108] with time delay systems, e.g. hydraulic impedance mechanisms -- E21B 34/108 covers valve arrangements for boreholes or wells that incorporate time-delay systems such as hydraulic impedance mechanisms, used in earth and rock drilling operations. These devices control fluid flow by delaying opening or closing of the valve to improve drilling efficiency or safety.
[E21B 34/12] operated by movement of casings or tubings -- E21B 34/12 classifies valve arrangements used in boreholes or wells that are actuated by the motion of casings or tubings during earth-or-rock drilling. These devices employ the downward or upward movement of pipe strings to open or close a valve, providing a simple mechanical means of controlling fluid flow in drilling operations.
[E21B 34/125] with time delay systems, e.g. hydraulic impedance mechanisms -- A valve arrangement for boreholes or wells that incorporates a time-delay system, such as a hydraulic impedance mechanism, to regulate fluid flow and pressure during drilling. The design provides controlled opening or closing actions, improving safety and efficiency in earth-drilling operations.
[E21B 34/14] operated by movement of tools, e.g. sleeve valves operated by pistons or wire line tools -- This subclass relates to valve arrangements in boreholes or wells that are actuated by the mechanical movement of drilling tools, such as pistons or wire-line devices, which open or close the valve as the tool moves. It applies to fixed-construction earth-drilling equipment used for drilling into soil or rock.
[E21B 34/142] unsupported or free-falling elements, e.g. balls, plugs, darts or pistons -- The code E21B 34/142 covers valve arrangements for boreholes or wells that use unsupported or free-falling components such as balls, plugs, darts or pistons to open or close the passage. These elements move solely under gravity or fluid pressure without mechanical support within the conduit. This subclass falls under earth-drilling fixed constructions for rock drilling.
[E21B 34/16] Control means therefor being outside the borehole -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for controlling valves in wellheads, downhole valves, blow out preventers.
[E21B 35/00] Methods or apparatus for preventing or extinguishing fires -- E21B 35/00 covers methods or apparatus used in earth drilling operations to prevent or extinguish fires. It falls under the fixed constructions group for earth or rock drilling, addressing fire safety measures in drilling equipment and sites.
[E21B 36/00] Heating, cooling or insulating arrangements for boreholes or wells, e.g. for use in permafrost zones -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Equipment for heating or cooling a wellbore, either at the surface or from within the wellbore.  Heating or cooling fluid produced or treatment fluids in the wellbore.  Illustrative example of the subject matter classified in this place:
[E21B 36/001] Cooling arrangements -- Cooling arrangements for boreholes or wells, especially in permafrost zones, are classified under E21B 36/001. The code is part of the fixed constructions hierarchy for earth drilling and covers heating, cooling or insulating installations for boreholes or wells.
[E21B 36/003] Insulating arrangements -- E21B 36/003 covers insulating arrangements for boreholes or wells produced by earth or rock drilling, such as heating, cooling or thermal protection systems used in permafrost zones or similar environments to maintain desired temperature conditions within the drilled cavity.
[E21B 36/005] Heater surrounding production tube
[E21B 36/006] Combined heating and pumping means -- Combined heating and pumping means are integrated devices that simultaneously provide thermal energy and fluid circulation in boreholes or wells, typically used to warm the surrounding ground in permafrost regions or to assist drilling operations. They are installed as part of fixed earth-drilling constructions and serve both heating and pumping functions in a single unit.
[E21B 36/008] using chemical heat generating means -- This subgroup covers methods that generate heat in boreholes or wells by means of exothermic chemical reactions, such as portable heat packs, self-heating fluids or thermite mixtures, to raise temperature and prevent freezing of the surrounding formation. It is intended for heating, cooling or insulating applications in cold environments like permafrost zones.
[E21B 36/02] using burners -- This subclass covers the use of burners to heat boreholes or wells, providing thermal management for earth or rock drilling in permafrost or similar environments. It falls under fixed constructions for earth drilling and addresses heating, cooling or insulating arrangements for boreholes.
[E21B 36/025] the burners being above ground or outside the bore hole -- E21B 36/025, a CPC subgroup within Fixed Constructions > Earth Drilling > Earth or Rock Drilling, pertains to heating, cooling or insulating arrangements for boreholes or wells where the burners are located above-ground or outside the borehole rather than inside it.
[E21B 36/04] using electrical heaters -- The E21B 36/04 classification covers the use of electrical heaters in borehole or well installations to provide heating, cooling or insulation, especially in permafrost or similar environments. It is part of the earth-drilling subclass dealing with fixed constructions for earth or rock drilling.
[E21B 37/00] Methods or apparatus for cleaning boreholes or wells -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods or apparatus for cleaning boreholes or wells. Cleaning by physical or chemical action.  Illustrative example of the subject matter classified in this place:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Methods or apparatus for flushing boreholes, e.g. by use of exhaust air from motor -> E21B21/00
[E21B 37/02] Scrapers specially adapted therefor -- E21B 37/02 is a CPC subclass within fixed constructions for earth-drilling, specifically covering methods or apparatus for cleaning boreholes or wells. It pertains to scrapers specially adapted for this purpose, designed to remove debris, scale or rock cuttings from the interior walls of wells during drilling or work-over operations.
[E21B 37/04] operated by fluid pressure, e.g. free-piston scrapers -- E21B 37/04 covers methods and apparatus for cleaning boreholes or wells that are driven by fluid pressure, such as free-piston scrapers. This subclass falls under the earth-drilling hierarchy, specifically Earth or Rock Drilling, and addresses techniques that use hydraulic forces to remove debris from drilled holes.
[E21B 37/045] Free-piston scrapers -- Free-piston scrapers are devices that clean boreholes or wells by employing a piston that moves freely within a housing to scrape and remove cuttings from the walls of drilled holes. They belong to the fixed-construction category of earth-or-rock drilling equipment and are used to maintain borehole integrity during or after drilling operations.
[E21B 37/06] using chemical means for preventing or limiting the deposition of paraffins or like substances -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The use of chemical means for preventing, limiting, or removing substances such as paraffins and filter cakes from the wellbore.
[E21B 37/08] cleaning or gravel packs -- E21B 37/08 covers techniques and equipment for cleaning boreholes or wells and for removing or replacing gravel packs used in earth or rock drilling operations. It includes methods to flush, scrub, or otherwise clear drilling fluids and debris from the drilled hole to maintain flow and integrity. The class falls under fixed constructions for earth drilling.
[E21B 37/10] Well swabs -- The E21B 37/10 "Well swabs" class covers tools or methods for cleaning boreholes or wells, usually by lowering a swab, brush or similar device into the drilled hole to remove cuttings, mud or fluids after drilling. It resides under Fixed Constructions → Earth Drilling → Earth or Rock Drilling → Methods or apparatus for cleaning boreholes or wells.
[E21B 4/00] Drives for drilling, used in the borehole -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Drives located inside the borehole for powering drilling units.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:

[E21B 40/001] in the borehole  anchoring tools in the borehole  E21B23/01 -- The code E21B 40/001 belongs to fixed constructions for earth drilling and covers anchoring tools placed in the borehole, such as automatic tubing catchers that stop a falling oil-well string. This sub-class falls under E21B23/01 for borehole anchoring devices.
[E21B 4/003] Bearing, sealing, lubricating details  for roller bits  E21B10/22 -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Bearing, lubrication or sealing details for roller bits -> E21B10/22
[E21B 4/006] Mechanical motion converting means, e.g. reduction gearings  E21B4/10  takes precedence -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Continuous unidirectional rotary motion of shaft or drilling pipe effecting consecutive impacts -> E21B4/10
[E21B 4/02] Fluid rotary type drives -- Fluid-rotary type drives are mechanisms that use hydraulic fluid to transmit power and rotate the drill bit, providing continuous rotation for earth or rock drilling in a borehole. They are fixed installations that deliver consistent torque and speed control, typically integrated with pumps and control valves.
[E21B 4/04] Electric drives -- E21B 4/04 relates to electric drives employed in fixed-construction earth-or-rock drilling systems, specifically those powering the drilling mechanism within a borehole. The code falls under the CPC section for fixed constructions, earth drilling, and rock drilling, and covers the motor and drive assemblies that provide rotational force for the drill bit.
[E21B 4/06] Down-hole impacting means, e.g. hammers -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Boring rams -> E21B11/02
[E21B 4/08] impact being obtained by gravity only, e.g. with lost-motion connection -- A drive for drilling where the striking force is produced only by the weight of the drill string, typically using a lost-motion connection that releases under gravity to generate impact. This subclass concerns gravity-driven impact mechanisms within fixed-construction earth-drilling equipment.
[E21B 4/10] continuous unidirectional rotary motion of shaft or drilling pipe effecting consecutive impacts -- The E21B 4/10 subclass covers drive systems for fixed earth-drilling that provide a continuous unidirectional rotary motion of the shaft or drilling pipe, converting that motion into successive hammer-like impacts to advance the bit in rock or soil.
[E21B 41/00] Equipment or details not covered by groups -- **Definition:** This place covers:
[E21B 41/0007] for underwater installations  E21B41/005 ,  E21B41/04 ,  E21B41/06 ,  E21B41/08 ,  E21B41/10  take precedence
[E21B 41/0014] Underwater well locating or reentry systems -- The CPC subclass E21B 41/0014 covers equipment and methods for locating or re-entering wells that are submerged underwater, typically used in offshore drilling operations. It includes positioning, acoustic, or sensor systems that enable precise well detection and safe re-entry, falling under the broader category of earth or rock drilling equipment not otherwise specified.
[E21B 41/0021] Safety devices, e.g. for preventing small objects from falling into the borehole -- This CPC subclass covers safety devices for earth-drilling equipment that prevent small tools, debris or other objects from falling into the borehole. It includes mechanisms such as guards, screens, chutes or catchers intended to protect the drill string and the work area.
[E21B 41/0035] Apparatus or methods for multilateral well technology, e.g. for the completion of or workover on wells with one or more lateral branches -- E21B 41/0035 covers apparatus and methods used in multilateral well technology, such as tools and techniques for completing or work-overing wells that have one or more lateral branches extending from a main borehole. It pertains to fixed-construction drilling equipment not classified elsewhere in the earth-drilling subclasses.
[E21B 41/0042] characterised by sealing the junction between a lateral and a main bore -- E21B 41/0042 covers earth-drilling equipment or methods that seal the junction between a main borehole and a lateral bore, preventing fluid leakage and stabilizing the connection during drilling operations.
[E21B 41/005] Waste disposal systems -- E21B 41/005 covers waste-disposal systems used in fixed-construction earth-drilling equipment for drilling rock or soil, specifically those components that are not classified elsewhere. The entry is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification for both the EPO and USPTO, nested under Fixed Constructions → Earth Drilling → Earth or Rock Drilling → Equipment or details not covered by groups. It defines the technical features of apparatus for handling and removing drilling waste.
[E21B 41/0057] Disposal of a fluid by injection into a subterranean formation -- The code E21B 41/0057 covers apparatus or methods for disposing of drilling fluids, cuttings or other liquids by injecting them directly into an underground formation, such as a geologic stratum or a borehole, during or after drilling operations. It includes systems that manage flow, pressure and sealing to ensure safe, controlled placement of the fluid beneath the earth's surface.
[E21B 41/0064] Carbon dioxide sequestration  storing fluids in porous layers  B65G5/005
[E21B 41/0071] Adaptation of flares, e.g. arrangements of flares in offshore installations  flares of waste gases or noxious gases  F23G7/08 -- E21B 41/0071 covers the design and integration of flare systems on offshore drilling installations, focusing on arrangements for combusting waste or noxious gases released during earth-or-rock drilling operations. It addresses adaptations of flares that are not covered by other detailed groups within the fixed-construction field.
[E21B 41/0078] Nozzles used in boreholes  drilling by liquid or gas jets  E21B7/18 ; drill bits with nozzles  E21B10/60 ; perforators using direct fluid action  E21B43/114 ; obtaining a slurry of minerals using nozzles  E21B43/29 -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Drilling by liquid or gas jets -> E21B7/18 - Drill bits with nozzle -> E21B10/60 - Perforators using direct fluid action -> E21B43/114 - Obtaining a slurry of minerals using nozzles -> E21B43/29
[E21B 41/0085] Adaptations of electric power generating means for use in boreholes -- E21B 41/0085 covers modifications to electric power generators that allow them to operate inside boreholes, such as specialized housings, cooling systems, and power transmission adaptations for the confined, high-pressure environment of drilling rigs. It addresses solutions not covered by other detailed groups within earth-or-rock drilling equipment.
[E21B 41/0099] specially adapted for drilling for or production of natural hydrate or clathrate gas reservoirs; Drilling through or monitoring of formations containing gas hydrates or clathrates -- This subclass covers tools and methods specially adapted for drilling into or producing natural gas-filled hydrate or clathrate reservoirs, including equipment for monitoring and traversing formations that contain these gas-filled ice structures.
[E21B 41/02] in situ  inhibition of corrosion in boreholes or wells -- E21B 41/02 covers techniques for preventing corrosion inside drilled boreholes or wells by applying corrosion-inhibiting agents directly in the hole during or after drilling. The class is nested under fixed constructions, earth drilling, earth or rock drilling, and equipment or details not covered by other groups.
[E21B 41/04] Manipulators for underwater operations, e.g. temporarily connected to well heads -- This CPC subclass covers devices used to handle tools or components underwater, such as manipulators that can be attached temporarily to wellheads during offshore drilling. They enable operations like connecting, disconnecting or servicing equipment on the seabed.
[E21B 41/06] Work chambers for underwater operations, e.g. temporarily connected to well heads -- The code E21B 41/06 covers work chambers designed for underwater drilling tasks that can be temporarily connected to well heads, providing a sealed environment for personnel and equipment during subsurface operations. These chambers are used in fixed-construction earth-drilling and earth-or-rock drilling applications.
[E21B 41/08] Underwater guide bases, e.g. drilling templates -- E21B 41/08 covers devices used as guide bases for drilling operations performed beneath water, such as templates that position and stabilize drill strings or other equipment on the seabed. These structures provide a stable reference for earth or rock drilling in offshore environments and are not covered by other more specific groups.
[E21B 41/10] Guide posts, e.g. releasable -- E21B 41/10 refers to guide posts used on earth-or-rock drilling equipment, especially those that can be released or repositioned to align the drill string. The subgroup covers fixed-construction components not classified elsewhere, such as removable or adjustable guide posts that assist in steering and stabilising the drill during operation.
[E21B 4/12] Electrically operated hammers -- E21B 4/12 covers electrically powered hammer drives employed in fixed earth-or-rock drilling installations, describing the construction and operation of electric hammers that deliver percussive force to the drill bit within the borehole. It pertains to the drive mechanisms that convert electrical energy into the repeated blows needed for efficient drilling of rock and soil.
[E21B 4/14] Fluid operated hammers -- Fluid-operated hammers are drilling drives that use hydraulic or pneumatic pressure to generate percussive force for earth or rock drilling. They are employed to advance drill bits or rods in boreholes, providing high-impact energy to break material. The devices are typically mounted on drilling rigs and powered by fluid pressure sources.
[E21B 4/145] of the self propelled-type, e.g. with a reverse mode to retract the device from the hole -- E21B 4/145 covers self-propelled drilling drives that can advance the drill bit and also include a reverse mode to pull the device out of the borehole, a feature used in earth-or-rock drilling equipment for managing insertion and retraction of the drill.
[E21B 4/16] Plural down-hole drives, e.g. for combined percussion and rotary drilling -- E21B 4/16 pertains to plural down-hole drives installed in a borehole that provide both percussion and rotary motion for drilling. It falls under the fixed-construction earth-drilling group, specifically for earth or rock drilling systems that use multiple drives to power the drill bit.
[E21B 4/18] Anchoring or feeding in the borehole -- The E21B 4/18 entry defines devices and methods that secure or advance drilling equipment within a borehole, such as anchors, feed mechanisms, and support structures used during earth or rock drilling. It covers the means of holding the drill string in place, providing stabilization and enabling controlled insertion or removal of tools while drilling.
[E21B 4/20] combined with surface drive -- E21B 4/20 covers drilling drives that are operated in conjunction with a surface-mounted drive, linking the power source at the ground level to a downhole drive unit to provide a combined drive system for earth or rock drilling.

[E21B 43/017] Production satellite stations, i.e. underwater installations comprising a plurality of satellite well heads connected to a central station -- E21B 43/017 refers to production satellite stations, which are underwater installations that include several satellite well heads connected to a central processing unit. These systems are used in earth drilling operations to gather fluids from multiple wells and route them to a main station for further handling.
[E21B 43/0175] Hydraulic schemes for production manifolds -- A production manifold is a system that distributes drilling fluid or oil from a well to various processing points. This subclass details the hydraulic arrangements used to control flow, pressure and routing within such manifolds, including piping networks, valves, pumps and pressure-regulating devices that manage fluid extraction from earth-drilling operations.

[E21B 43/025] Consolidation of loose sand or the like round the wells without excessively decreasing the permeability thereof -- E21B 43/025 addresses methods for consolidating loose sand or similar material around a wellbore while limiting the reduction in permeability, thereby stabilising the formation without markedly impeding fluid flow.
[E21B 43/04] Gravelling of wells -- E21B 43/04 covers the gravelling of wells, i.e., the insertion of gravel or similar granular material into a borehole to stabilize its walls and support drilling or production operations. It falls under fixed constructions for earth or rock drilling, specifically methods for obtaining fluids from wells.
[E21B 43/045] Crossover tools -- Crossover tools are devices used in earth-or-rock drilling operations to enable the connection of drill strings, suction lines or other tubular components of differing dimensions or thread types, allowing seamless transition between sections while maintaining pressure control during the extraction of fluids from wells.

[E21B 43/086] Screens with preformed openings, e.g. slotted liners  comprising porous materials  E21B43/082 -- This CPC subclass concerns fixed-construction screens that feature preformed openings such as slotted liners and incorporate porous materials, employed in earth or rock drilling to filter and regulate the flow of fluids from wells.
[E21B 43/088] Wire screens  comprising porous materials  E21B43/082 ; comprising woven materials  E21B43/084 -- This subclass covers wire screens made from porous or woven materials that are employed in fixed earth- or rock-drilling equipment for extracting fluids from wells.
[E21B 43/10] Setting of casings, screens, liners in wells -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Setting and securing of casings, screens, liners or the like in wells.  Running in and installation of tubulars, expandable tubulars.  Illustrative example of the subject matter classified in this place:
[E21B 43/101] for underwater installations -- E21B 43/101 covers equipment, structures and methods used for obtaining fluids from wells that are installed underwater, such as subsea drilling rigs, wellheads and related control systems. It falls under the hierarchy of fixed constructions, earth drilling, earth or rock drilling, and specifically the sub-category of fluid extraction from wells.
[E21B 43/103] of expandable casings, screens, liners, or the like -- The subclass E21B 43/103 covers techniques for producing fluids from wells that employ expandable casings, screens, liners or similar devices. It includes methods for installing or expanding such elements within a drilled hole to improve flow control, filtration or reinforcement, typically used in fixed-construction drilling of earth or rock formations.
[E21B 43/105] Expanding tools specially adapted therefor -- Expanding tools specially adapted for earth-or-rock drilling are devices inserted into a borehole that inflate or extend after placement to engage the surrounding formation, secure casings, or enhance fluid flow from wells. They are used in fixed-construction drilling operations to improve wellbore stability and increase production efficiency.
[E21B 43/106] Couplings or joints therefor -- The code E21B 43/106 classifies the couplings, joints and related connecting devices used in fixed-construction earth-drilling systems for extracting fluids from wells. It covers mechanical links that join drill strings, rods, or other components of rock-drilling apparatus, ensuring alignment and transmission of torque and axial forces during operation.
[E21B 43/108] Expandable screens or perforated liners -- Expandable screens or perforated liners are removable, flexible filters installed in boreholes to control the flow of fluids while allowing sand or gravel to pass, commonly used in well completions and gravel-pack operations to protect the formation and maintain productivity.
[E21B 43/11] Perforators -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Perforators and guns used to create apertures in casing or formation in order to establish a fluid communication with the reservoir for the production of oil or gas. Included are details about construction, ignition, communication and placement.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[E21B 43/112] Perforators with extendable perforating members, e.g. actuated by fluid means -- The entry covers perforating tools used in fixed well constructions where the perforating members can be extended, typically by fluid pressure, to create passages through the surrounding rock or formation. Such extendable perforators are employed during the completion stage of oil- or gas-well drilling to connect the wellbore to the reservoir and enable fluid flow.
[E21B 43/114] Perforators using direct fluid action , e.g. abrasive jets -- This subclass covers devices that create perforations in rock or earth by directing high-pressure fluid streams, often mixed with abrasive particles, to cut or erode a hole. Such perforators are used in well completion to establish communication between the borehole and the surrounding formation for fluid production.
[E21B 43/116] Gun or shaped-charge perforators -- Gun or shaped-charge perforators are devices used in oil or gas wells to create openings through the well casing and surrounding rock by detonating a gun cartridge or a shaped explosive charge. They provide a rapid, controlled method for establishing communication between the wellbore and the reservoir, facilitating fluid flow during production or testing.
[E21B 43/117] Shaped-charge perforators -- E21B 43/117 concerns shaped-charge perforators used in fixed earth-drilling installations for well completion, where specially shaped explosive charges create precise openings in casing or surrounding rock to allow fluids to flow from the reservoir.
[E21B 43/118] characterised by lowering in vertical position and subsequent tilting to operating position -- The E21B 43/118 entry covers devices for obtaining fluids from wells in which the drilling assembly is first lowered in a vertical orientation and then pivoted into its working angle, allowing a fixed-construction drill to transition from transport to operational position.
[E21B 43/1185] Ignition systems -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Ignition systems, e.g. electrically-actuated.
[E21B 43/11852] hydraulically actuated -- The subclass E21B 43/11852 designates hydraulic actuation systems employed in fixed earth-or-rock drilling installations for the extraction of fluids from wells. It covers machines and mechanisms that use fluid pressure to power drilling or pumping tools in permanent drilling rigs.
[E21B 43/11855] mechanically actuated, e.g. by movement of a wireline or a drop-bar  E21B43/11852  takes precedence -- This CPC subclass concerns fixed drilling constructions for extracting fluids from wells where the downhole tool is mechanically actuated by the movement of a wireline or a drop-bar rather than hydraulic or electric power. It falls under Earth or rock drilling and is numbered E21B 43/11855, superseded by the broader subclass E21B 43/11852.
[E21B 43/11857] firing indication systems
[E21B 43/119] Details, e.g. for locating perforating place or direction -- The E21B 43/119 subclass covers specific techniques and apparatus for identifying the exact location and orientation of perforations in an earth-or-rock drilling operation, such as the placement and direction of holes made to extract fluids from wells. It includes methods for locating the perforating point and determining the optimal drilling angle.
[E21B 43/1193] Dropping perforation guns after gun actuation -- E21B 43/1193 covers the practice of releasing perforation guns from a deployment device after the gun has been fired, allowing the gun to fall within the wellbore to a predetermined position or to exit the drill string. The method is used in oil and gas well completion to create perforations in the surrounding formation for fluid flow.
[E21B 43/1195] Replacement of drilling mud; decrease of undesirable shock waves -- The code E21B 43/1195 pertains to methods for replacing drilling mud in earth-or-rock drilling operations in order to lessen undesirable shock waves generated during well fluid extraction. It covers techniques for swapping or supplementing drilling fluids with alternative substances or processes that reduce pressure pulses and vibrations, thereby improving well stability and safety.
[E21B 43/12] Methods or apparatus for controlling the flow of the obtained fluid to or in wells -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production pumps, lifting wellbore fluids, production chokes. [E21B 43/121] Lifting well fluids  monitoring of down-hole pump systems  E21B47/008 -- **Definition:** This place covers:  The use of fluid from outside the well, or tool or system to move obtained fluid to the surface.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Monitoring of down-hole pump systems -> E21B47/008
[E21B 43/122] Gas lift -- Gas lift is a well-production technique that injects gas into the tubing or annulus of an oil or gas well to reduce the hydrostatic pressure of the fluid column, thereby assisting the upward flow of crude or water to the surface. The method is employed in earth-or-rock drilling operations for obtaining fluids from wells.
[E21B 43/123] Gas lift valves -- E21B 43/123 designates gas-lift valves, components mounted in fixed well-head constructions that regulate the injection of gas to reduce the hydrostatic pressure of fluids in a well. These valves are used in earth-or-rock drilling systems for extracting oil or gas, enabling continuous flow of fluids from underground reservoirs.
[E21B 43/1235] characterised by electromagnetic actuation -- E21B 43/1235 covers drilling apparatus or techniques for extracting fluids from wells in which the drilling action is driven or controlled by electromagnetic devices, such as electric motors or actuators, rather than conventional mechanical or hydraulic means. This entry falls within the fixed-construction, earth-drilling, earth-or-rock drilling hierarchy.
[E21B 43/124] Adaptation of jet-pump systems -- The E21B 43/124 class covers modifications to jet-pump systems used in drilling operations to enhance the extraction of fluids from wells. It includes design changes that improve pump efficiency, handling of cuttings, or adaptation to specific geological conditions within earth-or-rock drilling contexts. This subclass falls under Fixed Constructions → Earth Drilling → Earth or Rock Drilling → Obtaining fluids from wells.
[E21B 43/126] Adaptations of down-hole pump systems powered by drives outside the borehole, e.g. by a rotary or oscillating drive  powered by fluid  E21B43/129 -- A down-hole pump system that receives its power from a drive located outside the borehole, such as a rotary or oscillating mechanism driven by fluid pressure, rather than having an internal motor. This adaptation is used in fixed-construction well drilling to lift fluids from earth or rock formations.
[E21B 43/127] Adaptations of walking-beam pump systems -- The E21B 43/127 entry covers modifications to walking-beam pump arrangements used in earth-drilling operations for extracting fluids from wells, focusing on design changes that improve performance, reliability, or adaptability of the pump system.
[E21B 43/128] Adaptation of pump systems with down-hole electric drives -- The entry covers modifications of pump systems where the drive motor is located down-hole and powered electrically, allowing fluid to be pumped from boreholes. It addresses integrating down-hole electric drives into conventional pump setups for earth-drilling and well-production operations.
[E21B 43/129] Adaptations of down-hole pump systems powered by fluid supplied from outside the borehole  gas-lift  E21B43/122 ; jet pumps  E21B43/124 -- The entry E21B 43/129 covers adaptations of down-hole pump systems that receive driving fluid from outside the borehole, including gas-lift and jet-pump arrangements used to lift fluids in earth-or-rock drilling operations for fluid extraction.
[E21B 43/13] specially adapted to dewatering of wells of gas producing reservoirs, e.g. methane producing coal beds -- The CPC subclass E21B 43/13 covers fixed drilling constructions used to remove water from wells that produce gas, such as methane from coal seams. It relates to equipment and methods specially adapted for dewatering gas-producing reservoirs.
[E21B 43/14] Obtaining from a multiple-zone well -- The code E21B 43/14 covers methods and equipment for extracting fluids from a well that contains multiple separate zones, such as producing oil, gas or water from different strata within the same borehole. It includes techniques for isolating zones and controlling flow to enable simultaneous or sequential production.
[E21B 43/16] Enhanced recovery methods for obtaining hydrocarbons -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Injecting fluids to mobilize hydrocarbons towards a production well.  Increasing the fraction of original oil recovered in place in a reservoir, including waterflooding, chemical flooding, gas injection, and thermal methods.  Illustrative example of the subject matter classified in this place:
[E21B 43/162] Injecting fluid from longitudinally spaced locations in injection well -- The entry covers techniques for delivering fluid into an injection well at several points arranged along the well's length, rather than from a single location. It addresses the placement of injection devices or ports at longitudinally spaced intervals to improve fluid distribution, pressure management, or reservoir stimulation during operations such as enhanced oil recovery or groundwater remediation.
[E21B 43/164] Injecting CO 2  or carbonated water  in combination with organic material  C09K8/594
[E21B 43/166] Injecting a gaseous medium; Injecting a gaseous medium and a liquid medium  CO 2  injection   E21B43/164 ; steam injection  E21B43/24 -- The class E21B 43/166 covers techniques for injecting a gaseous medium, alone or together with a liquid, into a borehole to improve the extraction of fluids. It includes methods such as CO₂ injection and steam injection used in earth-or-rock drilling operations for obtaining fluids from wells, and falls under the hierarchy Fixed Constructions → Earth Drilling → Earth or Rock Drilling.
[E21B 43/168] Injecting a gaseous medium -- This CPC subclass (E21B 43/168) covers methods and apparatus for injecting a gaseous medium into a well to aid the extraction of fluids, such as oil, gas or water, including equipment for delivering and controlling the gas during drilling or production operations.
[E21B 43/17] Interconnecting two or more wells by fracturing or otherwise attacking the formation -- **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Enhanced recovery methods for obtaining hydrocarbons using heat, e.g. steam injection, in association with fracturing or crevice forming processes -> E21B43/2405 - Enhanced recovery methods for obtaining hydrocarbons using combustion in situ in association with fracturing processes or crevice forming processes -> E21B43/247
[E21B 43/18] Repressuring or vacuum methods -- The class E21B 43/18 covers methods that apply repressurizing or create a vacuum in wells to aid the extraction of fluids, including techniques that use pressure differentials or suction to draw liquids or gases upward during drilling or production operations.
[E21B 43/20] Displacing by water -- E21B 43/20 relates to techniques for displacing fluids in earth-or-rock drilling operations, specifically using water to push out cuttings, mud or other materials from wells and improve the flow of obtained fluids.
[E21B 43/24] using heat, e.g. steam injection -- E21B 43/24 covers methods that apply heat to a well, for example by injecting steam, to improve the extraction of fluids such as oil or gas from underground formations. The approach is used in fixed-construction drilling systems to enhance recovery by reducing fluid viscosity.
[E21B 43/2401] by means of electricity -- E21B 43/2401 designates technologies for obtaining fluids from wells in fixed earth-drilling constructions that are powered or controlled by electricity. It covers electric drill rigs, motor-driven drill strings, and electrically driven down-hole tools used to circulate or lift fluids. The code is part of the Cooperative Patent Classification under the Earth or Rock Drilling group.
[E21B 43/2403] by means of nuclear energy -- The subclass E21B 43/2403 covers techniques for obtaining fluids from wells that employ nuclear energy, such as nuclear-powered drilling rigs or processes that use nuclear reactions to assist the extraction of oil, gas or geothermal fluids. It falls within the earth-drilling hierarchy under fixed constructions for earth or rock drilling.
[E21B 43/2405] in association with fracturing or crevice forming processes  E21B43/247  takes precedence -- The subclass E21B 43/2405 refers to fixed earth-or-rock drilling constructions used to obtain fluids from wells when the drilling is performed together with fracturing or crevice-forming processes, and it is overridden by the broader subclass E21B 43/247.
[E21B 43/2406] Steam assisted gravity drainage [SAGD] -- Steam assisted gravity drainage (SAGD) is a fixed-construction method for extracting oil or bitumen from underground reservoirs by injecting steam into a horizontal well to heat the fluid and then allowing the heated oil to flow by gravity to a lower production well. The technique is used in earth or rock drilling operations to obtain fluids from wells.
[E21B 43/2408] SAGD in combination with other methods -- The subclass E21B 43/2408 covers the use of steam-assisted gravity drainage (SAGD) in combination with additional recovery or drilling methods for extracting fluids from wells, especially in oil-sand or heavy-oil formations. It falls under fixed-construction earth-drilling technologies related to obtaining fluids from subsurface reservoirs.
[E21B 43/241] combined with solution mining of non-hydrocarbon minerals, e.g. solvent pyrolysis of oil shale -- This subclass deals with techniques that integrate earth-or-rock drilling for fluid production with solution-mining processes targeting non-hydrocarbon minerals. It includes operations such as using a solvent in the drill hole to pyrolyze oil-shale or similar mineral formations, thereby extracting hydrocarbons while the well serves both drilling and in-situ chemical conversion functions.
[E21B 43/243] Combustion -- The CPC subclass E21B 43/243 covers combustion processes used in earth-or-rock drilling systems for obtaining fluids from wells, such as flame-based drilling, heating of drill bits, or the use of combustible gases to aid penetration and fluid recovery. It falls under Fixed Constructions → Earth Drilling → Earth or Rock Drilling → Obtaining fluids from wells.
[E21B 43/247] in association with fracturing processes -- This CPC subclass covers methods and apparatus where drilling for fluid extraction is combined with hydraulic fracturing or other fracturing techniques to enhance well productivity. It pertains to fixed-construction drilling operations that incorporate fracturing processes.
[E21B 43/248] using explosives -- E21B 43/248 covers methods that employ explosives to facilitate the drilling or completion of wells for the extraction of fluids such as oil, gas, or water. The classification pertains to fixed construction techniques in earth or rock drilling where controlled detonations break rock and create or enlarge boreholes.
[E21B 43/25] Methods for stimulating production -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Treatment of production wells to increase production of hydrocarbons, typically hydraulic fracturing treatments and matrix treatments (acidizing). [E21B 43/255] including the injection of a gaseous medium as treatment fluid into the formation -- The class covers fixed drilling constructions for obtaining fluids from wells in which a gaseous medium is injected as a treatment fluid into the formation to enhance recovery or conditioning.
[E21B 43/26] by forming crevices or fractures -- E21B 43/26 covers techniques for obtaining fluids from wells in which the formation is fractured or creviced to enhance flow. The method creates controlled cracks in earth or rock around the borehole, increasing permeability and enabling more efficient extraction of oil, gas, or water.
[E21B 43/2605] using gas or liquefied gas -- Methods that employ pressurised gas or liquefied gas to aid the extraction of fluids from drilled wells, including cleaning, stimulating or lifting the produced liquids. Such techniques use gases such as nitrogen, carbon dioxide or methane to improve well performance and facilitate fluid flow.
[E21B 43/2607] Surface equipment specially adapted for fracturing operations -- The code E21B 43/2607 covers surface apparatus designed specifically for fracturing wells, such as hydraulic fracturing, including pumps, manifolds, high-pressure containers, and related handling gear used to inject fracturing fluid into a borehole. It falls under the fixed-construction Earth drilling hierarchy, relating to equipment for obtaining fluids from wells.
[E21B 43/261] Separate steps of (1) cementing, plugging or consolidating and (2) fracturing or attacking the formation -- E21B 43/261 covers drilling operations in which the cementing, plugging or consolidation of the wellbore is performed as a distinct step separate from any subsequent fracturing or formation-attacking treatment, typically used in earth or rock drilling for fluid extraction.
[E21B 43/263] using explosives -- E21B 43/263 covers the use of explosives to aid the drilling or completion of wells for the purpose of extracting fluids. It includes techniques that employ controlled detonations to break rock, enlarge boreholes, or stimulate permeability in earth or rock drilling operations. This subclass falls under Fixed Constructions, Earth Drilling, Earth or Rock Drilling, and Obtaining fluids from wells.
[E21B 43/2635] by means of nuclear energy -- The E21B 43/2635 entry classifies methods for obtaining fluids from wells that employ nuclear energy as the power source for earth-or-rock drilling. It falls under the Fixed Constructions category, within Earth Drilling and specifically Earth or Rock Drilling, covering nuclear-driven drilling techniques for fluid extraction.
[E21B 43/267] reinforcing fractures by propping -- E21B 43/267 covers techniques for reinforcing fractures in a wellbore by inserting propping material, such as sand or resin-bonded pellets, to keep the cracks open and enhance fluid flow. The subclass is part of the Earth drilling hierarchy, specifically under earth or rock drilling and obtaining fluids from wells.
[E21B 43/27] by use of eroding chemicals, e.g. acids -- E21B 43/27 classifies techniques for obtaining fluids from wells in earth-or-rock drilling that employ eroding chemicals, such as acids, to dissolve rock or scale and facilitate fluid flow. It covers chemical based well-cleaning, stimulation and drilling assistance methods that use these reactive agents.
[E21B 43/28] Dissolving minerals other than hydrocarbons, e.g. by an alkaline or acid leaching agent -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Production of minerals other than hydrocarbons by dissolving them in situ with a dissolving agent.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Enhanced recovery methods for obtaining hydrocarbons using heat combined with solution mining of non-hydrocarbon minerals -> E21B43/241
[E21B 43/281] using heat -- This group deals with techniques that apply heat to facilitate drilling or to enhance the recovery of fluids from wells, such as circulating hot water, steam, or other thermal sources to soften rock, reduce viscosity, or increase permeability.
[E21B 43/283] in association with a fracturing process -- E21B 43/283 covers fixed drilling installations designed to obtain fluids from wells while being used together with a fracturing operation, such as hydraulic or stimulation treatment that cracks the formation to increase flow.

[E21B 43/29] Obtaining a slurry of minerals, e.g. by using nozzles -- E21B 43/29 covers methods for producing a mineral slurry during drilling operations, typically by directing fluid through nozzles to break and transport rock fragments. The technique is used in fixed construction drilling of earth or rock to retrieve fluids from wells, facilitating mineral extraction and transport to the surface.
[E21B 43/292] using steerable or laterally extendable nozzles -- This CPC subgroup covers well-drilling techniques that employ steerable or laterally extendable nozzles to direct drilling fluid for fluid extraction. The nozzles can be adjusted during operation to steer the drill bit or to reach lateral zones, improving control and recovery in earth or rock drilling.
[E21B 43/295] Gasification of minerals, e.g. for producing mixtures of combustible gases -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Obtention of combustible gas from mineral bodies, usually from coal underground.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Enhanced recovery methods for obtaining hydrocarbons using heat, the combustion being in situ -> E21B43/243  **Glossary:** - Coal underground: A method for exploiting poor-quality mineral and thin seams by burning the mineral in place to produce combustible gas which can be burned to generate power or processed into chemicals and fuels
[E21B 43/30] Specific pattern of wells, e.g. optimising the spacing of wells -- E21B 43/30 relates to the design and arrangement of well patterns, such as the optimal spacing and geometric layout of multiple wells within a drilling field, to enhance fluid recovery and operational efficiency. It falls under fixed constructions for earth or rock drilling aimed at obtaining fluids from wells.
[E21B 43/305] comprising at least one inclined or horizontal well -- This CPC subclass covers methods and equipment for obtaining fluids from wells that are inclined or horizontal, including the design, drilling and completion techniques specific to non-vertical boreholes. It falls under Fixed Constructions, Earth Drilling, Earth or Rock Drilling and Obtaining fluids from wells.
[E21B 43/32] Preventing gas- or water-coning phenomena, i.e. the formation of a conical column of gas or water around wells -- E21B 43/32 covers measures for avoiding gas- or water-coning, the formation of a conical column of gas or water around a producing well, typically by controlling draw-down, wellbore geometry or fluid-injection practices. It is classified under Fixed Constructions → Earth Drilling → Earth or Rock Drilling → Obtaining fluids from wells.
[E21B 43/34] Arrangements for separating materials produced by the well -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Separation of materials produced by the well.  Illustrative example of subject matter classified in this place:
[E21B 43/35] specially adapted for separating solids  down-hole drilling fluid separation systems  E21B21/002 ; separating solids from drilling fluids  E21B21/065 -- E21B 43/35 covers down-hole systems that separate solid particles from drilling fluid while a well is being drilled. These devices are integrated into the drill string to continuously clean the circulating mud, improving cuttings removal and preventing equipment blockage. The classification falls under fixed constructions for earth drilling and obtaining fluids from wells.
[E21B 43/36] Underwater separating arrangements -- E21B 43/36 defines underwater separating arrangements used in fixed-construction drilling systems for extracting fluids from wells. It covers subsea equipment that separates oil, gas, water, sand or other particulates at the drill site, including separators, hydrocyclones, and related devices that operate below the water surface.
[E21B 43/38] in the well -- E21B 43/38 covers activities, tools and methods applied inside the wellbore while extracting fluids, encompassing procedures and equipment used during well operation for obtaining oil, gas or water.
[E21B 43/385] by reinjecting the separated materials into an earth formation in the same well -- The code covers a technique used while obtaining fluids from a well where the separated solids, liquids or gases are returned to the same earth formation through the same borehole. It belongs to fixed-construction methods for earth-or-rock drilling and enables in-well reinjection of cuttings or other extracted materials.
[E21B 43/40] Separation associated with re-injection of separated materials -- E21B 43/40 covers methods and apparatus for separating materials that have been extracted during earth or rock drilling and subsequently re-injecting the separated fractions back into the formation. It addresses handling of cuttings, fluids or gases to improve well productivity and environmental compliance.
[E21B 44/00] Automatic control, surveying or testing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and devices such as sensors for monitoring drilling operations and for controlling the drilling operation in response to the information obtained.  Illustrative example of the subject matter classified in this place:
[E21B 44/005] Below-ground automatic control systems -- The CPC subclass E21B 44/005 pertains to automatic control systems used in underground drilling operations, covering devices and methods that monitor, regulate and test drill performance while the drill penetrates earth or rock. It includes sensors, controllers and software that enable precise, self-adjusting drilling beneath the surface.
[E21B 44/02] Automatic control of the tool feed -- This subclass deals with systems that automatically regulate the feed rate of drilling tools in fixed earth-drilling machines, including sensors and control loops that adjust advance based on load, torque or position. It covers automatic control, surveying or testing functions integrated into the drill feed mechanism.
[E21B 44/04] in response to the torque of the drive -- The code E21B 44/04 covers systems for earth or rock drilling that automatically react to the torque generated by the drive, typically by adjusting feed rate, rotation speed or applying braking to maintain optimal drilling conditions and prevent overload. This falls under fixed-construction drilling equipment with built-in automatic control or testing functions.
[E21B 44/06] in response to the flow or pressure of the motive fluid of the drive -- A control device for earth-drilling equipment that automatically adjusts the drill's operation based on the measured flow rate or pressure of the hydraulic fluid powering the drive, enabling responsive regulation of speed, torque or feed as conditions change.
[E21B 44/08] in response to the amplitude of the movement of the percussion tool, e.g. jump or recoil -- The code E21B 44/08 designates control or monitoring devices for fixed earth-drilling machines that react to the amplitude of the percussion tool's movement, such as jumps or recoil, to adjust drilling parameters automatically. It falls under automatic control, surveying or testing within the earth-or-rock drilling subclass.
[E21B 44/10] Arrangements for automatic stopping when the tool is lifted from the working face -- The E21B 44/10 subclass concerns drilling equipment that automatically halts operation when the drill or cutting tool is raised away from the work face. It includes mechanisms such as lift-detected shut-off controls, sensors, or hydraulic systems that stop power transmission to prevent damage or injury. This feature is applied to earth-or-rock drilling machines used in fixed construction contexts.
[E21B 45/00] Measuring the drilling time or rate of penetration -- The E21B 45/00 class covers methods and devices for measuring how long a drill operates and the speed at which it penetrates earth or rock. It includes timing instruments, penetration-rate sensors, and data-recording systems used to monitor and optimise drilling performance.
[E21B 47/00] Survey of boreholes or wells -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Specific applications in a downhole environment, including:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Monitoring pressure or flow of drilling fluid -> E21B21/08  **Glossary:** - borehole: an unlined shaft drilled into the ground, typically for the purposes of extracting water, crude oil or natural gas. This is often referred to as the wellbore.
[E21B 47/001] for underwater installation
[E21B 47/002] by visual inspection -- E21B 47/002 covers the visual inspection of boreholes or wells, where the interior of the drilled hole is examined directly-often with cameras, endoscopes or other optical tools-to assess condition, geometry or deposits. It is a subcategory of survey methods within fixed-construction earth-drilling techniques.
[E21B 47/0025] generating an image of the borehole wall using down-hole measurements, e.g. acoustic or electric -- E21B 47/0025 pertains to techniques for visualising the wall of a borehole by using down-hole sensors such as acoustic or electric probes, producing an image of the formation during a drilling survey. The classification falls under fixed constructions, earth drilling, earth or rock drilling, and survey of boreholes or wells.
[E21B 47/003] Determining well or borehole volumes -- Methods and devices for measuring the total volume of a drilled borehole or well, typically by assessing the space occupied by fluid, gas, or solids during or after drilling. Such techniques support geological surveying, well planning, and evaluation of drilling performance.
[E21B 47/005] Monitoring or checking of cementation quality or level -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes or apparatus for tracking or examining the extent or integrity of the plugging or consolidating material.
[E21B 47/006] Detection of corrosion or deposition of substances -- E21B 47/006 covers techniques for detecting corrosion or material deposits inside boreholes, wells or drill strings during or after earth-or-rock drilling operations. It includes inspection, monitoring and diagnostic methods that identify degradation of metal components or buildup of substances such as scale, cement or sediments to ensure structural integrity.
[E21B 47/007] Measuring stresses in a pipe string or casing -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes or apparatus for determining the tensile or compressive force per cross-sectional area acting upon a pipe string or casing.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Locating blocked portions of pipes -> E21B47/09
[E21B 47/008] Monitoring of down-hole pump systems, e.g. for the detection of "pumped-off" conditions -- The CPC entry E21B 47/008 addresses techniques and apparatus for monitoring down-hole pump systems in earth-drilling or borehole surveys, especially to detect "pumped-off" conditions where a pump operates without adequate fluid. It helps ensure safe and efficient well operation.
[E21B 47/009] Monitoring of walking-beam pump systems -- The CPC entry E21B 47/009 refers to techniques for monitoring walking-beam pump systems used in earth-or-rock drilling, particularly in the surveying of boreholes or wells. It includes sensors, control devices and data-processing methods that observe pump performance, fluid flow and mechanical condition to ensure efficient and reliable operation.
[E21B 47/01] Devices for supporting measuring instruments on drill bits, pipes, rods or wirelines -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Devices for mounting a data collection instrument upon a boring tool, boring tool string or cable-carried tool. Devices for shielding down-hole data collection instruments from damage.
[E21B 47/013] Devices specially adapted for supporting measuring instruments on drill bits
[E21B 47/017] Protecting measuring instruments -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes or apparatus for shielding the data collection device against thermal effect, vibration, pressure, debris or the like.
[E21B 47/0175] Cooling arrangements -- The classification E21B 47/0175 covers cooling arrangements used during the surveying of boreholes or wells, a subcategory of fixed constructions for earth-or-rock drilling. It includes systems that manage temperature of drilling tools or measurement equipment to maintain performance and protect components while performing borehole surveys.
[E21B 47/02] Determining slope or direction -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Processes or apparatus for measuring the inclination or orientation of boreholes or devices in boreholes or wells.
[E21B 47/022] of the borehole, e.g. using geomagnetism -- The E21B 47/022 classification covers methods for surveying boreholes or wells by measuring variations in the Earth's magnetic field. It falls under Fixed Constructions → Earth Drilling → Earth or Rock Drilling → Survey of boreholes or wells, focusing on geomagnetic techniques to assess borehole geometry and position.
[E21B 47/0224] using seismic or acoustic means -- This subclass covers techniques that employ seismic or acoustic methods to survey boreholes or wells during earth-or-rock drilling, enabling the detection of formation characteristics, faults or other subsurface features without direct physical contact.
[E21B 47/0228] using electromagnetic energy or detectors therefor -- E21B 47/0228 covers the use of electromagnetic energy or associated detectors for surveying boreholes or wells during earth-or-rock drilling. It includes methods that emit, receive or measure electromagnetic fields to identify geological layers, fluid presence or other subsurface characteristics within the drilled hole. This sub-group is part of the broader fixed-construction and earth-drilling hierarchy.
[E21B 47/0232] at least one of the energy sources or one of the detectors being located on or above the ground surface -- The subclass concerns earth-drilling survey methods in which either the energy source used for probing or the detection equipment is placed on or above the ground surface rather than downhole, enabling surface-based operation and data acquisition during borehole or well surveys.
[E21B 47/0236] using a pendulum -- The classification covers the use of a pendulum as a measuring instrument when surveying boreholes or wells.
