[E21D 15/002] Auxiliary devices in which the prop type is not essential  setting or withdrawing props or chocks  E21D15/58 ,  E21D15/60 -- E21D 15/002 covers auxiliary devices used for setting or withdrawing props or chocks in shaft drilling where the specific type of prop is not critical, including mechanisms that assist in positioning, locking, or releasing support elements during drilling operations. This subclass falls under Fixed Constructions → Earth Drilling → Shafts → Working-face supports.
[E21D 15/005] characterised by the material -- E21D 15/005 covers working-face supports used in earth-drilling shafts where the defining feature is the material from which the support is made, such as steel, concrete, composite or polymer elements, distinguishing them from supports defined by geometry or function.
[E21D 15/007] spark-reducing -- E21D 15/007 covers measures and devices that minimise spark generation on working-face supports used in shaft construction for earth-drilling operations, such as spark-suppressing tools, protective coatings or water-spray systems applied to the support elements. This technology aims to reduce fire-hazard risks in underground drilling environments.
[E21D 15/02] Non-telescopic props -- Non-telescopic props are fixed-length support members installed in shafts used for earth drilling to brace the working face and maintain stability of the borehole. They differ from telescopic props by having a constant size, providing a simple, rigid means of holding down drilling equipment and surrounding rock.
[E21D 15/04] with wooden prop parts joined by double conical connectors -- E21D 15/04 covers working-face support systems for earth-drilling shafts that employ wooden prop elements linked together by double-conical connectors. The design provides a fixed construction arrangement where the conical fittings secure adjacent wooden props, creating a stable framework that reinforces the shaft's excavation face. This solution is used to maintain shaft integrity during drilling operations.
[E21D 15/06] with parts joined by a lock, with or without slight axial adjustability -- The code E21D 15/06 covers working-face supports for drilling shafts whose components are fastened together by a lock, allowing the assembly to remain securely in place while offering slight axial adjustability for precise positioning during earth-drilling operations.
[E21D 15/08] with toggle joint connection -- E21D 15/08 designates working-face support structures for earth-drilling shafts that are assembled with a toggle-joint connection. The classification falls under Fixed Constructions → Earth Drilling → Shafts → Working-face supports, and is used to index patents covering toggle-linked support systems that stabilize the drill face.
[E21D 15/10] with dog-clutch or pin-and-hole connection -- The code E21D 15/10 designates working-face supports for earth-drilling shafts that are fixed constructions and are joined by a dog-clutch or pin-and-hole coupling, enabling secure, interchangeable connections between shaft sections.
[E21D 15/12] with locking devices located near head or foot -- This CPC subclass covers supports mounted on the working face of a drilling shaft that incorporate locking mechanisms positioned close to the shaft head or foot. The devices secure the support during earth-drilling operations and allow quick release or adjustment from either end of the shaft.
[E21D 15/14] Telescopic props -- Telescopic props are adjustable, extendable supports installed in the working face of an earth-drilling shaft to brace the surrounding rock or soil. They allow length variation to fit differing trench depths and maintain stability while excavation progresses, and are typically made of steel or composite materials that can be locked in place after extension. This entry falls under working-face supports within shaft construction.
[E21D 15/15] Means counteracting entry of dirt -- E21D 15/15 covers devices installed in shaft working-face supports that prevent soil or debris from entering the excavation area. Typical solutions include liners, seals, grates or other barrier structures that block dirt while allowing required access and ventilation.
[E21D 15/155] Built-in cleaning devices -- Built-in cleaning devices are integral components of working-face supports used in earth-drilling shafts. They are designed to automatically remove cuttings, debris or fluids from the drill face or borehole wall, helping maintain clearance and stability while drilling continues. These devices are fitted directly within the support structure, eliminating the need for separate cleaning tools.
[E21D 15/16] with parts held together by positive means, with or without relative sliding movement when the prop is subject to excessive pressure -- The code E21D 15/16 covers shaft working-face supports in earth-drilling shafts whose parts are fastened by positive mechanical means, permitting either fixed or limited sliding movement when the prop is subjected to excessive pressure. It applies to designs that stay securely attached while allowing pressure-induced displacement.
[E21D 15/18] with one part resting on a supporting medium, e.g. rubber, sand, bitumen, lead, located in the other part, with or without expulsion or displacement of the medium upon excessive pressure -- This subclass covers shaft supports in drilling where one component rests on a medium such as rubber, sand, bitumen or lead placed in the other component, and may allow the medium to be expelled or displaced when pressure exceeds a limit. It pertains to fixed constructions used as working-face supports in earth-drilling shafts.
[E21D 15/20] with pawl, pin, cross-piece, or the like engaging with ratchet teeth, notches, holes, or the like, spaced apart at intervals -- This CPC subclass E21D 15/20 covers shaft support systems for earth-drilling rigs where the working-face support includes a pawl, pin, cross-piece or similar element that engages ratchet teeth, notches or holes spaced at intervals to lock the shaft in position. It is classified under Fixed Constructions, Earth Drilling, Shafts, Working-face supports.
[E21D 15/22] with member, pin, cross- piece, or the like, ruptured, sheared through, or permanently deformed upon excessive pressure -- E21D 15/22 covers fixed earth-drilling shaft working-face supports that include a member, pin, cross-piece or similar element intended to rupture, shear through, or permanently deform when excessive pressure is applied, providing a controlled failure mechanism to protect the drilling assembly from overload.
[E21D 15/24] with axial screw-and-nut, rack-and-worm, or like mechanism
[E21D 15/26] with screw, worm, or the like, not self-locking but normally prevented from rotation by friction members which slip upon excessive pressure -- E21D 15/26 covers working-face supports for drilling shafts that are driven by a screw, worm or similar thread and are not self-locking; instead their rotation is restrained by friction devices that slip when pressure exceeds a set limit, protecting the drill structure from overload.
[E21D 15/28] with parts held relatively to each other by friction or gripping -- E21D 15/28 covers working-face shaft supports for earth-drilling equipment in which the constituent parts are held together by friction or gripping forces rather than by bolts, welds or other mechanical fasteners. This subclass includes designs that rely on tight clearances, sleeves, clamps or other friction-based mechanisms to secure the support structure while the drill operates.
[E21D 15/285] by means of wedges or wedge combinations  details of wedges  E21D15/43 -- The subclass E21D 15/285 deals with wedge-based or wedge-combination devices employed as working-face supports in earth-drilling shafts. It covers the construction, arrangement and detailed features of individual wedges that transfer load from the drill string to the surrounding formation, ensuring stable shaft advancement.
[E21D 15/30] by means expanded or contracted by pressure applied through the medium of a fluid or quasi- fluid, e.g. rubber -- E21D 15/30 covers working-face supports for earth-drilling shafts that are expanded or contracted by fluid or quasi-fluid pressure, such as rubber bladders. These devices provide adaptable support by inflating or deflating to match the shaft bore.
[E21D 15/303] by means of granular material  supporting by means of granular material  E21D15/18 -- The entry E21D 15/303 covers working-face support systems for shafts that rely on granular material such as sand, gravel or shotcrete to provide bearing and stabilization during earth-drilling operations. It is a specific subclass of shaft support methods within the broader fixed-construction classification.
[E21D 15/306] by means of a liquid -- The code E21D 15/306 covers methods of supporting the working face of a shaft during earth-drilling operations by employing a liquid medium. It includes techniques where water, oil, slurry or other fluids provide pressure or buoyancy to stabilize the excavation area and prevent collapse.
[E21D 15/32] by a deformable collar -- This subclass covers shaft support systems in earth-drilling where the working face is held by a collar that can deform to fit irregularities or changes in the borehole wall, allowing flexible, self-adjusting support during drilling operations.
[E21D 15/325] by a clamping ring -- The code E21D 15/325 designates working-face supports for earth-drilling shafts that are installed by means of a clamping ring. The clamping ring secures the support structure to the shaft wall, providing a simple, removable means of holding drill rods, casings or liners in place during drilling operations. This method is used where rapid installation or adjustment of the support is required.
[E21D 15/34] by axially- moving balls, rollers, or the like -- This subclass defines shaft-working-face supports for earth-drilling installations that employ axially moving balls, rollers or similar elements to carry the load, providing a low-friction, self-aligning bearing within a fixed construction.
[E21D 15/36] by a tiltable collar surrounding one or both parts -- E21D 15/36 defines working-face support structures in earth-drilling shafts that employ a tiltable collar surrounding one or both shaft parts to provide adjustable bearing or guidance. The collar can be angled to accommodate changes in alignment or load direction during drilling operations.
[E21D 15/38] with longitudinally-divided upper or lower prop parts, e.g. interfitting laminations -- E21D 15/38 relates to working-face supports for drilling shafts that incorporate upper or lower prop parts split lengthwise, such as interlocking laminations, enabling adjustable or modular support structures in earth-drilling applications.
[E21D 15/40] Collar or other support gripped to one or both parts by toggle-action, cam, or other member pivoted or similarly mounted -- In earth-drilling shaft constructions, this subclass covers collars or other support devices that are attached to one or both shaft parts by means of a toggle-action, cam, or similar pivoted mechanism. The design allows rapid gripping and release, facilitating quick installation or adjustment of the support during drilling operations.
[E21D 15/42] with special parts to influence the friction -- E21D 15/42 refers to shafts or work-face support structures in earth-drilling equipment that incorporate special elements designed to alter friction, such as friction-reducing pads, lubricating inserts or surfaces that increase resistance to control the movement of the drilling face.
[E21D 15/43] Details of wedges -- E21D 15/43 covers the design and specification of wedges employed as working-face supports in shaft constructions for earth-drilling operations. It includes details of wedge geometry, dimensions, material selection and mounting arrangements that secure the shaft and distribute load at the drilling face.
[E21D 15/435] hydraulic -- E21D 15/435 covers hydraulic devices that support the working face of shaft sections in fixed-construction earth-drilling operations. These systems use fluid pressure to provide temporary or permanent support to the shaft lining or borehole wall while excavation proceeds, improving stability and safety during mining or tunneling activities.
[E21D 15/44] Hydraulic, pneumatic, or hydraulic-pneumatic props -- Hydraulic, pneumatic, or hydraulic-pneumatic props are fluid-pressured devices used to support the working face of an earth-drilling shaft. By providing adjustable, load-bearing pressure, they stabilize the excavation wall and can be quickly set or released, facilitating safe and efficient shaft construction in mining or tunneling operations.
[E21D 15/445] comprising a fluid cushion -- A working-face support for shaft drilling that incorporates a fluid cushion, using a pressurised liquid or gas layer to absorb loads and maintain stability of the excavated face. The fluid cushion reduces friction and pressure spikes, improving safety and efficiency during earth-drilling operations.
[E21D 15/45] having closed fluid system, e.g. with built-in pumps or accumulators -- The code E21D 15/45 covers supports installed at the working-face of a drilling shaft that incorporate a sealed fluid circuit, typically with built-in pumps or accumulators, to provide hydraulic assistance or damping. These devices are part of fixed construction elements used in earth-drilling shafts.
[E21D 15/46] with load-measuring devices -- This subclass covers working-face supports for earth-drilling shafts that are equipped with load-measuring devices, enabling monitoring of forces exerted on the support during drilling operations. It pertains to fixed construction elements used to stabilize and assess shaft loads.
[E21D 15/465] with essential hydraulic or pneumatic details -- E21D 15/465 covers working-face supports for drilling shafts that incorporate essential hydraulic or pneumatic components. The classification applies to structures that provide mechanical support at the drill face while using fluid power for positioning, pressure control or actuation, typical of fixed earth-drilling installations.
[E21D 15/48] Chocks or the like -- E21D 15/48 covers chocks and similar devices used as working-face supports in fixed earth-drilling shafts. The entry includes wedges, blocks or other temporary supports placed against the shaft to hold it in position during drilling operations.
[E21D 15/483] made of flexible containers, e.g. inflatable, with or without reinforcement, e.g. filled with water, backfilling material or the like  laggings making use of fluid cushions  E21D11/157 -- This subclass relates to working-face supports for shafts that consist of flexible or inflatable containers, optionally reinforced and filled with water, backfill material or other fluids to act as fluid-cushioned laggings. Such elements provide adjustable, pressure-responsive support during earth-drilling and shaft construction.
[E21D 15/486] with essential hydraulic or pneumatic details -- E21D 15/486 covers working-face supports used in earth-drilling shafts that incorporate essential hydraulic or pneumatic features, such as pressure-controlled hydraulic pistons, pneumatic cylinders, or fluid-filled elements that provide adjustable support, sealing or anchoring of the shaft lining during drilling operations.
[E21D 15/50] Component parts or details of props -- E21D 15/50 covers the individual components and detailed features of props that support the working face in shafts used for earth-drilling. It includes elements such as brackets, joints, adjustment mechanisms and other parts that form or modify the prop structure. This subclass is part of the broader Fixed Constructions → Earth Drilling → Shafts → Working-face supports hierarchy.
[E21D 15/502] Prop bodies characterised by their shape, e.g. of specified cross-section -- The E21D 15/502 group covers prop bodies used as working-face supports in earth-drilling shafts, distinguished primarily by their geometric shape such as a defined cross-section. It includes any prop whose design is characterised by its profile, whether rectangular, circular, or other specified forms, to provide stability to the drilling shaft.
[E21D 15/51] specially adapted to hydraulic, pneumatic, or hydraulic-pneumatic props, e.g. arrangements of relief valves -- The CPC subclass E21D 15/51 covers working-face supports for earth-drilling shafts that are specially adapted to hydraulic, pneumatic or combined hydraulic-pneumatic props, including arrangements of relief valves and related control devices. It falls under the hierarchy of Fixed Constructions → Earth Drilling → Shafts → Working-face supports.
[E21D 15/512] Arrangement of valves  external control of roof support installations  E21D23/12 ,  E21D23/26 -- This subclass covers the design and interconnection of valves that provide external control of roof-support equipment in underground shafts used for earth drilling. It details how valve arrangements regulate pressure, flow or actuation of support devices, relating to fixed-construction shaft systems and roof-support installations.
[E21D 15/515] Particular fluids not covered by any chemical subdivision -- E21D 15/515 pertains to the use of specific fluids in working-face support systems for earth-drilling shafts, where the fluids are not classified under any existing chemical subclass. It includes lubricants, cooling or stabilizing liquids applied to support the drill face, but excludes standard drilling muds that have their own chemical codes.
[E21D 15/517] Extension elements -- In earth-drilling shaft construction, this subclass covers auxiliary components that extend or augment the working-face support structure, such as brackets, plates or other elements that lengthen, reinforce or adapt the support to the drilling face.
[E21D 15/52] Extensible units located above or below standard props -- E21D 15/52 covers devices that can be lengthened or shortened and are installed either on top of or beneath conventional props to support the working face of a shaft in earth-drilling operations. These extensible units improve adjustability and load distribution for shaft support systems.
[E21D 15/523] with hydraulic or pneumatic control -- E21D 15/523 covers working-face support devices for shafts in earth-drilling operations that are operated by hydraulic or pneumatic control. These systems provide temporary reinforcement of the shaft wall during excavation and can be adjusted or released using fluid-power mechanisms.
[E21D 15/526] for step-by-step extension of the prop -- E21D 15/526 covers devices that extend a shaft-support prop in a series of incremental steps, allowing the prop to be lengthened progressively as drilling advances. The system provides controlled, step-by-step adjustment of the prop's length to maintain support of the working face in deep earth-drilling shafts.
[E21D 15/54] Details of the ends of props -- The classification covers the design and construction of the terminal portions of support props employed in shaft working-face supports for earth drilling, including caps, heads, bearings, fastening or anchoring means, and protective elements that engage the surrounding rock or steel.
[E21D 15/545] hydraulic or pneumatic -- E21D 15/545 covers the use of hydraulic or pneumatic devices to support the working face of an earth-drilling shaft. It includes pressure-actuated jacks, lifts, or other fluid-driven mechanisms that hold or position the drilling assembly during operation.
[E21D 15/55] of prop heads -- The class E21D 15/55 covers prop heads employed as working-face supports in shafts for earth-drilling operations. It includes devices that brace and stabilize the excavation wall, typically part of fixed-construction shaft support systems.
[E21D 15/56] Details of locks of telescopic props -- This subgroup covers the design and functional features of locking devices used on telescopic props that support the working face of shafts in earth-drilling and other fixed-construction projects, detailing how the mechanisms secure the extended sections. It includes specifications of lock types, actuation methods and safety criteria for maintaining prop stability.
[E21D 15/563] by which a series of similarly shaped props can obtain an almost equal load-capacity  setting props or chocks  E21D15/584 ,  E21D15/589 -- E21D 15/563 concerns working-face supports for earth-drilling shafts in which a series of similarly shaped props or chocks are arranged so that each carries nearly the same load capacity. It covers methods of setting these props to achieve uniform bearing on the working face.
[E21D 15/566] comprising a fluid cushion -- E21D 15/566 defines working-face supports for earth-drilling shafts that incorporate a fluid cushion, such as a hydraulic or pneumatic pad, to absorb forces and maintain stability during excavation. The fluid-cushioned design reduces vibration and wear on the support structure, improving safety and efficiency of shaft construction.
[E21D 15/58] Devices for setting props or chocks -- These devices are mechanisms used to position and secure temporary supports, known as props or chocks, in the working face of a drill shaft. They enable rapid and reliable placement of the supports to stabilize the shaft walls during excavation and can be manually or hydraulically operated. The classification covers various designs such as clamps, inserters, and pneumatic pushers that lock the props in place.
[E21D 15/581] in which the props are pre- loaded  extensible elements at strut extremities  E21D15/52 -- E21D 15/581 is a CPC subgroup within fixed constructions for earth-drilling shafts, classified under working-face supports. It specifically addresses designs in which the props are pre-loaded and equipped with extensible elements at the strut extremities, a detailed implementation of the broader E21D15/52 category.
[E21D 15/5813] by means of power accumulators to be pre-charged by rock pressure  by means of hydraulically operated devices  E21D15/587
[E21D 15/5817] in which the lock is also activated -- E21D 15/5817 covers working-face supports for earth-drilling shafts in which a locking device is engaged at the same time the support is installed, ensuring the support remains fixed and preventing unintended movement during drilling operations.
[E21D 15/584] in which a series of similarly shaped props obtain an almost equal load capacity by removable devices  idem for extensible props  E21D15/563 -- E21D 15/584 covers working-face supports for earth-drilling shafts in which a series of similarly shaped props are designed to share load equally by using removable devices, and it also includes analogous arrangements for extensible props. This concept ensures uniform load capacity across the prop series during drilling operations.
[E21D 15/585] by means of hydraulically operated devices for mechanical props, or hydraulic props in which the hydraulic part is not important  hydraulic props in which the hydraulic part is important  E21D15/44 -- E21D 15/585 covers hydraulic support systems for the working face of earth-drilling shafts. It includes mechanical props driven by hydraulic devices as well as hydraulic props where the hydraulic component is either primary or secondary to the support function, falling under the broader group E21D15/44.
[E21D 15/586] the props being pre-loaded -- E21D 15/586 covers the use of props that are pre-loaded to support the working face of earth-drilling shafts. By applying a predetermined load to the props before installation, the system provides immediate, stable support and helps distribute stresses across the shaft lining.
[E21D 15/587] by means of power accumulators to be pre-charged by rock pressure -- E21D 15/587 concerns supports for the working face of mine shafts that are powered by accumulators that are charged by the pressure of the surrounding rock. The invention uses stored hydraulic or pneumatic energy generated by rock stress to provide active support during drilling or excavation.
[E21D 15/588] the lock also being activated -- E21D 15/588 covers shafts or working-face support structures in earth-drilling installations that incorporate a locking mechanism which is automatically or manually activated to secure the support in place, such as locks that engage when the support is set, when hydraulic pressure reaches a preset level, or when a safety device is triggered. This subclass deals with the design and operation of those activation features for maintaining shaft stability during drilling.
[E21D 15/589] in which a series of similarly shaped props are given almost equal load capacity by removable devices -- The entry covers shaft working-face supports in earth-drilling where a series of similarly shaped props are equipped with removable devices that balance the load, giving each prop nearly equal capacity. This design ensures uniform support and easy adjustment of individual props.
[E21D 15/60] Devices for withdrawing props or chocks -- Devices for withdrawing props or chocks are mechanisms used to lift or remove the support members that hold a shaft's working face in place during earth-drilling operations. They typically incorporate hydraulic, pneumatic or mechanical actuators that disengage the prop or chock and retrieve it safely, allowing the shaft to be advanced or abandoned without disturbing surrounding soil.
[E21D 15/603] by means of hydraulic devices -- E21D 15/603 covers methods and apparatus for supporting the working face of an earth-drilling shaft using hydraulic devices. It pertains to fixed construction systems where hydraulic pistons, cylinders or pressure mechanisms provide vertical or lateral support to the drill cavity.
[E21D 15/606] the props being moved systematically  mine roof supports for step-by-step movement  E21D23/00
[E21D 17/00] Caps for supporting mine roofs -- **Definition:** This place covers:  A cap is understood as the roof or top piece in a three-piece , support or beam set used for tunnel support. A piece of plank, timber, support or beam placed on top of a prop, stull, or post. The horizontal member of a set of timber, support or beam used as a roadway support:
[E21D 17/003] with essential hydraulic elements -- The subgroup E21D 17/003 covers mining shaft caps that incorporate essential hydraulic components, such as pressure-controlled seals or adjustable hydraulic mechanisms, which are used to support and stabilize mine roofs during drilling operations.
[E21D 17/006] characterised by the material -- E21D 17/006 classifies caps for supporting mine roofs in shafts that are distinguished by the material from which they are made. The code applies to caps of metal, concrete, polymer or other substances used to prevent roof collapse in underground drilling operations.
[E21D 17/01] characterised by the shape of the cap, e.g. of specified cross-section -- This subclass concerns fixed constructions for earth-drilling shafts, specifically caps used to support mine roofs, where the defining feature is the shape or cross-section of the cap. It classifies inventions whose novelty lies in the geometric profile of the roof-supporting cap.
[E21D 17/02] Cantilever extension or similar protecting devices -- E21D 17/02 covers cantilever-type extensions and comparable protective devices used as caps to support mine roof sections in shaft constructions. The classification applies to fixed construction elements that reinforce or shield the roof of underground shafts during or after earth-drilling operations.
[E21D 17/0206] Hydraulically operated extension devices -- This CPC subclass E21D 17/0206 pertains to hydraulically operated extension devices that lengthen caps used to support mine roofs in shafts. The devices employ fluid power to adjust the support length during installation or operation, facilitating flexible and safe fixed-construction reinforcement in earth-drilling contexts.
[E21D 17/0213] Assembling devices for pivoting caps upwardly -- This subclass refers to devices that assemble and pivot caps upwardly to support mine roofs in fixed earth-drilling shaft constructions. It includes mechanisms that lift and lock the caps in place, enabling safe roof reinforcement during shaft work.
[E21D 17/03] Brackets for roof-supporting bars -- E21D 17/03 pertains to brackets that attach to or secure the bars used to support a mine roof, forming part of caps that reinforce shaft linings in fixed construction earth-drilling applications.
[E21D 17/04] for use in longwall working
[E21D 17/06] for use in drifting galleries -- E21D 17/06 designates caps or roof-supporting devices intended for installation in drifting galleries, the horizontal passages driven through underground rock during mining or tunnelling. These fixed constructions are used to reinforce the roof of shafts and ensure stability while the gallery is being excavated.
[E21D 17/08] Cap joints for obtaining a coal-face free of pit-props -- E21D 17/08 covers cap joints designed to create a coal-face that does not require pit-props, forming part of the fixed-construction category for earth-drilling shafts and mine-roof caps. The code addresses devices that join roof-support caps to maintain stability while eliminating the need for individual props.
[E21D 17/082] of sliding type -- E21D 17/082 covers sliding-type caps used to support mine roofs in earth-drilling shaft constructions. These fixed-construction devices allow the roof cap to slide along the shaft for easier installation or adjustment, providing a reliable means of holding back roof material in underground mining.
[E21D 17/086] or articulated type -- The subclass E21D 17/086 covers caps employed to support the roof of mine shafts that are of an articulated type, allowing movement or adjustment to follow the roof contour. It falls under fixed constructions for earth-drilling shafts and concerns roof-support caps.
[E21D 17/10] Details of mine caps for engaging the tops of pit-props, with or without retaining-plates -- E21D 17/10 covers the design details of mine caps that engage the tops of pit-props, optionally incorporating retaining plates, used in shaft earth-drilling fixed constructions to support mine roofs.
[E21D 19/00] Provisional protective covers for working space -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Means for protecting a work area from rocks falling from the roof or tunnels other than the final lining. This temporary roof support can be in the form of movable shields, caps or covers, of mobile or temporary roof supports, or of linings fixed to the walls and intended to be supplemented by additional lining at a subsequent stage:
[E21D 19/02] for use in longwall working -- E21D 19/02 covers provisional protective covers that are installed in shafts to protect the working space during longwall mining operations, providing temporary shielding against rock falls, water ingress and other hazards while the longwall face is active.
[E21D 19/04] for use in drifting galleries -- E21D 19/04 covers provisional protective structures installed in drifting galleries, the horizontal passages of an earth-drilling shaft. These covers shield the working space from debris, water ingress and rockfalls while the tunnel is being excavated or equipped. They are fixed constructions that form a temporary barrier until permanent lining is installed.
[E21D 20/00] Setting anchoring-bolts -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Methods and machines for installing anchoring bolts or rock bolts, including machines for drilling a hole and inserting an bolt in combination. Under this main group is also classified the use of resins or mortars for fixing anchoring-bolts:  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Anchoring bolts for shafts, tunnels or galleries -> E21D21/00 - Means for anchoring structural elements or bulkheads specially adapted to foundation engineering -> E02D5/74 - Dowels or other devices fastened in walls or the like by inserting them in holes made therein for that purpose -> F16B13/00
[E21D 20/003] Machines for drilling anchor holes and setting anchor bolts -- E21D 20/003 covers machines that both drill holes in soil or rock and simultaneously or subsequently install anchoring bolts, typically used in construction of foundations, retaining structures and slope stabilization. The devices combine drilling, cleaning and bolt placement mechanisms to create secure anchored connections in shafts.
[E21D 20/006] having magazines for storing and feeding anchoring-bolts  combined feeding from racks and connecting of drill rods or the like  E21B19/20
[E21D 20/02] with provisions for grouting -- E21D 20/02 relates to shafts in earth-drilling fixed constructions where anchoring bolts are installed and the design includes provisions for grouting to secure the bolts in place.
[E21D 20/021] Grouting with inorganic components, e.g. cement -- The code E21D 20/021 covers the use of inorganic grouting materials, such as cement-based mixes, to fill and secure spaces around anchoring bolts in earth-drilling shafts. It includes methods for preparing, placing and curing these grouts to provide structural stability and corrosion protection.
[E21D 20/023] Cartridges; Grouting charges -- E21D 20/023 refers to cartridges or pre-packaged grouting charges used in fixed-construction earth-drilling shafts for setting anchoring bolts, where the grout is released from a container to flow into the borehole and solidify to secure the bolt.
[E21D 20/025] Grouting with organic components, e.g. resin -- This subclass covers the use of organic binding agents, such as synthetic resins, for grouting shafts or anchoring bolts in fixed construction earth-drilling applications. It includes methods of preparing, injecting and curing resin-based grout to secure bolts within drilled shafts, offering alternatives to traditional cementitious grouts.
[E21D 20/026] Cartridges; Grouting charges -- The CPC subclass E21D 20/026 covers cartridges and grouting charges used to set anchoring bolts in shafts created for earth-drilling operations. It includes devices that deliver binding or cementitious material into the borehole to secure the bolt in place. This subclass is part of the Fixed Constructions - Earth Drilling - Shafts hierarchy.
[E21D 20/028] Devices or accesories for injecting a grouting liquid in a bore-hole  E21D20/003  takes precedence; improving soil or rock by injections  E21D9/001 -- This subclass covers equipment used to deliver grout or similar liquids into drilled holes for stabilising shafts or anchoring-bolts. It includes pumps, hoses, nozzles and auxiliary devices that inject the fluid directly into the borehole. The code falls under fixed constructions, earth drilling, shafts and setting anchoring-bolts and is subordinate to broader groups for soil or rock improvement by injection.
[E21D 21/00] Anchoring-bolts for roof, floor , or shaft-lining protection -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Anchoring bolts, also known as rock bolts or roof bolts, characterised by their construction or by additional elements forming part of them, such as washer or anchoring heads.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Ground anchors E02D5/80 -> E02D5/08 - Anchorings for building constructions -> E04B1/41 - Dowels or other devices fastened in walls or the like by inserting them in holes made therein for that purpose -> F16B13/00 - Bolts with visible load -> G01L1/00
[E21D 21/0006] characterised by the bolt material -- This subclass defines anchoring bolts employed to protect roof, floor or shaft linings in fixed-construction earth-drilling shafts, where the distinguishing factor is the bolt material, such as steel, alloy, composite or other specific substances.
[E21D 21/0013] Protection against corrosion -- E21D 21/0013 relates to measures that prevent or reduce corrosion of anchoring bolts used to secure the roof, floor or lining of earth-drilling shafts. It includes protective coatings, corrosion-resistant alloys, cathodic protection and other treatments applied to the bolts and their mounting sites.
[E21D 21/002] using explosives charges -- The classification covers techniques that employ explosive charges to install anchoring bolts for the protection of roofs, floors or shaft linings in underground shafts. It relates to fixed-construction earth-drilling methods where controlled detonations secure bolts into the surrounding rock or soil.
[E21D 21/0026] characterised by constructional features of the bolts -- It relates to bolts used to anchor and protect the roof, floor or shaft-lining of a shaft in earth drilling. The classification covers bolts whose design includes specific constructional features such as shape, material, thread form or attachment method. It falls under fixed constructions for shaft anchoring.
[E21D 21/0033] having a jacket or outer tube -- E21D 21/0033 classifies shafts that incorporate a protective jacket or outer tube, a structure that encases the shaft lining to improve stability and resistance to collapse. The jacket may be combined with anchoring bolts that secure the roof, floor or lining, and is used primarily in fixed-construction earth-drilling projects where shaft integrity is critical.
[E21D 21/004] Bolts held in the borehole by friction all along their length, without additional fixing means -- The code E21D 21/004 classifies anchoring bolts that achieve fixation by friction along their entire length within a borehole, without any supplementary fixing devices. These bolts are used in fixed constructions such as earth-drilling shafts and shaft linings.
[E21D 21/0046] formed by a plurality of elements arranged longitudinally -- This subclass deals with anchoring bolts used to secure roof, floor or shaft linings, consisting of multiple longitudinally arranged elements such as shank, head, sleeve or expander that act together to transfer load into surrounding rock or concrete. It is part of the fixed constructions, earth drilling, shafts group.
[E21D 21/0053] Anchoring-bolts in the form of lost drilling rods -- It refers to anchoring bolts formed by leaving drilling rods in the borehole after drilling, providing permanent support for the roof, floor or lining of shafts used in fixed construction earth-drilling.
[E21D 21/006] Anchoring-bolts made of cables or wires -- E21D 21/006 covers anchoring bolts that are formed from cables or wires and used to secure roof, floor or shaft-lining structures in earth-drilling shafts. The classification applies to cable-type bolts that provide reinforcement or protection for the lining of shafts and similar underground constructions.
[E21D 21/0066] Anchoring-bolts formed by a bundle of radially arranged rigid elements -- This subclass defines anchoring bolts that consist of a bundle of rigid elements positioned radially outward, forming a single fastening unit. The bolts are intended to secure roof, floor or shaft-lining structures in earth-drilling shafts, providing fixed construction reinforcement.
[E21D 21/0073] Anchoring-bolts having an inflatable sleeve, e.g. hollow sleeve expanded by a fluid -- This CPC subclass covers anchoring bolts employed in shaft construction that incorporate an inflatable sleeve, which is expanded by a fluid to lock the bolt in place for roof, floor or shaft-lining protection.
[E21D 21/008] Anchoring or tensioning means -- Anchoring or tensioning means are components used to secure and apply tension to structural elements in fixed constructions such as earth-drilling shafts, roof, floor, or shaft-lining. They include bolts, plates, clamps or other devices that fasten or tension lining, reinforcement or equipment to the surrounding rock or concrete, ensuring stability and load transfer. The CPC code E21D 21/008 specifically covers these anchoring elements.
[E21D 21/0086] Bearing plates -- E21D 21/0086 covers bearing plates, flat metal elements that distribute loads from anchoring bolts used to secure roof, floor or shaft-lining protective structures in fixed earth-drilling constructions such as shafts. They help transfer forces from the bolts to the surrounding material.
[E21D 21/0093] Accessories -- This subclass covers accessories used with anchoring bolts that secure roof, floor, or shaft-lining elements in fixed earth-drilling shafts. It includes devices such as clamps, plates, spacers and other supporting fittings designed to protect and stabilize shaft linings.
[E21D 21/02] having means for indicating tension -- The subclass E21D 21/02 covers anchoring bolts used in roof, floor or shaft-lining protection that incorporate devices to indicate the tension applied to the bolt, such as load-cell sensors, visual gauges or mechanical indicators, allowing real-time monitoring of bolt preload during installation. This aids in ensuring proper anchorage and structural safety in shafts and earth-drilling constructions.
[E21D 23/00] Mine roof supports for step- by- step movement, e.g. in combination with provisions for shifting of conveyors, mining machines, or guides therefor -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Hydraulic jacks and frame for supporting the roof of the coal seam, as in longwall mining. A shearer cuts the coalface ahead of the support, and the support moves forward as the cutting progresses. The overburden collapses behind the support after passage of the same:
[E21D 23/0004] along the working face -- The CPC subclass E21D 23/0004 covers mine roof support structures that can be moved step-by-step along the working face, often with mechanisms for shifting attached conveyors, mining machines or guides as excavation advances. It is part of the fixed constructions group for earth-drilling shafts.
[E21D 23/0008] comprising a pair of joined struts -- This subclass concerns mine roof support systems that advance in a step-by-step manner, often integrated with shifting conveyors or mining machines, and that are formed from a pair of joined struts. The design provides a fixed construction within shafts for earth-drilling operations, enabling adjustable support as the roof is excavated.
[E21D 23/0013] Frame type supports -- E21D 23/0013 classifies frame-type mine roof supports designed for incremental, step-by-step movement, often combined with mechanisms that shift conveyors, mining machines, or guide systems along the shaft.
[E21D 23/0017] Pile type supports -- In the CPC, E21D 23/0017 covers pile-type roof supports used in underground shafts for earth-drilling operations. These supports are designed to advance step by step, often incorporating mechanisms that allow conveyors, mining machines or guides to be shifted as the roof is secured.
[E21D 23/0021] comprising two walking elements -- This CPC subclass E21D 23/0021 falls under Fixed Constructions - Earth Drilling - Shafts and covers mine roof support systems that advance in a step-by-step fashion, often linked to movable conveyors, mining machines or guides. The defining characteristic of the subclass is that the support comprises two walking elements which enable the incremental movement.
[E21D 23/0026] comprising three walking elements -- A mine-roof support designed for shafts that advances incrementally, allowing the roof, conveyors or mining machines to be shifted step by step. The device incorporates three walking elements that provide controlled, step-by-step movement while maintaining roof stability. This fixed-construction solution is classified under earth-drilling shaft equipment.
[E21D 23/003] consisting of a rigid pile -- E21D 23/003 designates mine roof supports formed as a rigid pile that can be advanced step-by-step, often together with provisions for shifting conveyors, mining machines or guide systems. The classification falls under fixed constructions for earth-drilling shafts and addresses mechanisms that allow controlled, incremental movement of the support.
[E21D 23/0034] comprising a goaf shield articulated to a base member -- The subclass E21D 23/0034 covers mine-roof support systems that move in a step-by-step fashion and include a goaf shield hinged to a base member, enabling the shield to advance while remaining attached to the roof. It is intended for use in shaft constructions where the support can be coordinated with shifting conveyors, mining machines or guide rails.
[E21D 23/0039] and supported by a strut or by a row of struts parallel to the working face -- This subclass covers mine roof support installations that advance incrementally and include one or a series of struts aligned parallel to the working face for added stability, often used with conveyor or machine shifting provisions.
[E21D 23/0043] and supported by two or more rows of struts parallel to the working face -- Mine roof support systems designed for step-by-step advancement feature two or more parallel rows of struts positioned alongside the working face, providing a fixed construction that can accommodate shifting conveyors, mining machines or guides while maintaining roof stability.
[E21D 23/0047] without essential shifting devices -- Mine roof supports designed for step-by-step advancement in underground shafts, allowing incremental relocation of the support and associated equipment such as conveyors or mining machines, but lacking any integral shifting mechanisms for larger repositioning. This subclass covers designs that rely on manual or external means to move the support rather than built-in shifting devices.
[E21D 23/0052] with advancing shifting devices connected therewith -- This subclass covers mine roof supports designed for step-by-step movement, with integrated advancing shifting devices that connect to conveyors, mining machines or their guides, allowing the support to be repositioned as the excavation progresses.
[E21D 23/0056] with individual advancing shifting devices -- The entry E21D 23/0056 covers mine roof supports that advance in a step-by-step manner, equipped with individual shifting devices that allow precise repositioning of conveyors, mining machines or guides. It falls under fixed constructions for earth-drilling shafts, focusing on systems that enable incremental movement of roof support structures.
[E21D 23/006] provided with essential hydraulic devices -- The E21D 23/006 subclass covers mine roof support structures that move incrementally, often coordinated with shifting conveyors, mining machines or guides, and that incorporate essential hydraulic devices for the step-by-step adjustment of the support. It falls under fixed constructions for earth-drilling shafts.
[E21D 23/0065] driven, or automatically, e.g. electrically-hydraulically controlled -- This subclass covers mine roof support systems that move step-by-step along a shaft and are powered or automatically operated, typically using electric or hydraulic drives to shift supports and associated equipment such as conveyors or guides.
[E21D 23/0069] without essential advancing shifting devices -- E21D 23/0069 classifies mine roof-support structures that are capable of step-by-step movement but do not incorporate essential advancing or shifting devices within the support itself, requiring external conveyors, mining machines or guides to effect the shift. It falls under fixed constructions for earth-drilling shafts.
[E21D 23/0073] with advancing shifting devices connected therewith -- This subclass relates to mine roof support structures that incorporate advancing or shifting mechanisms allowing step-by-step movement along a shaft, often integrated with provisions for shifting conveyors, mining machines or guides. It applies to fixed constructions used in earth-drilling shafts where the support can be repositioned during operation.
[E21D 23/0078] with individual shifting devices -- The classification E21D 23/0078 covers mine roof support systems that incorporate individual shifting devices, allowing the support to move step-by-step. It includes arrangements that enable the roof support to be adjusted while shifting conveyors, mining machines or guides attached to it. This falls under fixed constructions for earth-drilling shafts.

[E21F 13/00] Transport specially adapted to underground conditions -- **Definition:** This place covers:  Transport of staff, equipment or extracted rocks or minerals in underground operations. The transport can be vertical, inclined or horizontal. It can be continuous by conveyors or discontinuous by shuttle cars.  This main groups and its subgroups also includes the constructive details of scraper chain conveyors for collecting the coal as it is cut in longwall operations.  **Limiting references (this place does not cover):** - Transport in general, loading unless specially adapted to underground conditions -> B65G - Removal and transport of material extracted during tunnel excavation -> E21D9/12  **Glossary:** - Highwall mining: Method of mining by which a train typically formed by a continuous miner and multiple conveyors acting as pushbeams is propelled into a coal seam from the outside
